  plaintext  string          Data to encrypt
  password   string          User-provided password (derived to a 256-bit key via scrypt)
  options    EncryptOptions  Optional configuration

EncryptOptions fields:
  algorithm  'aes-256-gcm' | 'chacha20-poly1305' | 'xchacha20-poly1305'
             Default: 'aes-256-gcm'. Choose chacha20-poly1305 on platforms without hardware AES acceleration.

Returns: EncryptedData
  algorithm   'aes-256-gcm' | 'chacha20-poly1305' | 'xchacha20-poly1305'
  ciphertext  string   Base64-encoded ciphertext (includes GCM/Poly1305 authentication tag)
  nonce       string   Base64-encoded nonce/IV
  salt        string   Base64-encoded 32-byte salt
  kdf         'scrypt'
  kdfParams   { N, r, p }   scrypt cost parameters (N=131072, r=8, p=1 by default)

--- decrypt ---

Decrypt an EncryptedData envelope produced by encrypt. Reads KDF parameters from the envelope, re-derives the key, and authenticates + decrypts.

  import { decrypt } from '@reactjit/crypto';

  const plaintext: string = decrypt(data: EncryptedData, password: string);

Parameters:
  data      EncryptedData   Envelope from encrypt (or stored JSON)
  password  string          The same password used to encrypt

Returns: string — decrypted plaintext
Throws on wrong password (authentication tag mismatch) or malformed input.

--- encryptRaw ---

Encrypt raw bytes with a caller-managed 32-byte key. No KDF step. Use this when you already have a key from a Diffie-Hellman exchange or another key derivation flow.

  import { encryptRaw } from '@reactjit/crypto';

  const { ciphertext, nonce } = encryptRaw(
    plaintext: Uint8Array,
    key: Uint8Array,
    algo?: 'aes-256-gcm' | 'chacha20-poly1305' | 'xchacha20-poly1305',
  );

Parameters:
  plaintext  Uint8Array   Data to encrypt
  key        Uint8Array   32-byte key
  algo       string       Encryption algorithm. Default: 'aes-256-gcm'

Returns: { ciphertext: Uint8Array, nonce: Uint8Array }

--- decryptRaw ---

Decrypt raw bytes with a caller-managed key and nonce.

  import { decryptRaw } from '@reactjit/crypto';

  const plaintext: Uint8Array = decryptRaw(
    ciphertext: Uint8Array,
    key: Uint8Array,
    nonce: Uint8Array,
    algo?: 'aes-256-gcm' | 'chacha20-poly1305' | 'xchacha20-poly1305',
  );

Parameters:
  ciphertext  Uint8Array
  key         Uint8Array   32-byte key
  nonce       Uint8Array   Nonce used during encryption
  algo        string       Must match the algorithm used during encryption. Default: 'aes-256-gcm'

Returns: Uint8Array — decrypted bytes
Throws on authentication failure.

--- generateSigningKeys ---

Generate an Ed25519 key pair for digital signatures.

  import { generateSigningKeys } from '@reactjit/crypto';

  const keys: KeyPair = generateSigningKeys();

Parameters: none

Returns: KeyPair
  publicKey   string   Hex-encoded Ed25519 public key (64 chars)
  privateKey  string   Hex-encoded Ed25519 private key (64 chars)
  curve       'ed25519'

--- sign ---

Sign a message string with an Ed25519 private key.

  import { sign } from '@reactjit/crypto';

  const signed: SignedMessage = sign(privateKeyHex: string, message: string);

Parameters:
  privateKeyHex  string   Hex-encoded private key from generateSigningKeys
  message        string   Message to sign

Returns: SignedMessage
  message    string   The original message
  signature  string   Hex-encoded Ed25519 signature (128 chars)
  publicKey  string   Hex-encoded public key derived from the private key
  algorithm  'ed25519'

--- verify ---

Verify a SignedMessage produced by sign.

  import { verify } from '@reactjit/crypto';

  const valid: boolean = verify(signed: SignedMessage);

Parameters:
  signed  SignedMessage   Envelope from sign (message + signature + publicKey)

Returns: boolean

--- verifyDetached ---

Verify a detached signature (when you have the three components separately rather than a SignedMessage envelope).

  import { verifyDetached } from '@reactjit/crypto';

  const valid: boolean = verifyDetached(
    message: string,
    signatureHex: string,
    publicKeyHex: string,
  );

Parameters:
  message       string   Original message
  signatureHex  string   Hex-encoded signature
  publicKeyHex  string   Hex-encoded public key

Returns: boolean

--- generateDHKeys ---

Generate an X25519 key pair for Diffie-Hellman key exchange.

  import { generateDHKeys } from '@reactjit/crypto';

  const keys: KeyPair = generateDHKeys();

Parameters: none

Returns: KeyPair
  publicKey   string   Hex-encoded X25519 public key (64 chars)
  privateKey  string   Hex-encoded X25519 private key (64 chars)
  curve       'x25519'

--- diffieHellman ---

Compute a shared secret from your X25519 private key and the other party's public key. Both sides derive the same secret without transmitting it.

  import { diffieHellman } from '@reactjit/crypto';

  const sharedSecret: string = diffieHellman(privateKeyHex: string, publicKeyHex: string);

Parameters:
  privateKeyHex  string   Your hex-encoded X25519 private key
  publicKeyHex   string   The other party's hex-encoded X25519 public key

Returns: string — hex-encoded 32-byte shared secret

--- randomBytes ---

Generate cryptographically random bytes. Re-exported from @noble/hashes.

  import { randomBytes } from '@reactjit/crypto';

  const bytes: Uint8Array = randomBytes(length: number);

Parameters:
  length  number   Number of bytes

Returns: Uint8Array

--- randomToken ---

Generate a cryptographically random hex string. Use for API keys, session IDs, and CSRF tokens.

  import { randomToken } from '@reactjit/crypto';

  const token: string = randomToken(bytes?: number);

Parameters:
  bytes  number   Byte length. Default: 32 (produces a 64-char hex string)

Returns: string — lowercase hex (length = bytes * 2)

--- randomBase64 ---

Generate a cryptographically random base64 string.

  import { randomBase64 } from '@reactjit/crypto';

  const token: string = randomBase64(bytes?: number);

Parameters:
  bytes  number   Byte length. Default: 32 (produces ~44 base64 chars)

Returns: string

--- randomId ---

Generate a cryptographically random alphanumeric string. URL-safe with no special characters.

  import { randomId } from '@reactjit/crypto';

  const id: string = randomId(length?: number);

Parameters:
  length  number   Character length of the output. Default: 16

Returns: string — alphanumeric characters only (A-Z, a-z, 0-9)

--- Encoding utilities ---

  import { toHex, fromHex, toBase64, fromBase64 } from '@reactjit/crypto';

  toHex(bytes: Uint8Array): string            — Uint8Array to lowercase hex
  fromHex(hex: string): Uint8Array            — hex string to Uint8Array
  toBase64(bytes: Uint8Array): string         — Uint8Array to base64 string
  fromBase64(base64: string): Uint8Array      — base64 string to Uint8Array
seeAlso: useWebhook — uses hmacSHA256 for incoming webhook signature verification
useStorage — pairs well with encrypt/decrypt for persisting sensitive settings
useFetch — for authenticated API requests where you need to sign request bodies

metadata.title: Hooks
metadata.description: Overview of ReactJIT hooks for Love2D communication, state sharing, and event handling.

sections.overview:
ReactJIT provides a set of React hooks for communicating between the React component tree and the Lua runtime (or any target-specific backend). These hooks consume the bridge interface (IBridge) via React context, so they work identically regardless of the transport layer (Module.FS for web, QuickJS FFI for native Love2D, WebSocket for remote targets).

The hooks cover three patterns: event subscription (useLove, useLoveEvent), RPC calls (useLoveRPC), and shared state (useLoveState). Additional utility hooks provide readiness detection (useLoveReady), fire-and-forget sending (useLoveSend), and DOM overlay positioning (useLoveOverlays, web mode only).
sections.api:
All hooks are imported from '@reactjit/core':

  import {
    useLove,
    useLoveEvent,
    useLoveRPC,
    useLoveState,
    useLoveReady,
    useLoveSend,
    useLoveOverlays,
  } from '@reactjit/core';

All hooks require a BridgeProvider ancestor in the component tree, which is set up
automatically by the framework's entry point.

examples.title: Subscribe to events and send commands
Bidirectional shared state

platformNotes.by target:
- Love2D native: QuickJS FFI bridge (bridge_quickjs.lua)
- Web: Module.FS bridge (in-memory event queue)
- Terminal/grid targets: Bridge over IPC or WebSocket
useLoveOverlays is only meaningful in web mode where React controls the DOM and needs to
position HTML overlays relative to a canvas element.

criticalRules: All hooks require BridgeProvider context -- they will throw if used outside it
useLoveEvent uses a ref for the handler to avoid re-subscribing on handler changes
useLoveState updates both local React state and Lua-side state bidirectionally
useLoveRPC returns an async function -- always await the result
Hook event type strings must match the event types dispatched by the Lua side
seeAlso: uselove.txt -- Event subscription with send function

metadata.title: Media Hooks
metadata.description: Hooks for scanning media libraries, listing archive contents, reading files from inside archives, and deep-indexing directories.

sections.overview: The @reactjit/media package provides two sets of hooks: archive hooks for working with compressed files (RAR, ZIP, 7z, TAR, and others), and media library hooks for scanning directories of media files. The archive hooks delegate to a Lua-side libarchive integration; the scan hooks walk the filesystem. useMediaIndex combines both — it looks inside archives to surface their contents as first-class MediaFile entries alongside regular files.
sections.api:
--- useArchive ---

List all entries inside an archive. Fetches automatically when filepath changes. Provides a refresh function for manual re-fetching.

  import { useArchive } from '@reactjit/media';

  const { entries, loading, error, refresh } = useArchive(filepath: string | null);

Parameters:
  filepath  string | null  Absolute path to the archive file. Pass null to idle.

Returns:
  entries   ArchiveEntry[]  All entries in the archive (files, directories, symlinks)
  loading   boolean
  error     string | null
  refresh   () => Promise<void>   Re-fetch the entry list

ArchiveEntry fields:
  path       string                        Path within the archive
  size       number                        Size in bytes
  type       'file' | 'directory' | 'symlink'
  mtime      number                        Modification timestamp (Unix seconds)
  encrypted  boolean

--- useArchiveInfo ---

Fetch a summary of an archive without listing every entry. Returns counts, total size, and an extension histogram.

  import { useArchiveInfo } from '@reactjit/media';

  const { info, loading, error } = useArchiveInfo(filepath: string | null);

Parameters:
  filepath  string | null

Returns:
  info     ArchiveInfo | null
  loading  boolean
  error    string | null

ArchiveInfo fields:
  totalEntries  number
  totalSize     number                        Total uncompressed bytes
  fileCount     number
  dirCount      number
  extensions    Record<string, number>        Counts per extension, e.g. { mp4: 3, srt: 3 }

--- useArchiveRead ---

Returns an imperative function that reads a single file from inside an archive. Call it on demand rather than on mount. Throws on error.

  import { useArchiveRead } from '@reactjit/media';

  const read = useArchiveRead();
  const { content, size } = await read(filepath, entryPath, maxBytes?);

Parameters (of the returned function):
  filepath   string   Absolute path to the archive
  entryPath  string   Path of the entry within the archive
  maxBytes   number   (optional) Byte limit on the returned content

Returns: Promise<{ content: string; size: number }>
  content  string   File content as a string
  size     number   Full entry size in bytes (may exceed maxBytes if truncated)

Throws: Error with message from Lua if the entry cannot be read.

--- useArchiveSearch ---

Search archive entries by a Lua pattern. Fetches automatically when filepath or pattern changes.

  import { useArchiveSearch } from '@reactjit/media';

  const { matches, loading } = useArchiveSearch(filepath: string | null, pattern: string);

Parameters:
  filepath  string | null  Archive to search. Pass null to idle.
  pattern   string         Lua pattern matched against each entry path (e.g. '%.mp4$')

Returns:
  matches  ArchiveEntry[]
  loading  boolean

--- useMediaLibrary ---

Scan a directory for media files. Returns typed MediaFile entries with source set to 'filesystem'. Does not look inside archives; use useMediaIndex for that.

  import { useMediaLibrary } from '@reactjit/media';

  const { files, stats, loading, error, rescan } = useMediaLibrary(
    directory: string | null,
    options?: MediaLibraryOptions,
  );

Parameters:
  directory  string | null
  options    MediaLibraryOptions (optional)

MediaLibraryOptions fields:
  recursive  boolean      Scan subdirectories (default true)
  maxDepth   number       Maximum recursion depth (default 10)
  autoScan   boolean      Scan on mount (default true)
  filter     MediaType[]  Only return entries of these types

Returns:
  files    MediaFile[]   All scanned files matching the filter
  stats    DirStats      Computed from the returned files (see below)
  loading  boolean
  error    string | null
  rescan   () => Promise<void>

MediaFile fields:
  path          string                        Full filesystem path
  name          string                        Filename only
  size          number                        Bytes
  mtime         number | undefined            Modification timestamp (Unix seconds)
  type          MediaType                     Classified type
  source        'filesystem' | 'archive'
  archivePath   string | undefined            Set when source is 'archive'
  archiveEntry  string | undefined            Set when source is 'archive'

MediaType values:
  'video' | 'audio' | 'image' | 'subtitle' | 'document' | 'archive' | 'metadata' | 'unknown'

DirStats fields:
  total        number
  byType       Partial<Record<MediaType, number>>
  totalSize    number
  largestFile  MediaFile | null

--- useMediaStats ---

Fetch quick directory statistics without loading every file. Lighter than useMediaLibrary when you only need counts and sizes.

  import { useMediaStats } from '@reactjit/media';

  const { stats, loading } = useMediaStats(directory: string | null);

Parameters:
  directory  string | null

Returns:
  stats    DirStats | null
  loading  boolean

--- useMediaIndex ---

Deep-index a directory, scanning both filesystem files and the contents of archives. Archive contents appear as MediaFile entries with source set to 'archive'. This is the archive walker.

  import { useMediaIndex } from '@reactjit/media';

  const { index, loading, error, rescan } = useMediaIndex(
    directory: string | null,
    options?: MediaIndexOptions,
  );

Parameters:
  directory  string | null
  options    MediaIndexOptions (optional)

MediaIndexOptions fields:
  indexArchives    boolean   Look inside archives (default true)
  archivePattern   string    Only index archives whose filename matches this Lua pattern
  filter           MediaType[]   Filter results by type

Returns:
  index    MediaFile[]   Flat list of all discovered files including archive contents
  loading  boolean
  error    string | null
  rescan   () => Promise<void>
seeAlso: useLoveRPC — low-level RPC bridge used internally by all media hooks
classifyFile — exported utility that classifies a filename to a MediaType without an RPC call
formatSize — exported utility that formats a byte count to a human-readable string (KB/MB/GB/TB)

metadata.title: RSS Hooks
metadata.description: Hooks for subscribing to RSS 2.0, Atom, and RSS 1.0 feeds with polling, deduplication, and multi-feed aggregation.

sections.overview: The @reactjit/rss package turns any RSS/Atom feed URL into a reactive data source. It ships a zero-dependency XML parser that runs in QuickJS (Love2D), Node.js, and browsers without requiring DOMParser. The two main hooks — useRSSFeed and useRSSAggregate — handle fetching, polling, deduplication, and limit enforcement. Imperative helpers (fetchFeed, parseFeed) and OPML import/export utilities round out the package for building complete feed readers.
sections.api:
--- useRSSFeed ---

Subscribe to a single RSS, Atom, or RSS 1.0 feed. Fetches immediately on mount, re-fetches on url change or manual refetch, and polls if an interval is configured. New items are merged on top of the existing list with deduplication by item ID.

  import { useRSSFeed } from '@reactjit/rss';

  const result: RSSFeedResult = useRSSFeed(url: string | null, options?: RSSFeedOptions);

Parameters:
  url      string | null     Feed URL to fetch. Pass null to disable and reset state.
  options  RSSFeedOptions    Optional configuration (see below)

RSSFeedOptions fields:
  interval   number    Polling interval in milliseconds. 0 or omitted = no polling.
  limit      number    Maximum items to keep (newest first). Default: keep all.
  dedupe     boolean   Deduplicate items by their id/guid across polls. Default: true.
  corsProxy  string    CORS proxy prefix prepended to the URL (e.g. 'https://corsproxy.io/?'). Useful for the web target.

Returns: RSSFeedResult
  feed     Feed | null   Parsed feed metadata, or null while loading/on error
  items    FeedItem[]    Items from the feed (empty array until first successful fetch)
  loading  boolean       True during the initial fetch and each re-fetch
  error    Error | null  Last fetch or parse error, or null on success
  refetch  () => void    Trigger an immediate re-fetch outside the polling interval

Feed fields:
  title          string
  description    string
  link           string
  language       string | null
  lastBuildDate  string | null   ISO date string
  image          string | null   Feed logo URL
  type           'rss2' | 'atom' | 'rss1' | 'unknown'
  items          FeedItem[]

FeedItem fields:
  id           string              Unique identifier (guid/id, falls back to link or title)
  title        string
  link         string
  description  string              Summary or full HTML content
  content      string | null       Full content (content:encoded or Atom content element)
  pubDate      string | null       ISO date string
  author       string | null
  categories   string[]
  enclosure    { url, type, length } | null   Podcast audio, attached files, etc.
  thumbnail    string | null       Image URL from media:thumbnail, media:content, or itunes:image

--- useRSSAggregate ---

Subscribe to multiple feeds simultaneously and receive a single merged, chronologically sorted item list. All feeds are fetched in parallel. Each feed failure is isolated — one broken feed does not prevent the others from rendering.

  import { useRSSAggregate } from '@reactjit/rss';

  const result: RSSAggregateResult = useRSSAggregate(urls: string[], options?: RSSAggregateOptions);

Parameters:
  urls     string[]              Array of feed URLs to subscribe to
  options  RSSAggregateOptions   Optional configuration (see below)

RSSAggregateOptions fields:
  interval   number   Polling interval in milliseconds. Applied to all feeds. Default: no polling.
  limit      number   Maximum total items after merge. Default: keep all.
  corsProxy  string   CORS proxy prefix applied to every URL.

Returns: RSSAggregateResult
  items    Array<FeedItem & { feedTitle: string; feedUrl: string }>
           Merged item list sorted by pubDate descending. Each item carries feedTitle and feedUrl
           from its originating feed.
  feeds    Array<{ url: string; feed: Feed | null; error: Error | null }>
           Per-feed status — inspect individual failures without losing the rest.
  loading  boolean     True while any feed is still being fetched on the current cycle.
  refetch  () => void  Trigger an immediate re-fetch of all feeds.

--- fetchFeed (imperative) ---

Fetch and parse a feed outside of React. Returns a Promise<Feed>. Use this in event handlers, background tasks, or server-side scripts where hooks are not available.

  import { fetchFeed } from '@reactjit/rss';

  const feed: Feed = await fetchFeed(url: string, corsProxy?: string);

Parameters:
  url        string   Feed URL to fetch
  corsProxy  string   Optional CORS proxy prefix

Returns: Promise<Feed>
Throws on network errors or non-2xx HTTP responses.

--- parseFeed (utility) ---

Parse a raw XML string into a normalized Feed object. Supports RSS 2.0, Atom 1.0, and RSS 1.0 (RDF). No DOMParser — works in QuickJS.

  import { parseFeed } from '@reactjit/rss';

  const feed: Feed = parseFeed(xml: string);

Parameters:
  xml  string  Raw XML string (RSS or Atom document)

Returns: Feed — never throws; returns an empty feed with type 'unknown' on unrecognized input.

--- parseOPML (utility) ---

Parse an OPML subscription list into an array of feed descriptors. Useful for importing browser bookmark exports or shared subscription bundles.

  import { parseOPML } from '@reactjit/rss';

  const outlines: OPMLOutline[] = parseOPML(xml: string);

Parameters:
  xml  string  Raw OPML XML string

Returns: OPMLOutline[]

OPMLOutline fields:
  text      string   Display name
  title     string   Feed title (falls back to text)
  xmlUrl    string   RSS/Atom feed URL
  htmlUrl   string   Website URL
  type      string   Usually 'rss'
  category  string   Optional category tag

--- generateOPML (utility) ---

Serialize a list of subscriptions as an OPML 2.0 document. Use this to export a user's feed list for sharing or backup.

  import { generateOPML } from '@reactjit/rss';

  const xml: string = generateOPML(
    title: string,
    feeds: Array<{ title: string; xmlUrl: string; htmlUrl?: string; category?: string }>,
  );

Parameters:
  title  string   Title for the OPML document
  feeds  Array    Subscription entries

Returns: string — valid OPML 2.0 XML
seeAlso: useFetch — general-purpose HTTP hook for non-XML APIs
useWebSocket — live data streams that don't require polling

metadata.title: Server Hooks
metadata.description: HTTP server hooks for Love2D — static file serving, dynamic routes, and media library indexing

sections.overview:
@reactjit/server provides React hooks that start and manage a non-blocking HTTP server running inside Love2D via lua/httpserver.lua. Static files (media, assets) are served entirely from the Lua side with zero bridge overhead. Dynamic API routes forward requests across the QuickJS bridge to React handlers that return response objects. useLibrary adds on-demand filesystem indexing so you can build searchable media servers with a single call.

Import: import { useServer, useStaticServer, useLibrary } from '@reactjit/server';
sections.api:
---- useServer(config) -------------------------------------------------------

Start an HTTP server. The server is bound on mount and torn down on unmount.
Changing port or host restarts the server; changing routes or static entries
does not restart it (route table updates are live).

  useServer(config: ServerConfig | null): UseServerResult

  Arguments:
    config  ServerConfig | null   Pass null to disable the server without
                                  unmounting the hook.

  ServerConfig:
    port     number           Required. TCP port to listen on.
    host?    string           Bind address. Default: '0.0.0.0' (all interfaces).
    routes?  Route[]          Dynamic API routes handled in React.
    static?  StaticRoute[]    Static directories served from Lua.

  Route:
    method?   string         HTTP method filter ('GET', 'POST', ...).
                             Omit to match any method.
    path      string         Path pattern. Supports :param and * wildcards.
    handler   RouteHandler   Function called when a request matches.

  RouteHandler:
    (req: HttpRequest) => HttpResponse | Promise<HttpResponse>

  HttpRequest:
    clientId  number                   Internal client socket id.
    serverId  string                   Internal server instance id.
    method    string                   HTTP method (uppercase).
    path      string                   Matched path with params resolved.
    rawPath   string                   Exact path as received from the client.
    query     Record<string, string>   Parsed query string parameters.
    headers   Record<string, string>   Request headers (lowercase keys).
    body      string                   Raw request body string.
    params    Record<string, string>   Named URL parameters from the route pattern.
    route     string                   The route pattern that matched.

  HttpResponse:
    status    number                     HTTP status code.
    headers?  Record<string, string>     Response headers.
    body?     string                     Response body string.

  StaticRoute:
    path   string   URL prefix to match (e.g. '/media', '/').
    root   string   Absolute OS filesystem path (e.g. '/home/user/music').

  UseServerResult:
    ready     boolean         True once the socket is bound and listening.
    port      number | null   Listening port, or null if not ready.
    requests  HttpRequest[]   Recent requests, most recent first. Capped at 50.
    close     () => void      Manually shut down the server.

---- useStaticServer(port, root, host?) -------------------------------------

One-liner for serving a single directory over HTTP. A convenience wrapper
around useServer with a single static route at path '/'.

  useStaticServer(
    port:   number | null,
    root:   string,
    host?:  string,
  ): UseServerResult

  Arguments:
    port   number | null   Port to listen on. Pass null to disable.
    root   string          Absolute path to the directory to serve.
    host   string          Bind address. Default: '0.0.0.0'.

  Returns the same UseServerResult as useServer.

---- useLibrary(port, directories, host?) -----------------------------------

Index one or more directories, start a server that serves the files directly,
and expose a searchable JSON index endpoint. Files are served by Lua (zero
bridge overhead). The React side receives a full in-memory file index with
category breakdowns and per-directory stats.

  useLibrary(
    port:         number | null,
    directories:  string[],
    host?:        string,
  ): UseLibraryResult

  Arguments:
    port         number | null   Port. Pass null to disable.
    directories  string[]        Absolute OS paths to index and serve.
                                 Directory name (last path segment) becomes
                                 the URL namespace.
    host         string          Bind address. Default: '0.0.0.0'.

  UseLibraryResult:
    ready        boolean              True once server is up and index is built.
    port         number | null        Listening port, or null if not ready.
    files        IndexedFile[]        Full flat file index.
    stats        LibraryIndex.stats   Count totals by category.
    directories  DirectoryStats[]     Per-directory name/path/count/size.
    reindex      () => Promise<void>  Re-scan all directories and rebuild index.
    close        () => void           Shut down the server.

  IndexedFile:
    name      string        Filename with extension (e.g. 'song.mp3').
    path      string        Absolute OS path.
    relPath   string        Path relative to the indexed directory root.
    size      number        File size in bytes.
    modified  number        Last modified epoch seconds.
    ext       string | null Lowercase file extension (e.g. 'mp3'), or null.
    category  FileCategory  'audio' | 'video' | 'image' | 'document' | 'other'
    dir       string        Name of the parent directory (matches URL namespace).

  DirectoryStats:
    name   string   Directory name (last path segment).
    path   string   Absolute OS path.
    count  number   Total files indexed in this directory.
    size   number   Total bytes of all files.

  Auto-served endpoints (directory name derived from last path segment):
    GET /files/<dir>/<relPath>   Serve actual file (Lua, zero bridge).
    GET /api/library             Full JSON index.
    GET /api/library?type=audio  Filter by category (audio/video/image/...).
    GET /api/library?dir=music   Filter by directory name.
    GET /api/library?q=<term>    Search by filename (case-insensitive).

metadata.title: Storage Hooks
metadata.description: Schema-validated CRUD storage with pluggable adapters for every target

sections.overview:
@reactjit/storage provides schema-validated CRUD operations with reactive React hooks and a plain-JS interface for non-React code. The same API works on every target: Love2D stores data in love.filesystem, terminals use SQLite via Node.js built-in node:sqlite, web targets use localStorage or IndexedDB, and any target can use the in-memory adapter for tests or ephemeral state. Schema validation uses the built-in z builder — a lightweight Zod-inspired system that gives you runtime validation and TypeScript type inference with no external dependencies.

Import: import { StorageProvider, useCRUD, useStorage, createCRUD, z, MemoryAdapter, Love2DFileAdapter, TerminalSQLiteAdapter, LocalStorageAdapter, IndexedDBAdapter } from '@reactjit/storage';
sections.api:
---- z — Schema builder ----------------------------------------------------

The z object is the entry point for defining schemas. All methods return a
Schema<T> instance that carries both a TypeScript type and a runtime parse
function.

  z.string()                       StringSchema
  z.number()                       NumberSchema
  z.boolean()                      BooleanSchema
  z.date()                         DateSchema
  z.array(itemSchema)              ArraySchema<T>
  z.object(shape)                  ObjectSchema<S>
  z.enum(values)                   EnumSchema<T>
  z.literal(value)                 LiteralSchema<T>

Common modifiers (chainable on any schema):
  .optional()                      Makes field optional (T | undefined)
  .nullable()                      Allows null (T | null)
  .default(value)                  Provides a default (implies optional)

String-specific:
  .min(n)                          Minimum character count
  .max(n)                          Maximum character count
  .email()                         Email format check
  .url()                           http/https URL format check
  .matches(regexp, message?)       Custom regex

Number-specific:
  .min(n)                          Minimum value (inclusive)
  .max(n)                          Maximum value (inclusive)
  .int()                           Integer only

Array-specific:
  .min(n)                          Minimum item count

Object-specific:
  .strict()                        Strip unknown fields
  .merge(other)                    Merge two object schemas
  .pick(...keys)                   New schema with only named keys
  .omit(...keys)                   New schema without named keys
  .partial()                       Make all fields optional

Schema methods:
  schema.parse(value): T           Parse and validate. Throws ValidationError.
  schema.safeParse(value):         Returns { success, data } | { success, error }
    SafeParseResult<T>

Type inference:
  type MyType = z.infer<typeof MySchema>

---- StorageProvider -------------------------------------------------------

Context provider. Wrap your app or a subtree with one StorageProvider. All
useCRUD and useStorage calls in that subtree will use the provided adapter by
default.

  <StorageProvider adapter={StorageAdapter}>
    {children}
  </StorageProvider>

  Props:
    adapter   StorageAdapter   Required. See adapter section below.
    children  ReactNode        Required.

---- useStorage() ----------------------------------------------------------

Returns the raw StorageAdapter from context. Use this when you need direct
adapter access beyond what useCRUD provides.

  useStorage(): StorageAdapter

  Throws if called outside a <StorageProvider>.

  StorageAdapter interface:
    get(collection, id): Promise<any | null>
    set(collection, id, data): Promise<void>
    delete(collection, id): Promise<boolean>
    list(collection, query?): Promise<any[]>
    format?: StorageFormat             // 'json' | 'markdown' | 'text'

---- useCRUD<T>(collection, schema, options?) --------------------------------

Full CRUD interface for a single collection. All operations validate data
against the provided schema. Returns an object with async methods and two
reactive query hooks for subscribing to data in components.

  useCRUD<T extends Record<string, any>>(
    collection: string,
    schema:     Schema<T>,
    options?:   CRUDOptions,
  ): CRUDHandle<T>

  Arguments:
    collection  string       Collection name (acts as a namespace / table name).
    schema      Schema<T>    z.object(...) schema for type T.
    options     CRUDOptions  Optional overrides.

  CRUDOptions:
    adapter?:      StorageAdapter                  Override context adapter.
    format?:       'json' | 'markdown' | 'text'   Storage format.
    migrations?:   Record<number, MigrationFn>     Version migration map.
    autoMigrate?:  boolean                         Apply migrations on read.

  CRUDHandle<T>:
    create(data: T): Promise<string>
      Validates data, generates an id if not present, writes record.
      Returns the id string.

    get(id: string): Promise<T | null>
      Reads and validates one record. Returns null if not found.

    update(id: string, partial: Partial<T>): Promise<void>
      Merges partial into existing record, re-validates, writes back.
      Throws if record does not exist.

    delete(id: string): Promise<void>
      Removes a record. Resolves even if the record was not found.

    list(query?: Query): Promise<T[]>
      Returns all records matching the optional query, schema-validated.

    useQuery(id: string): QueryResult<T>
      Reactive hook. Fetches one record and subscribes to refetch.
      QueryResult<T>: { data: T | null; loading: boolean;
                        error: Error | null; refetch: () => void }

    useListQuery(query?: Query): ListResult<T>
      Reactive hook. Fetches all matching records.
      ListResult<T>: { data: T[]; loading: boolean;
                       error: Error | null; refetch: () => void }

  Note: useQuery and useListQuery are hooks returned from useCRUD, not
  standalone hooks. They follow React hook rules and must be called
  unconditionally inside a component.

---- createCRUD<T>(collection, schema, adapter, options?) -------------------

Non-React version of useCRUD. Identical CRUD operations (create, get, update,
delete, list) but without useQuery / useListQuery. Use in scripts, CLI tools,
background workers, or any context without a React tree.

  createCRUD<T extends Record<string, any>>(
    collection: string,
    schema:     Schema<T>,
    adapter:    StorageAdapter,
    options?:   CreateCRUDOptions,
  ): CRUDMethods<T>

  CreateCRUDOptions:
    migrations?:   Record<number, MigrationFn>
    autoMigrate?:  boolean

  CRUDMethods<T>:
    create(data: T): Promise<string>
    get(id: string): Promise<T | null>
    update(id: string, partial: Partial<T>): Promise<void>
    delete(id: string): Promise<void>
    list(query?: Query): Promise<T[]>

---- Query -----------------------------------------------------------------

All list() calls and useListQuery() accept an optional Query object.

  interface Query {
    where?:   WhereClause        Field-level filter conditions.
    orderBy?: string             Field name to sort by.
    order?:   'asc' | 'desc'    Sort direction. Default: 'asc'.
    limit?:   number             Maximum records to return.
    offset?:  number             Number of records to skip.
  }

  WhereClause:
    Record<string, value | Partial<Record<ComparisonOp, value>>>

  ComparisonOp values:
    '$eq'        Equal (same as plain value)
    '$ne'        Not equal
    '$gt'        Greater than
    '$gte'       Greater than or equal
    '$lt'        Less than
    '$lte'       Less than or equal
    '$in'        Value is in array
    '$contains'  String contains substring

---- Adapters ---------------------------------------------------------------

MemoryAdapter
  In-memory. No persistence. Works on every target. Useful for tests and
  development.

    new MemoryAdapter()
    .clear()          Wipes all data across all collections.

Love2DFileAdapter
  Love2D target only. Reads/writes files via love.filesystem RPC.
  Data lives in the Love2D save directory: save/<collection>/<id>.<ext>

    new Love2DFileAdapter({ rpc, format? })

    Options:
      rpc     (method, args?) => Promise<T>   Required. Pass the result of
                                              useLoveRPC or bridge.rpc.
      format  'json' | 'markdown' | 'text'   Default: 'json'.

TerminalSQLiteAdapter
  Terminal target. Uses Node.js v22+ built-in node:sqlite for a SQLite file.
  Each collection is a separate table. Synchronous reads/writes wrapped in
  async interface.

    new TerminalSQLiteAdapter({ dbPath? })

    Options:
      dbPath  string   Path to SQLite file. Default: './data.db'.

    Extra methods:
      rawQuery(sql, params?): Promise<any[]>   Parameterized raw SQL.
      transaction(fn): Promise<void>           Wraps fn in BEGIN/COMMIT/ROLLBACK.
      close(): void                            Release the database file handle.

LocalStorageAdapter
  Web target. Uses window.localStorage. Suitable for small data (< 5 MB total).

    new LocalStorageAdapter({ prefix? })

    Options:
      prefix  string   Key prefix to namespace this adapter. Default: 'ilr:'.

    Extra method:
      clearCollection(collection): Promise<void>

IndexedDBAdapter
  Web target. Uses window.indexedDB. Better for larger datasets.

    new IndexedDBAdapter({ dbName?, collections? })

    Options:
      dbName?      string     IndexedDB database name. Default: 'reactjit-storage'.
      collections? string[]   Known collection names for initial schema creation.

    Extra method:
      clearCollection(collection): Promise<void>

---- Migrations -------------------------------------------------------------

Migrations track schema evolution via a _version integer on each record. When
autoMigrate is true, records are migrated on every read and written back with
the new version.

  migrations: Record<number, MigrationFn>
  MigrationFn = (data: any) => any

  Migrations run sequentially. A record at version 0 with migrations defined
  for versions 1, 2, 3 will have all three functions applied in order.

  Helper utilities (importable from @reactjit/storage):
    getLatestVersion(migrations): number
    migrateRecord(data, migrations, autoMigrate): any
    stampVersion(data, migrations): any
    createMigratingAdapter(adapter, migrations, autoMigrate): StorageAdapter

metadata.title: useBreakpoint
metadata.description: React hook that returns the current responsive breakpoint based on viewport width

sections.overview:
useBreakpoint returns the active responsive breakpoint ('sm', 'md', 'lg', or 'xl') based on the current viewport width. It re-evaluates whenever the window is resized and drives conditional rendering and layout decisions in a single expression.

Breakpoints use min-width thresholds: sm starts at 0px, md at 640px, lg at 1024px, and xl at 1440px. The companion utilities resolveSpan and spanToFlexBasis convert semantic span words ('full', 'half', 'third', etc.) to numeric 12-column grid spans and CSS flexBasis percentages, and are used internally by the Col component.
sections.api:
```tsx
import { useBreakpoint } from '@reactjit/core';
import type { Breakpoint, SemanticSpan, SpanValue } from '@reactjit/core';
import { BREAKPOINTS, RESPONSIVE_DEFAULTS, resolveSpan, spanToFlexBasis } from '@reactjit/core';
```

Return value:
| Value | Min-width | Description               |
|-------|-----------|---------------------------|
| 'sm'  | 0px       | Small / mobile            |
| 'md'  | 640px     | Medium / tablet           |
| 'lg'  | 1024px    | Large / desktop           |
| 'xl'  | 1440px    | Extra-large / wide screen |

SemanticSpan values (for resolveSpan):
| SemanticSpan    | Numeric span (of 12) | flexBasis  |
|-----------------|----------------------|------------|
| 'full'          | 12                   | 100%       |
| 'half'          | 6                    | 50%        |
| 'third'         | 4                    | 33.33%     |
| 'quarter'       | 3                    | 25%        |
| 'two-thirds'    | 8                    | 66.67%     |
| 'three-quarters'| 9                    | 75%        |

Utility functions:
| Function                          | Description                                          |
|-----------------------------------|------------------------------------------------------|
| resolveSpan(value: SpanValue)     | Convert number or SemanticSpan to numeric 1–12 span  |
| spanToFlexBasis(span: number)     | Convert numeric span to flexBasis percentage string  |

RESPONSIVE_DEFAULTS (used by Col when no per-breakpoint overrides given):
| Breakpoint | Default span |
|------------|-------------|
| sm         | 12 (full)   |
| md         | 6 (half)    |
| lg         | 4 (third)   |
| xl         | 3 (quarter) |

examples.title: Conditional layout per breakpoint
Breakpoint-aware font sizing
Using resolveSpan for manual grid math

criticalRules: useBreakpoint reads the viewport width, not a container width. It cannot detect whether a specific panel is narrow — only the whole window.
RESPONSIVE_DEFAULTS assumes a 12-column grid and is primarily intended for the Col component. If you are doing manual layout, call spanToFlexBasis(resolveSpan(span)) for the same percentages.
Breakpoints are min-width (mobile-first). 'xl' means ≥1440px — you do not need to check lower bounds.

metadata.title: useBridge
metadata.description: Access the IBridge transport instance for direct bridge communication

sections.overview: Returns the `IBridge` transport instance that handles communication between JavaScript (React) and the native runtime (Lua for Love2D, Node.js IPC for grid targets). Allows direct access to low-level bridge methods: pushing events, calling Lua functions, subscribing to bridge events, and querying runtime state. Required for custom integrations, native API bindings, and performance-critical operations that bypass the reconciler. Only works on native targets — throws error if used in web mode.

examples.title: Direct Lua function call (Love2D)
Subscribe to custom bridge events
Query runtime capabilities
Batched mutations for performance
Direct mutation push (advanced)
Frame tick subscription (Love2D)
Provider setup at app root
File system integration (Love2D)

criticalRules: Must be used within a `<BridgeProvider>`. Throws error if context is missing.
Only works on native targets (Love2D, terminal, nvim, cc, hs, awesome). Web mode has no bridge.
Direct mutations bypass React reconciler state. Use sparingly and only for performance-critical hot paths.
Event handlers registered via `bridge.on()` MUST be cleaned up via `bridge.off()` in useEffect cleanup. Memory leaks otherwise.
`bridge.call()` is synchronous on Love2D (QuickJS FFI), asynchronous on grid targets (IPC). Check target before use.
Batched mutations improve performance for bulk updates (10+ mutations per frame). Non-batched is fine for typical component updates.
Bridge events are lowercase (`pointerDown`, not `PointerDown`). Event names are target-specific — check bridge implementation.
`bridge.query()` is target-specific. Love2D supports `version`, `platform`, `renderer`. Grid targets vary.
Mutation format is internal and unstable. Prefer reconciler methods (setState, props) unless implementing a native binding.
Bridge is NOT reactive. Changes pushed directly don't trigger React re-renders. Use state if you need reactivity.
seeAlso: BridgeProvider: Context provider for bridge instance
IBridge interface: Full bridge API documentation
useRendererMode: Detect web vs native mode
Event dispatcher: High-level event handling via props (onPress, onPointerMove, etc.)
createBridge: Factory for bridge instances (native targets)

metadata.title: useCapabilities
metadata.description: AI discovery hook that lists all registered native capabilities and their schemas

sections.overview:
useCapabilities queries the Lua-side capability registry and returns every registered native capability with its full schema. The primary use case is AI agent introspection: an agent calls this hook once to discover what components exist, what props they accept, what events they emit, and then generates JSX accordingly — without needing hardcoded knowledge of the framework's capabilities.

The hook fires one RPC call on mount (capabilities:list) and caches the result. It does not poll for changes. During the initial load, loading is true and capabilities is null.

Capabilities are registered on the Lua side via Capabilities.register() in lua/capabilities.lua. The schema describes every prop, its type, default value, and description, plus the events the capability emits. Any new Lua capability automatically appears in useCapabilities results without framework changes.
sections.api:
```tsx
import { useCapabilities } from '@reactjit/core';
import type { CapabilitySchema } from '@reactjit/core';
```

Return value:
| Field        | Type                                    | Description                             |
|--------------|-----------------------------------------|-----------------------------------------|
| capabilities | Record<string, CapabilitySchema> \| null | Map of capability name to schema, null until loaded |
| loading      | boolean                                 | True while the RPC call is in flight    |

CapabilitySchema shape (per capability entry):
| Field  | Type                              | Description                                       |
|--------|-----------------------------------|---------------------------------------------------|
| schema | Record<string, PropDef>           | Map of prop name to type, default, and description|
| events | string[]                          | Names of events the capability emits              |

examples.title: Log all available capabilities
AI agent that generates JSX from discovered capabilities

criticalRules: capabilities is null until the RPC call completes — always check for null or check loading before accessing capability entries.
This hook is native-only. In web mode the bridge is unavailable and the hook returns { capabilities: null, loading: false } immediately.
The schema reflects capabilities registered at the time of the query. If you register capabilities dynamically after the component mounts, remount the component to re-query.
useCapabilities is a discovery tool, not a control surface. Calling a capability requires JSX, not direct RPC.

metadata.title: useClipboard
metadata.description: Read and write the system clipboard with a transient "copied" feedback boolean.

sections.overview: useClipboard provides async copy and paste functions that route through the bridge RPC layer to reach the host clipboard — love.system on Love2D and the Web Clipboard API on web targets. After a successful copy call the `copied` boolean flips to true automatically and resets to false after 2 seconds, making it easy to show transient "Copied!" feedback in the UI without managing a timer manually. The cleanup timer is cancelled automatically on unmount.
sections.api:
```tsx
import { useClipboard } from '@reactjit/core';
```

Parameters:
None.

Returns:
| Field  | Type                        | Description |
|--------|-----------------------------|-------------|
| copy   | (text: string) => Promise<void> | Write the given string to the system clipboard. Sets copied to true for 2 seconds after success. |
| paste  | () => Promise<string>       | Read the current system clipboard contents and return them as a string. |
| copied | boolean                     | True for 2 seconds after the last successful copy call, then resets to false. Use this for "Copied!" UI feedback. |

examples.title: Copy button with transient feedback
Paste button that reads and displays clipboard text
Trigger copy via a hotkey
Copy a JSON payload to clipboard

metadata.title: useDebug
metadata.description: Register live component data in a global registry accessible from the inspector console.

sections.overview: useDebug registers arbitrary component data under a named key on `globalThis.__debug`, making it available for live evaluation in the Love2D inspector console (F12) or any JavaScript REPL attached to the runtime. Data is written on every render so values are always current, and the entry is automatically removed when the component unmounts. This hook adds zero visible output — it is purely a debugging side-channel, not an overlay or HUD.
sections.api:
```tsx
import { useDebug, getDebugData, registerDebug } from '@reactjit/core';
```

Hook signature:
```tsx
useDebug(key: string, data: any): void
```

Parameters:

| Parameter | Type   | Description                                                           |
|-----------|--------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------|
| key       | string | Unique identifier for the debug entry. Usually the component name.    |
| data      | any    | Any value to expose. Updated every render. Cleaned up on unmount.     |

Returns: void

Companion utilities:
```tsx
// Read all currently registered debug entries (non-hook, for tooling use).
getDebugData(): Record<string, any>

// Register debug data imperatively outside of React (e.g. in a game loop).
// Returns an unregister function — call it when done.
registerDebug(key: string, data: any): () => void
```

Console access patterns:
```
> __debug                         // all registered entries
> __debug['PlayerHUD']            // specific component
> __debug['PlayerHUD'].health     // specific field
:watch __debug['PlayerHUD'].fps   // live-updating watch expression
```

examples.title: Basic component state inspection
FPS and frame counter via watch expression
Multiple components sharing the debug registry
Imperative registration from a game service
Read all debug data from tooling code

seeAlso: getDebugData — non-hook read of the full debug registry
registerDebug — imperative registration for non-React code
Inspector (F12) — Love2D dev overlay with console and watch expressions

metadata.title: useFetch
metadata.description: Fetch JSON data from a URL or local file path with loading and error state.

sections.overview: useFetch wraps the global fetch() API in a React hook that manages loading, data, and error state. It works across all ReactJIT targets: on Love2D the fetch polyfill routes HTTP requests through LuaSocket and local file paths through love.filesystem; on web and Node targets the platform's native fetch is used. Pass null as the URL to clear state and skip any in-flight request, which is useful for conditional fetching. The options argument accepts standard RequestInit and is kept in a ref so changing it does not trigger a re-fetch.
sections.api:
```tsx
import { useFetch } from '@reactjit/core';
```

Parameters:
| Param   | Type           | Default   | Description |
|---------|----------------|-----------|-------------|
| url     | string \| null | required  | The URL or local file path to fetch. Pass null to skip fetching and clear state. |
| options | RequestInit    | undefined | Standard fetch options (method, headers, body, etc.). Changes to this object do not trigger a re-fetch. |

Returns:
| Field   | Type        | Description |
|---------|-------------|-------------|
| data    | T \| null   | The parsed JSON response body, or null while loading or on error. |
| error   | Error \| null | The fetch or HTTP error, or null when successful or still loading. |
| loading | boolean     | True while the request is in flight. Starts as true when url is non-null. |

examples.title: Fetch a list of users from an API
Load a local config file bundled with the Love2D project
Conditional fetch — only fetch when an ID is selected
POST request with custom options

metadata.title: useHotkey
metadata.description: Register a global keyboard shortcut that fires a handler when a key combo is pressed.

sections.overview: useHotkey binds a callback to a keyboard combination string such as "ctrl+z" or "ctrl+shift+s". The combo is parsed once on mount and matched against every keydown event received from the bridge. The handler reference is always kept current without re-subscribing, so passing an inline arrow function is safe. Pass `enabled: false` in options to conditionally disable the binding without unmounting the component.
sections.api:
```tsx
import { useHotkey } from '@reactjit/core';
```

Parameters:
| Param   | Type                       | Default   | Description |
|---------|----------------------------|-----------|-------------|
| combo   | string                     | required  | Key combination string. Use "+" as separator. Modifiers: ctrl, control, shift, alt, meta, cmd, gui. Key names are lowercased (e.g. "s", "z", "escape", "f1"). |
| handler | (event: LoveEvent) => void | required  | Called when the combo is matched. Receives the raw LoveEvent payload. |
| options | { enabled?: boolean }      | undefined | Optional config object. Set enabled: false to disable the binding temporarily. |

Returns:
void — this hook has no return value.

examples.title: Undo and redo shortcuts
Escape closes a modal only while it is open
Save with Ctrl+S and Ctrl+Shift+S for save-as
Access the raw LoveEvent in the handler

metadata.title: useLocalStore
metadata.description: Persistent useState backed by SQLite — values survive app restarts

sections.overview:
useLocalStore is a drop-in replacement for useState that persists values across app restarts using SQLite on the Lua side. The API is identical to useState — it returns a [value, setValue] tuple. On first mount the stored value is loaded from the database; on every subsequent setValue the new value is written back, debounced at 300ms to avoid write storms.

Values are stored under a (namespace, key) pair in a local SQLite database (localstore.db). The default namespace is 'app'. Use custom namespaces to avoid key collisions between unrelated parts of your application.

The hook is native-only. In web mode or when the bridge is unavailable, it falls back to plain in-memory state using the defaultValue — no crash, no warning.
sections.api:
```tsx
import { useLocalStore } from '@reactjit/core';
import type { UseLocalStoreOptions } from '@reactjit/core';
```

Signature:
```tsx
function useLocalStore<T>(
  key: string,
  defaultValue: T,
  options?: UseLocalStoreOptions,
): [T, (value: T | ((prev: T) => T)) => void]
```

Parameters:
| Parameter    | Type                   | Default   | Description                              |
|--------------|------------------------|-----------|------------------------------------------|
| key          | string                 | required  | Storage key within the namespace         |
| defaultValue | T                      | required  | Value to use before storage loads        |
| options      | UseLocalStoreOptions   | undefined | Optional configuration                   |

UseLocalStoreOptions:
| Option    | Type   | Default | Description                        |
|-----------|--------|---------|------------------------------------|
| namespace | string | 'app'   | Namespace prefix for the key       |

Return value: `[value, setValue]` — identical interface to `useState<T>`.

examples.title: Persisted counter
Persisted theme preference with namespace
Persisted complex object (panel layout)

criticalRules: useLocalStore is native-only. In web builds the bridge is unavailable and it silently falls back to plain useState with the defaultValue. This means web builds never persist state — it is not an error.
The 300ms write debounce means rapid state changes do not hammer the database. Do not call setCount(0) and immediately read from the DB expecting the new value — the write may not have flushed yet.
Keys must be unique within their namespace. Two unrelated useLocalStore calls sharing a key in the same namespace will conflict. Use the namespace option to scope keys by feature area.

defaultValue is used only until the stored value loads on first mount. This means there is a brief flash of the default value on every app start. If you need to avoid the flash, check a separate loading state before rendering.
Values are stored via JSON serialization. Any non-JSON-safe type (functions, class instances, circular references) will lose type fidelity or fail to persist.

metadata.title: useLove
metadata.description: Subscribe to Love2D events and get a send function for bidirectional communication.

sections.overview:
useLove is the primary hook for subscribing to events from the Lua runtime and sending commands back to it. It returns a tuple of [state, send], where state is automatically updated whenever an event of the given type is received, and send is a function for dispatching commands to the Lua side.

The hook maintains a React state that updates whenever the bridge receives an event matching the specified event type. The initial state is provided as the second argument and is used before any events arrive.
sections.api:
Signature:
  function useLove<T>(eventType: string, initialState: T): [T, (cmd: string, payload?: any) => void]

Parameters:
  eventType: string
    The event type to subscribe to. Must match the event type dispatched by the Lua side
    (e.g., 'game:state', 'player:health', 'inventory:update').

  initialState: T
    The initial value for the state before any events are received.

Returns:
  [state, send]

  state: T
    The current state, updated whenever an event of the given type is received. The event
    payload replaces the state entirely.

  send: (cmd: string, payload?: any) => void
    Function to send commands to the Lua side. The cmd string identifies the command type,
    and the optional payload provides additional data.

examples.title: Track game state
Player movement controls

platformNotes.differences:
- Love2D: Events arrive via QuickJS FFI bridge. Send dispatches via the same bridge.
- Web: Events arrive via in-memory queue (Module.FS bridge). Send writes to the queue.
- Grid targets: Events arrive via IPC/WebSocket. Send dispatches via the same channel.

criticalRules: The event payload replaces the state entirely -- it is not merged
The send function is memoized (stable reference) and safe to use in callbacks
The hook re-subscribes when eventType changes -- use a stable string, not a computed value
Must be used inside a BridgeProvider context
The event type string must match exactly what the Lua side dispatches
seeAlso: useLoveEvent -- Fire-and-forget event listener (no state tracking)
useLoveState -- Bidirectional shared state with automatic sync
useLoveSend -- Just the send function without event subscription

metadata.title: useLoveEvent
metadata.description: Fire-and-forget event listener for Love2D events without state tracking.

sections.overview:
useLoveEvent subscribes to events from the Lua runtime and calls a handler function when events arrive. Unlike useLove, it does not track state -- it is a fire-and-forget listener for side effects like logging, triggering animations, playing sounds, or updating external state.

The hook uses a ref internally to store the handler function, which means the subscription does not re-register when the handler reference changes. This avoids unnecessary unsubscribe/resubscribe cycles when the handler is an inline function.
sections.api:
Signature:
  function useLoveEvent(eventType: string, handler: (payload: any) => void): void

Parameters:
  eventType: string
    The event type to listen for. Must match the event type dispatched by the Lua side.

  handler: (payload: any) => void
    Callback invoked when an event of the given type is received. The handler receives
    the event payload as its argument. Because the handler is stored in a ref, the
    latest handler is always called, even if the component re-renders with a new function.

Returns:
  void (no return value)

examples.title: Log spawned entities
Trigger animation on event
Update external state

criticalRules: The handler is stored in a ref -- always calls the latest version, no re-subscription on change
The subscription is re-created only when eventType or the bridge instance changes
This hook does NOT return state -- if you need to track the latest event value, use useLove instead
Must be used inside a BridgeProvider context
The handler should be fast -- it runs synchronously when the event arrives
seeAlso: uselove.txt -- Event subscription with state tracking
uselovesend.txt -- Send commands without subscribing to events

metadata.title: useLoveOverlays
metadata.description: Position DOM overlays relative to Love2D entity coordinates in web mode.

sections.overview:
useLoveOverlays positions DOM overlay elements based on Love2D entity coordinates received from the Lua runtime. This hook is only meaningful in web mode, where React controls both the Love2D canvas and surrounding DOM elements. It maps normalized coordinates (0-1) from the Lua runtime to pixel positions on the canvas DOM element.

The hook listens for 'overlays' events from the bridge, which provide a list of entities with normalized coordinates. It uses a ResizeObserver on the canvas element to track its size and position, then computes DOM pixel coordinates for each overlay.
sections.api:
Signature:
  function useLoveOverlays(canvasRef: React.RefObject<HTMLCanvasElement | null>): Overlay[]

Parameters:
  canvasRef: React.RefObject<HTMLCanvasElement | null>
    A React ref to the canvas element that Love2D renders into. Used to get the canvas
    bounding rectangle for coordinate mapping.

Returns:
  Overlay[]

Overlay interface:
  interface Overlay {
    id: string        // Unique identifier for the overlay entity
    nx: number        // Normalized X coordinate (0-1, from Lua)
    ny: number        // Normalized Y coordinate (0-1, from Lua)
    px: number        // Pixel X coordinate in game space (from Lua)
    py: number        // Pixel Y coordinate in game space (from Lua)
    domX?: number     // Computed DOM X pixel position on the canvas
    domY?: number     // Computed DOM Y pixel position on the canvas
    [key: string]: any  // Additional data from the Lua side
  }

Coordinate mapping:
  domX = nx * canvasRect.width
  domY = ny * canvasRect.height

examples.title: Render name labels above entities
Tooltip on hover

criticalRules: Only use in web mode -- has no effect on other platforms
The canvasRef must point to the actual <canvas> element Love2D renders into
Overlays are computed from 'overlays' events dispatched by the Lua side
If the canvas ref is null or not yet mounted, the hook returns an empty array
Overlay positions are recomputed when the canvas resizes (via ResizeObserver)
Must be used inside a BridgeProvider context

metadata.title: useLoveReady
metadata.description: Detect when the Love2D bridge is initialized and ready for communication.

sections.overview:
useLoveReady returns a boolean indicating whether the Love2D bridge is initialized and ready for communication. This is useful for showing loading states or deferring operations that depend on the bridge being available.

The hook checks the bridge's isReady() method on initial render. If the bridge is not yet ready, it registers an onReady callback that flips the state to true once initialization completes.
sections.api:
Signature:
  function useLoveReady(): boolean

Parameters:
  (none)

Returns:
  boolean
    true when the bridge is initialized and ready for communication.
    false while the bridge is still initializing.

examples.title: Loading screen
Defer data fetching until ready

platformNotes.The readiness condition depends on the t:
- Love2D: Ready when the QuickJS FFI bridge has established communication with the Lua runtime.
- Web: Ready when the Module.FS bridge has initialized its event queue.
- Grid targets: Ready when the IPC/WebSocket connection is established.
In most cases, the bridge becomes ready very quickly (within the first frame). The loading
state is most visible when the bridge requires asynchronous initialization (e.g., WebSocket
connection).

criticalRules: The hook returns false synchronously on first render if the bridge is still initializing
Once ready becomes true, it never reverts to false
Must be used inside a BridgeProvider context
Do not send commands or subscribe to events before the bridge is ready -- use this hook to gate those operations
seeAlso: uselove.txt -- Event subscription (works only after ready)
useloverpc.txt -- RPC calls (works only after ready)
uselovesend.txt -- Send commands (works only after ready)

metadata.title: useLoveRPC
metadata.description: Call Lua RPC methods and await results from the React side.

sections.overview:
useLoveRPC provides a way to call Lua-side RPC (Remote Procedure Call) methods from React and await the results. Unlike the event-based hooks (useLove, useLoveEvent), this hook supports request-response patterns where you need a return value from the Lua runtime.

The hook returns a memoized async function. When called, it sends the RPC request via the bridge, waits for the response, and resolves the promise with the result. An optional timeout prevents hanging on unresponsive calls.
sections.api:
Signature:
  function useLoveRPC<T = any>(method: string): (args?: any, timeoutMs?: number) => Promise<T>

Type parameter:
  T    The expected return type of the RPC call. Defaults to any.

Parameters:
  method: string
    The name of the Lua-side RPC method to call. Must be registered on the Lua side.

Returns:
  (args?: any, timeoutMs?: number) => Promise<T>

  An async function that:
    args?: any         Optional arguments passed to the Lua method.
    timeoutMs?: number Optional timeout in milliseconds. If the Lua side does not respond
                       within this window, the promise rejects.

  Resolves with the return value from the Lua method (typed as T).

examples.title: Query nearby entities
RPC with timeout

platformNotes.target:
- Love2D: RPC call is dispatched via QuickJS FFI. The Lua side processes the call and sends
the response back through the bridge.
- Web: RPC is processed via the in-memory bridge queue.
- Grid targets: RPC travels over IPC/WebSocket.
The Lua-side RPC method must be registered to handle the call. Calling an unregistered
method will result in an error or timeout.

criticalRules: The returned function is memoized (stable reference) -- safe to include in dependency arrays
Always handle promise rejections (timeouts, errors from Lua side)
The RPC method name must match a registered handler on the Lua side
RPC is async -- do not use it in render functions, use it in effects or event handlers
Must be used inside a BridgeProvider context
Large payloads may impact performance due to serialization overhead
seeAlso: useLoveState -- Shared state (auto-sync, no manual RPC)

metadata.title: useLoveSend
metadata.description: Returns a fire-and-forget send function for dispatching commands to the Lua runtime.

sections.overview:
useLoveSend returns a send function for dispatching fire-and-forget commands to the Lua runtime. Unlike useLove (which also subscribes to events and tracks state), this hook only provides the send capability. It is the lightest-weight hook for components that only need to send commands without receiving responses.

The returned function is memoized (stable reference), making it safe to include in dependency arrays and pass to child components.
sections.api:
Signature:
  function useLoveSend(): (type: string, payload?: any) => void

Parameters:
  (none)

Returns:
  (type: string, payload?: any) => void

  A function that sends a command to the Lua runtime:
    type: string       The command type identifier.
    payload?: any      Optional data payload sent with the command.

examples.title: Send a command on click
Pass send to child components

criticalRules: The returned send function is memoized -- safe for dependency arrays
Fire-and-forget: no confirmation or response. Use useLoveRPC for request-response patterns
Must be used inside a BridgeProvider context
Commands are processed asynchronously by the Lua runtime on the next update cycle
seeAlso: uselove.txt -- Send + event subscription + state tracking
useloveevent.txt -- Listen for events without sending

metadata.title: useLoveState
metadata.description: Bidirectional shared state between React and Lua with automatic synchronization.

sections.overview:
useLoveState provides bidirectional shared state between React and the Lua runtime. It works like React's useState but with automatic synchronization: when you call setValue from React, it updates both the local React state and the Lua-side state via the bridge. When the Lua side updates the state, the change propagates to React via a `state:${key}` event.

This hook is ideal for settings, configuration values, and any state that both React and Lua need to read and write.
sections.api:
Signature:
  function useLoveState<T>(key: string, initialValue: T): [T, (value: T) => void]

Parameters:
  key: string
    A unique key identifying this shared state. The hook subscribes to `state:${key}` events
    from the bridge. Use dot notation for namespacing (e.g., 'player.health', 'settings.volume').

  initialValue: T
    The initial value before any sync events arrive from the Lua side.

Returns:
  [value, setValue]

  value: T
    The current value of the shared state.

  setValue: (value: T) => void
    Updates both the local React state and the Lua-side state. Calls setLocal(value) for
    immediate React update and bridge.setState(key, value) to sync to Lua.

examples.title: Shared volume setting
Player health synced from Lua

platformNotes.The bidirectional sync works on all plat:
- React to Lua: setValue calls bridge.setState(key, value)
- Lua to React: Lua dispatches a `state:${key}` event, which the hook subscribes to
The sync is eventual -- there may be a frame of latency between setValue and the Lua side
seeing the update, and vice versa. For time-critical state, consider using RPC instead.

criticalRules: setValue updates React state immediately (optimistic) and then syncs to Lua
State from Lua arrives as events with type `state:${key}` -- the key must match exactly
The setValue function is memoized (stable reference) when the key does not change
Multiple components can share the same key -- they will all receive updates
Must be used inside a BridgeProvider context
The initial value is used only on first render -- Lua-side state takes precedence once synced
seeAlso: uselove.txt -- Event subscription with manual send

metadata.title: usePeerServer
metadata.description: Host a WebSocket server to accept peer connections, broadcast messages, and receive incoming data.

sections.overview: usePeerServer starts a WebSocket server bound to the given port and manages the full lifecycle of peer connections: tracking connected client IDs, exposing the last received message with its sender ID, and providing send and broadcast functions. It routes through the bridge's __wsListen global, which is provided by the Love2D native target. The hook tears down the server automatically when the component unmounts or the port changes to a new value. Pass null as the port to keep the server stopped.
sections.api:
```tsx
import { usePeerServer } from '@reactjit/core';
import type { PeerMessage } from '@reactjit/core';
```

Parameters:
| Param | Type           | Default  | Description |
|-------|----------------|----------|-------------|
| port  | number \| null | required | TCP port to listen on (e.g. 8080). Pass null to keep the server stopped. |

Returns:
| Field       | Type                             | Description |
|-------------|----------------------------------|-------------|
| ready       | boolean                          | True once the server is bound and listening for connections. |
| peers       | number[]                         | Array of currently connected client IDs. Updated on connect and disconnect. |
| broadcast   | (data: string) => void           | Send a string message to every connected peer simultaneously. |
| send        | (clientId: number, data: string) => void | Send a string message to a single peer identified by its numeric client ID. |
| lastMessage | PeerMessage \| null              | The most recently received message, or null if nothing has arrived yet. |
| error       | string \| null                   | Human-readable error string if the server failed to start, otherwise null. |

PeerMessage shape:
| Field    | Type   | Description |
|----------|--------|-------------|
| clientId | number | Numeric ID of the peer that sent this message. |
| data     | string | The raw string payload received from that peer. |

examples.title: Echo server — broadcast each message back to all peers
Reply only to the peer that sent a message
Broadcast world state to all peers whenever it changes
Conditional server — activate only when hosting is toggled on

metadata.title: usePixelArt
metadata.description: Convert Unicode symbol names or custom string art into memoized Box-based pixel geometry.

sections.overview: usePixelArt converts a built-in symbol name or an array of string art lines into a memoized React element made entirely from <Box> components. Because Love2D's default font cannot render Unicode symbols (arrows, checkmarks, hearts, etc.), this hook is the correct way to display icons and pixel-art graphics across all targets. Any non-space character in the art grid is treated as a filled pixel; spaces are transparent. The result is memoized via useMemo and only recalculates when the art source or options change.
sections.api:
```tsx
import { usePixelArt, PixelArt, getPixelArtSymbols } from '@reactjit/core';
```

Hook signature:
```tsx
usePixelArt(art: string | string[], options?: PixelArtOptions): React.ReactElement
```

Parameters:

| Parameter     | Type     | Default   | Description                                                          |
|---------------|----------|-----------|----------------------------------------------------------------------|
| art           | string   | —         | Built-in symbol name (e.g. 'play', 'heart', 'arrow-up')             |
| art           | string[] | —         | Custom string art lines. Any non-space char = filled pixel.          |
| options.size  | number   | 4         | Width and height of each pixel in logical units.                     |
| options.color | string   | '#ffffff' | Fill color for all pixels. Overridden per-row by options.colors.     |
| options.colors| string[] | undefined | Per-row fill colors. Row index maps to art row. Falls back to color. |
| options.gap   | number   | 0         | Gap in logical units between pixels (horizontal and vertical).       |

Returns: React.ReactElement — a column Box containing row Boxes of sized pixel Boxes.

Built-in symbol names:
play, pause, stop, skip-next, skip-prev, check, close, arrow-up, arrow-down,
arrow-left, arrow-right, circle, circle-outline, dot, plus, minus, heart, star,
volume, mute, chevron-right, chevron-left, menu

Component variant (declarative):
```tsx
<PixelArt art="play" size={4} color="#fff" style={containerStyle} />
```

PixelArtProps extends PixelArtOptions and adds:

| Prop  | Type             | Description                                    |
|-------|------------------|------------------------------------------------|
| art   | string | string[] | Symbol name or array of string art lines. |
| style | Style            | Optional container Box style overrides.        |

Utility function:
```tsx
getPixelArtSymbols(): string[]
// Returns the list of all built-in symbol names.
```

examples.title: Built-in play icon in a media control bar
State-toggled mute/volume icon
Multi-color custom heart art with per-row gradient
PixelArt component in a toolbar
Enumerate all built-in symbols in a gallery
Custom logo art with pixel gap for retro look

seeAlso: Box — the underlying primitive used for all pixel rendering
PixelArt — declarative component variant of this hook
getPixelArtSymbols — utility to list all built-in symbol names
Linter rule: no-unicode-symbol-in-text (enforces this pattern at build time)

metadata.title: usePorts
metadata.description: Monitor open TCP/UDP ports with process details and kill processes by PID.

sections.overview: usePorts polls the host system for all listening TCP and UDP ports, returning each port's number, bound address, protocol, PID, and process name. It also provides a kill function to send signals to processes by PID. Data is refreshed on a configurable interval via the Love2D bridge RPC, and each port entry carries a .toSysLog(path) method for structured logging. The whole result set can also be logged in one call via the top-level toSysLog method.
sections.api:
```tsx
import { usePorts } from '@reactjit/core';
```

Hook signature:
```tsx
usePorts(interval?: number): PortMonitor
```

Parameters:

| Parameter | Type   | Default | Description                        |
|-----------|--------|---------|------------------------------------|
| interval  | number | 2000    | ms between port list refreshes.    |

Returns: PortMonitor

PortMonitor shape:

| Field    | Type       | Description                                                      |
|----------|------------|------------------------------------------------------------------|
| list     | PortInfo[] | All currently listening ports.                                   |
| loading  | boolean    | true until the first fetch completes.                            |
| kill     | fn         | (pid: number, signal?: string) => Promise<boolean>               |
| toSysLog | fn         | (path: string) => void — log all ports to file.                  |

kill parameters:

| Parameter | Type   | Default  | Description                                             |
|-----------|--------|----------|---------------------------------------------------------|
