| pid       | number | —        | Process ID to signal.                                   |
| signal    | string | 'TERM'   | Signal name: 'TERM', 'KILL', 'HUP', 'INT', etc.       |

kill returns Promise<boolean> — true if the signal was sent successfully.

PortInfo shape:

| Field    | Type           | Description                                |
|----------|----------------|--------------------------------------------|
| port     | number         | Listening port number.                     |
| host     | string         | Bound address, e.g. "0.0.0.0", "127.0.0.1"|
| pid      | number         | Process ID owning the socket.              |
| process  | string         | Process name, e.g. "node", "nginx".        |
| protocol | 'tcp' | 'udp'  | Socket protocol.                           |
| state    | string         | Socket state, e.g. "LISTEN".               |
| toSysLog | fn             | (path: string) => void — log this entry.   |

examples.title: Display all listening ports in a table
Kill a process by clicking its port row
Filter to a specific protocol or address
Force-kill with SIGKILL
Log current port snapshot to file

seeAlso: useSystemMonitor — per-core CPU, process list, network I/O, GPU, disk
useSystemInfo — static OS facts (hostname, CPU model, shell, uptime)

metadata.title: usePTY
metadata.description: React hook for spawning and communicating with PTY (pseudo-terminal) processes

sections.overview:
usePTY manages a PTY session paired with a Terminal capability node. It accumulates output, tracks connection state, and provides stable send helpers — all backed by a named session ID that stays consistent across re-renders.

The hook does not render anything. Spread terminalProps onto a Terminal element in your JSX tree — that element owns the PTY lifecycle on the Lua side, and the hook receives its events.

Three send helpers cover common patterns: sendLine appends a newline (most commands), interrupt sends Ctrl+C (kill foreground process), sendEOF sends Ctrl+D (logout). For template sessions, use runCommand to spawn a fresh bash -c process per invocation.

output accumulates all PTY data chunks in a rolling buffer. The buffer is trimmed to maxOutput characters (default 100,000) so long-running sessions do not consume unbounded memory. Call clearOutput to reset it.
sections.api:
```tsx
import { usePTY } from '@reactjit/core';
import type { UsePTYOptions, UsePTYResult } from '@reactjit/core';
```

Options (UsePTYOptions):
| Option      | Type                            | Default     | Description                                                        |
|-------------|---------------------------------|-------------|--------------------------------------------------------------------|
| type        | 'user' \| 'root' \| 'template' | 'user'      | Session archetype                                                  |
| shell       | string                          | 'bash'      | Shell executable (any PATH-resolvable binary)                      |
| cwd         | string                          | undefined   | Working directory for the shell process                            |
| env         | Record<string, string \| false> | undefined   | Environment overrides. false = unset the variable.                 |
| rows        | number                          | 24          | Terminal rows                                                      |
| cols        | number                          | 80          | Terminal columns                                                   |
| session     | string                          | auto        | Stable session name for pty:write / pty:resize RPCs                |
| transport   | 'bridge' \| 'ws' \| 'http' \| 'tor' | 'bridge' | Transport backend                                              |
| autoConnect | boolean                         | true        | Auto-spawn shell on mount                                          |
| maxOutput   | number                          | 100_000     | Max output buffer size in characters (rolling window)              |

Return value (UsePTYResult):
| Field         | Type                              | Description                                                  |
|---------------|-----------------------------------|--------------------------------------------------------------|
| output        | string                            | Accumulated PTY output (rolling, trimmed to maxOutput)       |
| connected     | boolean                           | Whether the shell process is currently running               |
| send          | (data: string) => void            | Write raw bytes to the PTY                                   |
| sendLine      | (command: string) => void         | Send a command followed by newline                           |
| interrupt     | () => void                        | Send Ctrl+C (interrupt running process)                      |
| sendEOF       | () => void                        | Send Ctrl+D (EOF / logout)                                   |
| runCommand    | (command: string) => void         | Template mode: run command in a fresh ephemeral PTY          |
| resize        | (rows, cols) => void              | Notify PTY of window size change (sends SIGWINCH)            |
| clearOutput   | () => void                        | Clear the accumulated output buffer                          |
| terminalProps | TerminalCapabilityProps           | Spread onto a Terminal element in JSX                        |

examples.title: Capture command output
Interactive shell with interrupt control

criticalRules: Always include a Terminal element in your JSX tree with {...terminalProps} spread onto it. Without the Terminal node, usePTY has no PTY to manage and all sends are no-ops.
session must be unique per app. Two Terminal nodes with the same session ID will collide.
output grows until clearOutput() is called or it is trimmed by maxOutput. For long-running sessions, set maxOutput explicitly.
PTY is native only. Web builds will have connected=false and output will never update.
type="template" keeps a persistent shell open but spawns a fresh bash -c per runCommand call. Use sendLine for interactive commands; use runCommand for isolated one-shot invocations.

metadata.title: useRendererMode
metadata.description: Returns the current renderer mode ('web' or 'native')

sections.overview: Returns the current renderer mode as either `'web'` or `'native'`. The mode is set by `<RendererProvider>` at the root of the application. Primitives like `Box`, `Text`, and `Image` use this internally to switch between DOM elements (web) and reconciler instances (native). Application code can use it for conditional rendering based on the rendering target.
sections.api:
Import:
```typescript
import { useRendererMode, RendererProvider } from '@reactjit/core';
```

Hook signature:
```typescript
useRendererMode(): RendererMode
```

Type:
```typescript
type RendererMode = 'web' | 'native';
```

Return value: `'web'` or `'native'`

Provider signature:
```typescript
<RendererProvider mode={'web' | 'native'}>
  {children}
</RendererProvider>
```

examples.title: Conditional feature based on renderer
Platform-specific styling
Conditional hook usage
Debug overlay for native targets
Provider setup at app root
Web overlay setup
Feature detection pattern
Logging wrapper

criticalRules: Must be used within a `<RendererProvider>`. Throws error if context is missing.
The mode is static for the lifetime of the app. Switching modes requires remounting the entire tree.
Primitives (`Box`, `Text`, `Image`) automatically detect mode. Application code rarely needs to switch on mode unless implementing platform-specific features.
`'native'` mode = Love2D, terminal, nvim, cc, hs, awesome targets. `'web'` mode = browser DOM.
Default context value is `'web'` if no provider exists (for compatibility), but production apps should always use explicit provider.
Do NOT use this for target-specific builds. Use esbuild `--define` or environment variables for compile-time target detection.
Use this for runtime conditional rendering, not bundling logic.
Mode does NOT indicate specific target (Love2D vs terminal vs nvim). Use environment variables or bridge queries for target-specific logic.
seeAlso: RendererProvider: Context provider for renderer mode
useBridge: Access the transport layer (native targets only)
Primitives: Box, Text, Image — use this hook internally
BridgeProvider: Required for native targets, provides IBridge instance

metadata.title: useSearch / Search Hooks
metadata.description: Family of 7 pure React hooks for in-memory, fuzzy, async, and command-palette search

sections.overview:
The search hooks are a family of seven pure React hooks that deal with data filtering and matching. They contain no input lifecycle logic — debouncing and keystroke handling live in the Lua-owned SearchBar component. Each hook is a stateless transformation that you compose with your data and a query string.

useSearch performs synchronous substring matching on an array of items. useFuzzySearch adds fuzzy scoring so results are ranked by match quality. useAsyncSearch fetches from any async source and manages loading and error state. useSearchHistory persists recent queries to SQLite via useLocalStore. useSearchHighlight splits a string into matched and unmatched segments for highlight rendering. useCommandSearch filters CommandDef arrays for a CommandPalette. useSearchSchema introspects a dataset to describe which fields are being searched.

Field detection is automatic: if you do not specify a key option, the hooks detect all short string and number fields on the first item, skipping known ID-like keys (id, uuid, href, url, etc.).
sections.api:
```tsx
import {
  useSearch,
  useFuzzySearch,
  useAsyncSearch,
  useSearchHistory,
  useSearchHighlight,
  useCommandSearch,
  useSearchSchema,
  detectSearchableFields,
} from '@reactjit/core';

import type {
  UseSearchOptions,
  UseFuzzySearchOptions,
  FuzzySearchResult,
  UseAsyncSearchOptions,
  UseSearchHistoryOptions,
  HighlightPart,
  UseCommandSearchOptions,
  SearchSchema,
} from '@reactjit/core';
```

useSearch<T>(items, query, options?): T[]
| Option        | Type                          | Default    | Description                                            |
|---------------|-------------------------------|------------|--------------------------------------------------------|
| key           | keyof T \| (keyof T)[]        | auto       | Field(s) to search. Auto-detects if omitted.           |
| matcher       | (item, query) => boolean      | undefined  | Custom match function. Overrides key.                  |
| limit         | number                        | unlimited  | Max results to return.                                 |
| minLength     | number                        | 0          | Minimum query length before filtering begins.          |
| showAllOnEmpty| boolean                       | true       | Return all items when query is empty.                  |

useFuzzySearch<T>(items, query, options?): { results: FuzzySearchResult<T>[], items: T[] }
| Option        | Type     | Default | Description                                            |
|---------------|----------|---------|--------------------------------------------------------|
| sortByScore   | boolean  | true    | Sort results by match score descending.                |
| minScore      | number   | 1       | Minimum score to include (0 = always include).         |
| ...           |          |         | All useSearch options also apply.                      |

FuzzySearchResult<T>:
| Field | Type   | Description                                |
|-------|--------|--------------------------------------------|
| item  | T      | The original item                          |
| score | number | Match score (higher = better match)        |
| index | number | Index in the original items array          |

useAsyncSearch<T>(fetcher, query, options?): { results: T[], loading: boolean, error: Error | null }
| Option    | Type   | Default | Description                                              |
|-----------|--------|---------|----------------------------------------------------------|
| debounce  | number | 0       | Optional React-side debounce in ms (for non-SearchBar use)|
| minLength | number | 1       | Minimum query length before fetching.                    |

useSearchHistory(options?): { history: string[], push, remove, clear }
| Option      | Type   | Default         | Description                           |
|-------------|--------|-----------------|---------------------------------------|
| maxEntries  | number | 20              | Maximum entries to store.             |
| storeKey    | string | 'searchHistory' | SQLite store key.                     |

useSearchHighlight(text, query): HighlightPart[]
HighlightPart: { text: string, match: boolean }

useCommandSearch(commands, query, options?): CommandDef[]
| Option | Type   | Default   | Description        |
|--------|--------|-----------|--------------------|
| limit  | number | unlimited | Max results.       |

useSearchSchema<T>(items, options?): SearchSchema
SearchSchema: { allFields: string[], activeFields: string[], description: string, isAutoDetected: boolean }

examples.title: Basic substring search over a list
Fuzzy search with score-ranked results
Async search with loading state
Highlight matching text in results

criticalRules: These hooks deal with data — they are not input components. The query string comes from your state, typically driven by a SearchBar's onSearch event. Do not try to wire TextInput onChange directly to useFuzzySearch — let SearchBar do the debouncing and call onSearch with the final value.

useSearch is case-insensitive substring matching. useFuzzySearch allows non-contiguous character matching with ranking. Use fuzzy for command palettes; use plain for lists where users type exact substrings.

Auto field detection inspects only the first item in the array. It skips keys that look like IDs (id, uuid, href, url, etc.) and keys with values longer than 200 characters. If your data has non-obvious searchable fields, specify them explicitly with key.

useAsyncSearch cancels stale requests on query change. You do not need to manage AbortController manually — but you must ensure your fetcher handles cancellation gracefully if it uses external abort signals.
useSearchHistory is native-only (backed by useLocalStore which requires the bridge). In web mode history will not persist.

metadata.title: useSemanticTerminal
metadata.description: React hook for managing SemanticTerminal state, classification tracking, and playback controls

sections.overview:
useSemanticTerminal manages all React-side state for a SemanticTerminal session. It tracks classified rows, the semantic graph state, and playback transport position. It also provides the full set of playback control methods: play, pause, seek, step, stepBack, setSpeed, and saveRecording.

The hook does not render anything. Spread terminalProps onto a SemanticTerminal element in your JSX tree — that element is the visual capability node that owns the PTY lifecycle and renders the classified terminal.

Use this hook whenever you need to read classification data or drive playback from React. For simple one-liner use cases where you only want the visual terminal and don't need access to classified rows or transport controls, use SemanticTerminal directly without the hook.

classifiedRows is an array of all rows seen in the current session, updated as each row is classified. graphState tracks the structural state of the semantic graph — how many turns and nodes the classifier has identified. playerState contains the playback timeline — only non-null in playback mode.
sections.api:
```tsx
import { useSemanticTerminal } from '@reactjit/core';
import type {
  UseSemanticTerminalOptions,
  UseSemanticTerminalResult,
  ClassifiedRow,
  GraphState,
  PlayerState,
} from '@reactjit/core';
```

Options (UseSemanticTerminalOptions):
| Option        | Type                           | Default   | Description                                                  |
|---------------|--------------------------------|-----------|--------------------------------------------------------------|
| mode          | 'live' \| 'playback'           | 'live'    | Live PTY session or recorded session playback                |
| command       | string                         | 'bash'    | Command to run in live mode                                  |
| args          | string                         | undefined | Command arguments (space-separated)                          |
| cwd           | string                         | undefined | Working directory                                            |
| rows          | number                         | 40        | Terminal rows                                                |
| cols          | number                         | 120       | Terminal columns                                             |
| classifier    | string                         | 'basic'   | Classifier name from lua/classifiers/                        |
| recording     | boolean                        | false     | Record PTY output for later playback                         |
| playbackSrc   | string                         | undefined | Path to .rec.lua file for playback mode                      |
| playbackSpeed | number                         | 1.0       | Playback speed multiplier                                    |
| showTokens    | boolean                        | false     | Show token type badges on each row                           |
| showGraph     | boolean                        | false     | Show semantic graph panel                                    |
| showTimeline  | boolean                        | false     | Show timeline scrubber (playback mode)                       |

Return value (UseSemanticTerminalResult):
| Field          | Type                           | Description                                                  |
|----------------|--------------------------------|--------------------------------------------------------------|
| classifiedRows | ClassifiedRow[]                | All classified rows received so far                          |
| graphState     | GraphState \| null             | Current semantic graph state, or null until first update     |
| playerState    | PlayerState \| null            | Current playback state, or null if not in playback mode      |
| play           | () => void                     | Resume or start playback                                     |
| pause          | () => void                     | Pause playback                                               |
| seek           | (timeSeconds: number) => void  | Seek to a specific time in seconds                           |
| step           | () => void                     | Advance one frame forward                                    |
| stepBack       | () => void                     | Go back one frame                                            |
| setSpeed       | (speed: number) => void        | Set playback speed multiplier                                |
| saveRecording  | (path: string) => void         | Save the current recording to a file path                    |
| terminalProps  | SemanticTerminalProps          | Spread onto a SemanticTerminal element                       |

ClassifiedRow:
| Field | Type   | Description                          |
|-------|--------|--------------------------------------|
| row   | number | Row index in the terminal buffer     |
| token | string | Classifier token (e.g. 'command')    |
| text  | string | Plain text content of the row        |

GraphState:
| Field     | Type    | Description                                               |
|-----------|---------|-----------------------------------------------------------|
| nodeCount | number  | Total nodes in the semantic graph                         |
| turnCount | number  | Number of turns identified (each isTurnStart = new turn)  |
| mode      | string  | Current mode string from the capability                   |
| streaming | boolean | Whether output is currently streaming                     |

PlayerState:
| Field       | Type    | Description                                         |
|-------------|---------|-----------------------------------------------------|
| playing     | boolean | Whether playback is currently running               |
| time        | number  | Current playback position in seconds                |
| duration    | number  | Total recording duration in seconds                 |
| progress    | number  | Normalized position 0.0–1.0                         |
| frame       | number  | Current frame index                                 |
| totalFrames | number  | Total frames in the recording                       |
| speed       | number  | Current playback speed multiplier                   |

examples.title: Live session with classification tracking
Playback with full transport controls

criticalRules: Include a SemanticTerminal element with {...terminalProps} spread. Without it, the hook has no visual node and all controls are no-ops.
In playback mode, playerState is null until the recording is loaded. Always null-check before reading playerState fields.
classifiedRows can grow large in long sessions. If you only need counts, derive from graphState instead of scanning the array on every render.
saveRecording requires recording: true on the options. Calling it when recording is disabled is a no-op.
seek takes a time in seconds (absolute, not relative). Read playerState.duration to know the valid seek range.

metadata.title: useSystemInfo
metadata.description: Fetch real OS, CPU, memory, uptime, and user information from the host system.

sections.overview: useSystemInfo retrieves static and semi-static system facts — OS name, kernel version, hostname, logged-in user, shell, CPU model and core count, architecture, memory usage, and uptime — via the Love2D bridge RPC layer. On Love2D, data is gathered by Lua from /proc, io.popen, and os.getenv. Every returned sub-object carries a .toSysLog(path) method for structured append-only JSON logging. The hook fetches once on mount by default; pass a refresh interval in milliseconds to poll periodically for dynamic fields like memory and uptime.
sections.api:
```tsx
import {
  useSystemInfo,
  formatUptime,
  formatMemory,
  formatBytes,
} from '@reactjit/core';
```

Hook signature:
```tsx
useSystemInfo(refreshInterval?: number): SystemInfo
```

Parameters:

| Parameter       | Type   | Default | Description                                                      |
|-----------------|--------|---------|------------------------------------------------------------------|
| refreshInterval | number | 0       | ms between re-polls. 0 = fetch once on mount, no further polls. |

Returns: SystemInfo

SystemInfo shape:

| Field          | Type        | Description                                          |
|----------------|-------------|------------------------------------------------------|
| os             | string      | Distro name, e.g. "Ubuntu 24.04.1 LTS"              |
| kernel         | string      | Kernel release string, e.g. "6.8.0-51-generic"      |
| hostname       | string      | Machine hostname                                     |
| user           | string      | Logged-in username                                   |
| shell          | string      | Shell name, e.g. "bash", "zsh"                       |
| cpu            | string      | CPU model + core count, e.g. "Ryzen 9 7950X (32)"   |
| arch           | string      | Architecture, e.g. "x86_64"                         |
| memory         | MemoryInfo  | { used, total, unit: 'GiB' | 'MiB', toSysLog }     |
| uptime         | UptimeInfo  | { days, hours, minutes, toSysLog }                   |
| loading        | boolean     | true until the first fetch completes                 |
| toSysLog       | fn          | (path: string) => void — append all fields to file   |

MemoryInfo:

| Field    | Type          | Description                  |
|----------|---------------|------------------------------|
| used     | number        | Used memory in unit          |
| total    | number        | Total memory in unit         |
| unit     | 'GiB' | 'MiB' | Display unit              |
| toSysLog | fn            | Log this section to a file   |

UptimeInfo:

| Field    | Type   | Description                |
|----------|--------|----------------------------|
| days     | number | Whole days of uptime       |
| hours    | number | Remainder hours            |
| minutes  | number | Remainder minutes          |
| toSysLog | fn     | Log this section to a file |

Formatting utilities:
```tsx
formatUptime(u: { days: number; hours: number; minutes: number }): string
// "2 days, 5 hours, 30 mins" or "< 1 min"

formatMemory(m: { used: number; total: number; unit: string }): string
// "7.2 GiB / 31.2 GiB"

formatBytes(bytes: number): string
// "14.5 GiB" or "512 MiB"
```

examples.title: Display a neofetch-style system summary
Live memory polling every 5 seconds
Log a system snapshot to a file on mount
Conditional content based on available memory

seeAlso: useSystemMonitor — per-core CPU, process list, GPU, network, disk I/O
formatUptime — format UptimeInfo into a human-readable string
formatMemory — format MemoryInfo into "used / total unit"
formatBytes — format raw byte counts into GiB/MiB strings

metadata.title: useSystemMonitor
metadata.description: Real-time htop/nvtop-level system monitoring — per-core CPU, processes, GPU, network, and disk I/O.

sections.overview: useSystemMonitor provides comprehensive real-time system telemetry — per-core CPU usage, load averages, detailed memory breakdown (buffers, cached, swap), a sorted process list, GPU stats (NVIDIA and AMD), per-interface network rates, and per-device disk I/O rates. Data is refreshed on a configurable interval via the Love2D bridge RPC. Every sub-object exposes a .toSysLog(path) method for structured append-only JSON logging. Unlike useSystemInfo (which fetches static facts once), useSystemMonitor is designed for continuous live dashboards.
sections.api:
```tsx
import {
  useSystemMonitor,
  formatRate,
  formatTotalBytes,
} from '@reactjit/core';
```

Hook signature:
```tsx
useSystemMonitor(interval?: number, opts?: { processLimit?: number }): SystemMonitor
```

Parameters:

| Parameter          | Type   | Default | Description                                              |
|--------------------|--------|---------|----------------------------------------------------------|
| interval           | number | 1000    | ms between data refreshes.                               |
| opts.processLimit  | number | 20      | Maximum number of processes to return in the list.       |

Returns: SystemMonitor

SystemMonitor shape:

| Field     | Type              | Description                                              |
|-----------|-------------------|----------------------------------------------------------|
| cpu       | CpuInfo           | Aggregate and per-core CPU usage, load averages.         |
| memory    | DetailedMemory    | Full memory breakdown in GiB including swap.             |
| processes | ProcessInfo[]     | Top processes sorted by CPU usage.                       |
| tasks     | TaskCounts        | Total, running, sleeping, stopped, zombie counts.        |
| gpu       | GpuInfo[] or null | GPU stats if a supported GPU is present, else null.      |
| network   | NetworkInterface[]| Per-interface byte totals and transfer rates.            |
| disk      | DiskDevice[]      | Per-device read/write totals and rates.                  |
| loading   | boolean           | true until the first fetch completes.                    |
| toSysLog  | fn                | (path: string) => void — log full snapshot to file.      |

CpuInfo:

| Field    | Type                    | Description                              |
|----------|-------------------------|------------------------------------------|
| cores    | CoreInfo[]              | Per-core breakdown with toSysLog each.   |
| total    | number                  | Aggregate CPU usage 0-100.               |
| loadAvg  | [number, number, number]| 1-min, 5-min, 15-min load averages.      |
| toSysLog | fn                      | Log this section.                        |

CoreInfo:

| Field   | Type   | Description                          |
|---------|--------|--------------------------------------|
| id      | number | Core index (0-based).                |
| usage   | number | Total usage 0-100.                   |
| user    | number | User-space usage 0-100.              |
| system  | number | Kernel usage 0-100.                  |
| iowait  | number | I/O wait 0-100.                      |

DetailedMemory (all values in GiB):

| Field     | Type              | Description              |
|-----------|-------------------|--------------------------|
| total     | number            | Total physical RAM.      |
| used      | number            | Used (total - available).|
| free      | number            | Unused RAM.              |
| available | number            | Actually reclaimable.    |
| buffers   | number            | Kernel buffers.          |
| cached    | number            | Page cache.              |
| swap      | { total, used }   | Swap totals in GiB.      |
| unit      | 'GiB'             | Always GiB.              |

ProcessInfo:

| Field   | Type   | Description                        |
|---------|--------|------------------------------------|
| user    | string | Owning username.                   |
| pid     | number | Process ID.                        |
| cpu     | number | CPU usage % for this process.      |
| mem     | number | Memory usage % for this process.   |
| vsz     | number | Virtual size in KB.                |
| rss     | number | Resident set size in KB.           |
| tty     | string | Controlling terminal.              |
| stat    | string | Process state string (e.g. "S").   |
| command | string | Command name or path.              |

GpuInfo:

| Field       | Type             | Description                       |
|-------------|------------------|-----------------------------------|
| name        | string           | GPU model name.                   |
| vendor      | 'nvidia' | 'amd' | GPU vendor.                    |
| utilization | number           | GPU compute usage 0-100.          |
| memUsed     | number           | Used VRAM.                        |
| memTotal    | number           | Total VRAM.                       |
| memUnit     | string           | Display unit for VRAM values.     |
| temperature | number           | Degrees Celsius.                  |
| power       | number           | Power draw in watts.              |

NetworkInterface:

| Field   | Type   | Description                  |
|---------|--------|------------------------------|
| name    | string | Interface name, e.g. "eth0". |
| rxBytes | number | Total bytes received.        |
| txBytes | number | Total bytes transmitted.     |
| rxRate  | number | Current receive rate B/s.    |
| txRate  | number | Current transmit rate B/s.   |

DiskDevice:

| Field     | Type   | Description                        |
|-----------|--------|------------------------------------|
| name      | string | Device name, e.g. "sda".          |
| readRate  | number | Current read rate in B/s.          |
| writeRate | number | Current write rate in B/s.         |
| reads     | number | Cumulative read operations.        |
| writes    | number | Cumulative write operations.       |

Formatting utilities:
```tsx
formatRate(bytesPerSec: number): string
// "12.3 MB/s", "512.0 KB/s", "800 B/s"

formatTotalBytes(bytes: number): string
// "4.2 GB", "512.0 MB", "1.0 KB"
```

examples.title: CPU usage bar per core
Process list sorted by CPU (top 10)
GPU temperature and VRAM gauge
Network I/O rates per interface
Log full monitor snapshot every 10 seconds

seeAlso: useSystemInfo — one-shot static system facts (OS, hostname, kernel, shell)
formatRate — format bytes-per-second into KB/s, MB/s, GB/s strings
formatTotalBytes — format total byte counts into human-readable strings

metadata.title: useWebSocket
metadata.description: Open a persistent WebSocket client connection and expose send, lastMessage, and status state.

sections.overview: useWebSocket opens a WebSocket client connection to the given URL and keeps it alive for the lifetime of the component. It works across all ReactJIT targets: on Love2D the WebSocket global is polyfilled through the bridge using lua-websocket; on web and Node targets the platform's native WebSocket is used. The hook tracks the connection lifecycle through a typed status field and exposes the most recently received message string. Pass null as the URL to remain disconnected. The send function is a no-op when the socket is not in the open state, so it is always safe to call.
sections.api:
```tsx
import { useWebSocket } from '@reactjit/core';
import type { WebSocketStatus } from '@reactjit/core';
```

Parameters:
| Param | Type           | Default  | Description |
|-------|----------------|----------|-------------|
| url   | string \| null | required | WebSocket server URL (e.g. "ws://localhost:9050"). Pass null to stay closed. |

Returns:
| Field       | Type            | Description |
|-------------|-----------------|-------------|
| status      | WebSocketStatus | Current connection state: "connecting", "open", "closed", or "error". |
| send        | (data: string) => void | Send a string message. No-op if the connection is not in the "open" state. |
| lastMessage | string \| null  | The most recently received message string, or null if nothing has arrived yet. |
| error       | string \| null  | Human-readable error string if status is "error", otherwise null. |

examples.title: Connect to a server and display live data
Send commands to a server on button press
Conditional connection — connect only when a toggle is on
Display an error when the connection fails

metadata.title: useWindowDimensions
metadata.description: Get the current viewport width and height with reactive updates on window resize.

sections.overview:
useWindowDimensions returns an object containing the current viewport width and height. The values update reactively whenever the window is resized, allowing components to respond to layout changes dynamically.

The hook subscribes to the 'viewport' bridge event and updates state whenever new dimensions are received from the Lua runtime. Initial dimensions may be {width: 0, height: 0} until the first viewport event is dispatched.
sections.api:
```tsx
import { useWindowDimensions } from '@reactjit/core';
```

Signature:
  function useWindowDimensions(): { width: number; height: number }

Parameters:
  (none)

Returns:
  Object with two properties:
    width: number
      Current viewport width in pixels (or character columns for grid targets)
    height: number
      Current viewport height in pixels (or character rows for grid targets)

examples.title: Responsive layout
Conditional rendering based on viewport size
Calculate aspect ratio

criticalRules: Initial dimensions may be {width: 0, height: 0} until the first viewport event fires
Check for zero dimensions before dividing (e.g., calculating aspect ratio)
Must be used inside a BridgeProvider context
Updates are reactive -- components re-render automatically on resize
For Love2D, dimensions are set in conf.lua via t.window.width and t.window.height
Grid targets measure in character units, not pixels
seeAlso: useloveevent.txt -- Subscribe to custom viewport-related events
Box primitive -- Use percentage widths/heights relative to these dimensions

metadata.title: Webhook Hooks
metadata.description: Hooks for receiving incoming webhooks over HTTP and sending signed webhook payloads with retries.

sections.overview: The @reactjit/webhooks package covers both sides of the webhook pattern: receiving HTTP POST payloads from external services (GitHub, Stripe, CI systems, etc.) and sending signed JSON payloads to external endpoints. The receiver hook (useWebhook) starts an HTTP server via the Love2D bridge and verifies HMAC-SHA256 signatures using the same algorithm GitHub, Slack, and most modern platforms use. The sender hook (useWebhookSender) wraps fetch with automatic signing, exponential-backoff retries, and React state for loading/error tracking. An imperative sendWebhook function is also exported for use outside React.
sections.api:
--- useWebhook ---

Start an HTTP listener and receive incoming webhook events as React state. Each arriving request is parsed, optionally signature-verified, and prepended to the events array. Requires the Love2D HTTP server bridge (Lua-side httpserver module).

  import { useWebhook } from '@reactjit/webhooks';

  const result: WebhookReceiverResult = useWebhook(
    port: number | null,
    path: string,
    options?: WebhookReceiverOptions,
  );

Parameters:
  port     number | null   Port to listen on. Pass null to disable the server.
  path     string          URL path to handle (e.g. '/webhook/github')
  options  WebhookReceiverOptions   Optional configuration (see below)

WebhookReceiverOptions fields:
  secret           string     Shared secret for HMAC-SHA256 signature verification. If omitted, event.verified is always null.
  signatureHeader  string     Header name carrying the signature. Default: 'x-hub-signature-256' (GitHub format).
  maxEvents        number     Maximum events to keep in the queue (newest first). Default: 100.
  responseStatus   number     HTTP status code sent back to the caller. Default: 200.
  responseBody     string     Response body sent back. Default: '{"ok":true}'.
  methods          string[]   Filter by HTTP method. Default: accepts all methods.
  host             string     Host/interface to bind to. Default: Lua server default.

Returns: WebhookReceiverResult
  events         WebhookEvent[]      All received events, newest first (up to maxEvents)
  latest         WebhookEvent | null The most recently received event, or null if empty
  ready          boolean             True once the HTTP server is bound and listening
  totalReceived  number              Running count of all events received (including evicted ones)
  clear          () => void          Empty the event queue without stopping the server

WebhookEvent fields:
  id        string                        Auto-generated unique event ID (e.g. 'wh_1700000000_abc123')
  timestamp string                        ISO timestamp of when the event was received
  method    string                        HTTP method (usually 'POST')
  path      string                        Request path
  headers   Record<string, string>        All request headers
  body      T                             Parsed body — JSON object if Content-Type is application/json, raw string otherwise
  rawBody   string                        Original unparsed request body
  query     Record<string, string>        Query parameters from the URL
  verified  boolean | null                HMAC verification result. true = valid, false = invalid, null = no secret configured

--- useWebhookSender ---

React hook for sending webhook payloads with loading and error state tracking. Wraps sendWebhook with default options that apply to every call made through the returned send function.

  import { useWebhookSender } from '@reactjit/webhooks';

  const result: WebhookSenderResult = useWebhookSender(options?: WebhookSenderOptions);

Parameters:
  options  WebhookSenderOptions   Optional defaults for all sends

WebhookSenderOptions fields:
  headers  Record<string, string>   Default headers merged into every request
  secret   string                   Default shared secret for HMAC signing
  retries  number                   Default retry count. Default: 3

Returns: WebhookSenderResult
  send        (url: string, payload: any, options?: WebhookSendOptions) => Promise<WebhookSendResult>
              Send a payload. Per-call options override the hook-level defaults.
  sending     boolean                  True while a send is in flight
  lastResult  WebhookSendResult | null Result of the last completed send
  error       Error | null             Error from the last failed send, or null

WebhookSendResult fields:
  ok        boolean   True if the server responded with a 2xx status
  status    number    HTTP response status code (0 on network failure)
  body      string    Raw response body
  attempts  number    Total number of attempts made (1 + retries used)

--- sendWebhook (imperative) ---

Send a webhook payload to a URL without React state. Use this in event handlers, Lua RPC callbacks, or any context where a hook is not appropriate.

  import { sendWebhook } from '@reactjit/webhooks';

  const result: WebhookSendResult = await sendWebhook(
    url: string,
    payload: any,
    options?: WebhookSendOptions,
  );

Parameters:
  url      string               Destination URL
  payload  any                  Payload to send. Objects are serialized as JSON automatically.
  options  WebhookSendOptions   Optional per-call configuration

WebhookSendOptions fields:
  method           string                   HTTP method. Default: 'POST'
  headers          Record<string, string>   Additional headers merged with Content-Type: application/json
  secret           string                   Shared secret — generates an HMAC-SHA256 signature header
  signatureHeader  string                   Header name for the signature. Default: 'x-hub-signature-256'
  retries          number                   Retry count on 5xx or network failure. Default: 0 (no retries)
  retryDelay       number                   Base delay in ms between retries. Doubles each retry (exponential backoff). Default: 1000

Returns: Promise<WebhookSendResult>
Does not throw — errors are captured in the result object.

--- hmacSHA256 (utility) ---

Compute an HMAC-SHA256 hex digest. Exported for use in custom signing schemes or signature verification outside of webhooks. Backed by @noble/hashes (audited).

  import { hmacSHA256 } from '@reactjit/webhooks';

  const hex: string = hmacSHA256(key: string, message: string);

Parameters:
  key      string   Shared secret
  message  string   Message to sign

Returns: string — lowercase hex digest (64 characters)

--- timingSafeEqual (utility) ---

Compare two hex strings in constant time to prevent timing side-channel attacks. Use this when comparing received signatures against computed ones.

  import { timingSafeEqual } from '@reactjit/webhooks';

  const equal: boolean = timingSafeEqual(a: string, b: string);

Parameters:
  a  string   First hex string
  b  string   Second hex string

Returns: boolean — false immediately if lengths differ (length leakage is acceptable; value is not)
seeAlso: useServer — lower-level HTTP server hook that useWebhook builds upon
useFetch — outbound HTTP requests without webhook-specific signing logic

metadata.title: AnimatedValue
metadata.description: Mutable value container that drives animations through timing, spring, and interpolation methods.

sections.overview:
AnimatedValue is the core class of the animation system. It holds a mutable numeric value and provides methods to animate it (timing and spring), interpolate it to other ranges, and listen for changes. When an animation is active, the value updates every frame and notifies all listeners.

AnimatedValue instances are typically created via the useAnimation hook, which handles React lifecycle integration. You can also create them directly for non-React usage.
sections.api:
Constructor:
  new AnimatedValue(initialValue: number)

Methods:

  getValue(): number
    Returns the current numeric value.

  setValue(value: number): void
    Immediately sets the value. Stops any active animation first. Notifies all listeners.

  addListener(callback: (value: number) => void): () => void
    Registers a callback that fires whenever the value changes (from animation or setValue).
    Returns an unsubscribe function.

  timing(config: TimingConfig): Animation
    Creates a timing-based animation. Returns an Animation object with start() and stop().

    TimingConfig:
      toValue: number              Target value
      duration?: number            Duration in milliseconds (default: 300)
      easing?: EasingFunction      Easing function (default: Easing.easeInOut)
      delay?: number               Delay before animation starts (default: 0)

  spring(config: SpringConfig): Animation
    Creates a physics-based spring animation. Returns an Animation object.

    SpringConfig:
      toValue: number              Target value
      stiffness?: number           Spring stiffness (default: 100)
      damping?: number             Damping coefficient (default: 10)
      mass?: number                Mass of the object (default: 1)
      velocity?: number            Initial velocity (default: 0)
      restThreshold?: number       Threshold for considering the spring at rest (default: 0.001)

  interpolate(config: InterpolationConfig): number | string
    Maps the current value through input/output ranges. Returns the interpolated result.

    InterpolationConfig:
      inputRange: number[]           Input values to map from (ascending)
      outputRange: number[] | string[]  Output values to map to (same length as inputRange)
      extrapolate?: 'clamp' | 'extend'  Behavior outside inputRange (default: 'extend')

    When outputRange contains strings (e.g., color values in "rgba(...)" format), color
    interpolation is performed automatically.

Animation interface:
  start(callback?: (result: { finished: boolean }) => void): void
    Starts the animation. Optional callback fires when animation completes.
    result.finished is true if animation completed naturally, false if stopped.

  stop(): void
    Stops the animation at its current value.

examples.title: Basic timing animation
Spring animation
Interpolation
Chained animations with callback

criticalRules: setValue() stops any active animation before setting the value
Starting a new animation via start() automatically stops any previously running animation on the same AnimatedValue
The start() callback receives { finished: false } when stop() is called
InterpolationConfig inputRange must be in ascending order
inputRange and outputRange must have the same length (minimum 2 elements)
Color interpolation only works with "rgba(...)" format strings
AnimatedValue is mutable -- it does not trigger React re-renders on its own. Use useAnimation hook for React integration.
seeAlso: useanimation.txt -- React hook that wraps AnimatedValue
easing.txt -- Easing functions for timing animations
usespring.txt -- Convenience hook for spring transitions

metadata.title: Composite Animations
metadata.description: Combining animations with parallel, sequence, stagger, and loop.

sections.overview:
ReactJIT provides four functions for composing multiple animations into coordinated sequences: parallel (run simultaneously), sequence (run one after another), stagger (start each with a delay offset), and loop (repeat an animation). All return an Animation object with start() and stop() methods, so they can themselves be composed into larger animations.

These functions operate on Animation objects (returned by AnimatedValue.timing() and AnimatedValue.spring()), not on AnimatedValues directly. This means you create individual animations first, then compose them.
sections.api:
All functions are imported from '@reactjit/core':

  import { parallel, sequence, stagger, loop } from '@reactjit/core';

parallel(animations: Animation[]): Animation
  Starts all animations simultaneously. Completes when ALL animations finish.
  The callback receives { finished: true } only if every animation finished naturally.
  If any animation is stopped, finished is false.
  Passing an empty array completes immediately with { finished: true }.

sequence(animations: Animation[]): Animation
  Runs animations one after another in order. Each animation starts when the previous
  one finishes. If any animation in the sequence does not finish (stopped), the sequence
  halts and reports { finished: false }.

stagger(delay: number, animations: Animation[]): Animation
  Starts each animation with an incremental delay. The first starts immediately, the
  second after `delay` ms, the third after `2 * delay` ms, etc. Completes when all
  animations finish.

  delay: number    Milliseconds between each animation start.
  animations: Animation[]    The animations to stagger.

loop(animation: Animation, config?: { iterations?: number }): Animation
  Repeats a single animation.

  animation: Animation    The animation to repeat.
  config.iterations?: number
    Number of iterations. Default: -1 (infinite).
    -1 = infinite loop (must be stopped manually).
    Positive number = run that many times, then complete.

All return Animation:
  {
    start(callback?: (result: { finished: boolean }) => void): void
    stop(): void
  }

examples.title: Parallel fade + slide
Sequential animation chain
Staggered list appearance
Infinite pulsing loop
Loop with finite iterations

criticalRules: All functions return Animation objects -- you must call .start() to begin
Always call .stop() in effect cleanup to prevent memory leaks and runaway animations
parallel completes when ALL animations finish -- one slow animation holds up the group
sequence halts if any animation is stopped (reports finished: false)
stagger delay is in milliseconds, applied cumulatively (0, delay, 2*delay, 3*delay, ...)
loop with iterations: -1 runs forever -- you MUST stop it manually
Composite animations can be nested: loop(sequence([parallel([...]), ...])) is valid
Empty animation arrays complete immediately with { finished: true }
seeAlso: useanimation.txt -- React hook for animated values
easing.txt -- Easing functions used with timing animations

metadata.title: Easing Functions
metadata.description: Library of easing functions for timing-based animations.

sections.overview:
ReactJIT provides a set of easing functions that control the rate of change during timing-based animations. Easing functions take a progress value t (0 to 1) and return a transformed value that determines the animation's velocity curve. They are used with AnimatedValue.timing() and useTransition().

The library includes standard easing curves (linear, easeIn, easeOut, easeInOut), a cubic bezier function for custom curves, a bounce effect, and an elastic overshoot effect. All easing functions conform to the EasingFunction type: (t: number) => number.
sections.api:
Type:
  type EasingFunction = (t: number) => number

  Input: t is the linear progress from 0 (start) to 1 (end).
  Output: the eased progress value (may overshoot 0-1 range for elastic/bounce effects).

Static easing functions:

  Easing.linear
    (t) => t
    Constant velocity. No acceleration or deceleration.

  Easing.easeIn
    (t) => t * t
    Quadratic ease-in. Starts slow, accelerates.

  Easing.easeOut
    (t) => t * (2 - t)
    Quadratic ease-out. Starts fast, decelerates.

  Easing.easeInOut
    (t) => t < 0.5 ? 2 * t * t : -1 + (4 - 2 * t) * t
    Quadratic ease-in-out. Starts slow, accelerates, then decelerates.
    This is the default easing for AnimatedValue.timing().

  Easing.bounce
    Standard bounce curve. Simulates a ball bouncing and settling.
    Uses four quadratic segments with decreasing amplitude.

Factory easing functions (return an EasingFunction):

  Easing.bezier(x1: number, y1: number, x2: number, y2: number): EasingFunction
    Creates a cubic bezier easing function. The four parameters define two control points
    (P1 and P2) of a cubic bezier curve from (0,0) to (1,1). Uses Newton-Raphson iteration
    (8 iterations) to solve the cubic for the given t value.

    Common presets:
      Easing.bezier(0.25, 0.1, 0.25, 1)     // CSS ease
      Easing.bezier(0.42, 0, 1, 1)           // CSS ease-in
      Easing.bezier(0, 0, 0.58, 1)           // CSS ease-out
      Easing.bezier(0.42, 0, 0.58, 1)        // CSS ease-in-out
      Easing.bezier(0.16, 1, 0.3, 1)         // Expo ease-out
      Easing.bezier(0.34, 1.56, 0.64, 1)     // Back ease-out (overshoot)

  Easing.elastic(bounciness?: number): EasingFunction
    Creates an elastic easing that overshoots the target and oscillates before settling.
    Default bounciness: 1. Higher values = more oscillation. Lower values = less overshoot.
    Uses exponential decay with sinusoidal oscillation.

examples.title: Different easing curves
Custom bezier curve
Elastic animation

criticalRules: Easing functions must accept 0 and return 0, and accept 1 and return 1
Easing.easeInOut is the default when no easing is specified in timing()
Easing.bezier uses Newton-Raphson iteration (8 iterations) -- extremely fast but not exact for extreme curves
Easing.elastic and Easing.bounce may produce values outside the 0-1 range (overshoot)
Easing.elastic(0) will produce extreme oscillation -- use values >= 0.001
All easing functions are stateless and can be shared across animations

metadata.title: Animation System
metadata.description: Overview of the ReactJIT animation system including animated values, timing, springs, easing, and composite animations.

sections.overview:
ReactJIT includes a complete animation system built on the AnimatedValue class. It provides timing-based animations (with easing functions), physics-based spring animations, and composite animations (parallel, sequence, stagger, loop). Three React hooks make it easy to integrate animations into components: useAnimation for full control, useSpring for automatic spring transitions, and useTransition for automatic timing-based transitions.

The animation system works in both web mode (using requestAnimationFrame) and native mode (falling back to setTimeout at ~60fps when requestAnimationFrame is unavailable). All active animations share a single frame loop for efficiency. Animations are batched to avoid excessive re-renders.

examples.title: Fade-in animation
Spring animation on state change

criticalRules: AnimatedValue.setValue() stops any active animation before setting the value
Starting a new animation on an AnimatedValue automatically stops the previous one
useAnimation cleanup stops active animations on unmount
useSpring and useTransition skip animation on first render (value starts at target)
Composite animations (parallel, sequence, etc.) return Animation objects with start/stop
Animation callbacks receive { finished: boolean } -- false if stop() was called

metadata.title: useAnimation
metadata.description: Primary React hook for consuming animated values with automatic re-rendering.

sections.overview:
useAnimation is the primary hook for using animated values in React components. It creates an AnimatedValue instance (once, on first render), subscribes to its value changes, and triggers component re-renders when the value updates. Value updates are batched to animation frames so the component re-renders at most once per frame, even if the animated value changes multiple times.

The hook returns a tuple of [AnimatedValue, currentValue]. Use the AnimatedValue to create and start animations (.timing(), .spring()). Use currentValue in your JSX to render the animated style properties.
sections.api:
Signature:
  function useAnimation(initialValue: number): [AnimatedValue, number]

Parameters:
  initialValue: number
    The initial numeric value of the animated value.

Returns:
  [animatedValue, currentValue]

  animatedValue: AnimatedValue
    The AnimatedValue instance. Stable reference across re-renders.
    Use .timing(config) or .spring(config) to create animations, then call .start().
    Use .setValue(n) to set immediately.
    Use .interpolate(config) to map to other ranges.

  currentValue: number
    The current numeric value, updated each animation frame. Use this in your JSX
    for style props like opacity, width, translateX, etc.

Lifecycle:
  - AnimatedValue is created once (stored in a ref)
  - A listener is registered on mount to batch value changes into React state updates
  - On unmount, the listener is removed and any active animation is stopped

examples.title: Opacity fade-in
Spring press effect
Continuous rotation

criticalRules: The AnimatedValue instance is created once and stored in a ref -- it is a stable reference
The currentValue triggers a React re-render when it changes (batched to animation frames)
On unmount, active animations are stopped and the listener is removed
Do not create AnimatedValue instances manually for React components -- use this hook
The hook does not clean up animations started in effects -- you must stop them in effect cleanup
seeAlso: usespring.txt -- Convenience hook for automatic spring transitions
usetransition.txt -- Convenience hook for automatic timing transitions

metadata.title: useSpring
metadata.description: Convenience hook that automatically springs to a target value when it changes.

sections.overview:
useSpring is a convenience hook that automatically starts a spring animation whenever its target value changes. It is the simplest way to add spring-based motion to a component -- you provide the target value and optional spring configuration, and the hook returns the current interpolated value that updates each frame.

The hook skips animation on the first render (the value starts at the target immediately). On subsequent renders where the target changes, a spring animation is started to transition from the current value to the new target.
sections.api:
Signature:
  function useSpring(targetValue: number, config?: { stiffness?: number; damping?: number; mass?: number }): number

Parameters:
  targetValue: number
    The value to spring towards. When this changes, a new spring animation starts.

  config?: object (optional)
    stiffness?: number   Spring stiffness. Higher = snappier. Default: 100
    damping?: number     Damping coefficient. Higher = less oscillation. Default: 10
    mass?: number        Mass of the object. Higher = slower/heavier. Default: 1

Returns:
  number
    The current interpolated value. Updates each animation frame, triggering re-render.

Spring behavior:
  - stiffness controls how strongly the spring pulls toward the target
  - damping controls how quickly oscillation settles
  - mass controls how heavy the animated object feels
  - Low damping + high stiffness = bouncy
  - High damping + low stiffness = sluggish
  - The animation uses Verlet integration with clamped dt (max 64ms) to avoid instability

examples.title: Toggle width
Bouncy spring
Smooth height transition

criticalRules: Animation is skipped on first render -- value starts at targetValue immediately
A new spring animation starts every time targetValue changes
The previous animation is automatically stopped when a new one starts
Config changes do NOT restart the animation -- only targetValue changes trigger animation
Config is read from a ref at animation start time, so it can be dynamic
The returned value triggers React re-render every animation frame
seeAlso: usetransition.txt -- Timing-based convenience hook (alternative to spring)

metadata.title: useTransition
metadata.description: Convenience hook that automatically transitions to a value with timing-based easing.

sections.overview:
useTransition is a convenience hook that automatically starts a timing-based animation whenever its target value changes. It is the simplest way to add eased transitions to a component -- you provide the target value and optional timing configuration, and the hook returns the current interpolated value.

Like useSpring, the hook skips animation on the first render (the value starts at the target immediately). On subsequent renders where the target changes, a timing animation with the specified duration and easing is started.
sections.api:
Signature:
  function useTransition(value: number, config?: { duration?: number; easing?: EasingFunction }): number

Parameters:
  value: number
    The target value to transition towards. When this changes, a new timing animation starts.

  config?: object (optional)
    duration?: number            Duration in milliseconds. Default: 300
    easing?: EasingFunction      Easing function. Default: Easing.easeInOut

Returns:
  number
    The current interpolated value. Updates each animation frame, triggering re-render.

examples.title: Smooth opacity transition
Custom easing
Progress bar animation

criticalRules: Animation is skipped on first render -- value starts at the target immediately
A new timing animation starts every time value changes
The previous animation is automatically stopped when a new one starts
Config changes do NOT restart the animation -- only value changes trigger animation
Config is read from a ref at animation start time, so it can be dynamic
Default easing is Easing.easeInOut; default duration is 300ms

metadata.title: Routing
metadata.description: Brief overview of the @reactjit/router package for in-app navigation

sections.overview:
ReactJIT includes a @reactjit/router package for in-app navigation between screens. Since there is no browser URL bar or history API in most targets, routing is purely in-memory. The router manages a stack of screens and provides hooks for navigating between them.

The router follows a simple pattern: define your screens as React components, register them with the router, and use navigation hooks to push, pop, or replace screens.

metadata.title: Router
metadata.description: Client-side routing for ReactJIT apps across all targets

sections.overview:
@reactjit/router provides client-side routing with no external dependencies. It works on every ReactJIT target by abstracting history management behind a HistoryAdapter interface: memory-based for native targets, browser-based for web. Route matching supports named parameters (:id), optional parameters (:id?), and wildcards (*), with highest-specificity-wins semantics when multiple routes match.

Import: import { RouterProvider, Routes, Route, Link, Navigate, Outlet, useRouter, useNavigate, useLocation, useParams, useRoute, createMemoryHistory, createBrowserHistory } from '@reactjit/router';
sections.api:
---- createMemoryHistory(options?) ----------------------------------------

Creates an in-memory history stack. Use this on all non-web targets (love2d,
terminal, cc, nvim, hs, awesome).

  createMemoryHistory(options?: MemoryHistoryOptions): HistoryAdapter

  MemoryHistoryOptions:
    initialEntries?: string[]   // Initial path stack. Default: ['/']
    initialIndex?:  number      // Active index into initialEntries.
                                // Default: last entry.

  Returns: HistoryAdapter — pass to <RouterProvider history={...} />

---- createBrowserHistory() -----------------------------------------------

Creates a history adapter backed by the browser's History API. Web target only.
Listens to popstate for back/forward button support.

  createBrowserHistory(): HistoryAdapter

---- RouterProvider --------------------------------------------------------

Context provider. Wrap your entire app (or the routed subtree) with this once.
All routing hooks and components require a RouterProvider ancestor.

  <RouterProvider history={HistoryAdapter}>
    {children}
  </RouterProvider>

  Props:
    history   HistoryAdapter   Required. From createMemoryHistory() or
                               createBrowserHistory().
    children  ReactNode        Required.

---- Routes ----------------------------------------------------------------

Container that evaluates all child <Route> elements and renders the single
best-matching one. "Best" means highest specificity score among all patterns
that match the current pathname. Use this for exclusive routing.

  <Routes>
    {children}   // One or more <Route> elements
  </Routes>

---- Route -----------------------------------------------------------------

Declares a route. Must be a direct child of <Routes>. Has no visible output
on its own — Routes does the rendering.

  <Route path={string} element={ReactNode} />

  Props:
    path     string      Route pattern. Supports:
                           /users           — static segment
                           /users/:id       — named parameter
                           /users/:id?      — optional parameter
                           /files/*         — wildcard (rest of path)
                           *                — catch-all / 404 fallback
    element  ReactNode   What to render when this route matches.

  Specificity scoring (higher = matched first):
    static segment   4 points
    required param   3 points
    optional param   2 points
    wildcard         1 point

---- Link ------------------------------------------------------------------

Navigable wrapper. Calls navigate() on click. On web it renders as <a> so
the browser gets hover state, right-click, and native link behaviors.

  <Link to={string} replace? style? {...rest}>
    {children}
  </Link>

  Props:
    to        string     Required. Target path.
    replace?  boolean    Replace current history entry instead of pushing.
                         Default: false.
    style?    object     Style object forwarded to the underlying element.
    children  ReactNode  Required.

---- Navigate (declarative redirect) ----------------------------------------

When rendered, immediately navigates to `to`. Useful inside route elements for
conditional redirects or deprecated-path forwarding.

  <Navigate to={string} replace? />

  Props:
    to       string    Required. Target path.
    replace? boolean   Use replace instead of push. Default: false.

---- Outlet ----------------------------------------------------------------

Renders nested route content. Place inside a parent route's element component
to mark where child routes should appear. Backed by OutletContext.

  <Outlet />

  No props.

---- useRouter() -----------------------------------------------------------

Returns the full router state object. Prefer the focused hooks (useNavigate,
useLocation, useParams) unless you need everything at once.

  useRouter(): RouterState

  RouterState:
    location  Location                                    Current location.
    params    Record<string, string>                      Matched route params.
    navigate  (to: string, opts?: NavigateOptions) => void
    back      () => void
    forward   () => void

  Throws if called outside a <RouterProvider>.

---- useNavigate() ---------------------------------------------------------

Returns the navigate function. The most common hook for imperative navigation.

  useNavigate(): (to: string, options?: NavigateOptions) => void

  NavigateOptions:
    replace?  boolean   Replace history entry instead of pushing.

  Throws if called outside a <RouterProvider>.

---- useLocation() ---------------------------------------------------------

Returns the current Location object. Re-renders the component whenever the
location changes.

  useLocation(): Location

  Location:
    pathname  string   The path portion (e.g. '/users/42').
    search    string   Query string (e.g. '?tab=profile').
    hash      string   Hash fragment (e.g. '#bio').

  Throws if called outside a <RouterProvider>.

---- useParams<T>() --------------------------------------------------------

Returns the named URL parameters extracted from the matched route pattern.
Only populated inside a route rendered by <Routes> — parameters are provided
by RouteParamsContext.

  useParams<T extends Record<string, string> = Record<string, string>>(): T

  Returns: Object mapping parameter names to their decoded string values.
           Empty object if no parameters matched or used outside <Routes>.

  Example: route '/users/:id', pathname '/users/42' -> { id: '42' }

---- useRoute(pattern) -----------------------------------------------------

Imperatively match the current pathname against a pattern. Returns a tuple of
[matched, params]. Useful for conditional rendering without <Routes>.

  useRoute(pattern: string): [boolean, Record<string, string>]

  Arguments:
    pattern  string   Route pattern (same syntax as <Route path={...} />).

  Returns:
    [0]  boolean                 True if current pathname matches.
    [1]  Record<string, string>  Extracted params if matched, {} otherwise.

---- HistoryAdapter interface -----------------------------------------------

Implement this to add custom history backends (e.g., hash-based routing).

  interface HistoryAdapter {
    readonly location: Location
    push(path: string): void
    replace(path: string): void
    back(): void
    forward(): void
    subscribe(listener: (location: Location) => void): () => void
  }

---- Low-level matching utilities -------------------------------------------

  matchRoute(pattern: string, pathname: string): RouteMatch
    RouteMatch: { matched: boolean; params: Record<string, string>; path: string }

  scorePattern(pattern: string): number
    Returns specificity score. Higher = more specific.

  findBestMatch(patterns: string[], pathname: string): RouteMatch | null
    Returns the highest-scoring match among all patterns, or null.

  parsePath(path: string): Location
    Parses '/users/42?tab=info#bio' into { pathname, search, hash }.

  locationToString(loc: Location): string
    Reconstructs a full path string from a Location object.

metadata.title: Routing
metadata.description: URL-based navigation and route matching for all targets

sections.overview: The ReactJIT router provides declarative, URL-based navigation across all rendering targets. It uses a history adapter pattern to support both browser-based navigation (web) and in-memory navigation (Love2D, terminal, grid targets). Routes are matched via path patterns with parameter extraction, and the system provides both component-based navigation (Link) and imperative navigation (useNavigate).
criticalRules: Always wrap your app in RouterProvider with a history adapter
Use createMemoryHistory for non-browser targets (Love2D, terminal, grid)
Use createBrowserHistory for web targets
Routes must be children of Routes component for best-match semantics
Static routes (/users/new) are always matched before parameterized routes (/users/:id)
Path patterns are normalized — trailing slashes are removed (except for "/")
useParams only works inside a Route element's component tree
useRouter throws an error if used outside RouterProvider
Link components must have a "to" prop — empty strings navigate to "/"
Navigate triggers on mount — wrap in conditional rendering for guarded navigation
Optional parameters (:id?) match both /users and /users/123
Wildcards (*) capture the rest of the path as $rest param
Route matching is case-sensitive
Query strings and hashes are preserved but not used for route matching
Multiple Routes components can coexist (separate routing contexts)
Outlet only renders content when used with nested route definitions
back() and forward() do nothing at history boundaries (beginning/end of stack)
replace: true navigation replaces current entry without growing history stack
RouterProvider's history prop must be stable — don't recreate on every render
seeAlso: State Management — useContext for sharing route-derived state
Event Handling — Pressable and onClick for custom navigation UI
Layout Components — Box, FlexRow for navigation bars and route containers
Hooks — useState for client-side route state, useEffect for navigation side effects
Animation — useSpring for route transition animations
Modal — Navigate inside modals for dialog routing patterns
TextInput — Form navigation with query parameters

metadata.title: AwesomeWM Target
metadata.description: ReactJIT renderer for AwesomeWM Linux tiling window manager widgets

sections.overview:
The AwesomeWM target (@reactjit/awesome) renders ReactJIT components as widgets in the Awesome tiling window manager on Linux. Rendering uses Cairo for 2D graphics and Pango for text shaping, giving high-quality output with full antialiasing. Communication happens via stdio between the Node.js process and the AwesomeWM Lua runtime.

This target lets you build status bars, notification panels, system dashboards, or any custom widget that integrates into your AwesomeWM desktop.
sections.api:
esbuild Configuration:
  --platform=node
  --format=esm

Requirements:
  AwesomeWM running on Linux.
  The Node.js process is spawned by AwesomeWM and communicates via stdio.

examples.title: AwesomeWM Widget Entry Point

metadata.title: ComputerCraft Target
metadata.description: ReactJIT renderer for Minecraft ComputerCraft Tweaked terminals

sections.overview:
The ComputerCraft target (@reactjit/cc) renders ReactJIT components inside Minecraft via the ComputerCraft: Tweaked mod. The CC terminal provides a 51x19 character display with a fixed 16-color palette. Communication between the Node.js process (running your React app) and the in-game terminal happens over WebSocket.

This is the most constrained target in terms of display capabilities, but it demonstrates the framework's multi-target architecture: the same React component model works even on a 16-color 51x19 character grid inside a video game.
sections.api:
esbuild Configuration:
  --platform=node
  --format=esm
  --external:ws

Display Constraints:
  Width:  51 characters
  Height: 19 characters
  Colors: 16 (CC palette: white, orange, magenta, lightBlue, yellow, lime,
          pink, gray, lightGray, cyan, purple, blue, brown, green, red, black)

examples.title: CC App Setup

criticalRules: Always include --external:ws in the esbuild configuration. The ws package
uses native Node.js modules that cannot be bundled.
Design for the 51x19 grid. Components that work on larger displays will
overflow or truncate on CC terminals.
Use the 16-color palette consciously. Subtle color differences will be
lost when mapped to the nearest palette entry.
seeAlso: 09-targets/terminal.txt - Similar grid target without color constraint

metadata.title: Hammerspoon Target
metadata.description: ReactJIT renderer for Hammerspoon macOS automation overlays

sections.overview: The Hammerspoon target (@reactjit/hs) renders ReactJIT components as pixel-perfect overlays on macOS using Hammerspoon's hs.canvas API. Communication happens over WebSocket between the Node.js process (running your React app) and the Hammerspoon Lua runtime. This lets you build rich floating dashboards, HUDs, or status panels that render directly on your macOS desktop.
sections.api:
esbuild Configuration:
  --platform=node
  --format=esm
  --external:ws

Requirements:
  Hammerspoon installed on macOS.
  WebSocket connection from Hammerspoon Lua to the Node.js server.

examples.title: Hammerspoon App Entry Point

criticalRules: Always include --external:ws in the esbuild configuration.
The Hammerspoon Lua side must initiate the WebSocket connection.

metadata.title: Targets
metadata.description: Hub page listing all seven ReactJIT render targets

sections.overview:
ReactJIT renders to seven distinct targets. Each target has its own painter, transport mechanism, and build configuration. The core component API (Box, Text, Image, Pressable, and all higher-level components) works across all of them. The differences are in rendering fidelity, color support, input capabilities, and distribution format.

Choose your target based on your deployment environment. Love2D is the primary target with the most features. Web mode runs in browsers. The grid targets (terminal, CC, Neovim, HS, Awesome) operate on character grids or pixel canvases within their host applications.

examples.title: Target Comparison
examples.code:
Target          | Transport    | Colors     | Input              | Build Format
----------------|-------------|------------|--------------------|--------------
Love2D          | QuickJS FFI | Truecolor  | Mouse/KB/Touch/Pad | IIFE bundle
Web             | Module.FS   | Full CSS   | Mouse/KB/Touch     | ESM bundle
Terminal        | (built-in)  | Truecolor  | Keyboard           | Node.js ESM
ComputerCraft   | WebSocket   | 16 colors  | Keyboard           | Node.js ESM
Neovim          | stdio       | 256 color  | Keyboard           | Node.js ESM
Hammerspoon     | WebSocket   | Truecolor  | Mouse/Keyboard     | Node.js ESM
AwesomeWM       | stdio       | Truecolor  | Mouse/Keyboard     | Node.js ESM

metadata.title: Love2D Target
metadata.description: The primary ReactJIT target - pixel-perfect rendering via the Love2D game engine

sections.overview:
Love2D is the primary and most mature ReactJIT target. Your React components run inside QuickJS (a lightweight JavaScript engine embedded in the Love2D process via libquickjs.so). The Lua runtime handles layout computation and rendering using love.graphics draw calls. This gives you pixel-perfect rendering with full support for mouse, keyboard, touch, and gamepad input.

The Love2D target supports the complete ReactJIT feature set: flexbox layout, text measurement and wrapping, image loading, scroll containers, gradients, shadows, transforms, transitions, keyframe animations, and the F12 visual inspector.
sections.api:
esbuild Configuration:
  --format=iife
  --global-name=ReactLove

Build Commands:
  reactjit dev              - Watch mode with HMR
  reactjit build            - Lint gate + production bundle
  reactjit build dist:love  - Self-extracting Linux binary

Runtime Modes:
  native  - QuickJS FFI bridge (default for `love .`)
  web     - Module.FS bridge (for love.js / Emscripten builds)
  canvas  - Module.FS bridge + native rendering pipeline (hybrid web)

Love2D Callbacks (wired in main.lua):
  love.update(dt)       -> ReactLove.update(dt)
  love.draw()           -> ReactLove.draw()
  love.mousepressed()   -> ReactLove.mousepressed(x, y, button)
  love.mousereleased()  -> ReactLove.mousereleased(x, y, button)
  love.mousemoved()     -> ReactLove.mousemoved(x, y)
  love.keypressed()     -> ReactLove.keypressed(key, scancode, isrepeat)
  love.keyreleased()    -> ReactLove.keyreleased(key, scancode)
  love.textinput()      -> ReactLove.textinput(text)
  love.wheelmoved()     -> ReactLove.wheelmoved(x, y)
  love.touchpressed()   -> ReactLove.touchpressed(id, x, y, dx, dy, pressure)
  love.touchreleased()  -> ReactLove.touchreleased(id, x, y, dx, dy, pressure)
  love.touchmoved()     -> ReactLove.touchmoved(id, x, y, dx, dy, pressure)
  love.gamepadpressed() -> ReactLove.gamepadpressed(joystick, button)
  love.gamepadreleased()-> ReactLove.gamepadreleased(joystick, button)
