    datetime     string | undefined  ISO 8601 if time is set
    recurring    boolean
  labels         string[]            Label names
  project_id     string
  section_id     string | undefined
  parent_id      string | null       ID of parent task if nested
  order          number              Sort order within project
  created_at     string              ISO 8601
  url            string              Direct link to the task

--- useTodoistProjects ---

Fetch all projects in the account.

  import { useTodoistProjects } from '@reactjit/apis';

  const result: APIResult<TodoistProject[]> = useTodoistProjects(
    token: string | null,
  );

Parameters:
  token  string | null  Todoist API token

Returns: APIResult<TodoistProject[]>

TodoistProject fields:
  id                string
  name              string
  color             string   Color name (e.g. 'berry_red', 'sky_blue')
  order             number
  is_favorite       boolean
  is_inbox_project  boolean
  comment_count     number
  url               string

--- useTodoistSections ---

Fetch sections within a project.

  import { useTodoistSections } from '@reactjit/apis';

  const result: APIResult<TodoistSection[]> = useTodoistSections(
    token: string | null,
    projectId: string | null,
  );

Parameters:
  token      string | null  Todoist API token
  projectId  string | null  Project ID to fetch sections for

Returns: APIResult<TodoistSection[]>

TodoistSection fields:
  id          string
  name        string
  project_id  string
  order       number

--- useTodoistLabels ---

Fetch all personal labels in the account.

  import { useTodoistLabels } from '@reactjit/apis';

  const result: APIResult<TodoistLabel[]> = useTodoistLabels(
    token: string | null,
  );

Parameters:
  token  string | null  Todoist API token

Returns: APIResult<TodoistLabel[]>

TodoistLabel fields:
  id           string
  name         string
  color        string
  order        number
  is_favorite  boolean

--- useTodoistComments ---

Fetch comments for a task or project.

  import { useTodoistComments } from '@reactjit/apis';

  const result: APIResult<TodoistComment[]> = useTodoistComments(
    token: string | null,
    opts: { taskId?: string; projectId?: string },
  );

Parameters:
  token  string | null   Todoist API token
  opts   object          Exactly one of taskId or projectId must be provided
    taskId     string    Fetch comments for this task
    projectId  string    Fetch comments for this project

Returns: APIResult<TodoistComment[]>

TodoistComment fields:
  id          string
  content     string
  posted_at   string              ISO 8601
  task_id     string | undefined
  project_id  string | undefined

--- useTodoistMutation ---

Get imperative operations for creating and managing tasks.

  import { useTodoistMutation } from '@reactjit/apis';

  const mutation = useTodoistMutation(token: string | null);

Parameters:
  token  string | null  Todoist API token

Returns: object
  createTask    (content: string, opts?) => Promise<any>
                Create a new task. opts fields:
                  projectId   string    Target project ID (defaults to Inbox)
                  priority    number    1–4 where 4 is urgent
                  dueString   string    Natural language due date (e.g. 'tomorrow at 9am')
                  labels      string[]  Label names to apply
  completeTask  (taskId: string) => Promise<any>
                Mark a task complete (close it).
  reopenTask    (taskId: string) => Promise<any>
                Reopen a previously completed task.
  deleteTask    (taskId: string) => Promise<any>
                Permanently delete a task.
  updateTask    (taskId: string, updates: Partial<Pick<TodoistTask, 'content' | 'description' | 'priority' | 'labels'>>) => Promise<any>
                Update task fields. Only provided keys are changed.
  loading       boolean        True while any mutation is in flight
  error         Error | null   Last error, or null
seeAlso: useNotionDatabase — read and write rows in Notion databases
useGoogleCalendarEvents — upcoming calendar events from Google Calendar

metadata.title: Trakt Hooks
metadata.description: Hooks for watch history, watchlists, live now-watching status, trending content, and user statistics from Trakt.tv.

sections.overview:
The Trakt hooks in @reactjit/apis connect to the Trakt.tv REST API. Most endpoints require a Client ID (public) and a user OAuth token (private). The now-watching hook is especially useful for building media dashboards because it reflects the current scrobble state in near real-time.

Credentials: create an application at https://trakt.tv/oauth/applications to get a Client ID. User tokens are obtained through OAuth — store them in your environment or config file.

All hooks return null data until credentials are provided. Passing null for clientId or token suppresses the fetch and is the recommended way to conditionally disable a hook without breaking the component tree.
sections.api:
--- useTraktWatching ---

Fetch what a user is currently watching. Returns null data when nothing is playing. Supports polling.

  import { useTraktWatching } from '@reactjit/apis';

  const result: APIResult<TraktWatching | null> = useTraktWatching(
    clientId: string | null,
    token: string | null,
    username?: string,
    opts?: { interval?: number },
  );

Parameters:
  clientId  string | null   Trakt application Client ID
  token     string | null   OAuth Bearer token for the user
  username  string          Trakt username (default: 'me' — the authenticated user)
  opts      object | undefined
    interval  number        Polling interval in milliseconds (default: 30000)

Returns: APIResult<TraktWatching | null>
  data is null when nothing is currently playing.

TraktWatching fields:
  expires_at   string              ISO 8601 datetime when the scrobble expires
  started_at   string              ISO 8601 datetime when playback started
  action       string              Typically 'scrobble'
  type         'movie' | 'episode'
  movie        TraktMovie | undefined   Present when type is 'movie'
  show         TraktShow | undefined    Present when type is 'episode'
  episode      TraktEpisode | undefined Present when type is 'episode'

--- useTraktHistory ---

Fetch a user's watch history, optionally filtered by media type.

  import { useTraktHistory } from '@reactjit/apis';

  const result: APIResult<TraktHistoryItem[]> = useTraktHistory(
    clientId: string | null,
    token: string | null,
    opts?: { username?: string; type?: 'movies' | 'shows' | 'episodes'; limit?: number },
  );

Parameters:
  clientId  string | null   Trakt Client ID
  token     string | null   OAuth Bearer token
  opts      object | undefined
    username  string        Trakt username (default: 'me')
    type      string        Filter to 'movies', 'shows', or 'episodes'
    limit     number        Max items to return (default: 20)

Returns: APIResult<TraktHistoryItem[]>

TraktHistoryItem fields:
  id          number
  watched_at  string              ISO 8601 datetime
  action      string              'watch' or 'scrobble'
  type        'movie' | 'episode'
  movie       TraktMovie | undefined
  show        TraktShow | undefined
  episode     TraktEpisode | undefined

--- useTraktWatchlist ---

Fetch a user's watchlist, optionally filtered to movies or shows only.

  import { useTraktWatchlist } from '@reactjit/apis';

  const result: APIResult<TraktWatchlistItem[]> = useTraktWatchlist(
    clientId: string | null,
    token: string | null,
    opts?: { username?: string; type?: 'movies' | 'shows' },
  );

Parameters:
  clientId  string | null   Trakt Client ID
  token     string | null   OAuth Bearer token
  opts      object | undefined
    username  string        Trakt username (default: 'me')
    type      string        'movies' or 'shows' — omit for both

Returns: APIResult<TraktWatchlistItem[]>

TraktWatchlistItem fields:
  listed_at  string            ISO 8601 datetime added to list
  type       'movie' | 'show'
  movie      TraktMovie | undefined
  show       TraktShow | undefined

--- useTraktTrending ---

Fetch currently trending movies or shows across all Trakt users. Does not require a user token.

  import { useTraktTrending } from '@reactjit/apis';

  const result: APIResult<Array<{ watchers: number; movie?: TraktMovie; show?: TraktShow }>> = useTraktTrending(
    clientId: string | null,
    type?: 'movies' | 'shows',
    opts?: { limit?: number },
  );

Parameters:
  clientId  string | null   Trakt Client ID
  type      string          'movies' or 'shows' (default: 'movies')
  opts      object | undefined
    limit   number          Max items to return (default: 10)

Returns: APIResult<Array<{ watchers: number; movie?: TraktMovie; show?: TraktShow }>>
  watchers — number of Trakt users watching this item right now

--- useTraktStats ---

Fetch aggregate statistics for a user: total plays, time watched, shows and episodes seen.

  import { useTraktStats } from '@reactjit/apis';

  const result: APIResult<TraktStats> = useTraktStats(
    clientId: string | null,
    token: string | null,
    username?: string,
  );

Parameters:
  clientId  string | null   Trakt Client ID
  token     string | null   OAuth Bearer token
  username  string          Trakt username (default: 'me')

Returns: APIResult<TraktStats>

TraktStats fields:
  movies    { plays: number; watched: number; minutes: number }
  shows     { watched: number; collected: number }
  episodes  { plays: number; watched: number; minutes: number }

--- Shared Types ---

TraktMovie fields:
  title  string
  year   number
  ids    { trakt: number; slug: string; imdb?: string; tmdb?: number }

TraktShow fields:
  title  string
  year   number
  ids    { trakt: number; slug: string; imdb?: string; tmdb?: number; tvdb?: number }

TraktEpisode fields:
  season  number
  number  number
  title   string
  ids     { trakt: number; tvdb?: number; imdb?: string; tmdb?: number }
seeAlso: useTMDB — movie and show metadata, posters, and ratings

metadata.title: TypeScript Types Reference
metadata.description: Complete TypeScript type definitions for all ReactJIT interfaces

sections.overview: This reference lists all TypeScript types exported from @reactjit/core (packages/shared). Types are organized by category: core types, primitive component props, higher-level component props, animation types, and utility types. All types are importable from '@reactjit/core'.
sections.api:
--- CORE TYPES ---

Color = string | [number, number, number, number?]
  CSS string ("#ff0000", "rgba(255,0,0,0.5)") or Love2D array [r, g, b, a] (0-1 range).
  The alpha channel is optional and defaults to 1.

Style
  Full style interface. See style-properties.txt for every property.
  All properties are optional.

RendererMode = 'web' | 'native'
  Which rendering path the primitives use.
  'web': DOM elements (div/span/img).
  'native': Host elements for react-reconciler (View/Text/Image).

--- EVENT TYPE ---

LoveEvent
  type: string
  targetId?: number
  x?: number
  y?: number
  button?: number
  key?: string
  scancode?: string
  isRepeat?: boolean
  text?: string
  deltaX?: number
  deltaY?: number
  touchId?: number | string
  dx?: number
  dy?: number
  pressure?: number
  gamepadButton?: string
  axis?: string
  axisValue?: number
  joystickId?: number
  startX?: number
  startY?: number
  totalDeltaX?: number
  totalDeltaY?: number
  bubblePath?: number[]
  currentTarget?: number
  stopPropagation?: () => void

--- PRIMITIVE COMPONENT PROPS ---

BoxProps
  style?: Style
  hoverStyle?: Style                          -- Lua-side hover overlay (0-frame latency)
  activeStyle?: Style                         -- Lua-side active/pressed overlay
  onClick?: (event: LoveEvent) => void
  onRelease?: (event: LoveEvent) => void
  onPointerEnter?: (event: LoveEvent) => void
  onPointerLeave?: (event: LoveEvent) => void
  onKeyDown?: (event: LoveEvent) => void
  onKeyUp?: (event: LoveEvent) => void
  onTextInput?: (event: LoveEvent) => void
  onWheel?: (event: LoveEvent) => void
  onTouchStart?: (event: LoveEvent) => void
  onTouchEnd?: (event: LoveEvent) => void
  onTouchMove?: (event: LoveEvent) => void
  onGamepadPress?: (event: LoveEvent) => void
  onGamepadRelease?: (event: LoveEvent) => void
  onGamepadAxis?: (event: LoveEvent) => void
  onDragStart?: (event: LoveEvent) => void
  onDrag?: (event: LoveEvent) => void
  onDragEnd?: (event: LoveEvent) => void
  children?: React.ReactNode
  key?: string | number

TextProps
  style?: Style
  numberOfLines?: number                      -- Max visible lines (truncation)
  onKeyDown?: (event: LoveEvent) => void
  onKeyUp?: (event: LoveEvent) => void
  onTextInput?: (event: LoveEvent) => void
  children?: React.ReactNode
  key?: string | number

ImageProps
  src: string                                 -- Image file path (required)
  style?: Style
  onClick?: (event: LoveEvent) => void
  onWheel?: (event: LoveEvent) => void
  key?: string | number

--- SCROLL ---

ScrollEvent
  scrollX: number
  scrollY: number
  contentWidth: number
  contentHeight: number

ScrollViewProps
  style?: Style
  horizontal?: boolean
  showScrollIndicator?: boolean
  onScroll?: (event: ScrollEvent) => void
  onScrollBegin?: () => void
  onScrollEnd?: () => void
  children?: React.ReactNode
  key?: string | number

ScrollViewRef
  scrollTo(options: { x?: number; y?: number; animated?: boolean }): void

--- INPUT ---

TextInputProps
  value?: string
  defaultValue?: string
  onChangeText?: (text: string) => void
  onSubmit?: (text: string) => void
  onFocus?: () => void
  onBlur?: () => void
  placeholder?: string
  placeholderColor?: Color
  maxLength?: number
  multiline?: boolean
  editable?: boolean
  secureTextEntry?: boolean
  style?: Style
  textStyle?: Style
  autoFocus?: boolean
  cursorColor?: Color
  key?: string | number

TextEditorProps
  initialValue?: string
  value?: string
  onChangeText?: (text: string) => void
  onSubmit?: (text: string) => void
  onFocus?: () => void
  onBlur?: (text: string) => void
  placeholder?: string
  readOnly?: boolean
  lineNumbers?: boolean                       -- Show line numbers (default: true)
  style?: Style
  textStyle?: Style
  key?: string | number

--- LISTS ---

FlatListProps<T>
  data: T[]
  renderItem: (info: { item: T; index: number }) => React.ReactNode
  keyExtractor?: (item: T, index: number) => string
  horizontal?: boolean
  itemHeight?: number                         -- Required for virtualization
  itemWidth?: number
  estimatedItemSize?: number
  onEndReached?: () => void
  onEndReachedThreshold?: number
  onScroll?: (event: ScrollEvent) => void
  ListHeaderComponent?: React.ReactNode
  ListFooterComponent?: React.ReactNode
  ListEmptyComponent?: React.ReactNode
  ItemSeparatorComponent?: React.ReactNode
  style?: Style
  contentContainerStyle?: Style
  initialNumToRender?: number
  windowSize?: number
  maxToRenderPerBatch?: number
  inverted?: boolean
  numColumns?: number
  key?: string | number

FlatListRef
  scrollToIndex(params: { index: number; animated?: boolean }): void
  scrollToOffset(params: { offset: number; animated?: boolean }): void

--- INTERACTION ---

PressableState
  pressed: boolean
  hovered: boolean
  focused: boolean

HitSlop
  top?: number
  bottom?: number
  left?: number
  right?: number

PressableProps
  onPress?: (event: LoveEvent) => void
  onLongPress?: (event: LoveEvent) => void
  onPressIn?: (event: LoveEvent) => void
  onPressOut?: (event: LoveEvent) => void
  onHoverIn?: (event: LoveEvent) => void
  onHoverOut?: (event: LoveEvent) => void
  disabled?: boolean
  delayLongPress?: number                     -- Milliseconds (default: 500)
  style?: Style | ((state: PressableState) => Style)
  children: React.ReactNode | ((state: PressableState) => React.ReactNode)
  hitSlop?: number | HitSlop
  accessibilityRole?: string

ModalProps
  visible: boolean
  onRequestClose?: () => void
  onShow?: () => void
  animationType?: 'none' | 'fade' | 'slide'
  transparent?: boolean
  backdropColor?: Color
  backdropDismiss?: boolean
  children: React.ReactNode

SliderProps
  value?: number
  defaultValue?: number
  onValueChange?: (value: number) => void
  onSlidingStart?: () => void
  onSlidingEnd?: (value: number) => void
  minimumValue?: number
  maximumValue?: number
  step?: number
  disabled?: boolean
  style?: Style
  trackColor?: Color
  activeTrackColor?: Color
  thumbColor?: Color
  thumbSize?: number

SwitchProps
  value?: boolean
  defaultValue?: boolean
  onValueChange?: (value: boolean) => void
  disabled?: boolean
  style?: Style
  trackColor?: { true: Color; false: Color }
  thumbColor?: Color
  width?: number
  height?: number

--- FORM PRIMITIVES ---

CheckboxProps
  value?: boolean
  defaultValue?: boolean
  onValueChange?: (value: boolean) => void
  disabled?: boolean
  label?: string
  size?: number
  color?: Color
  uncheckedColor?: Color
  style?: Style

RadioGroupProps
  value?: string
  defaultValue?: string
  onValueChange?: (value: string) => void
  disabled?: boolean
  children: React.ReactNode

RadioProps
  value: string
  label?: string
  size?: number
  color?: Color
  style?: Style

SelectOption
  label: string
  value: string

SelectProps
  value?: string
  defaultValue?: string
  onValueChange?: (value: string) => void
  options: SelectOption[]
  placeholder?: string
  disabled?: boolean
  style?: Style
  color?: Color

--- DATA VISUALIZATION ---

TableColumn<T>
  key: string
  title: string
  width?: number | string
  align?: 'left' | 'center' | 'right'
  render?: (value: any, row: T, index: number) => React.ReactNode

TableProps<T>
  columns: TableColumn<T>[]
  data: T[]
  rowKey?: string | ((row: T, index: number) => string)
  striped?: boolean
  borderless?: boolean
  headerStyle?: Style
  rowStyle?: Style
  cellStyle?: Style
  style?: Style

BarChartBar
  label: string
  value: number
  color?: Color

BarChartProps
  data: BarChartBar[]
  height?: number                             -- Default: 120
  barWidth?: number                           -- Default: 20
  gap?: number                                -- Default: 8
  showLabels?: boolean                        -- Default: true
  showValues?: boolean                        -- Default: false
  color?: Color                               -- Default: '#3b82f6'
  style?: Style

ProgressBarProps
  value: number                               -- 0-1 range
  color?: Color                               -- Default: '#3b82f6'
  trackColor?: Color                          -- Default: '#1e293b'
  height?: number                             -- Default: 8
  showLabel?: boolean                         -- Default: false
  label?: string
  animated?: boolean                          -- Default: false
  style?: Style

SparklineProps
  data: number[]
  width?: number                              -- Default: 80
  height?: number                             -- Default: 24
  color?: Color                               -- Default: '#3b82f6'
  style?: Style

--- NAVIGATION ---

BreadcrumbItem
  id: string
  label: string

BreadcrumbsProps
  items: BreadcrumbItem[]
  onSelect?: (id: string) => void
  separator?: string                          -- Default: '/'
  style?: Style

NavItem
  id: string
  label: string

NavSection
  title: string
  items: NavItem[]

NavPanelProps
  sections: NavSection[]
  activeId?: string
  onSelect?: (id: string) => void
  header?: React.ReactNode
  width?: number                              -- Default: 180
  style?: Style

Tab
  id: string
  label: string

TabsProps
  tabs: Tab[]
  activeId: string
  onSelect?: (id: string) => void
  variant?: 'underline' | 'pill'              -- Default: 'underline'
  style?: Style

ToolbarEntry
  = { type: 'item'; id: string; label: string; disabled?: boolean }
  | { type: 'divider' }

ToolbarProps
  items: ToolbarEntry[]
  onSelect?: (id: string) => void
  style?: Style

--- LAYOUT HELPERS ---

BadgeVariant = 'default' | 'success' | 'warning' | 'error' | 'info'

BadgeProps
  label: string
  variant?: BadgeVariant                      -- Default: 'default'
  style?: Style

CardProps
  title?: string
  subtitle?: string
  children: React.ReactNode
  style?: Style
  headerStyle?: Style
  bodyStyle?: Style

FlexRowProps
  gap?: number
  justify?: Style['justifyContent']
  align?: Style['alignItems']
  wrap?: boolean
  style?: Style
  children: React.ReactNode

FlexColumnProps
  gap?: number
  justify?: Style['justifyContent']
  align?: Style['alignItems']
  style?: Style
  children: React.ReactNode

SpacerProps
  size?: number
  (When size is provided, renders a fixed-height Box.
   When omitted, renders a Box with flexGrow: 1.)

DividerProps
  direction?: 'horizontal' | 'vertical'      -- Default: 'horizontal'
  color?: string                              -- Default: '#334155'
  thickness?: number                          -- Default: 1
  style?: Style

--- ANIMATION TYPES ---

Animation
  start(callback?: (result: { finished: boolean }) => void): void
  stop(): void

EasingFunction = (t: number) => number

TimingConfig
  toValue: number
  duration?: number                           -- Milliseconds (default: 300)
  easing?: EasingFunction                     -- Default: Easing.easeInOut
  delay?: number                              -- Milliseconds (default: 0)

SpringConfig
  toValue: number
  stiffness?: number                          -- Default: 100
  damping?: number                            -- Default: 10
  mass?: number                               -- Default: 1
  velocity?: number                           -- Default: 0
  restThreshold?: number                      -- Default: 0.001

InterpolationConfig
  inputRange: number[]
  outputRange: number[] | string[]
  extrapolate?: 'clamp' | 'extend'            -- Default: 'extend'

--- BRIDGE TYPES ---

IBridge
  send(type: string, payload?: any): void
  flush(): void
  subscribe(type: string, fn: Listener): Unsubscribe
  rpc<T>(method: string, args?: any, timeoutMs?: number): Promise<T>
  setState(key: string, value: any): void
  isReady(): boolean
  onReady(callback: () => void): void
  destroy(): void

BridgeEvent
  type: string
  payload: any

Listener = (payload: any) => void
Unsubscribe = () => void

--- OVERLAY TYPE ---

Overlay
  id: string
  nx: number                                  -- Normalized X (0-1)
  ny: number                                  -- Normalized Y (0-1)
  px: number                                  -- Pixel X
  py: number                                  -- Pixel Y
  domX?: number                               -- Computed DOM X (from canvas rect)
  domY?: number                               -- Computed DOM Y (from canvas rect)
  [key: string]: any                          -- Additional custom data

metadata.title: Weather API Hooks
metadata.description: Hooks for the OpenWeatherMap API — current conditions, 5-day forecast, and geocoding.

sections.overview:
The weather hooks wrap the OpenWeatherMap API (data/2.5 endpoint). Authentication uses an API key passed as a query parameter — get one at https://openweathermap.org/api. Pass the key as the first argument to every hook.

Location can be specified either as a city name string (e.g. "London,UK") or as latitude/longitude coordinates. If neither is provided the request is skipped.

The package also exports a weatherIcon() utility for building OpenWeatherMap icon image URLs from the icon code returned in API responses.
sections.api:
--- useWeatherCurrent ---

Fetch current weather conditions for a city or coordinate pair.

  import { useWeatherCurrent } from '@reactjit/apis/weather';

  const result: APIResult<WeatherCurrent> = useWeatherCurrent(apiKey, opts);

Parameters:
  apiKey   string | null
  opts     object (required — at least one of city or lat/lon must be provided)
    city    string                          City name, optionally with country code: "Paris,FR"
    lat     number                          Latitude (use with lon)
    lon     number                          Longitude (use with lat)
    units   'metric' | 'imperial'           Default: 'metric'

Returns: APIResult<WeatherCurrent>

WeatherCurrent fields:
  name       string   City name as returned by the API
  coord      { lat: number; lon: number }
  weather    WeatherCondition[]
  main       WeatherMain
  wind       { speed: number; deg: number; gust?: number }
  clouds     { all: number }   Cloud coverage percent
  visibility number   Visibility in metres
  sys        { sunrise: number; sunset: number; country: string }   Unix timestamps
  dt         number   Unix timestamp of data calculation
  timezone   number   Timezone offset in seconds from UTC

WeatherCondition fields:
  id           number   Condition code
  main         string   Group: 'Rain', 'Snow', 'Clear', etc.
  description  string   Human-readable description
  icon         string   Icon code — use with weatherIcon()

WeatherMain fields:
  temp       number   Temperature in the requested units
  feels_like number
  temp_min   number
  temp_max   number
  humidity   number   Percent
  pressure   number   hPa

--- useWeatherForecast ---

Fetch a 5-day / 3-hour forecast for a city or coordinate pair.

  import { useWeatherForecast } from '@reactjit/apis/weather';

  const result: APIResult<WeatherForecast> = useWeatherForecast(apiKey, opts);

Parameters:
  apiKey   string | null
  opts     object (same shape as useWeatherCurrent)
    city    string
    lat     number
    lon     number
    units   'metric' | 'imperial'   Default: 'metric'

Returns: APIResult<WeatherForecast>

WeatherForecast fields:
  list   WeatherForecastItem[]   Up to 40 entries (5 days x 8 per day)
  city   { name: string; country: string; coord: { lat: number; lon: number } }

WeatherForecastItem fields:
  dt      number             Unix timestamp
  main    WeatherMain        Same as WeatherCurrent.main
  weather WeatherCondition[]
  wind    WeatherCurrent['wind']
  clouds  { all: number }
  pop     number             Probability of precipitation (0–1)
  dt_txt  string             Human-readable datetime string

--- useGeocode ---

Resolve a city name or address query to latitude/longitude coordinates.

  import { useGeocode } from '@reactjit/apis/weather';

  const result: APIResult<GeoLocation[]> = useGeocode(apiKey, query, opts?);

Parameters:
  apiKey   string | null
  query    string | null   City name or address. Pass null to skip.
  opts     object (optional)
    limit  number   Maximum results to return. Default: 5.

Returns: APIResult<GeoLocation[]>

GeoLocation fields:
  name     string
  lat      number
  lon      number
  country  string   Two-letter country code
  state    string (optional)

--- weatherIcon ---

Build an OpenWeatherMap icon URL from the icon code returned in any WeatherCondition.

  import { weatherIcon } from '@reactjit/apis/weather';

  const url: string = weatherIcon(icon, size?);

Parameters:
  icon   string   Icon code from WeatherCondition.icon (e.g. '10d')
  size   '1' | '2' | '4'   Scale multiplier. Default: '2' (50x50 px at 2x).

Returns: string — full https URL to the PNG icon image.
seeAlso: home-assistant — fetch sensor data including weather stations from a local Home Assistant instance

metadata.title: YNAB Hooks
metadata.description: Hooks for reading budget data, accounts, categories, transactions, and monthly summaries from You Need A Budget (YNAB).

sections.overview:
The YNAB hooks in @reactjit/apis connect to the YNAB REST API v1. Authentication requires a Personal Access Token generated at https://app.ynab.com/settings/developer.

All monetary values returned by the YNAB API are in milliunits (e.g., $12.34 is returned as 12340). Use the ynabAmount() utility to convert to dollars before displaying. Negative values represent outflows (spending); positive values represent inflows (income).

Most hooks accept an optional budgetId. When omitted or passed as null, they default to 'last-used', which resolves to the budget most recently opened in the YNAB app.
sections.api:
--- useYNABBudgets ---

Fetch all budgets accessible with the token.

  import { useYNABBudgets } from '@reactjit/apis';

  const result: APIResult<{ data: { budgets: YNABBudget[] } }> = useYNABBudgets(
    token: string | null,
  );

Parameters:
  token  string | null  YNAB Personal Access Token

Returns: APIResult<{ data: { budgets: YNABBudget[] } }>

YNABBudget fields:
  id               string
  name             string
  last_modified_on string   ISO 8601 datetime of last change
  first_month      string   Earliest month in the budget (YYYY-MM-DD)
  last_month       string   Latest month in the budget (YYYY-MM-DD)
  currency_format  object
    iso_code           string   e.g. 'USD'
    decimal_digits     number
    decimal_separator  string

--- useYNABAccounts ---

Fetch all accounts in a budget.

  import { useYNABAccounts } from '@reactjit/apis';

  const result: APIResult<{ data: { accounts: YNABAccount[] } }> = useYNABAccounts(
    token: string | null,
    budgetId: string | null,
  );

Parameters:
  token     string | null  YNAB Personal Access Token
  budgetId  string | null  Budget ID, or null to use last-used

Returns: APIResult<{ data: { accounts: YNABAccount[] } }>

YNABAccount fields:
  id                  string
  name                string
  type                string    'checking', 'savings', 'creditCard', 'cash', etc.
  on_budget           boolean   Whether the account is on-budget
  closed              boolean
  balance             number    Current balance in milliunits
  cleared_balance     number    Cleared balance in milliunits
  uncleared_balance   number    Uncleared balance in milliunits
  transfer_payee_id   string    Payee ID for transfers to this account

--- useYNABCategories ---

Fetch all category groups and their categories for a budget month.

  import { useYNABCategories } from '@reactjit/apis';

  const result: APIResult<{ data: { category_groups: YNABCategoryGroup[] } }> = useYNABCategories(
    token: string | null,
    budgetId?: string | null,
  );

Parameters:
  token     string | null   YNAB Personal Access Token
  budgetId  string | null   Budget ID, or null/omitted to use last-used

Returns: APIResult<{ data: { category_groups: YNABCategoryGroup[] } }>

YNABCategoryGroup fields:
  id          string
  name        string
  hidden      boolean
  categories  YNABCategory[]

YNABCategory fields:
  id                        string
  name                      string
  category_group_id         string
  budgeted                  number   Amount budgeted this month in milliunits
  activity                  number   Amount spent this month in milliunits (negative = spending)
  balance                   number   Remaining balance in milliunits
  goal_type                 string | null   'TB', 'TBD', 'MF', 'NEED', or null
  goal_percentage_complete  number | null   0–100 when a goal exists

--- useYNABTransactions ---

Fetch transactions with optional date and type filtering.

  import { useYNABTransactions } from '@reactjit/apis';

  const result: APIResult<{ data: { transactions: YNABTransaction[] } }> = useYNABTransactions(
    token: string | null,
    opts?: { budgetId?: string; sinceDate?: string; type?: 'uncategorized' | 'unapproved' },
  );

Parameters:
  token  string | null   YNAB Personal Access Token
  opts   object | undefined
    budgetId   string    Budget ID (default: last-used)
    sinceDate  string    Only return transactions on or after this date (YYYY-MM-DD)
    type       string    'uncategorized' or 'unapproved' to filter

Returns: APIResult<{ data: { transactions: YNABTransaction[] } }>

YNABTransaction fields:
  id               string
  date             string              YYYY-MM-DD
  amount           number              Transaction amount in milliunits (negative = outflow)
  memo             string | null
  cleared          'cleared' | 'uncleared' | 'reconciled'
  approved         boolean
  payee_name       string | null
  category_name    string | null
  account_name     string
  subtransactions  Array<{ amount: number; memo: string | null; category_name: string | null }>

--- useYNABMonths ---

Fetch monthly summary data for a budget: income, budgeted, spending, and age of money.

  import { useYNABMonths } from '@reactjit/apis';

  const result: APIResult<{ data: { months: YNABMonth[] } }> = useYNABMonths(
    token: string | null,
    budgetId?: string | null,
  );

Parameters:
  token     string | null   YNAB Personal Access Token
  budgetId  string | null   Budget ID (default: last-used)

Returns: APIResult<{ data: { months: YNABMonth[] } }>

YNABMonth fields:
  month             string   YYYY-MM-DD (first day of the month)
  income            number   Total income in milliunits
  budgeted          number   Total budgeted in milliunits
  activity          number   Total spending in milliunits (negative)
  to_be_budgeted    number   Unallocated funds in milliunits
  age_of_money      number | null   Average age of money in days

--- ynabAmount (utility) ---

Convert YNAB milliunits to a dollar (or local currency) amount. Not a hook — call it inline.

  import { ynabAmount } from '@reactjit/apis';

  const dollars: number = ynabAmount(milliunits: number);

Parameters:
  milliunits  number  YNAB milliunit value (e.g. 12340)

Returns: number — human-readable amount (e.g. 12.34)
seeAlso: useGoogleSheet — read financial data from Google Sheets
useNotionDatabase — structured data and notes in Notion

metadata.title: Storage System
metadata.description: Cross-platform persistent storage with schema validation, CRUD operations, and reactive queries

sections.overview: The storage system provides persistent data storage across all ReactJIT targets with a unified API. It combines schema validation, CRUD operations, reactive queries, and migrations. Multiple adapters handle platform differences: Love2DFileAdapter uses love.filesystem, LocalStorageAdapter and IndexedDBAdapter work in browsers, TerminalSQLiteAdapter uses Node.js SQLite, and MemoryAdapter provides in-memory storage for testing.
sections.api:
Core Components:
- `StorageProvider` — React context provider for the default adapter
- `useCRUD(collection, schema, options?)` — React hook for CRUD + reactive queries
- `createCRUD(collection, schema, adapter, options?)` — Non-React CRUD for scripts/backends
- `useStorage()` — Direct access to the underlying storage adapter
- `z` — Schema builder for validation (exported from @reactjit/storage)

Adapters:
- `MemoryAdapter()` — In-memory storage (lost on close)
- `Love2DFileAdapter({ rpc, format? })` — Love2D filesystem (JSON/Markdown/Text)
- `LocalStorageAdapter({ prefix? })` — Browser localStorage (< 5MB)
- `IndexedDBAdapter({ dbName?, version? })` — Browser IndexedDB (larger datasets)
- `TerminalSQLiteAdapter({ dbPath? })` — Node.js SQLite (default: ./data.db)

Schema Types (z.*):
- `z.string()`, `z.number()`, `z.boolean()`, `z.date()`
- `z.object({ ... })`, `z.array(schema)`
- `.optional()`, `.nullable()`, `.default(value)`

CRUD Methods (from useCRUD):
- `create(data)` → Promise<string> (returns new ID)
- `get(id)` → Promise<T | null>
- `update(id, partial)` → Promise<void>
- `delete(id)` → Promise<void>
- `list(query?)` → Promise<T[]>

Reactive Hooks (from useCRUD):
- `useQuery(id)` → { data, loading, error, refetch }
- `useListQuery(query?)` → { data, loading, error, refetch }

Query Object:
{
  where?: { field: value, field2: { $gt: 10 } },
  orderBy?: 'fieldName',
  order?: 'asc' | 'desc',
  limit?: number,
  offset?: number
}

Query Operators:
$eq, $ne, $gt, $gte, $lt, $lte, $in, $contains

examples.title: Basic CRUD with MemoryAdapter
Love2D File Storage
Web with IndexedDB
Query Filtering
Schema Validation
Non-React CRUD (Scripts/Backends)
Migrations

criticalRules: Every collection must have a schema — schema.parse() is called on all reads/writes
Schema must include an `id: z.string()` field — CRUD methods expect it
Use StorageProvider at app root OR pass { adapter } to useCRUD options
Query operators ($gt, $gte, etc.) work in-memory for all adapters except TerminalSQLiteAdapter (which uses SQL WHERE)
Reactive hooks (useQuery, useListQuery) do NOT auto-refetch on mutations — call refetch() manually
Love2DFileAdapter requires an RPC caller from useLoveRPC() — it calls Lua-side storage:get/set/list/delete handlers
IndexedDBAdapter is async — all operations return Promises
LocalStorageAdapter stores everything as JSON strings — do not store large objects
TerminalSQLiteAdapter requires Node.js v22+ for node:sqlite — does not work in browsers
Migrations run on every read — keep migration functions fast and idempotent
autoMigrate: true runs migrations automatically; autoMigrate: false requires manual migration trigger
createCRUD is for scripts/backends without React — do not use it in components
Do not mutate data returned from get() or list() — it may be cached
seeAlso: State Management — useSignal, useSharedState for ephemeral state
Hooks — useEffect for side effects, useMemo for computed values
Event Handling — onPress, onChange for user interactions

metadata.title: Storage API Reference
metadata.description: Detailed API documentation for storage hooks, adapters, schema validation, queries, and migrations

sections.overview: Comprehensive reference for the storage system's API surface: StorageProvider, useCRUD, createCRUD, useStorage, schema validation with z, query syntax, and migration configuration. All storage operations are async and return Promises. Schema validation is automatic on all reads and writes.
sections.api:
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
StorageProvider
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

<StorageProvider adapter={adapter}>
  {children}
</StorageProvider>

Props:
  adapter: StorageAdapter — Any adapter instance
  children: ReactNode

Purpose:
  Provides a default storage adapter to all child components.
  Components can access it via useStorage() or useCRUD() without
  passing { adapter } in options.

Example:
  const adapter = new MemoryAdapter();
  <StorageProvider adapter={adapter}>
    <App />
  </StorageProvider>

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
useStorage()
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

const adapter = useStorage();

Returns: StorageAdapter

Purpose:
  Returns the storage adapter from StorageProvider context.
  Throws if used outside StorageProvider.

Methods on StorageAdapter:
  get(collection, id) → Promise<any | null>
  set(collection, id, data) → Promise<void>
  delete(collection, id) → Promise<boolean>
  list(collection, query?) → Promise<any[]>
  format?: 'json' | 'markdown' | 'text'

Example:
  const adapter = useStorage();
  const raw = await adapter.get('users', 'u1');
  await adapter.set('users', 'u2', { name: 'Bob' });

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
useCRUD(collection, schema, options?)
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

const crud = useCRUD<T>(collection, schema, options?);

Parameters:
  collection: string — Collection name (e.g. 'users', 'notes')
  schema: Schema<T> — Validation schema (z.object(...))
  options?: CRUDOptions
    adapter?: StorageAdapter — Override default from context
    format?: 'json' | 'markdown' | 'text'
    migrations?: Record<number, MigrationFn>
    autoMigrate?: boolean

Returns: CRUDHandle<T> with methods:
  create(data: T) → Promise<string>
  get(id: string) → Promise<T | null>
  update(id: string, partial: Partial<T>) → Promise<void>
  delete(id: string) → Promise<void>
  list(query?: Query) → Promise<T[]>
  useQuery(id: string) → { data, loading, error, refetch }
  useListQuery(query?: Query) → { data, loading, error, refetch }

CRUD Method Details:

create(data: T) → Promise<string>
  - Validates data against schema
  - Generates ID if not provided (format: timestamp-random-seq)
  - Saves to storage
  - Returns the ID

get(id: string) → Promise<T | null>
  - Fetches record by ID
  - Validates against schema
  - Returns null if not found

update(id: string, partial: Partial<T>) → Promise<void>
  - Fetches existing record
  - Merges with partial data
  - Validates merged result
  - Saves back to storage
  - Throws if record not found

delete(id: string) → Promise<void>
  - Removes record from storage
  - No-op if record doesn't exist

list(query?: Query) → Promise<T[]>
  - Fetches all records in collection
  - Applies query filters (where, orderBy, limit, offset)
  - Validates each record against schema
  - Returns array (empty if no matches)

useQuery(id: string)
  - Reactive hook for single record
  - Returns { data, loading, error, refetch }
  - data: T | null
  - loading: boolean
  - error: Error | null
  - refetch: () => void
  - Automatically re-fetches when id changes
  - Does NOT auto-refetch on mutations — call refetch()

useListQuery(query?: Query)
  - Reactive hook for collection query
  - Returns { data, loading, error, refetch }
  - data: T[]
  - loading: boolean
  - error: Error | null
  - refetch: () => void
  - Does NOT auto-refetch on mutations — call refetch()

Example:
  const notes = useCRUD('notes', NoteSchema);

  // Create
  const id = await notes.create({ title: 'Hello', body: 'World' });

  // Read
  const note = await notes.get(id);

  // Update
  await notes.update(id, { title: 'Updated' });

  // Delete
  await notes.delete(id);

  // List
  const all = await notes.list();
  const filtered = await notes.list({ where: { archived: false } });

  // Reactive
  const { data, loading, refetch } = notes.useQuery(id);
  const { data: list } = notes.useListQuery();

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
createCRUD(collection, schema, adapter, options?)
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

const crud = createCRUD<T>(collection, schema, adapter, options?);

Parameters:
  collection: string
  schema: Schema<T>
  adapter: StorageAdapter — Required (no context)
  options?: CreateCRUDOptions
    migrations?: Record<number, MigrationFn>
    autoMigrate?: boolean

Returns: CRUDMethods<T> (same as useCRUD but without reactive hooks)
  create(data: T) → Promise<string>
  get(id: string) → Promise<T | null>
  update(id: string, partial: Partial<T>) → Promise<void>
  delete(id: string) → Promise<void>
  list(query?: Query) → Promise<T[]>

Purpose:
  Non-React CRUD interface for scripts, CLI tools, and backends.
  Same validation and migration support as useCRUD.
  No hooks, no context — pass adapter explicitly.

Example:
  import { createCRUD, z, MemoryAdapter } from '@reactjit/storage';

  const adapter = new MemoryAdapter();
  const users = createCRUD('users', UserSchema, adapter);

  await users.create({ id: 'u1', name: 'Alice' });
  const all = await users.list();

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Schema Validation (z)
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

import { z } from '@reactjit/storage';

Primitive Types:
  z.string()
  z.number()
  z.boolean()
  z.date()

Complex Types:
  z.object({ field1: z.string(), field2: z.number() })
  z.array(z.string())

Modifiers:
  .optional() — allows undefined
  .nullable() — allows null
  .default(value) — uses value if undefined (implies optional)

Schema Methods:
  parse(value) → T — throws ValidationError if invalid
  safeParse(value) → SafeParseResult<T>
    Success: { success: true, data: T }
    Failure: { success: false, error: ValidationError }

ValidationError Properties:
  issues: ValidationIssue[]
  message: string (formatted from issues)

ValidationIssue:
  path: string[] — field path (e.g. ['user', 'email'])
  message: string — error description
  code: string — error code (e.g. 'invalid_type')

Example:
  const UserSchema = z.object({
    id: z.string(),
    name: z.string(),
    age: z.number().optional(),
    bio: z.string().nullable(),
    role: z.string().default('user'),
    tags: z.array(z.string()),
  });

  // Parse (throws on error)
  const user = UserSchema.parse(rawData);

  // Safe parse (returns result object)
  const result = UserSchema.safeParse(rawData);
  if (result.success) {
    console.log(result.data);
  } else {
    console.error(result.error.issues);
  }

Type Inference:
  type User = z.infer<typeof UserSchema>;
  // Infers: { id: string; name: string; age?: number; bio: string | null; role: string; tags: string[]; }

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Query Syntax
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

interface Query {
  where?: WhereClause;
  orderBy?: string;
  order?: 'asc' | 'desc';
  limit?: number;
  offset?: number;
}

type WhereClause = Record<string, any | Partial<Record<ComparisonOp, any>>>;
type ComparisonOp = '$eq' | '$ne' | '$gt' | '$gte' | '$lt' | '$lte' | '$in' | '$contains';

Query Operators:

Equality:
  { field: value } — exact match (shorthand for $eq)
  { field: { $eq: value } } — exact match (explicit)
  { field: { $ne: value } } — not equal

Comparison:
  { field: { $gt: value } } — greater than
  { field: { $gte: value } } — greater than or equal
  { field: { $lt: value } } — less than
  { field: { $lte: value } } — less than or equal

Array/String:
  { field: { $in: [val1, val2] } } — value in array
  { field: { $contains: substring } } — string contains substring

Multiple Conditions:
  { field1: value1, field2: { $gt: 10 } } — AND logic
  All conditions must match

Sorting:
  orderBy: 'fieldName'
  order: 'asc' or 'desc' (default: 'asc')

Pagination:
  limit: number — max records to return
  offset: number — skip first N records

Examples:

// Exact match
await crud.list({ where: { status: 'active' } });

// Greater than
await crud.list({ where: { age: { $gt: 18 } } });

// Range
await crud.list({ where: { price: { $gte: 10, $lte: 100 } } });

// In array
await crud.list({ where: { category: { $in: ['electronics', 'books'] } } });

// Contains substring
await crud.list({ where: { title: { $contains: 'react' } } });

// Multiple conditions
await crud.list({
  where: { status: 'published', views: { $gt: 1000 } }
});

// Sort and paginate
await crud.list({
  where: { archived: false },
  orderBy: 'createdAt',
  order: 'desc',
  limit: 20,
  offset: 40,
});

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Migrations
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

type MigrationFn = (data: any) => any;

interface MigrationConfig {
  migrations?: Record<number, MigrationFn>;
  autoMigrate?: boolean;
}

Pass to useCRUD or createCRUD options:
  migrations: Record<number, MigrationFn>
    Keys: version numbers (1, 2, 3, ...)
    Values: functions that transform data

  autoMigrate: boolean
    true — migrations run automatically on every read
    false — migrations must be triggered manually

Migration Execution:
  - Runs on get() and list() operations
  - Applies migrations in version order (1 → 2 → 3)
  - Stores __version field in data to track migration state
  - Each migration receives output of previous migration
  - Migrations are not saved back to storage unless record is updated

Best Practices:
  - Keep migration functions pure and idempotent
  - Add default values for new fields
  - Rename/transform fields as needed
  - Avoid async operations in migration functions
  - Test migrations with real data before deploying

Example:
  const UserSchema = z.object({
    id: z.string(),
    fullName: z.string(),
    email: z.string(),
    preferences: z.object({
      theme: z.string(),
      notifications: z.boolean(),
    }),
  });

  const users = useCRUD('users', UserSchema, {
    migrations: {
      // v1: Add email field
      1: (data) => ({
        ...data,
        email: data.email || `${data.username}@example.com`,
      }),

      // v2: Rename 'name' to 'fullName'
      2: (data) => {
        const { name, ...rest } = data;
        return { ...rest, fullName: name || 'Unknown' };
      },

      // v3: Add preferences object
      3: (data) => ({
        ...data,
        preferences: data.preferences || {
          theme: 'dark',
          notifications: true,
        },
      }),
    },
    autoMigrate: true,
  });

  // Old records are migrated on read
  const user = await users.get('old-user-id');
  // user.__version === 3

Manual Migration Trigger:
  // autoMigrate: false
  const adapter = new MemoryAdapter();
  const migrating = createMigratingAdapter(adapter, migrations, false);
  await migrating.migrate('users'); // Migrates all records in collection

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Adapters
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

All adapters implement the StorageAdapter interface:
  get(collection, id) → Promise<any | null>
  set(collection, id, data) → Promise<void>
  delete(collection, id) → Promise<boolean>
  list(collection, query?) → Promise<any[]>
  format?: 'json' | 'markdown' | 'text'

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
MemoryAdapter
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

new MemoryAdapter()

No options. Data stored in Map<string, Map<string, any>>.
Data lost when app closes.

Use for:
  - Testing
  - Prototyping
  - Ephemeral state
  - Targets without persistent storage

Example:
  const adapter = new MemoryAdapter();

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Love2DFileAdapter
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

new Love2DFileAdapter({ rpc, format? })

Options:
  rpc: (method, args?) => Promise<T> — RPC caller (from useLoveRPC)
  format?: 'json' | 'markdown' | 'text' — Default: 'json'

Storage Location:
  save/<collection>/<id>.<ext>
  (in Love2D's save directory)

RPC Methods Called:
  storage:get — read file
  storage:set — write file
  storage:delete — delete file
  storage:list — list files in collection

Formats:
  json — JSON.parse/stringify (best for structured data)
  markdown — .md extension (best for text/notes)
  text — plain text (best for logs)

Example:
  import { useLoveRPC } from '@reactjit/core';
  const rpc = useLoveRPC();
  const adapter = new Love2DFileAdapter({ rpc, format: 'json' });

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
LocalStorageAdapter
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

new LocalStorageAdapter({ prefix? })

Options:
  prefix?: string — Key prefix (default: 'ilr:')

Storage Key Format:
  <prefix><collection>:<id>
  Example: 'ilr:notes:n1'

Limits:
  - 5-10MB total (browser-dependent)
  - Synchronous API (may block on large operations)
  - No structured queries (all done in-memory)

Best for:
  - Small datasets
  - Simple key-value storage
  - User preferences

Example:
  const adapter = new LocalStorageAdapter({ prefix: 'myapp:' });

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
IndexedDBAdapter
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

new IndexedDBAdapter({ dbName?, version? })

Options:
  dbName?: string — Database name (default: 'reactjit')
  version?: number — Schema version (default: 1)

Storage:
  - One object store per collection
  - Records keyed by 'id' field
  - Async transactional API

Limits:
  - Much larger than localStorage (50MB+)
  - Structured queries run in-memory (no indexes)

Best for:
  - Larger datasets
  - Complex data structures
  - Offline-first apps

Example:
  const adapter = new IndexedDBAdapter({ dbName: 'taskdb', version: 1 });

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TerminalSQLiteAdapter
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

new TerminalSQLiteAdapter({ dbPath? })

Options:
  dbPath?: string — Database file path (default: './data.db')

Storage:
  - One table per collection
  - Schema: id TEXT PRIMARY KEY, data TEXT (JSON)
  - Uses node:sqlite (Node.js v22+)

Queries:
  - WHERE clauses compile to SQL
  - ORDER BY, LIMIT, OFFSET use SQL
  - Faster than in-memory filtering

Requirements:
  - Node.js v22 or later
  - Does not work in browsers

Best for:
  - Terminal apps
  - CLI tools
  - Large datasets with structured queries

Example:
  const adapter = new TerminalSQLiteAdapter({ dbPath: './data.db' });

examples.title: Override Adapter Per Collection
Manual Refetch on Mutation
Schema with Nested Objects
Pagination
Error Handling with safeParse
Non-React Script with SQLite
Multi-Collection App

criticalRules: Schema MUST include `id: z.string()` field — CRUD methods depend on it
Reactive hooks (useQuery, useListQuery) do NOT auto-refetch — call refetch() after mutations
Love2DFileAdapter requires useLoveRPC() — pass rpc function to constructor
TerminalSQLiteAdapter only works in Node.js v22+ — not available in browsers
IndexedDBAdapter operations are async — always await
LocalStorageAdapter has 5-10MB total limit across all collections
Query operators ($gt, $in, $contains) work in-memory for most adapters except TerminalSQLiteAdapter (uses SQL)
Migration functions must be pure and idempotent — no async operations
autoMigrate: true runs migrations on every read — keep migration logic fast
Schema validation runs on ALL reads and writes — throws ValidationError on failure
Do not mutate data returned from get() or list() — it may be cached
Use createCRUD for scripts/backends without React — useCRUD is for React components only
StorageProvider must wrap your app if you don't pass { adapter } to useCRUD
Generated IDs are globally unique but NOT guaranteed to be sortable — use timestamps if you need chronological ordering
delete() is idempotent — calling it on a non-existent ID does not throw
seeAlso: Storage System — High-level overview and quickstart
State Management — useSignal, useSharedState for ephemeral state
Hooks — useEffect, useMemo, useCallback for side effects
Event Handling — onPress, onChange for triggering mutations

metadata.title: Animation Examples
metadata.description: Demonstrates timing, spring, composite, and CSS-style animations in ReactJIT

sections.overview: This example demonstrates the ReactJIT animation system. It covers three approaches: imperative animations via AnimatedValue (timing and spring), convenience hooks (useSpring, useTransition), and declarative CSS-style animations (style.transition and style.animation). The example shows how to create smooth motion without causing React re-renders on every frame.
sections.explanation:
1. FadeInBox uses useAnimation to get an AnimatedValue and its current numeric value. The timing animation runs once on mount, easing opacity from 0 to 1 over 1 second. The component re-renders only when the batched value changes.

2. SpringBox uses the useSpring convenience hook. When the target value changes (80 to 150 or back), a spring animation automatically starts. The stiffness and damping parameters control the spring's feel (bouncy vs smooth).

3. CompositeBox demonstrates parallel, sequence, and loop. First, X and Y animations run in parallel (diagonal move). Then scale animates up and down in sequence. The whole thing loops infinitely.

4. TransitionBox uses CSS-style transitions. The transition property on the style declares that backgroundColor should animate over 300ms with easeInOut easing. The hoverStyle provides the target value. The Lua animate module handles interpolation when the hover state changes (0-frame latency, no JS round-trip).

5. PulseBox uses CSS-style keyframe animation. The animation property declares keyframes at 0%, 50%, and 100%, with infinite iterations. The Lua animate module runs this entirely on the Lua side, no React re-renders needed.

6. Key performance insight: useAnimation and useSpring batch updates to animation frames. AnimatedValue listeners fire every frame, but React state updates are debounced. CSS-style animations (transition/animation) run entirely in Lua without any React involvement.

metadata.title: Interactive Counter
metadata.description: A counter app with increment and decrement buttons using useState and Pressable

sections.overview: This example builds an interactive counter with two buttons. It demonstrates React state management (useState), user interaction (Pressable with render props for press feedback), row layout (flexDirection: 'row' with explicit width), and the template literal pattern for dynamic text content.
sections.explanation:
1. The Button component uses Pressable with a render prop: ({ pressed }) => ... This gives access to the pressed state for visual feedback (darker blue when pressed).

2. Each button has fixed width: 64, height: 64 for a square shape. The buttons use borderRadius: 12 for rounded corners.

3. The counter display uses a template literal: {`${count}`}. This is important. Do NOT write {count} as it creates a number child node. Always wrap dynamic content in template literals for Text nodes.

4. The button row has flexDirection: 'row' and width: '100%'. The width is critical: without it, justifyContent: 'center' has no effect because the row Box has no known width to distribute space within.

5. gap: 20 adds 20 pixels of space between the two buttons.

6. gap: 32 on the root container adds space between the counter text and the button row.

7. fontWeight: 'bold' on the button text triggers faux-bold rendering (the text is drawn twice with a slight offset on Love2D).

metadata.title: Multi-Panel Dashboard
metadata.description: A dashboard layout with navigation, content panels, and data visualization components

sections.overview: This example builds a multi-panel dashboard layout using higher-level components from @reactjit/core. It demonstrates NavPanel for navigation, Tabs for content switching, Card for content containers, Table for data display, ProgressBar for metrics, and Badge for status indicators. This is a realistic application layout that exercises the flexbox system with nested containers.
sections.explanation:
1. The root layout is a row (flexDirection: 'row') splitting the viewport into a NavPanel on the left (fixed 180px width, default) and a content area (flexGrow: 1).

2. NavPanel renders a sidebar with section headers and selectable items. The activeId prop controls which item is highlighted. onSelect updates the state.

3. Tabs provide content switching within the main panel. The 'underline' variant (default) renders an underline indicator on the active tab.

4. MetricCard is a custom component wrapping Card. Each card uses flexGrow: 1 to distribute equally across the row. The row has width: '100%' and gap: 12.

5. Table renders a data grid with columns and rows. The striped prop alternates row backgrounds for readability.

6. ProgressBar shows a visual progress indicator. The value prop is 0-1 range. showLabel displays the percentage text.

7. The content area uses padding: 16 and gap: 16 to space all its children evenly. This avoids manual margin management on each child.

metadata.title: Hello World
metadata.description: The simplest possible ReactJIT application - a centered greeting

sections.overview: This example demonstrates the absolute minimum ReactJIT application: a single Text node centered on a full-viewport Box. It covers the three essential concepts: importing primitives, filling the viewport, and centering content with flexbox.
sections.explanation:
1. Import Box and Text from @reactjit/core. These are the universal primitives that work on every target.

2. The root Box has width: '100%' and height: '100%'. This is required to fill the viewport. Without these, the root Box has zero size and nothing renders.

3. backgroundColor: '#0f172a' sets a dark blue background. Colors are CSS hex strings.

4. justifyContent: 'center' centers children along the main axis (vertical, because the default flexDirection is 'column').

5. alignItems: 'center' centers children along the cross axis (horizontal).

6. The Text node has an explicit fontSize: 32. Every Text node MUST have fontSize. This is enforced by the linter.

7. color: '#f8fafc' sets the text color to near-white.

metadata.title: Todo List App
metadata.description: A todo list with add and remove functionality using TextInput, FlatList, and state management

sections.overview: This example builds a complete todo list application. It demonstrates text input handling (TextInput with onSubmit), list rendering (mapping over state), dynamic state updates (adding and removing items), and scroll containers for overflow content.
sections.explanation:
1. TextInput with onSubmit handles adding new todos. When the user presses Enter, onSubmit fires with the current text value. The input clears automatically.

2. ScrollView wraps the todo list so it scrolls when items overflow the container. It has flexGrow: 1 to fill remaining vertical space (the parent has height: '100%').

3. Each TodoItem is a row (flexDirection: 'row') with three elements: a toggle circle, the todo text (with flexGrow: 1 to fill space), and a remove button.

4. The toggle circle uses conditional rendering: {todo.done && ...} to show a checkmark only when the todo is completed.

5. textDecorationLine: 'line-through' adds a strikethrough on completed todos.

6. Unicode characters (\u2713 for checkmark, \u00d7 for multiplication sign) are used for icons. In ReactJIT there are no icon fonts, so Unicode or Image components are the icon options.

7. State is managed with useState and useCallback. The functional update pattern (prev => ...) ensures correct state transitions when events fire rapidly.

metadata.title: First Interactive App Tutorial
metadata.description: Build a counter app step by step, learning core concepts along the way

sections.overview: This tutorial walks you through building an interactive counter application from scratch. You will learn how to import primitives, manage state with React hooks, handle user input with Pressable, and style your UI with flexbox properties. By the end you will have a working app with increment and decrement buttons, styled with colors and spacing.

examples.title: Step 1 - Create the Project
examples.code:
reactjit init counter-app
cd counter-app

examples.title: Step 2 - Basic Counter (src/App.tsx)
Step 3 - Add Buttons
Step 4 - Lint and Screenshot
examples.code:
# Run the static linter to catch layout errors
reactjit lint

# Take a screenshot to verify the visual result
reactjit screenshot --output /tmp/counter.png

# Build for distribution
reactjit build dist:love

criticalRules: Every Text node MUST have an explicit fontSize.
The root container MUST use width: '100%', height: '100%' -- not flexGrow: 1.
Row Boxes need explicit width for justifyContent to work.
Use template literals (backticks) for dynamic text: {`${count}`}, not mixed
children like {count} which creates multiple text nodes.
Always run `reactjit lint` after modifying components.
seeAlso: quick-start.txt - Abbreviated version of this tutorial
11-troubleshooting/common-errors.txt - If something does not render correctly

metadata.title: Getting Started
metadata.description: Hub page for all getting-started topics in ReactJIT

sections.overview:
Welcome to ReactJIT. This section covers everything you need to go from zero to a running application. Whether you are targeting Love2D, a terminal emulator, Neovim, or any other supported platform, the fundamentals are the same: install the toolchain, scaffold a project, write React components, and build.

Start with the philosophy document to understand what ReactJIT is and how it differs from browser-based React. Then follow the installation guide, run through the quick start, and build your first interactive app.

examples.title: Section Contents
examples.code:
01-getting-started/
  philosophy.txt   - What ReactJIT is and how it thinks
  installation.txt - Prerequisites and setup steps
  quick-start.txt  - 5-minute path to a running app
  first-app.txt    - Build a counter app step by step

seeAlso: quick-start.txt - Fastest path to running code
first-app.txt - Guided tutorial for your first interactive app

metadata.title: Installation Guide
metadata.description: Prerequisites and step-by-step setup for the ReactJIT monorepo and CLI

sections.overview:
ReactJIT is an npm workspaces monorepo. The core framework runs on Node.js for builds and development tooling. The Love2D target additionally requires the Love2D runtime and a compiled QuickJS shared library. This guide walks through every step from a fresh clone to a verified installation.

Grid targets (terminal, ComputerCraft, Neovim, Hammerspoon, AwesomeWM) only need Node.js. The Love2D target needs the full toolchain.
sections.api:
Prerequisites:
  - Node.js 18+ and npm
  - Love2D 11+ (for Love2D target only)
  - make + gcc (for QuickJS compilation, Love2D target only)
  - git

examples.title: Full Installation (All Targets)
examples.code:
# 1. Clone the repository
git clone <repo-url> && cd react-love

# 2. Install npm dependencies (workspaces monorepo)
npm install

# 3. Build QuickJS shared library (needed for Love2D target)
#    Clones quickjs-ng and compiles libquickjs.so
make setup

# 4. Propagate source-of-truth files to CLI runtime
#    Copies lua/*.lua, packages/shared/, packages/native/, libquickjs.so
#    into cli/runtime/ so the CLI can scaffold projects
make cli-setup

# 5. Link the CLI globally
cd cli && npm link && cd ..

# 6. Verify the installation
reactjit help

examples.title: Minimal Installation (Grid Targets Only)
examples.code:
# Grid targets (terminal, cc, nvim, hs, awesome) do not need Love2D or QuickJS

# 1. Clone and install
git clone <repo-url> && cd react-love
npm install

# 2. Build a terminal demo directly
npm run build:terminal-demo

# 3. Run it
node examples/terminal-demo/dist/main.js

platformNotes.Love2D:
- Install Love2D 11+ from https://love2d.org or your package manager.
- On Linux: `sudo apt install love` or download the AppImage.
- On macOS: `brew install love` or download from love2d.org.
- The `make setup` step compiles libquickjs.so which requires a C compiler (gcc or clang).
platformNotes.Terminal:
- Requires a terminal with truecolor support (most modern terminals).
- No additional system dependencies beyond Node.js.
platformNotes.ComputerCraft:
- Requires a running Minecraft instance with CC: Tweaked mod.
- The WebSocket bridge connects to the CC terminal.
platformNotes.Neovim:
- Requires Neovim 0.7+ with the `neovim` npm package.
- Communication via stdio.

criticalRules: Always run `make cli-setup` after pulling framework changes. This propagates
source-of-truth files into the CLI runtime directory.
Never edit files in cli/runtime/ directly. They are overwritten by make cli-setup.
The `npm link` step in cli/ must be done once to make the `reactjit` command
available globally.
seeAlso: quick-start.txt - Create your first project after installation

metadata.title: ReactJIT Philosophy
metadata.description: Core design principles behind ReactJIT - React stripped of browser magic, rendered as raw geometry

sections.overview:
ReactJIT is React stripped of browser magic and rendered as raw geometry. There is no DOM, no CSS cascade, no implicit sizing, no inherited font stacks, no default margins. Every pixel on screen exists because your code explicitly put it there.

If you did not say how wide it is, it is zero. If you said grow, it grows exactly. If you nested flex containers, you own the consequences. If text wraps, it is because you gave it nowhere to go. This is not a browser abstraction layer. This is direct rendering -- React's component model driving actual draw calls on real hardware.

examples.title: The Multi-Target Promise
The Rendering Pipeline
examples.code:
The data flows through a fixed pipeline on every frame:

  1. React reconciler    - Diffs your component tree, produces mutation commands
  2. Mutation commands   - JSON describing createNode, updateProps, appendChild, etc.
  3. Transport layer     - Delivers commands to the target runtime (QuickJS FFI, WebSocket, stdio, Module.FS)
  4. Layout engine       - Lua-side flexbox computes {x, y, width, height} for every node
  5. Target painter      - Draws actual pixels, characters, or API calls for the target platform

Each target has its own painter, but the reconciler, mutation protocol, and layout
engine are shared across all of them.

criticalRules: There is no implicit sizing. A Box without width and height is zero by zero.
There is no CSS cascade. Style properties do not inherit from parent to child
(except fontSize and color on nested Text nodes).
Every Text node MUST have an explicit fontSize. The linter enforces this.
flexGrow only works when the parent has a known size on the main axis.
This is NOT a browser. There are no default stylesheets, no user-agent margins,
no block/inline flow model. Everything is flexbox, always.
seeAlso: 02-architecture/layout-engine.txt - How the Lua flexbox engine works

metadata.title: 5-Minute Quick Start
metadata.description: The fastest path from installation to a running ReactJIT application

sections.overview: This guide gets you from zero to a running ReactJIT app in five minutes. You will scaffold a new Love2D project, edit the entry component, run it in watch mode, and optionally produce a standalone binary. All you need is a completed installation (see installation.txt).

examples.title: Scaffold and Run (Love2D)
examples.code:
# Scaffold a new project
reactjit init my-app
cd my-app

# Start watch mode (rebuilds on file changes)
reactjit dev

# In a second terminal, run Love2D
love .

examples.title: Edit src/App.tsx
Build a Standalone Binary
examples.code:
# Lint first (catches layout errors before build)
reactjit lint

# Take a screenshot to verify layout
reactjit screenshot --output /tmp/preview.png

# Build self-extracting Linux binary
reactjit build dist:love

criticalRules: Always use `reactjit init` to scaffold projects. Do not mkdir and copy manually.
Always use `reactjit dev` for watch mode. Do not run esbuild --watch manually.
Always use `reactjit build` for production builds. Do not run esbuild manually.
The CLI encodes correct esbuild flags, enforces lint gates, and handles runtime
file placement.
After modifying any component, run `reactjit lint` to catch layout errors.

metadata.title: Architecture
metadata.description: Hub page for ReactJIT architecture documentation

sections.overview:
ReactJIT's architecture is a multi-stage rendering pipeline. React components are reconciled into an instance tree, serialized as mutation commands, transported to the target runtime, laid out by a flexbox engine, and drawn by a target-specific painter. Each stage is independent and replaceable.

This section documents each stage in detail, from the React reconciler through the final paint calls. Understanding this pipeline is essential for debugging layout issues, building custom targets, or contributing to the framework.

examples.title: Section Contents
examples.code:
02-architecture/
  pipeline.txt        - End-to-end rendering pipeline overview
  reconciler.txt      - React reconciler and host config
  layout-engine.txt   - Lua-side flexbox layout engine
  transport.txt       - Transport layer (QuickJS FFI, Module.FS, WebSocket, stdio)
  painter.txt         - Target-specific painters
  source-of-truth.txt - File organization and sync pipeline

seeAlso: pipeline.txt - Start here for the big picture

metadata.title: Layout Engine
metadata.description: The Lua-side flexbox layout engine that computes positions and sizes for every node

sections.overview:
The layout engine (layout.lua) is a Lua-side flexbox implementation that runs inside the Love2D process. After the retained tree receives mutation commands, the layout engine walks the entire tree and computes a {x, y, w, h} rect for every node. It handles absolute units, percentages, vw/vh viewport units, flex-grow/shrink distribution, text wrapping via Love2D font metrics, and all standard flexbox properties.

The engine is designed for the Love2D fixed-canvas model, not for scrolling web pages. The viewport has a known size at all times, and every node's dimensions can be resolved in a single top-down pass with one re-measure step for flex-distributed text nodes.
sections.api:
Public API:
  Layout.init(config)                - Initialize with { measure = MeasureModule }
  Layout.layout(node, x, y, w, h)   - Lay out entire tree from root
  Layout.layoutNode(node, px, py, pw, ph) - Lay out a single node recursively
  Layout.resolveUnit(value, parentSize)   - Resolve a value to pixels

Supported Unit Types:
  number      - Pixels (e.g., 200)
  "50%"       - Percentage of parent dimension
  "10vw"      - Percentage of viewport width
  "5vh"       - Percentage of viewport height

Supported Flexbox Properties:
  flexDirection:  'row' | 'column' (default: 'column')
  flexWrap:       'nowrap' | 'wrap' (default: 'nowrap')
  justifyContent: 'start' | 'center' | 'end' | 'space-between' | 'space-around' | 'space-evenly'
  alignItems:     'start' | 'center' | 'end' | 'stretch' (default: 'stretch')
  alignSelf:      'auto' | 'start' | 'center' | 'end' | 'stretch'
  flexGrow:       number (default: 0)
  flexShrink:     number (default: 1, CSS spec default)
  flexBasis:      number | string | 'auto'
  gap:            number | string

examples.title: Layout Algorithm Overview
examples.code:
For each node, Layout.layoutNode performs these steps:

  1. Resolve own dimensions (width, height) from style + parent constraints
  2. Apply aspectRatio if set and one dimension is missing
  3. Apply flex-adjusted dimensions from parent distribution (_flexW, _stretchH)
  4. Resolve padding (all sides)
  5. Measure intrinsic text size if this is a Text node without explicit dimensions
  6. Apply min/max width/height clamping (re-measure text if width changed)
  7. Resolve margin (all sides)
  8. Compute inner dimensions (outer minus padding)
  9. Filter visible children (skip display: 'none')
  10. Split children into flex lines (single line if nowrap)
  11. For each flex line:
      a. Distribute flex-grow (positive free space) or flex-shrink (negative)
      b. Re-measure text nodes whose width changed from flex distribution
      c. Compute line cross-axis size
      d. Apply justifyContent offset and extra gap
      e. Position each child with alignItems/alignSelf
      f. Recurse into each child: Layout.layoutNode(child, ...)
  12. Auto-height: shrink to content if height was not explicit
  13. Track scroll content dimensions for scroll containers

examples.title: Text Measurement
examples.code:
Text measurement is the key difference from CSS flexbox. In a browser, the layout
engine has access to the full text shaping pipeline. In ReactJIT, text measurement
is done via Love2D's font:getWrap() and font:getWidth() APIs.

For a Text node without explicit width/height:
  1. Use parent's inner width as the wrap constraint
  2. Call measureText(text, fontSize, constraintWidth, fontFamily, lineHeight, letterSpacing, numberOfLines, fontWeight)
  3. Set node width = measured text width + padding
  4. Set node height = measured text height + padding

If flex-grow changes the node's width after initial measurement:
  5. Re-measure with the new width to get correct wrapped height

criticalRules: Nodes without explicit width default to their parent's width (not zero).
Nodes without explicit height auto-size to their content.
Scroll containers without explicit height default to 0 (they need explicit
height or flex-grow to be visible).
flexGrow only distributes positive free space. If the parent has no definite
size on the main axis, there is no free space to distribute.
The layout engine runs synchronously on every frame where the tree is dirty.

metadata.title: Target Painters
metadata.description: How each target platform converts computed layout into visible output

sections.overview:
After the layout engine computes positions and sizes for every node, the painter walks the tree and issues platform-specific draw calls. Each target has its own painter implementation. The painter reads the computed rect ({x, y, w, h}) and style properties from each node, then translates them into the target's native rendering API.

The Love2D painter is the most full-featured, supporting backgrounds, borders, rounded corners, gradients, shadows, text rendering with font metrics, image display with object-fit modes, opacity propagation, stencil-based clipping, scissor rectangles, scroll containers, transforms, and z-index ordering.

examples.title: Love2D Painter (painter.lua)
examples.code:
The Love2D painter uses love.graphics calls to render every node type:

View/Box nodes:
  - Box shadow: Multiple fading rectangles to simulate blur
  - Background gradient: Love2D mesh API with vertex colors
  - Background color: love.graphics.rectangle("fill", ...)
  - Border: love.graphics.rectangle("line", ...) or per-side lines
  - Per-corner border radius: Arc + polygon rendering
  - Outline: Drawn outside the border box
  - Overflow hidden: Stencil (rounded) or scissor (rectangular) clipping
  - Overflow scroll: Scissor clipping + love.graphics.translate for scroll offset
  - Scrollbar indicators: Small rectangles on right/bottom edges

Text nodes:
  - Font cache: Fonts loaded by (size, family, weight) tuple
  - Text alignment: love.graphics.printf with alignment parameter
  - Text truncation: Binary search for ellipsis cutoff point
  - numberOfLines: Wrap then truncate to line count
  - lineHeight override: Manual line-by-line rendering
  - letterSpacing: Character-by-character rendering (expensive)
  - Text shadow: Draw text twice with offset
  - Text decoration: Underline or strikethrough via love.graphics.line
  - Bold simulation: Draw text twice with 0.8px offset (faux bold)

Image nodes:
  - objectFit modes: fill, contain, cover, none
  - Border radius clipping via stencil
  - Image cache managed by images.lua module

Recursive tree walk:
  - Opacity propagates multiplicatively down the tree
  - Stencil depth tracks nested overflow:hidden contexts
  - zIndex reorders paint order within each parent's children
  - Transforms apply via love.graphics.push/pop matrix stack

examples.title: Web Painter (primitives.tsx)
examples.code:
