The web "painter" is not a separate module -- it is built into the primitives. When RendererMode is 'web', Box renders as
, Text as , Image as . The styleToCSS() function converts the ReactJIT Style object to React CSSProperties: - flexDirection, justifyContent, alignItems mapped with flex-start/flex-end - Colors converted from [r,g,b,a] (0-1 range) to rgba() strings - Gradients converted to CSS linear-gradient - Transforms converted to CSS transform string - Transitions mapped to CSS transition property - boxShadow, textShadow composed from offset/blur/color - outline composed from width/color/offset The browser handles layout natively via its own flexbox engine.

examples.title: Terminal Painter
examples.code:
The terminal painter (@reactjit/terminal) uses ANSI truecolor escape sequences.
The grid layout engine (@reactjit/grid) computes character-cell positions.

Each cell is a single character with foreground and background colors:
  ESC[38;2;R;G;Bm    -- Set foreground (truecolor)
  ESC[48;2;R;G;Bm    -- Set background (truecolor)
  <character>         -- The cell content

The painter writes to a buffer, then flushes the entire screen to stdout.

metadata.title: Rendering Pipeline
metadata.description: End-to-end walkthrough of how React components become pixels on screen

sections.overview:
Every frame in ReactJIT follows a fixed rendering pipeline. Your React components produce a virtual tree. The reconciler diffs that tree against the previous version and emits mutation commands (create, update, remove, reorder). Those commands travel over a transport layer to the target runtime. The layout engine computes positions and sizes for every node. Finally, the painter draws the result to the screen.

This pipeline is the same for every target. Only the transport and painter differ. The reconciler and layout engine are shared code.

examples.title: Pipeline Stages
examples.code:
Stage 1: React Reconciler (@reactjit/native)
  Input:  Your JSX component tree
  Output: Mutation commands (JSON)
  What:   react-reconciler 0.29 diffs the virtual tree.
          Host elements are 'View', 'Text', 'Image'.
          Commands: createNode, updateProps, appendChild, removeChild,
                    insertBefore, commitTextUpdate.

Stage 2: Mutation Commands
  Format: Array of JSON objects, each with a `type` field:
          { type: "createNode", nodeType: "View", id: 1, props: { style: {...} } }
          { type: "updateProps", id: 1, props: { style: {...} } }
          { type: "appendChild", parentId: 0, childId: 1 }
          { type: "removeChild", parentId: 0, childId: 1 }

Stage 3: Transport Layer
  Love2D native: QuickJS FFI (bridge_quickjs.lua) -- JS runs inside QuickJS embedded in Love2D
  Love2D web:    Module.FS (bridge_fs.lua) -- commands written to shared Emscripten filesystem
  Grid targets:  WebSocket or stdio -- commands sent over network or pipes

Stage 4: Layout Engine (layout.lua)
  Input:  Retained node tree with style properties
  Output: Computed rect {x, y, w, h} for every node
  What:   Flexbox algorithm: resolves units (px, %, vw, vh),
          distributes flex-grow/shrink, measures text, applies
          justifyContent, alignItems, padding, margin, gap.

Stage 5: Target Painter
  Love2D:  love.graphics calls -- rectangles, text, images, stencils, scissors
  Web:     CSS flexbox in DOM -- styleToCSS conversion, div/span/img elements
  Terminal: ANSI truecolor escape sequences to stdout
  CC:      ComputerCraft term API with 16-color palette
  Neovim:  nvim floating window API
  HS:      hs.canvas pixel drawing
  Awesome: Cairo/Pango rendering

examples.title: Native Mode Frame Loop
examples.code:
On every love.update(dt):

  1. bridge:tick()                    -- Advance JS timers and microtasks
  2. bridge:callGlobal("_poll...")    -- JS processes pending input events
  3. bridge:tick()                    -- Event handlers may trigger state updates
  4. bridge:drainCommands()           -- Collect mutation commands from JS
  5. tree.applyCommands(commands)     -- Apply to retained Lua tree
  6. animate.tick(dt)                 -- Advance Lua-side transitions/animations
  7. if tree.isDirty() then
       layout.layout(root)           -- Recompute layout
     end

On every love.draw():
  8. painter.paint(root)              -- Walk tree, issue draw calls
  9. inspector.draw(root)             -- Debug overlay (if F12 enabled)
  10. errors.draw()                   -- Error overlay (if any errors)

metadata.title: React Reconciler
metadata.description: How @reactjit/native uses react-reconciler to build the instance tree and emit mutation commands

sections.overview:
ReactJIT uses react-reconciler 0.29 (the same package React Native uses) to manage the component tree. The reconciler calls into a host config that creates, updates, and removes instances. In native mode, these instances are lightweight JavaScript objects that map 1:1 to nodes in the Lua-side retained tree.

The reconciler does not draw anything. It produces mutation commands -- JSON objects describing what changed. These commands are batched per frame and flushed to the transport layer, which delivers them to the Lua runtime.

examples.title: How createLove2DApp Works
The Instance Model
examples.code:
Each reconciler instance is a plain object:

  {
    type: 'View',           // host element type
    id: 1,                  // unique numeric ID
    props: { style: {...} }, // current props (style, event handlers extracted)
    children: [],           // child instances
    parent: null,           // parent instance reference
  }

Event handlers (onClick, onKeyDown, etc.) are extracted from props and stored
in the handlerRegistry on the JS side. They never cross the bridge to Lua.
Only the handler's existence is communicated (so Lua knows to dispatch events
back to JS for that node).

seeAlso: pipeline.txt - Where the reconciler fits in the pipeline
transport.txt - How mutations reach the Lua runtime
10-advanced/event-handling.txt - How events flow back from Lua to JS

metadata.title: Source-of-Truth Architecture
metadata.description: Critical guide to file organization, editing rules, and the sync pipeline

sections.overview:
ReactJIT has two categories of files: globally distributed framework files and project-specific application files. Editing the wrong copy is the number one source of "it builds but does not work" bugs. Framework files have a single source of truth at the monorepo root. They are copied into projects by the CLI. Application files are unique to each project and are safe to edit directly.

Understanding this distinction and following the sync pipeline is critical for any work that touches framework internals.
sections.api:
Source-of-Truth Locations:
  lua/*.lua                    -> Lua runtime (layout, painter, tree, events, etc.)
  packages/shared/src/         -> Core primitives, components, hooks, animation, types
  packages/native/src/         -> React reconciler, host config, bridge, entry points
  quickjs/libquickjs.so        -> QuickJS shared library

Copy Destinations (managed by tooling, NEVER edit directly):
  cli/runtime/lua/             <- make cli-setup copies lua/*.lua here
  cli/runtime/reactjit/shared/ <- make cli-setup copies packages/shared/ here
  cli/runtime/reactjit/native/ <- make cli-setup copies packages/native/ here
  cli/runtime/lib/             <- make cli-setup copies quickjs/libquickjs.so here
  <project>/lua/               <- reactjit update copies here
  <project>/reactjit/shared/ <- reactjit update copies here
  <project>/reactjit/native/ <- reactjit update copies here
  <project>/lib/               <- reactjit update copies here

Project-Specific Files (safe to edit directly):
  src/                         - Application code (App.tsx, stories, etc.)
  main.lua                     - Love2D entry point
  conf.lua                     - Love2D configuration
  package.json                 - Project dependencies
  packaging/                   - Build customizations

examples.title: The Sync Pipeline
examples.code:
When you edit a framework file (e.g., lua/layout.lua or packages/shared/src/types.ts):

  # Step 1: Edit the source-of-truth file at the monorepo root
  vim lua/layout.lua

  # Step 2: Propagate to CLI runtime
  make cli-setup

  # Step 3: For each project that needs the change
  cd storybook
  reactjit update          # Syncs lua/, lib/, reactjit/ from CLI runtime
  reactjit build dist:love # Rebuild with new code

examples.title: Adding a New Lua-Side Feature (Checklist)
examples.code:
1. Edit/create files in lua/ (the source of truth)
2. Edit/create files in packages/shared/src/ and packages/native/src/ as needed
3. Run: make cli-setup
4. For each example project that needs the feature:
   cd examples/<project>
   reactjit update
   reactjit build dist:love
5. For new projects: reactjit init <name> gets everything automatically

criticalRules: ALWAYS edit source-of-truth files (lua/, packages/shared/, packages/native/).
NEVER edit copies (cli/runtime/, <project>/lua/, <project>/reactjit/).
Those are disposable and will be overwritten by the next sync.
After editing any source-of-truth file, run the full sync pipeline:
make cli-setup, then reactjit update in affected projects.
reactjit update syncs lua/, lib/, and reactjit/ from CLI runtime into
the project. It NEVER touches src/ (your application code is safe).
reactjit init creates starter versions of src/, main.lua, conf.lua.
reactjit update never touches those files.
reactjit build dist:love has a fallback: if no local lua/ exists, it reads
from cli/runtime/lua/. But reactjit dev and love . require local copies,
so always run reactjit update for dev workflows.

metadata.title: Transport Layer
metadata.description: How mutation commands and events travel between React and target runtimes

sections.overview:
The transport layer is the communication channel between the React reconciler (running in JavaScript) and the target runtime (Lua, terminal process, etc.). All transports implement the IBridge interface, which provides subscribe, send, rpc, and setState methods. React hooks consume IBridge via context and never know which transport is active.

There are four transport implementations, each suited to a different deployment model. The choice of transport is automatic based on the target and mode configuration.
sections.api:
IBridge Interface:
  send(type, payload?)           - Queue a command for the Lua side (batched)
  flush()                        - Write all queued commands to transport
  subscribe(type, fn)            - Subscribe to events from Lua; returns unsubscribe
  rpc<T>(method, args?, timeout?) - Call an RPC method on Lua, await response
  setState(key, value)           - Set a shared state key (convenience over send)
  isReady()                      - Whether the transport is initialized
  onReady(callback)              - Register callback for when transport becomes ready
  destroy()                      - Tear down transport resources

Transport Implementations:
  NativeBridge  (bridge_quickjs.lua) - QuickJS FFI for Love2D native builds
  WebBridge     (bridge_fs.lua)      - Module.FS for Love2D web (Emscripten) builds
  WebSocket     (ws)                 - For CC, Hammerspoon targets
  stdio         (process.stdin/out)  - For Neovim, AwesomeWM targets

examples.title: Native Mode (QuickJS FFI)
examples.code:
In native Love2D builds, JavaScript runs inside QuickJS, which is embedded in the
Love2D process via libquickjs.so (a shared library loaded with LuaJIT FFI).

Data flow:
  JS -> Lua: Mutation commands written to a shared buffer, drained each frame
             via bridge:drainCommands()
  Lua -> JS: Events pushed via bridge:pushEvent(), picked up by JS on next tick
             via globalThis._pollAndDispatchEvents()

The bridge also provides:
  bridge:eval(code, filename)        - Evaluate JS code in the QuickJS context
  bridge:callGlobal(name)            - Call a named global JS function
  bridge:callGlobalReturn(name)      - Call and return result as Lua value
  bridge:tick()                      - Advance JS timers and microtask queue

This is the fastest transport -- zero serialization overhead for most operations
because JS and Lua share the same process memory.

examples.title: Web Mode (Module.FS)
examples.code:
In browser builds (love.js / Emscripten), the Lua runtime and JavaScript run in
separate contexts. Communication happens via JSON files on the Emscripten shared
filesystem (Module.FS).

Data flow:
  JS -> Lua: Commands written to /__reconciler_in.json, polled by Lua each frame
  Lua -> JS: Events written to /__bridge_<namespace>_out.json, polled by JS

Both sides poll their respective inbox files on each frame tick and remove them
after reading.

examples.title: IBridge in React Hooks

seeAlso: reconciler.txt - What produces the mutation commands
10-advanced/lua-runtime.txt - The Lua side that receives them

metadata.title: reactjit build
metadata.description: Bundle JS for development or produce self-contained distribution packages.

sections.overview:
The `reactjit build` command bundles JavaScript and optionally produces self-contained distribution packages. Without a target, it runs the lint gate and bundles JS for the `love .` dev workflow. With a target (dist:love or dist:terminal), it produces a standalone executable.

Every build automatically runs `reactjit update` first (unless --no-update) and passes through the lint gate -- builds fail on lint errors. This ensures that distribution packages always contain up-to-date framework files and pass layout validation.
sections.api:
Usage:
  reactjit build [target] [flags]

Targets:
  (none)          Lint gate + bundle JS for dev (outputs bundle.js for `love .` workflow)
  dist:love       Self-extracting Linux binary with bundled glibc, Love2D, and QuickJS
  dist:terminal   Single-file Node.js executable with shebang

Flags:
  --no-update     Skip the automatic `reactjit update` step
  --debug         Enable the visual inspector (F12) in dist:love builds
                  (inspector is disabled by default in dist builds)

Build steps for each target:

  No target (dev bundle):
    1. reactjit update (sync runtime files)
    2. Lint gate (fail on errors)
    3. esbuild --format=iife --global-name=ReactLove
    4. Output: bundle.js

  dist:love:
    1. reactjit update
    2. Bundle JS (IIFE for QuickJS)
    3. Stage .love archive (main.lua, conf.lua, lua/, bundle.js)
    4. Create .love zip archive
    5. Bundle Love2D binary + all shared libraries (including glibc)
    6. Create launcher script
    7. Pack into single self-extracting binary
    8. Output: dist/<project-name>
    Entry: src/main-love.tsx or src/native-main.tsx or src/main.tsx (first found)

  dist:terminal:
    1. Bundle JS (CJS for Node.js, --platform=node, --external:ws)
    2. Prepend shebang (#!/usr/bin/env node)
    3. Output: dist/<project-name>-terminal
    Entry: src/main-terminal.tsx

examples.title: Dev bundle
examples.code:
reactjit build
love .

examples.title: Self-extracting Love2D binary
examples.code:
reactjit build dist:love
./dist/my-app

examples.title: Debug build with inspector
examples.code: reactjit build dist:love --debug

examples.title: Terminal executable
examples.code:
reactjit build dist:terminal
./dist/my-app-terminal

examples.title: Skip runtime update
examples.code: reactjit build --no-update

criticalRules: Build always runs the lint gate -- fix all lint errors before building
Build always runs `reactjit update` unless --no-update is passed
The inspector (F12) is automatically disabled in dist:love builds (pass --debug to keep it)
dist:love only works on x86_64 Linux (bundles host system's glibc and shared libraries)
dist:love requires Love2D installed on the build machine
Never run raw esbuild manually -- the CLI encodes correct flags for each target
seeAlso: dev.txt -- Watch mode for iterative development

metadata.title: reactjit dev
metadata.description: Start esbuild in watch mode for live Love2D development.

sections.overview:
The `reactjit dev` command starts esbuild in watch mode, continuously rebuilding the JavaScript bundle whenever source files change. It uses the correct esbuild flags for the Love2D target (IIFE format, ReactLove global name, ES2020 target, automatic JSX).

Run `love .` in a separate terminal to see your app. Love2D reads the bundle.js file on each frame update, so changes appear in real time without restarting Love2D.
sections.api:
Usage:
  reactjit dev

Entry point:
  src/main.tsx (required -- the command exits with an error if this file does not exist)

Output:
  bundle.js in the project root

esbuild flags used:
  --bundle
  --format=iife
  --global-name=ReactLove
  --target=es2020
  --jsx=automatic
  --outfile=bundle.js
  --watch

examples.title: Standard development workflow
examples.code:
# Terminal 1:
cd my-project
reactjit dev

# Terminal 2:
cd my-project
love .

criticalRules: The project must have a src/main.tsx entry point
Do NOT run raw esbuild --watch manually -- the CLI encodes the correct flags
The dev command requires local lua/ and lib/ directories (run `reactjit update` first if missing)
Love2D must be running in a separate terminal -- the dev command only watches and bundles JS
Ctrl+C exits the watcher
seeAlso: lint.txt -- Run the linter separately during development
init.txt -- Creating new projects with the correct structure

metadata.title: CLI Reference
metadata.description: Overview of the reactjit CLI tool and its commands.

sections.overview:
The reactjit CLI is the primary tool for creating, developing, building, and testing ReactJIT projects. It encodes correct esbuild flags, enforces lint gates before builds, handles runtime file placement, and produces distribution packages. You should always use the CLI instead of running raw esbuild commands directly -- manual esbuild will use wrong flags, skip linting, and produce broken builds.

The CLI manages the distinction between framework files (lua/, lib/, reactjit/) and project-specific files (src/, main.lua, conf.lua). Framework files are synced from the CLI runtime; project files are yours and never touched by CLI commands.
sections.api:
Available commands:

  reactjit init <name>
    Create a new ReactJIT project.
    See: init.txt

  reactjit dev
    Start esbuild in watch mode for live development.
    See: dev.txt

  reactjit build [target] [flags]
    Bundle JS or produce distribution packages.
    Targets: (none), dist:love, dist:terminal
    Flags: --no-update, --debug
    See: build.txt

  reactjit update
    Sync framework runtime files into the current project.
    See: update.txt

  reactjit lint
    Run the static layout linter on src/ files.
    See: lint.txt

  reactjit screenshot [--output path]
    Capture a headless screenshot of the current app.
    See: screenshot.txt

examples.title: Typical new project workflow
examples.code:
reactjit init my-app
cd my-app
reactjit dev
# In another terminal:
love .

examples.title: Build and verify workflow
examples.code:
reactjit lint
reactjit screenshot --output /tmp/preview.png
reactjit build dist:love

examples.title: After framework changes
examples.code:
# In monorepo root:
make cli-setup
# In project directory:
cd storybook
reactjit update
reactjit build

criticalRules: Always use the CLI instead of running esbuild directly
Run `reactjit lint` after every component change
Run `reactjit screenshot` to visually verify layouts
After editing framework source-of-truth files, run `make cli-setup` then `reactjit update`
Never edit cli/runtime/ or project lua/reactjit/ directly -- those are disposable copies

metadata.title: reactjit init
metadata.description: Create a new ReactJIT project with scaffolded structure and dependencies.

sections.overview:
The `reactjit init` command creates a new ReactJIT project directory with the complete scaffolding needed to start development. It copies the project template, Lua runtime files, native libraries, and framework source packages from the CLI runtime directory, writes a package.json, and runs npm install.

After init completes, you can immediately start development with `reactjit dev` and `love .` in a second terminal.
sections.api:
Usage:
  reactjit init <name>

Arguments:
  name    The project name. Creates a directory with this name in the current working directory.

What it creates:
  <name>/
    src/
      App.tsx          Starter application component
      main.tsx         Entry point (renders App into the reconciler)
    lua/               Lua runtime files (layout.lua, painter.lua, bridge_quickjs.lua, etc.)
    lib/
      libquickjs.so    QuickJS shared library for FFI bridge
    reactjit/
      shared/          @reactjit/core package source (primitives, hooks, animation)
      native/          @reactjit/native package source (reconciler host config)
    main.lua           Love2D entry point
    conf.lua           Love2D configuration (window size, title)
    package.json       Project dependencies and scripts

Dependencies installed:
  react ^18.3.0
  react-reconciler ^0.29.0
  esbuild ^0.24.0 (dev)
  @types/react ^18.3.0 (dev)
  typescript ^5.5.0 (dev)

examples.title: Create a new project
examples.code:
reactjit init my-game
cd my-game
reactjit dev

examples.title: Create and immediately build
examples.code:
reactjit init dashboard
cd dashboard
reactjit build dist:love

criticalRules: The project name becomes the directory name -- it must not already exist
The CLI runtime directory must be populated first (run `make cli-setup` in the monorepo root)
If the runtime is missing, init will warn but still create the project structure
Never manually create project directories with mkdir + cp -- always use init
Project files (src/, main.lua, conf.lua) are yours to edit. Framework files (lua/, lib/, reactjit/) are managed by the CLI and will be overwritten by `reactjit update`
seeAlso: update.txt -- Sync framework files after changes

metadata.title: reactjit lint
metadata.description: Static layout linter that catches common ReactJIT mistakes at build time.

sections.overview:
The `reactjit lint` command runs a static layout linter on all .tsx files in the project's src/ directory. It uses TypeScript's AST parser (ts.createSourceFile) to analyze JSX elements and their style props without type checking. The linter catches layout mistakes that would produce invisible, overlapping, or incorrectly sized elements at runtime.

The linter is also run automatically as a lint gate during `reactjit build` -- builds fail when error-level rules are violated. You should run the linter after every component change to catch issues early.

NOTE: Seven layout-related lint rules were removed in recent versions because the layout engine now handles these cases correctly: no-text-without-fontsize, no-unicode-symbol-in-text, no-row-justify-without-width, no-flexrow-flexcolumn, no-uncontexted-flexgrow, no-deep-flex-nesting, and no-implicit-container-sizing. The engine is mature enough that these patterns work reliably.
sections.api:
Usage:
  reactjit lint

Runs on:
  All .tsx files under src/ (recursively, skipping node_modules, dist, .git, build, out)

Error rules (block builds):

  no-mixed-text-children
    Mixed literal text and JSX expressions in <Text> (e.g., Score: {score}) creates multiple
    __TEXT__ nodes that overlap. Use a template literal: {`Score: ${score}`}.

  no-invalid-style-props
    Style object contains properties not recognized by ReactJIT's style system. Only
    checked on ReactJIT primitives (Box, Text, Image, etc.), not on HTML elements.

  no-link-without-to
    <Link> component is missing the "to" prop for navigation target.

  no-image-without-src
    <Image> component is missing the "src" prop.

  no-usecrud-without-schema
    useCRUD() hook called without a schema argument (requires at least 2 arguments).

Warning rules (advisory):

  no-routes-without-fallback
    <Routes> without a <Route path="*"> catch-all for 404 handling.

  no-pressable-without-onpress
    <Pressable> component lacks an onPress handler.

Escape hatch:
  // ilr-ignore-next-line
  Place this comment on the line immediately before the JSX element to suppress all rules
  for that element.

Exit code:
  0 if no errors (warnings are OK)
  1 if any error-level rules are violated

examples.title: Run the linter
examples.code:
cd my-project
reactjit lint

examples.title: Suppress a lint rule
Typical lint output
examples.code:
src/App.tsx
    12:5  error  Mixed text and expressions in Text node  [no-mixed-text-children]
    28:3  error  Image element missing src prop            [no-image-without-src]
    45:7  warn   Pressable has no onPress handler          [no-pressable-without-onpress]

  2 errors, 1 warning in 3 files

criticalRules: Run the linter after EVERY component change
Error-level rules block builds -- they must be fixed or explicitly suppressed
The escape hatch (// ilr-ignore-next-line) should be used sparingly
Style passed via variables (style={myStyle}) cannot be analyzed -- the linter skips those
Style objects with spread operators ({...baseStyle, color: '#fff'}) are partially analyzed
Only ReactJIT primitives are linted (Box, Text, Image, etc.) -- HTML elements are skipped
seeAlso: build.txt -- Build command includes lint gate
../04-layout-system/critical-rules.txt -- Detailed explanations of each critical rule

metadata.title: reactjit screenshot
metadata.description: Capture a headless screenshot of the current app state for visual layout verification.

sections.overview:
The `reactjit screenshot` command runs the full lint + build pipeline, then launches Love2D in headless screenshot mode to capture the current app state as a PNG image. This is the primary tool for visual verification of layouts without manually inspecting a running Love2D window.

The command automatically detects xvfb-run for truly headless operation on servers or CI environments. If xvfb-run is not available, it launches Love2D normally (requires a display).
sections.api:
Usage:
  reactjit screenshot [--output path]

Options:
  --output, -o    Path for the output PNG file.
                  Default: screenshot.png in the project root.

Steps performed:
  1. Lint gate (fails on errors, same as `reactjit lint`)
  2. Bundle JS (esbuild with IIFE format for Love2D)
  3. Launch Love2D with ILOVEREACT_SCREENSHOT=1 environment variable
  4. Love2D renders one frame and saves to the specified output path
  5. Love2D exits automatically after saving

Environment variables set:
  ILOVEREACT_SCREENSHOT=1
  ILOVEREACT_SCREENSHOT_OUTPUT=<absolute output path>

Timeout:
  15 seconds. If Love2D does not produce a screenshot within this window, the command
  fails with a timeout error.

examples.title: Default screenshot
examples.code:
reactjit screenshot
# Output: screenshot.png in project root

examples.title: Custom output path
examples.code: reactjit screenshot --output /tmp/preview.png

examples.title: CI verification workflow
examples.code:
reactjit lint
reactjit screenshot --output /tmp/layout-check.png
# Inspect /tmp/layout-check.png or diff against a reference image

criticalRules: The lint gate runs before screenshot capture -- fix lint errors first
Love2D must be installed and available in PATH
For headless environments, install xvfb-run (apt install xvfb on Debian/Ubuntu)
The screenshot reflects the initial render state (no user interaction)
15-second timeout -- if your app hangs during initialization, the command will fail
The entry point must be src/main.tsx
seeAlso: lint.txt -- Lint gate that runs before screenshot

metadata.title: reactjit update
metadata.description: Sync framework runtime files from the CLI into the current project.

sections.overview:
The `reactjit update` command synchronizes the framework runtime files (lua/, lib/, and reactjit/) from the CLI runtime directory into the current project. It replaces these directories entirely, bringing the project up to date with the latest framework code.

This command never touches project-specific files (src/, main.lua, conf.lua, package.json). It is the second step in the framework update pipeline: first `make cli-setup` copies source-of-truth files into the CLI runtime, then `reactjit update` copies from the CLI runtime into each project.
sections.api:
Usage:
  reactjit update

Prerequisite:
  Must be run from inside an ReactJIT project directory (detected by presence of main.lua
  or src/ directory).

What it syncs:
  cli/runtime/lua/         ->  <project>/lua/
  cli/runtime/lib/         ->  <project>/lib/
  cli/runtime/reactjit/  ->  <project>/reactjit/

What it does NOT touch:
  src/              Project source code
  main.lua          Love2D entry point
  conf.lua          Love2D configuration
  package.json      Project dependencies
  packaging/        Build customizations

The update is destructive for the synced directories -- existing lua/, lib/, and reactjit/
directories are deleted and replaced entirely.

examples.title: After editing framework source files
examples.code:
# Edit source-of-truth files:
#   lua/layout.lua
#   packages/shared/src/primitives.tsx

# Propagate to CLI runtime:
make cli-setup

# Sync into project:
cd storybook
reactjit update

# Rebuild:
reactjit build

examples.title: Hydrate an existing project
examples.code:
cd my-project
reactjit update

criticalRules: Always run `make cli-setup` in the monorepo root BEFORE running `reactjit update`
The CLI runtime must be populated (`cli/runtime/lua/` and `cli/runtime/reactjit/` must exist)
Never edit lua/, lib/, or reactjit/ in a project directly -- those are disposable copies managed by update
The update deletes and replaces the target directories entirely -- any local modifications are lost
Must be run from inside an ReactJIT project directory
seeAlso: init.txt -- Initial project creation (includes the first sync)
build.txt -- Build command runs update automatically

metadata.title: Critical Layout Rules
metadata.description: The most important layout rules in ReactJIT. Violating these causes the most common bugs.

sections.overview:
These are the remaining critical rules in ReactJIT development. The layout engine has been refined to handle many cases that previously required strict rules: Text now has sensible defaults, Unicode symbols render correctly, rows resolve width from parents, deep flex nesting works, and large component trees handle correctly. Only 4 rules remain because they reflect fundamental constraints of the layout system.

The linter runs automatically during `reactjit build` (lint gate). You should also run `reactjit lint` after every component change to catch issues early. Rules can be bypassed on a per-line basis with the `// ilr-ignore-next-line` comment, but this should be rare.
sections.api:
Linter escape hatch:

  // ilr-ignore-next-line
  <Text style={{ color: '#cdd6f4' }}>Intentionally no fontSize</Text>

This suppresses all linter rules for the next line. Use sparingly.

examples.title: WRONG -- root uses flexGrow
RIGHT -- root uses explicit dimensions
WRONG -- mixed text children (linter error: no-mixed-text-children)
RIGHT -- template literal instead of mixed children
examples.code:
<Text style={{ fontSize: 14, color: '#cdd6f4' }}>
  {`Score: ${score}`}
</Text>

examples.title: RIGHT -- flexGrow for space-filling elements
examples.code:
<Box style={{ width: '100%', height: '100%', backgroundColor: '#1e1e2e' }}>
  <Header />
  <Box style={{ flexGrow: 1 }}>
    <MainContent />
  </Box>
  <Footer />
</Box>

examples.title: RIGHT -- ScrollView with explicit height
examples.code:
<Box style={{ width: '100%', height: '100%' }}>
  <ScrollView style={{ height: 300, width: '100%' }}>
    <Text style={{ fontSize: 14 }}>Scrollable content</Text>
  </ScrollView>
</Box>

criticalRules: 1. Root containers MUST have width: '100%', height: '100%'
Why: The root element IS the outermost container. Without explicit dimensions, it has zero
size and nothing will be visible. flexGrow on the root has no parent to grow into.
Linter: no-flexgrow-root (error)
2. Use flexGrow: 1 for space-filling elements
Why: When you want an element to absorb remaining space after its siblings measure, use
flexGrow: 1. This is the correct way to fill available space in a flex container. Never
hardcode pixel heights to fill a known window size — that creates dead air at different
resolutions.
Linter: Enforced at build time
3. ScrollView needs explicit height
Why: Scroll containers are excluded from auto-sizing. They need height: <number> or
flexGrow: 1 to define their scroll viewport. Without explicit height, a ScrollView has
zero size and no content is visible.
Linter: Enforced at build time
4. No mixed text and expressions in Text -- use template literals
Why: <Text>Score: {score}</Text> creates two separate __TEXT__ nodes ("Score: " and the
value of score). The layout engine lays these out as separate flex items at y=0, causing
them to overlap. Using {`Score: ${score}`} produces a single __TEXT__ node. This is a
pending reconciler fix and remains the most reliable pattern.
Linter: no-mixed-text-children (error)
seeAlso: sizing.txt -- Width, height, and sizing rules

metadata.title: Flexbox Layout Model
metadata.description: How ReactJIT uses Flexbox for all layout, with column as the default direction.

sections.overview:
ReactJIT uses Flexbox for all layout, mirroring React Native's approach. Every Box is implicitly a flex container -- there is no need to set display: 'flex' (it is the default). The critical difference from CSS is that flexDirection defaults to 'column', not 'row'. This means children stack vertically unless you explicitly set flexDirection: 'row'.

The layout engine on the Lua side resolves flex properties into absolute positions and sizes. It supports the standard flex properties: main axis distribution (justifyContent), cross axis alignment (alignItems, alignSelf), flex sizing (flexGrow, flexShrink, flexBasis), wrapping (flexWrap), and gap spacing. All values use the same names as CSS Flexbox except that 'start' and 'end' are used instead of 'flex-start' and 'flex-end'.
sections.api:
Flexbox style properties (all set via the `style` prop on Box):

  flexDirection: 'row' | 'column'
    Default: 'column'
    Sets the main axis. 'column' stacks children vertically, 'row' stacks horizontally.

  justifyContent: 'start' | 'center' | 'end' | 'space-between' | 'space-around' | 'space-evenly'
    Default: 'start'
    Distributes children along the main axis.
    IMPORTANT: For row containers, justifyContent only works when the Box has an explicit
    width. Box nodes have no intrinsic width, so without one the engine cannot distribute
    space. The linter enforces this.

  alignItems: 'start' | 'center' | 'end' | 'stretch'
    Default: 'stretch'
    Aligns children along the cross axis.

  alignSelf: 'auto' | 'start' | 'center' | 'end' | 'stretch'
    Default: 'auto' (inherits parent's alignItems)
    Overrides the parent's alignItems for a specific child.

  flexGrow: number
    Default: 0
    How much of the remaining space this child should consume.
    Must have sibling sizing context to work predictably -- the linter warns when all
    siblings grow without explicit main-axis dimensions.

  flexShrink: number
    Default: 1
    How much this child should shrink when the container is too small.

  flexBasis: number | string | 'auto'
    Default: 'auto'
    The initial size of the child before flexGrow/flexShrink are applied.

  flexWrap: 'nowrap' | 'wrap'
    Default: 'nowrap'
    Whether children wrap to the next line when they exceed the container size.

  gap: number | string
    Spacing between children (applied uniformly on the main axis).

  display: 'flex' | 'none'
    Default: 'flex'
    Set to 'none' to hide an element entirely (does not occupy space).

examples.title: Vertical stack (default)
examples.code:
<Box style={{ width: '100%', height: '100%' }}>
  <Text style={{ fontSize: 20, color: '#cdd6f4' }}>First</Text>
  <Text style={{ fontSize: 20, color: '#cdd6f4' }}>Second</Text>
  <Text style={{ fontSize: 20, color: '#cdd6f4' }}>Third</Text>
</Box>

examples.title: Horizontal row with space-between
examples.code:
<Box style={{
  flexDirection: 'row',
  width: '100%',
  justifyContent: 'space-between',
  padding: 12,
}}>
  <Text style={{ fontSize: 16, color: '#cdd6f4' }}>Left</Text>
  <Text style={{ fontSize: 16, color: '#cdd6f4' }}>Center</Text>
  <Text style={{ fontSize: 16, color: '#cdd6f4' }}>Right</Text>
</Box>

examples.title: Centered content
examples.code:
<Box style={{
  width: '100%',
  height: '100%',
  justifyContent: 'center',
  alignItems: 'center',
}}>
  <Text style={{ fontSize: 32, color: '#cdd6f4' }}>Centered</Text>
</Box>

examples.title: flexGrow with sibling sizing
examples.code:
<Box style={{ flexDirection: 'row', width: '100%', height: 50 }}>
  <Box style={{ width: 100, backgroundColor: '#f38ba8' }} />
  <Box style={{ flexGrow: 1, backgroundColor: '#a6e3a1' }} />
  <Box style={{ width: 100, backgroundColor: '#89b4fa' }} />
</Box>

examples.title: Wrapping row
examples.code:
<Box style={{
  flexDirection: 'row',
  flexWrap: 'wrap',
  width: 300,
  gap: 8,
}}>
  <Box style={{ width: 80, height: 80, backgroundColor: '#f38ba8' }} />
  <Box style={{ width: 80, height: 80, backgroundColor: '#a6e3a1' }} />
  <Box style={{ width: 80, height: 80, backgroundColor: '#89b4fa' }} />
  <Box style={{ width: 80, height: 80, backgroundColor: '#f9e2af' }} />
  <Box style={{ width: 80, height: 80, backgroundColor: '#cba6f7' }} />
</Box>

criticalRules: flexDirection defaults to 'column', not 'row' -- this is the opposite of CSS default
justifyContent on a row Box REQUIRES explicit width -- Box nodes have no intrinsic width
flexGrow without sibling sizing context produces unreliable layout
All siblings using flexGrow without any explicit main-axis size causes content-dependent layout
Use 'start'/'end', not 'flex-start'/'flex-end' -- the framework handles the conversion
Prefer Box with flexDirection: 'row' over FlexRow component (linter warns about FlexRow)
flexGrow on a root container is useless -- there is no parent to grow into

metadata.title: Layout System
metadata.description: Overview of the ReactJIT layout system, based on Flexbox with fixed-canvas semantics.

sections.overview:
ReactJIT uses a Flexbox layout model for all targets, similar to React Native. Every Box is a flex container by default, with flexDirection defaulting to 'column'. There are no block, inline, or grid display modes -- everything is flex. The layout engine runs on the Lua side (layout.lua), computing absolute positions and sizes from the style tree that React produces.

Because Love2D renders to a fixed canvas (not a scrolling web page), the layout system demands explicit sizing at the root level and encourages explicit dimensions throughout. The static linter (reactjit lint) enforces critical layout rules at build time, catching the most common mistakes before they reach the renderer. Understanding these rules is essential for productive development with ReactJIT.
sections.api:
The layout system is configured entirely through the `style` prop on Box, Text, and Image
primitives. All style properties are defined in the Style interface (packages/shared/src/types.ts).

Layout-related style categories:
  - Flexbox: flexDirection, justifyContent, alignItems, alignSelf, flexGrow, flexShrink, flexBasis, flexWrap, gap
  - Sizing: width, height, minWidth, minHeight, maxWidth, maxHeight, aspectRatio
  - Spacing: padding, paddingLeft/Right/Top/Bottom, margin, marginLeft/Right/Top/Bottom, gap
  - Visual: backgroundColor, borderRadius, borderWidth, borderColor, opacity, overflow, zIndex, shadows, gradients
  - Text: color, fontSize, fontFamily, fontWeight, textAlign, textOverflow, lineHeight, letterSpacing
  - Transforms: transform object with translateX/Y, rotate, scaleX/Y, skewX/Y, originX/Y

examples.title: Full-viewport root container
Row layout with centered content
examples.code:
<Box style={{
  flexDirection: 'row',
  width: '100%',
  justifyContent: 'space-between',
  alignItems: 'center',
  padding: 16,
}}>
  <Text style={{ fontSize: 18, color: '#cdd6f4' }}>Left</Text>
  <Text style={{ fontSize: 18, color: '#cdd6f4' }}>Right</Text>
</Box>

criticalRules: Root containers MUST have width: '100%', height: '100%' -- NOT flexGrow: 1
Every Text MUST have explicit fontSize -- the linter enforces this
Row Boxes with justifyContent MUST have explicit width
No flexGrow without sibling sizing context
Keep flex trees shallow -- prefer explicit dimensions over deep nesting
Fill the viewport -- Love2D is a fixed canvas, not a scrolling page
See critical-rules.txt for the complete list
seeAlso: critical-rules.txt -- All critical layout rules with explanations

metadata.title: Width, Height, and Min/Max Sizing
metadata.description: Sizing properties for controlling element dimensions in ReactJIT.

sections.overview:
Every element in ReactJIT has explicit sizing control through width, height, and their min/max variants. Values can be numbers (interpreted as pixels on Love2D/web, or character cells on grid targets) or percentage strings like '100%' or '50%'. Unlike the web, Box elements in ReactJIT have no intrinsic width -- if you do not specify a width, it is effectively zero unless flexGrow or alignItems: 'stretch' provides one.

Root containers must always use width: '100%', height: '100%' to fill the viewport. This is the most common mistake in ReactJIT development and is enforced by the linter. Using flexGrow: 1 on the root does not work because there is no parent container to grow into.
sections.api:
Sizing style properties:

  width: number | string
    Width in pixels (number) or percentage of parent ('50%', '100%').
    Box nodes have zero intrinsic width. Text nodes have intrinsic width from font measurement.

  height: number | string
    Height in pixels (number) or percentage of parent.
    Box nodes have zero intrinsic height. Text nodes have intrinsic height from fontSize.

  minWidth: number | string
    Minimum width constraint. The element will not shrink below this value.

  minHeight: number | string
    Minimum height constraint.

  maxWidth: number | string
    Maximum width constraint. The element will not grow beyond this value.

  maxHeight: number | string
    Maximum height constraint.

  aspectRatio: number
    Maintains a width-to-height ratio. For example, aspectRatio: 16/9 with width: 320
    produces height: 180. If only one dimension is set, the other is computed from the ratio.

examples.title: Root container filling viewport
examples.code:
<Box style={{ width: '100%', height: '100%', backgroundColor: '#1e1e2e' }}>
  <Text style={{ fontSize: 20, color: '#cdd6f4' }}>Full viewport</Text>
</Box>

examples.title: Fixed-size box
examples.code: <Box style={{ width: 200, height: 100, backgroundColor: '#89b4fa', borderRadius: 8 }} />

examples.title: Percentage-based sizing
examples.code:
<Box style={{ width: '100%', height: '100%' }}>
  <Box style={{ width: '50%', height: '30%', backgroundColor: '#a6e3a1' }} />
</Box>

examples.title: Min/max constraints
examples.code:
<Box style={{
  width: '80%',
  minWidth: 200,
  maxWidth: 600,
  height: 100,
  backgroundColor: '#f9e2af',
}} />

examples.title: Aspect ratio
examples.code:
<Box style={{
  width: 320,
  aspectRatio: 16 / 9,
  backgroundColor: '#cba6f7',
  borderRadius: 8,
}} />

criticalRules: Root containers MUST use width: '100%', height: '100%' -- NOT flexGrow: 1
Box nodes have zero intrinsic width and height -- always specify dimensions or use flex sizing
Text nodes have intrinsic dimensions based on fontSize and content -- but you still MUST set fontSize
Pre-compute grid dimensions rather than relying on child content to infer container size
Percentage values are relative to the parent's computed dimension, not the viewport (unless the parent is the root)
seeAlso: spacing.txt -- Padding and margin affect effective size
critical-rules.txt -- Root sizing and other critical rules

metadata.title: Padding, Margin, and Gap
metadata.description: Spacing properties for controlling element padding, margin, and flex gap.

sections.overview:
ReactJIT supports padding (inner spacing), margin (outer spacing), and gap (spacing between flex children). These work the same way as in CSS Flexbox: padding creates space inside the element's border, margin creates space outside, and gap creates uniform spacing between sibling children in a flex container.

All spacing values accept numbers (pixels on Love2D/web, character cells on grid targets) or percentage strings. Shorthand properties (padding, margin) apply to all four sides, and per-side properties (paddingLeft, marginTop, etc.) override the shorthand for specific sides.
sections.api:
Padding properties:

  padding: number | string
    Applies uniform padding to all four sides.

  paddingLeft: number | string
  paddingRight: number | string
  paddingTop: number | string
  paddingBottom: number | string
    Per-side padding. Overrides the shorthand `padding` for the specified side.

Margin properties:

  margin: number | string
    Applies uniform margin to all four sides.

  marginLeft: number | string
  marginRight: number | string
  marginTop: number | string
  marginBottom: number | string
    Per-side margin. Overrides the shorthand `margin` for the specified side.

Gap property:

  gap: number | string
    Spacing between children along the main axis of a flex container.
    Applied between each pair of adjacent children.

examples.title: Uniform padding
examples.code:
<Box style={{
  width: '100%',
  height: '100%',
  padding: 24,
  backgroundColor: '#1e1e2e',
}}>
  <Text style={{ fontSize: 16, color: '#cdd6f4' }}>
    {`24px of space on all sides`}
  </Text>
</Box>

examples.title: Per-side padding
examples.code:
<Box style={{
  width: 300,
  height: 100,
  paddingLeft: 20,
  paddingRight: 20,
  paddingTop: 8,
  paddingBottom: 8,
  backgroundColor: '#313244',
}}>
  <Text style={{ fontSize: 14, color: '#cdd6f4' }}>Asymmetric padding</Text>
</Box>

examples.title: Margin for spacing between siblings
examples.code:
<Box style={{ width: '100%', height: '100%', padding: 16 }}>
  <Box style={{ height: 50, marginBottom: 12, backgroundColor: '#f38ba8' }} />
  <Box style={{ height: 50, marginBottom: 12, backgroundColor: '#a6e3a1' }} />
  <Box style={{ height: 50, backgroundColor: '#89b4fa' }} />
</Box>

examples.title: Gap for uniform child spacing
examples.code:
<Box style={{ width: '100%', height: '100%', padding: 16, gap: 12 }}>
  <Box style={{ height: 50, backgroundColor: '#f38ba8' }} />
  <Box style={{ height: 50, backgroundColor: '#a6e3a1' }} />
  <Box style={{ height: 50, backgroundColor: '#89b4fa' }} />
</Box>

examples.title: Row with gap
examples.code:
<Box style={{
  flexDirection: 'row',
  width: '100%',
  gap: 8,
  padding: 16,
}}>
  <Box style={{ width: 80, height: 80, backgroundColor: '#f38ba8' }} />
  <Box style={{ width: 80, height: 80, backgroundColor: '#a6e3a1' }} />
  <Box style={{ width: 80, height: 80, backgroundColor: '#89b4fa' }} />
</Box>

criticalRules: Padding reduces the available content area inside an element
Margin does not collapse in Flexbox layout (unlike CSS block layout)
Gap is applied between children only, not before the first or after the last child
Per-side properties (paddingLeft, marginTop, etc.) override the shorthand for that side
Percentage spacing values are relative to the parent container's width (matching CSS behavior)
seeAlso: flexbox.txt -- Flexbox layout and gap usage
sizing.txt -- How sizing interacts with padding
visual.txt -- Border width also affects content area

metadata.title: Text-Specific Style Properties
metadata.description: Styling properties for the Text primitive, including font, color, alignment, and decoration.

sections.overview:
The Text primitive has its own set of style properties for controlling typography. The most important rule in ReactJIT is that every Text element MUST have an explicit fontSize in its style. This is not optional -- the layout engine needs fontSize to measure text and compute layout, and the linter will block builds that violate this rule.

Text styling covers font properties (size, family, weight), text layout (alignment, overflow, line height, letter spacing), text decoration (underline, strikethrough), and text shadow. Colors use the same Color type as visual styling (CSS strings or Love2D-style arrays).
sections.api:
Text style properties:

  color: Color
    Text color. Accepts CSS strings ("#cdd6f4") or Love2D arrays ([0.8, 0.84, 0.96]).

  fontSize: number (REQUIRED)
    Font size in pixels (Love2D/web) or character scale (grid targets).
    This property is MANDATORY on every Text element. The linter enforces this with the
    'no-text-without-fontsize' rule.

  fontFamily: string
    Font family name. On Love2D, this must be a font file available in the project.
    On web, it maps to CSS font-family.

  fontWeight: 'normal' | 'bold' | number
    Font weight. 'normal' is typically 400, 'bold' is 700. Numeric values (100-900)
    are supported on web and Love2D (if the font file supports weight variants).

  textAlign: 'left' | 'center' | 'right'
    Horizontal text alignment within the Text element's bounds.
    Default: 'left'

  textOverflow: 'clip' | 'ellipsis'
    How to handle text that overflows its container.
    'clip': text is cut off at the boundary.
    'ellipsis': text is truncated with "..." at the boundary.

  textDecorationLine: 'none' | 'underline' | 'line-through'
    Text decoration. 'underline' draws a line below the text, 'line-through' draws
    a line through the middle.

  lineHeight: number
    Line height in pixels. Controls the vertical spacing between lines of text.
    On web, this is converted to CSS line-height with 'px' units.

  letterSpacing: number
    Additional horizontal space between characters, in pixels.

  textShadowColor: Color
    Color of the text shadow. If not set, no text shadow is rendered.

  textShadowOffsetX: number
    Horizontal offset of the text shadow.

  textShadowOffsetY: number
    Vertical offset of the text shadow.

examples.title: Basic text with required fontSize
examples.code: <Text style={{ fontSize: 16, color: '#cdd6f4' }}>Hello, world</Text>

examples.title: Styled heading
examples.code:
<Text style={{
  fontSize: 32,
  fontWeight: 'bold',
  color: '#cba6f7',
  textAlign: 'center',
  letterSpacing: 2,
}}>
  Dashboard
</Text>

examples.title: Text with decoration
examples.code:
<Text style={{
  fontSize: 14,
  color: '#a6adc8',
  textDecorationLine: 'underline',
}}>
  Click here for details
</Text>

examples.title: Multiline text with lineHeight
examples.code:
<Text style={{
  fontSize: 14,
  color: '#cdd6f4',
  lineHeight: 22,
}}>
  {`This is a longer paragraph of text that demonstrates line height control. Each line has 22 pixels of vertical space.`}
</Text>

examples.title: Text overflow with ellipsis
examples.code:
<Box style={{ width: 200 }}>
  <Text style={{
    fontSize: 14,
    color: '#cdd6f4',
    textOverflow: 'ellipsis',
  }}>
    {`This is a very long string that will be truncated with an ellipsis`}
  </Text>
</Box>

examples.title: Text shadow
examples.code:
<Text style={{
  fontSize: 24,
  color: '#cdd6f4',
  textShadowColor: 'rgba(0, 0, 0, 0.5)',
  textShadowOffsetX: 2,
  textShadowOffsetY: 2,
}}>
  Shadow Text
</Text>

criticalRules: Every Text element MUST have explicit fontSize -- builds will fail without it (linter rule: no-text-without-fontsize)
Do NOT mix literal text and JSX expressions in Text children -- use template literals instead (linter rule: no-mixed-text-children)
WRONG: <Text style={{ fontSize: 14 }}>Score: {score}</Text>
RIGHT: <Text style={{ fontSize: 14 }}>{`Score: ${score}`}</Text>
Do NOT use the block character (U+2588) in Text elements -- it renders as a font glyph, not a filled pixel (linter rule: no-block-char-in-text)
Text nodes have intrinsic dimensions from font measurement -- they do not need explicit width/height in most cases
seeAlso: visual.txt -- Background and border styling for Box elements
flexbox.txt -- How text nodes participate in flex layout

metadata.title: Transform System
metadata.description: The transform property for translating, rotating, scaling, and skewing elements.

sections.overview:
ReactJIT supports a transform object on any element's style for applying geometric transformations. Transforms affect the visual rendering of the element without changing its position in the layout tree. A transformed element still occupies the same layout space it would without the transform -- sibling elements are not affected.

The transform system supports translation, rotation, scaling, and skewing. Transform origin defaults to the center of the element (0.5, 0.5) and can be adjusted with originX and originY values ranging from 0 (top-left) to 1 (bottom-right). Transforms are commonly combined with the animation system for motion effects.
sections.api:
Transform style property:

  transform: {
    translateX?: number     // Horizontal offset in pixels
    translateY?: number     // Vertical offset in pixels
    rotate?: number         // Rotation in degrees (clockwise)
    scaleX?: number         // Horizontal scale factor (1 = no change)
    scaleY?: number         // Vertical scale factor (1 = no change)
    skewX?: number          // Horizontal skew in degrees
    skewY?: number          // Vertical skew in degrees
    originX?: number        // Transform origin X, 0-1 (default: 0.5 = center)
    originY?: number        // Transform origin Y, 0-1 (default: 0.5 = center)
  }

All transform sub-properties are optional. Only the properties you specify are applied.

Transform origin:
  originX and originY are normalized values from 0 to 1, where:
    0, 0 = top-left corner
    0.5, 0.5 = center (default)
    1, 1 = bottom-right corner

examples.title: Simple translation
examples.code:
<Box style={{
  width: 100,
  height: 100,
  backgroundColor: '#89b4fa',
  transform: { translateX: 50, translateY: 20 },
}} />

examples.title: Rotation from center
examples.code:
<Box style={{
  width: 100,
  height: 100,
  backgroundColor: '#f38ba8',
  transform: { rotate: 45 },
}} />

examples.title: Rotation from top-left corner
examples.code:
<Box style={{
  width: 100,
  height: 100,
  backgroundColor: '#a6e3a1',
  transform: { rotate: 15, originX: 0, originY: 0 },
}} />

examples.title: Scale
examples.code:
<Box style={{
  width: 100,
  height: 100,
  backgroundColor: '#f9e2af',
  transform: { scaleX: 1.5, scaleY: 0.8 },
}} />

examples.title: Combined transforms
examples.code:
<Box style={{
  width: 100,
  height: 100,
  backgroundColor: '#cba6f7',
  transform: {
    translateX: 30,
    translateY: 10,
    rotate: 10,
    scaleX: 1.2,
    scaleY: 1.2,
  },
}} />

examples.title: Animated transform with useSpring

criticalRules: Transforms do NOT affect layout -- the element still occupies its original layout rectangle
Transform origin defaults to center (0.5, 0.5), not top-left
Rotation is specified in degrees, not radians
Scale values of 1.0 mean no change -- 2.0 doubles the size, 0.5 halves it
Transforms are visual-only and commonly used with the animation system for motion effects
OriginX/originY are 0-1 normalized, not pixel values

metadata.title: Visual Styling
metadata.description: Background colors, borders, border radius, shadows, gradients, opacity, and other visual properties.

sections.overview:
ReactJIT provides a comprehensive set of visual styling properties for Box elements. These include background colors, border styling (width, color, radius with per-corner and per-side control), box shadows, linear gradients, opacity, overflow clipping, z-index stacking, outlines, and visibility toggling.

Colors can be specified as CSS strings ("#ff0000", "rgba(255, 0, 0, 0.5)") or as Love2D-style number arrays [r, g, b, a] where each component is 0-1. The Color type accepts both formats and the renderer converts as needed for each target.
sections.api:
Color type:
  type Color = string | [number, number, number, number?]

  String format: "#ff0000", "rgba(255, 0, 0, 0.5)", "red"
  Array format: [1, 0, 0, 1] (Love2D-style, 0-1 range, alpha optional)

Background:

  backgroundColor: Color
    Fills the element background with a solid color.

  backgroundGradient: {
    direction: 'horizontal' | 'vertical' | 'diagonal',
    colors: [Color, Color]
  }
    Linear gradient background. Takes precedence over backgroundColor when set.
    'horizontal' goes left to right, 'vertical' top to bottom, 'diagonal' top-left to
    bottom-right.

Border:

  borderWidth: number
    Sets border width on all sides. Requires borderColor to be visible.

  borderColor: Color
    Color of the border. Only visible when borderWidth > 0.

  borderRadius: number
    Rounds all four corners uniformly.

  Per-side border width:
    borderTopWidth: number
    borderRightWidth: number
    borderBottomWidth: number
    borderLeftWidth: number

  Per-side border color:
    borderTopColor: Color
    borderRightColor: Color
    borderBottomColor: Color
    borderLeftColor: Color

  Per-corner border radius:
    borderTopLeftRadius: number
    borderTopRightRadius: number
    borderBottomLeftRadius: number
    borderBottomRightRadius: number

Shadow:

  shadowColor: Color
    Shadow color. If not set, no shadow is rendered.

  shadowOffsetX: number
    Horizontal shadow offset (default: 0).

  shadowOffsetY: number
    Vertical shadow offset (default: 0).

  shadowBlur: number
    Blur radius for the shadow (default: 0).

Outline:

  outlineColor: Color
    Color of the outline. Outlines render outside the element border.

  outlineWidth: number
    Width of the outline in pixels (default: 1 when outlineColor is set).

  outlineOffset: number
    Gap between the element border and the outline.

Stacking and visibility:

  opacity: number
    Element transparency, 0 (fully transparent) to 1 (fully opaque).

  overflow: 'visible' | 'hidden' | 'scroll'
    Controls whether child content that exceeds the element bounds is visible,
    clipped, or scrollable.

  zIndex: number
    Stacking order. Higher values render on top of lower values.

  visibility: 'visible' | 'hidden'
    Hides the element visually but it still occupies space in layout.
    Unlike display: 'none', hidden elements are not removed from the layout tree.

  display: 'flex' | 'none'
    Set to 'none' to completely remove from layout (no space occupied).

examples.title: Colored box with rounded corners
examples.code:
<Box style={{
  width: 200,
  height: 100,
  backgroundColor: '#89b4fa',
  borderRadius: 12,
}} />

examples.title: Box with border
examples.code:
<Box style={{
  width: 200,
  height: 100,
  borderWidth: 2,
  borderColor: '#cba6f7',
  borderRadius: 8,
}} />

examples.title: Per-corner border radius
examples.code:
<Box style={{
  width: 200,
  height: 100,
  backgroundColor: '#f38ba8',
  borderTopLeftRadius: 20,
  borderTopRightRadius: 20,
  borderBottomLeftRadius: 0,
  borderBottomRightRadius: 0,
}} />

examples.title: Box shadow
examples.code:
<Box style={{
  width: 200,
  height: 100,
  backgroundColor: '#cdd6f4',
  shadowColor: 'rgba(0, 0, 0, 0.3)',
  shadowOffsetX: 4,
  shadowOffsetY: 4,
  shadowBlur: 8,
  borderRadius: 8,
}} />

examples.title: Linear gradient
examples.code:
<Box style={{
  width: 300,
  height: 100,
  backgroundGradient: {
    direction: 'horizontal',
    colors: ['#f38ba8', '#89b4fa'],
  },
  borderRadius: 8,
}} />

examples.title: Opacity and z-index
examples.code:
<Box style={{ width: 200, height: 200, position: 'relative' }}>
  <Box style={{
    position: 'absolute',
    width: 150,
    height: 150,
    top: 0,
    left: 0,
    backgroundColor: '#f38ba8',
    zIndex: 1,
  }} />
  <Box style={{
    position: 'absolute',
    width: 150,
    height: 150,
    top: 50,
    left: 50,
    backgroundColor: '#89b4fa',
    opacity: 0.7,
    zIndex: 2,
  }} />
</Box>

criticalRules: borderWidth alone does not produce a visible border -- you must also set borderColor
backgroundGradient takes precedence over backgroundColor
overflow: 'hidden' clips children that extend beyond the element bounds
visibility: 'hidden' hides the element but it still occupies space (use display: 'none' to remove from layout)
Shadow rendering depends on the target platform (not available on all grid targets)
Colors can use either string format ("#ff0000") or array format ([1, 0, 0, 1])

metadata.title: AmbientLight
metadata.description: Uniform ambient illumination that prevents shadowed areas from going fully black

sections.overview: AmbientLight provides a base level of non-directional illumination for all meshes in a Scene. It ensures shadowed surfaces receive some light instead of rendering as pure black. AmbientLight has no position or direction — it contributes equally from all angles. Only one AmbientLight per Scene is supported. It renders nothing on web targets.
sections.api:
```tsx
import { AmbientLight } from '@reactjit/3d';
```

Props:
| Prop | Type | Default | Description |
|------|------|---------|-------------|
| color | string | '#1a1a2e' | Ambient light color as a hex string |
| intensity | number | 0.15 | Intensity multiplier applied to the color |

examples.title: Default ambient light for a dark space scene
Warm tinted ambient light for an interior scene
High ambient for a flat, evenly lit scene

criticalRules: AmbientLight renders nothing on web targets and returns null — it is Love2D only
Only one AmbientLight per Scene is supported — placing multiple will produce undefined behavior
AmbientLight must be a direct child of Scene, not nested inside other elements
intensity is a multiplier on the color — setting intensity to 0 effectively disables ambient lighting
AmbientLight alone without a DirectionalLight will cause all surfaces to be shaded with only the flat ambient color, losing all depth cues
seeAlso: Scene — the 3D viewport that AmbientLight must be placed inside
DirectionalLight — directional sun-like light that works alongside AmbientLight for full shading
Mesh — the geometry that receives ambient illumination
Camera — the viewpoint used to render the scene

metadata.title: AreaChart
metadata.description: Filled area chart; a LineChart preset with area fill enabled and dots disabled

sections.overview: AreaChart is a thin wrapper over LineChart with `showArea` forced to true, `showDots` forced to false, and `areaOpacity` defaulting to 0.4. It renders the same column-based layout as LineChart but emphasizes volume and trend through filled regions rather than point markers. All LineChart props are accepted and forwarded; any prop you pass overrides the preset defaults including re-enabling dots or disabling the area.
sections.api:
```tsx
import { AreaChart } from '@reactjit/core';
```

AreaChart accepts exactly the same props as LineChart:

Data shape:
```tsx
interface LineChartPoint {
  x?:    string;  // Optional x-axis label shown in the hover tooltip
  value: number;  // Y-axis value; min and max across the dataset define the scale
}
```

Props (inherited from LineChart):
| Prop          | Type                                                              | Default (AreaChart) | Description                                                           |
|---------------|-------------------------------------------------------------------|---------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------|
| data          | LineChartPoint[]                                                  | required            | Array of data points to plot                                          |
| width         | number                                                            | 280                 | Total chart width in pixels                                           |
| height        | number                                                            | 120                 | Total chart height in pixels                                          |
| color         | Color                                                             | '#3b82f6'           | Color used for the area fill                                          |
| showDots      | boolean                                                           | false               | Preset to false; pass true to re-enable dots                          |
| showArea      | boolean                                                           | true                | Preset to true; pass false to produce a dotless line chart            |
| areaOpacity   | number                                                            | 0.4                 | Opacity of the area fill (0–1)                                        |
| interactive   | boolean                                                           | false               | Enable hover crosshair and per-point tooltips                         |
| onPointHover  | (index: number \| null, point: LineChartPoint \| null) => void    | undefined           | Called when the pointer enters or leaves a column                     |
| onPointPress  | (index: number, point: LineChartPoint) => void                    | undefined           | Called when a column is clicked (requires interactive: true)          |
| style         | Style                                                             | undefined           | Additional styles applied to the outer container Box                  |

examples.title: Traffic volume over a week
Stacked feel with reduced opacity and custom color
Interactive area chart with dots re-enabled

criticalRules: AreaChart is not an independent component — it renders a LineChart internally. Every constraint that applies to LineChart applies here.

Props you pass to AreaChart override the presets. Passing `showArea={false}` produces a dotless, area-less chart (effectively invisible). Passing `showDots={true}` adds dots back on top of the area fill.
The vertical scale is relative to the dataset, not zero-based. A dataset with values [100, 110, 105] will show strong variation even though the values are close together.
`onPointPress` requires `interactive={true}` to fire.
Do not use Unicode symbols in the `x` label strings of data points.
seeAlso: LineChart — the underlying component; use it directly when you need dots-only or full control
Sparkline — a simpler no-configuration trend widget for embedding in text-dense layouts
BarChart — vertical bars for discrete comparison rather than trend display
ChartTooltip — standalone tooltip primitive used internally by the underlying LineChart

metadata.title: Badge
metadata.description: A small colored label/pill component for displaying status, categories, tags, or counts. Supports five semantic variants with pre-defined color schemes.

sections.overview: The Badge component renders a compact, pill-shaped label with semantic color variants. It automatically sizes to fit the label text and uses `alignSelf: 'start'` to prevent unwanted stretching in flex layouts. Badges are ideal for status indicators, category tags, notification counts, or any small textual label that needs visual emphasis. The component provides five built-in variants with carefully chosen background and text color pairings optimized for readability.
sections.api:
```tsx
import { Badge } from '@reactjit/core';

<Badge
  label="Active"
  variant="success"
  style={{ marginLeft: 8 }}
/>
```

| Prop | Type | Default | Description |
|------|------|---------|-------------|
| `label` | `string` | (required) | Text content displayed inside the badge |
| `variant` | `BadgeVariant` | `'default'` | Color scheme: `'default'`, `'success'`, `'warning'`, `'error'`, `'info'` |
| `style` | `Style` | `{}` | Overrides for the badge container (margins, explicit dimensions, etc.) |

**Variant Color Schemes:**

| Variant | Background | Text | Use Case |
|---------|-----------|------|----------|
| `default` | `#334155` (slate-700) | `#e2e8f0` (slate-200) | Neutral labels, defaults |
| `success` | `#166534` (green-800) | `#bbf7d0` (green-200) | Active, online, completed, success |
| `warning` | `#854d0e` (yellow-800) | `#fef08a` (yellow-200) | Caution, pending, warnings |
| `error` | `#991b1b` (red-800) | `#fecaca` (red-200) | Error, failed, critical, offline |
| `info` | `#1e40af` (blue-800) | `#bfdbfe` (blue-200) | Information, tips, metadata |

**Default Styling:**
- Border radius: 10px (pill shape)
- Padding: 2px vertical, 8px horizontal
- Font size: 11px
- Font weight: bold
- Alignment: `alignSelf: 'start'` (no stretch)

examples.title: Basic Badge
examples.code:
```tsx
<Badge label="New" variant="info" />
```

examples.title: Status Indicators
examples.code:
```tsx
<Box style={{ gap: 8 }}>
  <Badge label="Online" variant="success" />
  <Badge label="Offline" variant="error" />
  <Badge label="Away" variant="warning" />
  <Badge label="Idle" variant="default" />
</Box>
```

examples.title: Badge with Text Label (Row Layout)
examples.code:
```tsx
<Box style={{ flexDirection: 'row', gap: 8, alignItems: 'center' }}>
  <Text fontSize={14} style={{ color: '#e2e8f0' }}>Server Status:</Text>
  <Badge label="Running" variant="success" />
</Box>
```

examples.title: Notification Count
examples.code:
```tsx
<Box style={{ flexDirection: 'row', gap: 8, alignItems: 'center' }}>
  <Text fontSize={14} style={{ color: '#e2e8f0' }}>Messages</Text>
  <Badge label="12" variant="error" />
</Box>
```

examples.title: Category Tags
examples.code:
```tsx
<Box style={{ flexDirection: 'row', gap: 8, flexWrap: 'wrap' }}>
  <Badge label="React" variant="info" />
  <Badge label="TypeScript" variant="info" />
  <Badge label="Lua" variant="info" />
  <Badge label="Love2D" variant="success" />
</Box>
```

examples.title: Badges Inside Cards
examples.code:
```tsx
<Card title="System Services">
  <Box style={{ gap: 8 }}>
    <Box style={{ flexDirection: 'row', gap: 8, alignItems: 'center' }}>
      <Text fontSize={12} style={{ color: '#e2e8f0' }}>Web Server</Text>
      <Badge label="Active" variant="success" />
    </Box>
    <Box style={{ flexDirection: 'row', gap: 8, alignItems: 'center' }}>
      <Text fontSize={12} style={{ color: '#e2e8f0' }}>Database</Text>
      <Badge label="Error" variant="error" />
    </Box>
    <Box style={{ flexDirection: 'row', gap: 8, alignItems: 'center' }}>
      <Text fontSize={12} style={{ color: '#e2e8f0' }}>Cache</Text>
      <Badge label="Pending" variant="warning" />
    </Box>
  </Box>
</Card>
```

examples.title: Custom Styling
examples.code:
```tsx
<Badge
  label="Custom"
  variant="info"
  style={{
    marginLeft: 16,
    borderWidth: 1,
    borderColor: '#60a5fa',
  }}
/>
```

examples.title: Dynamic Variant Based on State

criticalRules: Do not use Unicode symbols in the `label` prop (see layout rules)
Badge auto-sizes to label text width (no explicit width needed)
Uses `alignSelf: 'start'` by default to prevent stretching in flex containers
For horizontal alignment with text, wrap in a Box with `flexDirection: 'row'` and `alignItems: 'center'`
The internal Text component uses `fontSize: 11` and `fontWeight: 'bold'` (not customizable without forking)
Variant colors are hardcoded; for custom colors, fork Badge or use a styled Box + Text
Badge has no onClick handler; wrap in Pressable for interactivity
seeAlso: Card component for containing badges in structured layouts
Box primitive for custom badge designs
Text styling rules (fontSize requirement, no Unicode symbols)
FlexRow helper for horizontal badge + text layouts

metadata.title: BarChart
metadata.description: Vertical bar chart that renders data as proportionally sized colored bars with optional labels, values, and interactive hover tooltips

sections.overview:
BarChart renders a vertical bar chart from an array of data points. Each bar's height is proportional to its value relative to the maximum value in the dataset. Bars can have individual colors or share a default color.

The component renders entirely with Box and Text primitives, so it works on all targets. An optional label row appears below the bars, and values can be shown above each bar. When barWidth is omitted, bars auto-size to fill the parent container evenly using flexGrow. When interactive mode is enabled, hovering a bar dims all other bars and reveals a ChartTooltip showing the label, raw value, and percentage of the maximum.
sections.api:
```tsx
import { BarChart } from '@reactjit/core';
```

Data shape:
```tsx
interface BarChartBar {
  label: string;  // Bar label shown below the chart
  value: number;  // Numeric value driving bar height
  color?: Color;  // Per-bar color override; falls back to the chart-level `color` prop
}
```

Props:
| Prop        | Type                                                        | Default    | Description                                                      |
|-------------|-------------------------------------------------------------|------------|------------------------------------------------------------------|
| data        | BarChartBar[]                                               | required   | Array of bar data points                                         |
| height      | number                                                      | 120        | Chart area height in pixels                                      |
| barWidth    | number                                                      | undefined  | Fixed width per bar in pixels; omit for auto-sizing via flexGrow |
| gap         | number                                                      | 8          | Horizontal gap between bars in pixels                            |
| showLabels  | boolean                                                     | true       | Show label text below each bar                                   |
| showValues  | boolean                                                     | false      | Show numeric value above each bar                                |
| color       | Color                                                       | '#3b82f6'  | Default bar color; overridden per bar via BarChartBar.color      |
| style       | Style                                                       | undefined  | Additional styles applied to the outer container Box             |
| interactive | boolean                                                     | false      | Enable hover highlighting and per-bar ChartTooltip               |
| onBarHover  | (index: number \| null, bar: BarChartBar \| null) => void   | undefined  | Called when the pointer enters or leaves a bar                   |
| onBarPress  | (index: number, bar: BarChartBar) => void                   | undefined  | Called when a bar is clicked (requires interactive: true)        |

examples.title: Basic bar chart with auto-sizing
Custom colors per bar with values displayed
Interactive chart with hover callback

criticalRules: The `data` prop is required. An empty array renders an empty placeholder Box at the specified height.
Bar heights are relative to the maximum value in the data. If all values are equal, all bars render at full height.
If `maxValue` is 0 (all values are 0), bars are normalized against 1, producing minimum-height (1px) bars.
Per-bar `color` overrides the chart-level `color` prop for that specific bar only.
BarChart does not handle negative values. All values should be non-negative.
When `showValues` is true, 14px of vertical space is reserved above bars for value labels — factor this into the `height` prop.
When `barWidth` is omitted, bars use `flexGrow: 1` and fill the parent container. The parent must have an explicit width for this to work.
When `barWidth` is set, the chart computes its own total width as `data.length * barWidth + (data.length - 1) * gap`.
`onBarPress` only fires when `interactive` is also `true`.
Hover events require pointer support — not available on all targets. Check platform capabilities before enabling `interactive`.
seeAlso: HorizontalBarChart — horizontal orientation for the same kind of comparison
StackedBarChart — multi-series bars stacked in a single column
Sparkline — compact inline trend bars with no axes or labels
ChartTooltip — standalone tooltip primitive used internally by this component

metadata.title: Box
metadata.description: Fundamental flexbox layout container for all visual structure

sections.overview:
Box is the fundamental layout primitive in ReactJIT. Every visual structure is built from nested Box elements. It renders as a flexbox container with configurable direction, alignment, spacing, and visual styling. In web mode it outputs a `<div>` with CSS flexbox; in native mode it outputs a `'View'` host element processed by the Lua layout engine.

Box also serves as the primary event target. It accepts handlers for pointer, keyboard, touch, gamepad, and drag interactions. For higher-level interaction patterns (press state tracking, long press, hover callbacks), use Pressable instead, which wraps Box internally.
sections.api:
```tsx
import { Box } from '@reactjit/core';
```

```tsx
<Box
  style={{ width: '100%', height: '100%', flexDirection: 'column', backgroundColor: '#1e1e2e' }}
  hoverStyle={{ backgroundColor: '#313244' }}
  activeStyle={{ backgroundColor: '#45475a' }}
  onClick={(event) => { /* handle click */ }}
>
  {children}
</Box>
```

Props:
| Prop                | Type                                       | Default   | Description                                            |
|---------------------|--------------------------------------------|-----------|--------------------------------------------------------|
| style               | Style                                      | undefined | Flexbox layout and visual styling                      |
| hoverStyle          | Style                                      | undefined | Style merged on pointer hover                          |
| activeStyle         | Style                                      | undefined | Style merged on press/active                           |
| backgroundVideo     | string                                     | undefined | Path to a video file to play as background             |
| backgroundVideoFit  | 'fill' \| 'contain' \| 'cover'             | undefined | How the background video fits the box                  |
| hoverVideo          | string                                     | undefined | Video to play on hover (swaps backgroundVideo)         |
| hoverVideoFit       | 'fill' \| 'contain' \| 'cover'             | undefined | Fit mode for the hover video                           |
