| focusable           | boolean                                    | undefined | Make this box focusable (receives keyboard events)     |
| focusGroup          | boolean                                    | undefined | Make this box a focus group (arrow-key navigation)     |
| fileDropMode        | 'upload' \| 'preview'                      | undefined | How file drops are handled — 'upload' for metadata only, 'preview' reads text content |
| onClick             | (event: LoveEvent) => void                 | undefined | Click/press handler                                    |
| onRelease           | (event: LoveEvent) => void                 | undefined | Release handler                                        |
| onPointerEnter      | (event: LoveEvent) => void                 | undefined | Pointer enters the box                                 |
| onPointerLeave      | (event: LoveEvent) => void                 | undefined | Pointer leaves the box                                 |
| onKeyDown           | (event: LoveEvent) => void                 | undefined | Key pressed while focused                              |
| onKeyUp             | (event: LoveEvent) => void                 | undefined | Key released while focused                             |
| onTextInput         | (event: LoveEvent) => void                 | undefined | Text input while focused                               |
| onWheel             | (event: LoveEvent) => void                 | undefined | Mouse wheel / scroll wheel                             |
| onTouchStart        | (event: LoveEvent) => void                 | undefined | Touch began                                            |
| onTouchEnd          | (event: LoveEvent) => void                 | undefined | Touch ended                                            |
| onTouchMove         | (event: LoveEvent) => void                 | undefined | Touch moved                                            |
| onGamepadPress      | (event: LoveEvent) => void                 | undefined | Gamepad button pressed                                 |
| onGamepadRelease    | (event: LoveEvent) => void                 | undefined | Gamepad button released                                |
| onGamepadAxis       | (event: LoveEvent) => void                 | undefined | Gamepad axis moved                                     |
| onMidiNote          | (event: LoveEvent) => void                 | undefined | MIDI note on/off event                                 |
| onMidiCC            | (event: LoveEvent) => void                 | undefined | MIDI control change event                              |
| onDragStart         | (event: LoveEvent) => void                 | undefined | Drag gesture began                                     |
| onDrag              | (event: LoveEvent) => void                 | undefined | Drag gesture moved                                     |
| onDragEnd           | (event: LoveEvent) => void                 | undefined | Drag gesture ended                                     |
| onFileDrop          | (event: LoveEvent) => void                 | undefined | File dropped on the box                                |
| onDirectoryDrop     | (event: LoveEvent) => void                 | undefined | Directory dropped on the box                           |
| onFileDragEnter     | (event: LoveEvent) => void                 | undefined | File drag enters the box                               |
| onFileDragLeave     | (event: LoveEvent) => void                 | undefined | File drag leaves the box                               |
| onFocus             | (event: LoveEvent) => void                 | undefined | Box gained focus                                       |
| onBlur              | (event: LoveEvent) => void                 | undefined | Box lost focus                                         |
| onLayout            | (event: LayoutEvent) => void               | undefined | Fires after layout with computed x, y, width, height   |
| children            | ReactNode                                  | undefined | Child elements                                         |

LayoutEvent fields:
| Field    | Type   | Description                              |
|----------|--------|------------------------------------------|
| targetId | number | Internal node ID                         |
| x        | number | Computed x position in parent            |
| y        | number | Computed y position in parent            |
| width    | number | Computed width after layout              |
| height   | number | Computed height after layout             |

The `style` prop accepts the full Style type, which includes flexbox properties (flexDirection, justifyContent, alignItems, flexGrow, flexShrink, flexBasis, flexWrap, gap), sizing (width, height, minWidth, minHeight, maxWidth, maxHeight, aspectRatio), spacing (padding*, margin*), visual (backgroundColor, borderRadius, borderWidth, borderColor, opacity, overflow, boxShadow), positioning (position, top/right/bottom/left, zIndex), gradients, transforms, transitions, and animations.

Default flexDirection is 'column'. Default display is 'flex'.

examples.title: Root container filling the viewport
examples.code:
```tsx
<Box style={{ width: '100%', height: '100%', backgroundColor: '#1e1e2e' }}>
  <Text style={{ fontSize: 24, color: '#cdd6f4' }}>Hello</Text>
</Box>
```

examples.title: Horizontal row with centered, spaced children
examples.code:
```tsx
<Box style={{
  width: '100%',
  flexDirection: 'row',
  justifyContent: 'space-between',
  alignItems: 'center',
  padding: 16,
}}>
  <Text style={{ fontSize: 14, color: '#cdd6f4' }}>Left</Text>
  <Text style={{ fontSize: 14, color: '#cdd6f4' }}>Right</Text>
</Box>
```

examples.title: Interactive box with hover and active states
Drag-enabled box

platformNotes.Love2D: Renders as a 'View' host element. Layout computed by layout.lua (yoga-like flexbox). Visual rendering by painter.lua. Supports all style properties including gradients, transforms, box shadows, and per-corner border radii. The Lua painter handles hover/active style merging on the native side for smooth interaction without JS round-trips.
platformNotes.Web: Renders as a <div> with CSS flexbox. Style is converted via styleToCSS(). The web renderer uses React state for hover/active style merging. Drag handlers use pointer capture for reliable cross-element dragging. Keyboard handlers automatically set tabIndex=0 for focus.
platformNotes.Terminal: Grid targets use <view> lowercase intrinsic, not the Box component. Layout is handled by @reactjit/grid.

criticalRules: Root containers MUST have width: '100%' and height: '100%'. Using flexGrow: 1 on the root will produce a zero-height container because there is no parent with a known size to grow into.

Row-direction Boxes (flexDirection: 'row') MUST have an explicit width for justifyContent to work. Box nodes have no intrinsic width (only Text nodes do), so without a width the container collapses and justifyContent has nothing to distribute.
Do not use flexGrow without sibling sizing context. flexGrow only works when the parent has a known size and there are siblings that establish the remaining space.
Default flexDirection is 'column'. If you expect horizontal layout, set flexDirection: 'row' explicitly.
Keep flex trees shallow. Prefer a single Box with flexDirection: 'row' over multiple nested wrapper Boxes. Deep hierarchies slow down layout computation.
Love2D is a fixed canvas, not a scrolling page. Fill the viewport; do not leave empty space expecting it to scroll.

metadata.title: Breadcrumbs
metadata.description: Navigation breadcrumb trail with clickable path segments and configurable separator.

sections.overview:
Breadcrumbs renders a horizontal trail of navigation items separated by a configurable separator character. Each item except the last is clickable via Pressable and triggers the `onSelect` callback with the item's id. The last item in the trail is displayed as bold text, indicating the current location.

The component is built with Box, Text, and Pressable primitives, making it work identically across all targets.

examples.title: Basic breadcrumbs
Custom separator
examples.code:
```tsx
<Breadcrumbs
  items={path}
  onSelect={navigateTo}
  separator=">"
/>
```

examples.title: Single item (current page only)
examples.code:
```tsx
<Breadcrumbs items={[{ id: 'home', label: 'Home' }]} />
```

criticalRules: The `items` prop is required. An empty array renders nothing.
The last item in the array is always rendered as non-clickable bold text. It does not trigger `onSelect`.
Only non-last items are wrapped in Pressable. The last item is a plain Text element.
Breadcrumbs renders in a row layout. Ensure the parent container has enough width for the trail, or items will wrap depending on the parent's flexWrap setting.

metadata.title: Camera
metadata.description: Defines the viewpoint for a 3D Scene

sections.overview: Camera sets the perspective viewpoint used when rendering a Scene. Place exactly one Camera as a direct child of Scene. All props are reactive — changing position, lookAt, or fov causes the camera to update each frame without full reconciliation overhead. If no Camera is present in a Scene, a default camera at position [0, -3, 2] looking at the origin is used automatically.
sections.api:
```tsx
import { Camera } from '@reactjit/3d';
```

Props:
| Prop | Type | Default | Description |
|------|------|---------|-------------|
| position | [number, number, number] | [0, -3, 2] | Camera position in world space as [x, y, z] |
| lookAt | [number, number, number] | [0, 0, 0] | The point in world space the camera looks at as [x, y, z] |
| fov | number | Math.PI / 3 | Field of view in radians (Math.PI / 3 = 60 degrees) |
| near | number | 0.01 | Near clipping plane distance — geometry closer than this is not rendered |
| far | number | 1000 | Far clipping plane distance — geometry beyond this is not rendered |

examples.title: Basic camera looking at the origin
Animated camera orbiting the scene
Narrow field of view for a telephoto effect

criticalRules: Place exactly one Camera per Scene — multiple Camera children in the same Scene produce undefined behavior
Camera renders nothing on web targets and returns null; it is Love2D only
The position and lookAt props use a right-handed coordinate system where Z is up
fov is in radians, not degrees — use Math.PI / 3 for 60 degrees, Math.PI / 4 for 45 degrees
Omitting Camera entirely is valid; the default camera at [0, -3, 2] will be used
seeAlso: Scene — the 3D viewport that Camera must be placed inside
Mesh — renderable geometry that Camera looks at
DirectionalLight — sun-like light that interacts with mesh shading
AmbientLight — base illumination applied across all meshes

metadata.title: Card
metadata.description: A styled container component with optional header, title, subtitle, and body sections. Provides a consistent visual structure for grouping related content with automatic borders, padding, and spacing.

sections.overview: The Card component is a pre-styled container that provides a visual boundary around related content. It automatically includes dark theme styling (slate colors), rounded corners, borders, and proper padding. Cards can optionally display a header section with a title and subtitle, separated from the body content by a horizontal divider. The component auto-sizes to its content unless explicit dimensions are provided via the `style` prop.
sections.api:
```tsx
import { Card } from '@reactjit/core';

<Card
  title="Optional Title"
  subtitle="Optional Subtitle"
  style={{ width: 300 }}
  headerStyle={{ backgroundColor: '#2d3748' }}
  bodyStyle={{ gap: 8 }}
>
  {children}
</Card>
```

| Prop | Type | Default | Description |
|------|------|---------|-------------|
| `children` | `React.ReactNode` | (required) | Content rendered in the card body section |
| `title` | `string` | `undefined` | Optional title displayed in the header (bold, 14px, light gray) |
| `subtitle` | `string` | `undefined` | Optional subtitle below title (11px, muted gray) |
| `style` | `Style` | `{}` | Overrides for the outer card container (dimensions, margins, etc.) |
| `headerStyle` | `Style` | `{}` | Overrides for the header section (padding, background, borders) |
| `bodyStyle` | `Style` | `{}` | Overrides for the body section (padding, gap, alignment) |

**Default Styling:**
- Background: `#1e293b` (slate-800)
- Border: 1px `#334155` (slate-700)
- Border radius: 8px
- Header padding: 12px
- Body padding: 12px
- Header border-bottom: 1px when title or subtitle present

examples.title: Basic Card with Title
examples.code:
```tsx
<Card title="System Status">
  <Text fontSize={14} style={{ color: '#e2e8f0' }}>
    All systems operational
  </Text>
</Card>
```

examples.title: Card with Title and Subtitle
examples.code:
```tsx
<Card
  title="Player Stats"
  subtitle="Last updated 2 minutes ago"
>
  <Box style={{ gap: 4 }}>
    <Text fontSize={12} style={{ color: '#e2e8f0' }}>Health: 100/100</Text>
    <Text fontSize={12} style={{ color: '#e2e8f0' }}>Mana: 85/100</Text>
  </Box>
</Card>
```

examples.title: Card with No Header
examples.code:
```tsx
<Card>
  <Text fontSize={14} style={{ color: '#e2e8f0' }}>
    This card has no header section.
    Use it for simple content grouping.
  </Text>
</Card>
```

examples.title: Custom Dimensions and Styling
examples.code:
```tsx
<Card
  title="Fixed Width Card"
  style={{ width: 400, height: 200 }}
  headerStyle={{ backgroundColor: '#2d3748', padding: 16 }}
  bodyStyle={{ gap: 12, padding: 16 }}
>
  <Text fontSize={14} style={{ color: '#e2e8f0' }}>
    Custom header background and increased padding.
  </Text>
</Card>
```

examples.title: Dashboard Card Grid
examples.code:
```tsx
<Box style={{ flexDirection: 'row', gap: 16, flexWrap: 'wrap' }}>
  <Card title="CPU Usage" style={{ width: 200 }}>
    <Text fontSize={24} style={{ color: '#22c55e' }}>42%</Text>
  </Card>
  <Card title="Memory" style={{ width: 200 }}>
    <Text fontSize={24} style={{ color: '#eab308' }}>67%</Text>
  </Card>
  <Card title="Disk" style={{ width: 200 }}>
    <Text fontSize={24} style={{ color: '#ef4444' }}>89%</Text>
  </Card>
</Box>
```

examples.title: Card with Badge
examples.code:
```tsx
<Card
  title="Task Manager"
  subtitle="Active processes"
>
  <Box style={{ gap: 8 }}>
    <Box style={{ flexDirection: 'row', gap: 8, alignItems: 'center' }}>
      <Text fontSize={12} style={{ color: '#e2e8f0' }}>Server</Text>
      <Badge label="Running" variant="success" />
    </Box>
    <Box style={{ flexDirection: 'row', gap: 8, alignItems: 'center' }}>
      <Text fontSize={12} style={{ color: '#e2e8f0' }}>Database</Text>
      <Badge label="Error" variant="error" />
    </Box>
  </Box>
</Card>
```

criticalRules: All `<Text>` children inside Card must have explicit `fontSize` (linter enforces this)
Card auto-sizes to content unless explicit `width`/`height` provided in `style`
Header section only renders if `title` or `subtitle` is provided
Do not use Unicode symbols in Text children (see layout rules)
For full-viewport cards, use `style={{ width: '100%', height: '100%' }}`
Use `bodyStyle={{ gap: X }}` instead of manual spacing between children
`headerStyle` and `bodyStyle` merge with defaults (use to override padding, colors, etc.)
Card uses `overflow: 'hidden'` to clip content at rounded corners
seeAlso: Badge component for status indicators inside cards
Box primitive for custom containers without styling
FlexRow/FlexCol helpers for card body layouts

metadata.title: ChartTooltip
metadata.description: Positioned overlay tooltip with typed sub-components for use inside chart elements

sections.overview: ChartTooltip is a compound component that renders a dark-themed floating overlay anchored to any of the four cardinal sides of its parent. When `visible` is false the component returns null, making it safe to always declare in the render tree and control via state. Three typed sub-components — ChartTooltip.Label, ChartTooltip.Value, and ChartTooltip.Detail — provide pre-styled text tiers: a bold blue label for the category name, a large white value for the primary number, and a small muted detail line for secondary information.
sections.api:
```tsx
import { ChartTooltip } from '@reactjit/core';
```

Props (ChartTooltip):
| Prop     | Type                              | Default   | Description                                                                            |
|----------|-----------------------------------|-----------|----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| visible  | boolean                           | required  | Controls whether the tooltip is rendered; false returns null                           |
| anchor   | 'top' \| 'right' \| 'bottom' \| 'left' | 'top' | Which side of the parent the tooltip floats toward                               |
| children | ReactNode                         | required  | Content to render inside the tooltip box; use the sub-components below                |

Sub-components (no props beyond children):
| Sub-component           | Text style                                    | Intended use                        |
|-------------------------|-----------------------------------------------|-------------------------------------|
| ChartTooltip.Label      | color: #61a6fa, fontSize: 10, fontWeight: bold | Category name or x-axis label       |
| ChartTooltip.Value      | color: #e1e4f0, fontSize: 12, fontWeight: bold | Primary numeric value               |
| ChartTooltip.Detail     | color: #8892a6, fontSize: 9               | Secondary info (percentage, series) |

Anchor positioning:
| Anchor | Position                          | Offset   |
|--------|-----------------------------------|----------|
| top    | bottom: '100%', alignSelf: center | 4px gap  |
| bottom | top: '100%', alignSelf: center    | 4px gap  |
| right  | left: '100%', top: 0             | 4px gap  |
| left   | right: '100%', top: 0            | 4px gap  |

examples.title: Basic tooltip inside an interactive bar
Right-anchored tooltip for a horizontal bar row
Dynamic tooltip driven by external state

criticalRules: The parent Box must have `position: 'relative'` set for the tooltip's absolute positioning to work correctly. Without it the tooltip will escape to the nearest positioned ancestor.
ChartTooltip uses `position: 'absolute'` and `zIndex: 10`. If neighboring elements have higher z-index values, the tooltip may render beneath them.
When `visible` is false, ChartTooltip returns null entirely — it does not render an invisible placeholder. This means no hit area or layout space is reserved for it.

The `anchor` prop positions the tooltip relative to the parent element boundaries, not relative to the pointer position. Use `top` for bars (tooltip floats above), `right` for horizontal bars (tooltip floats to the right of the bar end).
ChartTooltip.Label, ChartTooltip.Value, and ChartTooltip.Detail are plain Text wrappers with fixed styles. They do not accept style overrides — they are not general-purpose text components.
You may pass any ReactNode as children, including custom Box layouts, but the built-in sub-components provide consistent chart-wide styling.
Do not use Unicode symbols in any text passed to the sub-components.
seeAlso: HorizontalBarChart — uses ChartTooltip internally with anchor="right"
StackedBarChart — uses ChartTooltip internally with anchor="top"
LineChart — uses ChartTooltip internally with anchor="top"

metadata.title: Checkbox
metadata.description: A toggleable checkbox with optional label, supporting controlled and uncontrolled modes.

sections.overview:
Checkbox is a square toggle control that displays a filled inner square when checked. It optionally renders a text label to the right of the checkbox.

Checkbox supports both controlled and uncontrolled modes. Pass `value` for controlled behavior, or use `defaultValue` for uncontrolled. The checkbox box border color changes between `color` (checked) and `uncheckedColor` (unchecked), and the box fills with `color` when checked with a white inner indicator.
sections.api:
Import: import { Checkbox } from '@reactjit/core';

Usage:
```tsx
<Checkbox
  value={accepted}
  onValueChange={(v) => setAccepted(v)}
  label="Accept terms"
/>
```

Props:

| Prop           | Type                       | Default    | Description                              |
|----------------|----------------------------|------------|------------------------------------------|
| value          | boolean                    | -          | Controlled value. Omit for uncontrolled  |
| defaultValue   | boolean                    | false      | Initial value in uncontrolled mode       |
| onValueChange  | (value: boolean) => void   | -          | Called when the checkbox is toggled       |
| disabled       | boolean                    | false      | Disables interaction and dims opacity    |
| label          | string                     | -          | Text label rendered beside the checkbox  |
| size           | number                     | 20         | Checkbox box size in pixels              |
| color          | Color                      | '#3b82f6'  | Border and fill color when checked       |
| uncheckedColor | Color                      | '#6b7280'  | Border color when unchecked              |
| style          | Style                      | -          | Container style overrides                |

examples.title: Basic controlled checkbox with label
Uncontrolled with custom colors
examples.code:
```tsx
<Checkbox
  defaultValue={true}
  color="#22c55e"
  uncheckedColor="#94a3b8"
  size={24}
  label="Remember me"
/>
```

examples.title: Disabled checkbox
examples.code:
```tsx
<Checkbox value={false} disabled label="Unavailable option" />
```

criticalRules: The `size` prop controls only the checkbox square, not the label text size.
When `disabled` is true, opacity is set to 0.5 and click events are ignored.
In controlled mode, the visual state does not change unless the parent updates `value` in response to `onValueChange`.

metadata.title: CodeBlock
metadata.description: Syntax-highlighted code display with support for multiple languages

sections.overview:
CodeBlock displays source code with syntax highlighting. On Love2D, it's a Lua-owned primitive that renders directly via love.graphics, giving precise control over colors, fonts, and line spacing. On web, it renders as a <pre> element with monospace font.

Use CodeBlock for documentation, tutorials, error messages, REPLs, or any read-only code display. For editable code, use TextEditor instead.
sections.api:
<CodeBlock
  code={string}
  language?={string}
  fontSize?={number}
  style?={Style}
/>

code
  Required. The source code string to display.
  Supports multi-line code with \n line breaks.

language
  Optional. Language hint for syntax highlighting.
  Values: "auto" (default), "js", "jsx", "ts", "tsx", "lua", "py", "json", etc.
  "auto" attempts to detect; explicit language is more reliable.

fontSize
  Optional. Font size in pixels. Default: 10.

style
  Optional. Additional Style properties (backgroundColor, color, padding, etc.).
  On Love2D, these override the default theme.
  On web, these apply as CSS overrides.

examples.title: Basic Code Display
Custom Styling
Lua Code with Highlighting
Error Stack Trace

platformNotes.Love2D:
CodeBlock is a Lua-owned element (like TextEditor). Rendering is done directly
via love.graphics, not through React layout. Dimensions are computed based on
text metrics and padding. Multi-line code is supported. Line wrapping is not
automatic; long lines may extend off-screen.
platformNotes.Web:
CodeBlock renders as an HTML <pre> element with monospace font. Overflow is
scrollable (overflow: auto). Syntax highlighting is applied via CSS classes
(for server-side highlighting) or client-side JavaScript (if integrated).

sections.performance: CodeBlock rendering on Love2D is re-computed each frame. For very large code blocks (5000+ lines), consider splitting into multiple smaller blocks or lazy-loading visible sections. Web rendering is CSS-based and performant.
criticalRules: Do not use CodeBlock for user input; use TextEditor instead
CodeBlock on Love2D is not scrollable within its bounds; wrap in ScrollView for long code
Language hints are optional but recommended for accurate highlighting
Code strings must be raw strings (no special escaping needed for \n)
fontSize should be chosen to keep code readable; default 10 is often too small for complex code
seeAlso: texteditor.txt - Editable text with line numbers

metadata.title: ContextMenu
metadata.description: Right-click context menu with built-in text operations and custom actions

sections.overview: ContextMenu is a right-click context menu component that provides built-in text operations (Copy, Inspect) and supports custom menu items. It follows the "inverse pattern" where Lua handles all interaction state (rendering, hover, keyboard navigation) and JS receives only boundary events (open, close, select). On Love2D, the menu is rendered entirely by Lua; on web, it renders as a positioned dropdown overlay. The menu automatically detects text nodes under the cursor and enables/disables the Copy action based on active text selection.
sections.api:
```tsx
import { ContextMenu } from '@reactjit/core';

<ContextMenu
  items={[...]}
  onSelect={(event) => {...}}
  onOpen={() => {...}}
  onClose={() => {...}}
>
  {children}
</ContextMenu>
```

| Prop | Type | Default | Description |
|------|------|---------|-------------|
| `items` | `ContextMenuItem[]` | `undefined` | Custom menu items to append after built-in items |
| `onSelect` | `(event: ContextMenuEvent) => void` | `undefined` | Called when any menu item is selected (built-in or custom) |
| `onOpen` | `() => void` | `undefined` | Called when the context menu opens |
| `onClose` | `() => void` | `undefined` | Called when the context menu closes (click outside, Escape, or item selected) |
| `children` | `React.ReactNode` | `undefined` | Child elements that will trigger the context menu on right-click |

**ContextMenuItem interface:**
```tsx
interface ContextMenuItem {
  label: string;        // Display text
  action: string;       // Action identifier dispatched on selection
  disabled?: boolean;   // Gray out and make unclickable
  separator?: boolean;  // Render as divider line instead of item
}
```

**ContextMenuEvent interface:**
```tsx
interface ContextMenuEvent {
  action: string;           // Action identifier of selected item
  targetId?: number;        // ID of the node that was right-clicked
  hasSelection?: boolean;   // Whether text was selected when menu opened
  selectedText?: string;    // The selected text content (if any)
}
```

platformNotes.Love2D:
- Context menu is entirely Lua-owned (lua/contextmenu.lua)
- Right-click detection happens in init.lua → calls contextmenu.open()
- Lua performs hit testing to find the node under cursor and detect text nodes
- Built-in items: Copy (enabled when text selected), Inspect (opens inspector on clicked node)
- Copy action automatically writes to clipboard via love.system.setClipboardText()
- Keyboard navigation: Arrow keys move selection, Enter confirms, Escape closes
- Mouse interaction: Hover highlights items, click selects, click outside closes
- Rendering: Dark panel with rounded corners, hover highlight, separators
- Custom items from ContextMenu ancestors are appended after built-in Copy, before Inspect
- Menu position is clamped to stay within viewport bounds
platformNotes.Web:
- Renders as positioned div overlay with fixed positioning
- Uses native onContextMenu handler to intercept right-clicks
- Automatically prevents default browser context menu
- Closes on click outside (via window click listener)
- Closes on Escape key (via window keydown listener)
- CSS styling matches Love2D appearance (dark theme, rounded corners, hover states)
- Text selection and clipboard operations use browser APIs

criticalRules: ContextMenu wraps children and intercepts right-clicks anywhere within the subtree
Built-in "Copy" item is ALWAYS present (disabled when no text selected, enabled when text selected)
Built-in "Inspect" item is ALWAYS present on Love2D (opens visual inspector F12 on the clicked node)
Custom items (via `items` prop) are inserted between Copy and Inspect
Use `separator: true` to render divider lines between groups of items
Action identifiers starting with `__` are reserved for built-in actions (`__copy`, `__inspect`)
The nearest ContextMenu ancestor determines which custom items appear
Nested ContextMenus: inner menus take precedence over outer ones
On Love2D, text selection requires `userSelect: 'text'` style on Text nodes
Menu automatically closes after item selection, click outside, or Escape key
Disabled items are grayed out and unclickable but remain visible
Separator items do not trigger hover or selection
Empty items array is allowed (menu shows only built-in Copy and Inspect)
JS receives only boundary events: open, close, select (NO hover, keyboard nav, or render events)
On Love2D, the menu is positioned at click coordinates but clamped to viewport
seeAlso: TextInput — Single-line text input with cursor and selection (can be wrapped with ContextMenu for Copy)
TextEditor — Multi-line code editor (can be wrapped with ContextMenu for Copy)
Box — Container primitive (can be wrapped with ContextMenu for custom actions)
Modal — Overlay component (similar inverse pattern where Lua owns interaction)
Text — Text primitive with `userSelect: 'text'` for text selection support

metadata.title: DebugOverlay
metadata.description: Context wrapper that visualizes layout boundaries with colored borders and optional dimension labels

sections.overview:
DebugOverlay is a React context provider that wraps any subtree to visualize layout boundaries without opening the full inspector. It adds colored borders to every Box within its subtree and optionally displays dimension labels on each element. The overlay can be toggled on/off at runtime and customized per-subtree. Use DebugBox as a drop-in replacement for Box to respect the debug context automatically.

DebugOverlay is particularly useful when tracking down layout bugs, verifying flex container alignment, or understanding how nested boxes are sizing themselves. Unlike the full inspector (F12 in Love2D), DebugOverlay can be embedded directly in your component tree and toggled programmatically.
sections.api:
```tsx
import { DebugOverlay, DebugBox, useDebugOverlay } from '@reactjit/core';
```

```tsx
<DebugOverlay enabled={true} borderColor="rgba(255, 100, 100, 0.6)" showLabels={false}>
  <DebugBox style={{ width: '100%', height: '100%' }}>
    {children}
  </DebugBox>
</DebugOverlay>
```

DebugOverlay Props:
| Prop        | Type             | Default                      | Description                               |
|-------------|------------------|------------------------------|-------------------------------------------|
| children    | ReactNode        | (required)                   | Subtree to wrap with debug context        |
| enabled     | boolean          | true                         | Toggle overlay on/off                     |
| borderColor | string           | 'rgba(255, 100, 100, 0.6)'   | Border color for debug outlines           |
| showLabels  | boolean          | false                        | Show dimension labels on each Box         |

DebugBox Props:
| Prop     | Type      | Default   | Description                                |
|----------|-----------|-----------|-------------------------------------------|
| children | ReactNode | undefined | Child elements                            |
| style    | Style     | undefined | Box style (border added when debug enabled)|
| ...props | any       | undefined | All other Box props (onClick, etc.)       |

useDebugOverlay Hook:
Returns `{ enabled: boolean, borderColor: string, showLabels: boolean }` from the nearest DebugOverlay context.

examples.title: Basic overlay for a section
Toggle overlay with a button
Custom border color and labels
Using the hook to read debug state

criticalRules: **DebugBox is required** — Regular `<Box>` elements inside a DebugOverlay will NOT show debug borders. You must use `<DebugBox>` for context awareness.
**Context nesting** — If you nest multiple DebugOverlay providers, the innermost context wins. Each subtree can have its own border color and enabled state.
**Performance** — DebugOverlay adds a style merge operation to every DebugBox render. For large subtrees (100+ boxes), consider wrapping only the problematic section instead of the entire tree.
**Remove before production** — DebugOverlay is a development tool. Disable it or remove it entirely before building production distributions.
**showLabels not implemented** — The `showLabels` prop is accepted but dimension label rendering is not yet implemented. Currently it only affects the context value returned by useDebugOverlay.
seeAlso: Box — The fundamental layout primitive that DebugBox wraps
Visual Inspector (F12) — Full-featured inspector for Love2D with tree navigation and interaction state
Layout rules (CLAUDE.md) — Critical layout debugging tips and common pitfalls

metadata.title: DirectionalLight
metadata.description: A directional sun-like light source that provides shading and specular highlights

sections.overview: DirectionalLight simulates a distant light source such as a sun, with rays that are parallel across the entire scene. The direction prop points toward the light source (not away from it). It drives diffuse shading and Phong specular highlights on all lit meshes. Only one DirectionalLight per Scene is currently supported. It renders nothing on web targets.
sections.api:
```tsx
import { DirectionalLight } from '@reactjit/3d';
```

Props:
| Prop | Type | Default | Description |
|------|------|---------|-------------|
| direction | [number, number, number] | — | Direction toward the light source as [x, y, z] — the vector is normalized automatically |
| color | string | '#ffffff' | Light color as a hex string |
| intensity | number | 1.0 | Intensity multiplier applied to the color |

examples.title: Standard white directional light from above and to the side
Warm sunlight for a planet scene
Low-angle dramatic rim light

criticalRules: DirectionalLight renders nothing on web targets and returns null — it is Love2D only
Only one DirectionalLight per Scene is currently supported — placing multiple will produce undefined behavior
DirectionalLight must be a direct child of Scene, not nested inside other elements
The direction prop points TOWARD the light, not away from it — [1, -1, 1] means light comes from the positive-X, negative-Y, positive-Z direction
The direction vector is normalized automatically; you do not need to provide a unit vector
DirectionalLight with intensity set to 0 disables directional shading; only AmbientLight will remain
Specular highlights on Mesh are driven by DirectionalLight — adjusting Mesh's specular prop changes highlight tightness
seeAlso: Scene — the 3D viewport that DirectionalLight must be placed inside
AmbientLight — provides base fill light to complement DirectionalLight
Mesh — the geometry that receives directional shading and specular highlights
Camera — the viewpoint whose angle relative to DirectionalLight affects specular intensity

metadata.title: Divider
metadata.description: Horizontal or vertical separator line with configurable color and thickness

sections.overview: Divider renders a horizontal or vertical separator line between content sections. It's a styled Box with backgroundColor, defaulting to a 1-pixel horizontal line. Use it to visually separate groups of content, create borders, or add structure to complex layouts.
sections.api:
import { Divider } from '@reactjit/core';

Props:
| Prop | Type | Default | Description |
|------|------|---------|-------------|
| direction | 'horizontal' \| 'vertical' | 'horizontal' | Orientation of the divider line |
| color | string | '#334155' | Color of the line (any valid CSS color: hex, rgb, named) |
| thickness | number | 1 | Line thickness in pixels |
| style | Style | undefined | Additional style overrides (merged after direction/color/thickness) |

examples.title: Basic horizontal divider
examples.code:
<Box style={{ width: '100%', flexDirection: 'column' }}>
  <Text fontSize={18}>Section 1</Text>
  <Divider />
  <Text fontSize={18}>Section 2</Text>
</Box>

examples.title: Vertical divider in row
examples.code:
<Box style={{ width: 300, height: 100, flexDirection: 'row', alignItems: 'center' }}>
  <Text fontSize={16}>Left</Text>
  <Divider direction="vertical" />
  <Text fontSize={16}>Right</Text>
</Box>

examples.title: Custom color and thickness
examples.code:
<Box style={{ width: '100%', flexDirection: 'column' }}>
  <Text fontSize={20}>Header</Text>
  <Divider color="#ef4444" thickness={3} />
  <Text fontSize={14}>Content below the bold red divider</Text>
</Box>

examples.title: Divider with margin spacing
examples.code:
<Box style={{ width: '100%', flexDirection: 'column' }}>
  <Text fontSize={16}>Item 1</Text>
  <Divider style={{ marginTop: 10, marginBottom: 10 }} />
  <Text fontSize={16}>Item 2</Text>
</Box>

examples.title: Partial-width divider
examples.code:
<Box style={{ width: '100%', flexDirection: 'column', alignItems: 'center' }}>
  <Text fontSize={18}>Centered Content</Text>
  <Divider style={{ width: '50%' }} />
  <Text fontSize={18}>More Content</Text>
</Box>

criticalRules: Horizontal dividers default to width: '100%' — they fill the parent's width automatically
Vertical dividers default to height: '100%' — they fill the parent's height automatically
Vertical dividers require a parent with explicit height, or they collapse to zero
The style prop is merged last, so you can override any default behavior (width, height, margins, etc.)
Divider is purely visual — it doesn't affect layout spacing beyond its own thickness
Combine Divider with Spacer for both visual separation and spacing (Divider for the line, Spacer for the gap)
In grid targets (terminal, cc, nvim), dividers render as block characters at the cell level
Default color (#334155) is a neutral slate gray that works on most backgrounds — adjust for light/dark themes
seeAlso: Spacer — invisible spacing component (vs. Divider's visible line)
Box — primitive that Divider is built on (use Box directly for borders or complex separators)
Card — component that often contains Dividers to separate sections
Badge — another layout helper component often used with Dividers

metadata.title: Emulator
metadata.description: NES emulation surface — runs a .nes ROM in a canvas at layout position via Agnes

sections.overview:
Emulator renders a NES game into the layout tree using the Agnes emulation core. It is a visual surface node — give it explicit dimensions or flexGrow: 1 to fill available space, just like an Image. In web mode it returns null silently.

ROM loading uses the src prop for Love2D filesystem paths. If no src is given, you can drop a .nes file directly onto the Emulator surface and it loads immediately. Lua handles file drops without a bridge round-trip, so load latency is near-zero.

playing controls whether the emulation loop is running. Set playing={false} to pause without unmounting. The onROMLoaded callback fires once the ROM is parsed and ready.
sections.api:
```tsx
import { Emulator } from '@reactjit/core';
import type { EmulatorProps } from '@reactjit/core';
```

Props:
| Prop        | Type                                    | Default   | Description                                       |
|-------------|-----------------------------------------|-----------|---------------------------------------------------|
| src         | string                                  | undefined | Path to a .nes ROM file (Love2D filesystem path)  |
| playing     | boolean                                 | true      | Whether the emulation loop is running             |
| w           | number                                  | undefined | Shorthand for style.width                         |
| h           | number                                  | undefined | Shorthand for style.height                        |
| style       | Style                                   | undefined | Layout and positioning style                      |
| onROMLoaded | (event: { filename: string }) => void   | undefined | Fires once the ROM is parsed and ready            |

examples.title: Load a ROM from the filesystem
Drag-and-drop ROM loader
Pause / resume control

platformNotes.Love2D: Full support. Agnes runs as a native Lua module. Emulator uses the canvas API for per-pixel PPU output and maps Love2D keyboard events to NES controller inputs automatically.
platformNotes.Web: Not supported — returns null. The NES emulator requires native Lua/FFI access.

criticalRules: Always provide explicit dimensions or flexGrow: 1. Emulator is a surface node — without sizing it collapses to the proportional fallback (1/4 of parent), which is rarely what you want for a game canvas.
The native NES resolution is 256×240. Use integer scale factors (256×2=512, 240×2=480) to avoid blurry upscaling.
Emulator is native-only. Do not conditionally render it based on platform — it returns null on web automatically.
onROMLoaded fires once per ROM load, not on every frame. Do not rely on it for frame callbacks.

metadata.title: FlatList
metadata.description: Virtualized scrollable list that renders only visible items for efficient large dataset display.

sections.overview:
FlatList is a virtualized scrollable list component that renders only items visible within the scroll viewport plus a configurable buffer zone. It uses spacer elements above and below the visible window to maintain correct total scroll height without mounting every item in the data array.

FlatList supports vertical and horizontal scrolling, multi-column grid layouts, inverted lists, infinite scrolling via `onEndReached`, and imperative scrolling methods. It delegates all scrolling behavior to the ScrollView component internally. FlatList accepts a `ref` for imperative control via `scrollToIndex` and `scrollToOffset`.
sections.api:
Import: import { FlatList } from '@reactjit/core';

Usage:
```tsx
<FlatList
  data={items}
  renderItem={({ item, index }) => (
    <Box style={{ height: 50, padding: 8 }}>
      <Text style={{ fontSize: 14 }}>{item.name}</Text>
    </Box>
  )}
  itemHeight={50}
  style={{ height: 400 }}
/>
```

FlatListProps<T>:

| Prop                   | Type                                                  | Default  | Description                                        |
|------------------------|-------------------------------------------------------|----------|----------------------------------------------------|
| data                   | T[]                                                   | (required)| Array of items to render                          |
| renderItem             | (info: { item: T; index: number }) => ReactNode       | (required)| Render function for each item                     |
| keyExtractor           | (item: T, index: number) => string                    | -        | Unique key for each item (defaults to index)       |
| horizontal             | boolean                                               | false    | Enable horizontal scrolling                        |
| itemHeight             | number                                                | -        | Fixed item height (for vertical lists)             |
| itemWidth              | number                                                | -        | Fixed item width (for horizontal lists)            |
| estimatedItemSize      | number                                                | 50       | Estimated item size when exact size is unknown     |
| onEndReached           | () => void                                            | -        | Called when scroll nears the end of the list       |
| onEndReachedThreshold  | number                                                | 0.5      | Distance from end (in viewports) to trigger        |
| onScroll               | (event: ScrollEvent) => void                          | -        | Called on every scroll position change              |
| ListHeaderComponent    | ReactNode                                             | -        | Component rendered before the first item           |
| ListFooterComponent    | ReactNode                                             | -        | Component rendered after the last item             |
| ListEmptyComponent     | ReactNode                                             | -        | Component rendered when data is empty              |
| ItemSeparatorComponent | ReactNode                                             | -        | Component rendered between items                   |
| style                  | Style                                                 | -        | Outer ScrollView container style                   |
| contentContainerStyle  | Style                                                 | -        | Inner content container style                      |
| initialNumToRender     | number                                                | 10       | Number of items to render on first mount           |
| windowSize             | number                                                | 5        | Number of viewports to keep rendered (buffer)      |
| maxToRenderPerBatch    | number                                                | 10       | Max items to render per batch update               |
| inverted               | boolean                                               | false    | Reverses the item order                            |
| numColumns             | number                                                | -        | Number of columns for grid layout                  |

FlatListRef (via React.forwardRef):

| Method         | Signature                                              | Description                           |
|----------------|--------------------------------------------------------|---------------------------------------|
| scrollToIndex  | (params: { index: number; animated?: boolean }) => void| Scroll to a specific item by index    |
| scrollToOffset | (params: { offset: number; animated?: boolean }) => void| Scroll to a specific pixel offset    |

ScrollEvent type:
```ts
interface ScrollEvent {
  scrollX: number;
  scrollY: number;
  contentWidth: number;
  contentHeight: number;
}
```

examples.title: Basic vertical list
Grid layout with numColumns
Infinite scroll with onEndReached

criticalRules: FlatList MUST have an explicit `height` (or parent with known height) in its style. Without it, the scroll container collapses and virtualization breaks.
For best virtualization performance, provide `itemHeight` (vertical) or `itemWidth` (horizontal). Without these, `estimatedItemSize` (default: 50) is used, which may cause visual jumping.
The `renderItem` function should return elements with a consistent, fixed height matching `itemHeight`. Variable-height items will cause virtualization misalignment.
`keyExtractor` defaults to using the array index as a string. For data that changes order or gets inserted/removed, always provide a stable `keyExtractor`.

metadata.title: FlexColumn
metadata.description: Convenience wrapper around Box with vertical flex layout

sections.overview: FlexColumn is a convenience component that wraps Box with flexDirection='column' pre-configured. It provides simplified props for common vertical layout tasks: gap spacing, vertical justification, and horizontal alignment. While column is the default flexDirection for Box, FlexColumn offers a cleaner API when you want explicit vertical stacking with readable prop names.
sections.api:
import { FlexColumn } from '@reactjit/core';

Props:
| Prop | Type | Required | Default | Description |
|------|------|----------|---------|-------------|
| gap | number | No | undefined | Spacing between children (pixels) |
| justify | 'flex-start' \| 'flex-end' \| 'center' \| 'space-between' \| 'space-around' \| 'space-evenly' | No | undefined | Vertical distribution (justifyContent) |
| align | 'flex-start' \| 'flex-end' \| 'center' \| 'stretch' | No | undefined | Horizontal alignment (alignItems) |
| style | Style | No | {} | Additional style properties (merged after defaults) |
| children | React.ReactNode | Yes | - | Child elements to lay out vertically |

examples.title: Basic vertical stack with spacing
examples.code:
import { FlexColumn, Text } from '@reactjit/core';

<FlexColumn gap={12}>
  <Text fontSize={20}>Heading</Text>
  <Text fontSize={16}>Subheading</Text>
  <Text fontSize={14}>Body text goes here</Text>
</FlexColumn>

examples.title: Centered card layout
Space-between full height layout
examples.code:
import { FlexColumn, Text } from '@reactjit/core';

<FlexColumn justify="space-between" style={{ width: '100%', height: '100%' }}>
  <Text fontSize={18}>Header</Text>
  <Text fontSize={14}>Content in the middle</Text>
  <Text fontSize={12} style={{ color: '#999' }}>Footer</Text>
</FlexColumn>

examples.title: Left-aligned form layout
Vertically centered viewport content
examples.code:
import { FlexColumn, Text, Box } from '@reactjit/core';

<FlexColumn
  justify="center"
  align="center"
  style={{ width: '100%', height: '100%' }}
>
  <Box style={{
    padding: 32,
    backgroundColor: '#333',
    borderRadius: 16
  }}>
    <Text fontSize={24} style={{ color: 'white' }}>Welcome</Text>
  </Box>
</FlexColumn>

criticalRules: FlexColumn requires explicit height for justify to work properly. Without a height, justifyContent='center' or 'space-between' will have no effect on vertical distribution.
Root-level FlexColumn components should use width: '100%', height: '100%' to fill the viewport. Do NOT use flexGrow: 1 at the root.
Gap spacing is applied between children, not around the edges. Use padding in style prop for outer spacing.
The style prop is merged last, so you can override gap, justify, and align by passing conflicting properties in style (though this defeats the purpose of the convenience props).
FlexColumn auto-sizes to fit content if no width/height is specified. Height is sum of children heights plus gaps. Width is determined by the widest child.
align='stretch' only works if children do not have explicit widths. Children will stretch to match the container's width.
Do not use flexGrow on children without setting a height on FlexColumn. Percentage-based children need explicit parent dimensions.

FlexColumn is functionally equivalent to Box with flexDirection='column', since column is the default. Use FlexColumn when you want self-documenting code with convenient props. Use Box when you need full style control.
seeAlso: Box — The underlying primitive that FlexColumn wraps
Spacer — Creates flexible empty space in flex layouts
Layout Rules (CLAUDE.md) — Critical layout constraints and common pitfalls
Auto-Sizing (CLAUDE.md) — How containers size to fit content

metadata.title: FlexRow
metadata.description: Convenience wrapper around Box with horizontal flex layout

sections.overview: FlexRow is a convenience component that wraps Box with flexDirection='row' pre-configured. It provides simplified props for common horizontal layout tasks: gap spacing, horizontal justification, vertical alignment, and wrapping. Use FlexRow when you need to arrange children horizontally without manually setting flexDirection on every Box.
sections.api:
import { FlexRow } from '@reactjit/core';

Props:
| Prop | Type | Required | Default | Description |
|------|------|----------|---------|-------------|
| gap | number | No | undefined | Spacing between children (pixels) |
| justify | 'flex-start' \| 'flex-end' \| 'center' \| 'space-between' \| 'space-around' \| 'space-evenly' | No | undefined | Horizontal distribution (justifyContent) |
| align | 'flex-start' \| 'flex-end' \| 'center' \| 'stretch' \| 'baseline' | No | undefined | Vertical alignment (alignItems) |
| wrap | boolean | No | false | Allow children to wrap to next line (flexWrap: 'wrap') |
| style | Style | No | {} | Additional style properties (merged after defaults) |
| children | React.ReactNode | Yes | - | Child elements to lay out horizontally |

examples.title: Basic horizontal layout with spacing
examples.code:
import { FlexRow, Text } from '@reactjit/core';

<FlexRow gap={16}>
  <Text fontSize={16}>First</Text>
  <Text fontSize={16}>Second</Text>
  <Text fontSize={16}>Third</Text>
</FlexRow>

examples.title: Centered content with justify and align
examples.code:
import { FlexRow, Text, Box } from '@reactjit/core';

<FlexRow
  justify="center"
  align="center"
  style={{ width: '100%', height: 100, backgroundColor: '#f0f0f0' }}
>
  <Box style={{ width: 50, height: 50, backgroundColor: 'blue' }} />
  <Text fontSize={16}>Centered</Text>
</FlexRow>

examples.title: Space-between distribution (toolbar pattern)
examples.code:
import { FlexRow, Text } from '@reactjit/core';

<FlexRow justify="space-between" style={{ width: '100%', padding: 8 }}>
  <Text fontSize={14}>Left</Text>
  <Text fontSize={14}>Center</Text>
  <Text fontSize={14}>Right</Text>
</FlexRow>

examples.title: Wrapping horizontal grid
Combining with custom styles

criticalRules: FlexRow requires explicit width for justify to work properly. Row containers have no intrinsic width (only Text nodes do). Without a width, justifyContent='center' or 'space-between' will have no effect. Always set width: '100%' or a fixed width when using justify.
Do not use flexGrow on children without setting a width on FlexRow. Percentage-based children need explicit parent dimensions.
Gap spacing is applied between children, not around the edges. Use padding in style prop for outer spacing.
The style prop is merged last, so you can override gap, justify, and align by passing conflicting properties in style (though this defeats the purpose of the convenience props).
FlexRow auto-sizes to fit content if no width/height is specified. Height is determined by the tallest child. Width is sum of children widths plus gaps.
Use wrap={true} when you want a responsive horizontal grid that breaks to multiple rows. Requires explicit width on FlexRow.
align='stretch' only works if children do not have explicit heights. Children will stretch to match the tallest sibling.
seeAlso: Box — The underlying primitive that FlexRow wraps
Spacer — Creates flexible empty space in flex layouts
Layout Rules (CLAUDE.md) — Critical layout constraints and common pitfalls
Auto-Sizing (CLAUDE.md) — How containers size to fit content

metadata.title: FocusGroup
metadata.description: Scoped focus region for keyboard and gamepad navigation

sections.overview:
FocusGroup creates a scoped focus region for controller and keyboard navigation. It restricts Tab key and gamepad D-pad navigation to elements within the group, enabling isolated UI sections and multiplayer support where each controller navigates its own region. FocusGroups support automatic player-based ring colors (P1 blue, P2 red, P3 green, P4 yellow) and optional controller binding for split-screen or couch multiplayer scenarios.

When a FocusGroup is bound to a specific controller via the controller prop, only that controller can navigate elements within the group. If no controller is specified, the group accepts input from any controller. FocusGroups can be nested, and focus is automatically restored when switching between mouse and controller input modes. The framework tracks the last focused element per group and automatically scrolls focused elements into view within ScrollView parents.
sections.api:
```tsx
import { FocusGroup } from '@reactjit/core';
```

```tsx
<FocusGroup
  controller={0}
  ringColor={[0.3, 0.6, 1.0, 0.9]}
  style={{ padding: 16, backgroundColor: '#1e1e2e' }}
>
  <Pressable onPress={() => {}}>
    <Text fontSize={14}>Option 1</Text>
  </Pressable>
  <Pressable onPress={() => {}}>
    <Text fontSize={14}>Option 2</Text>
  </Pressable>
</FocusGroup>
```

Props:
| Prop       | Type                           | Default   | Description                                       |
|------------|--------------------------------|-----------|---------------------------------------------------|
| controller | number                         | undefined | Joystick ID (0-based). Omit for any controller.  |
| ringColor  | [number, number, number, number?] | undefined | Focus ring color as [r, g, b, a] (0-1 range). Default: player-based (P1 blue, P2 red, P3 green, P4 yellow). |
| style      | Style                          | undefined | Container style (wraps Box internally).           |
| children   | ReactNode                      | REQUIRED  | Focusable elements (Pressable, TextInput, etc.).  |

RingColor format:
```ts
type RingColor = [number, number, number, number?];
// [r, g, b, a] where each is 0-1
// Example: [0.3, 0.6, 1.0, 0.9] = semi-transparent blue
```

examples.title: Basic focus group (any controller)
examples.code:
```tsx
<FocusGroup style={{ padding: 16, gap: 8 }}>
  <Pressable onPress={() => console.log('A')}>
    <Text fontSize={14}>Button A</Text>
  </Pressable>
  <Pressable onPress={() => console.log('B')}>
    <Text fontSize={14}>Button B</Text>
  </Pressable>
  <Pressable onPress={() => console.log('C')}>
    <Text fontSize={14}>Button C</Text>
  </Pressable>
</FocusGroup>
```

examples.title: Multiplayer split-screen (controller-bound groups)
examples.code:
```tsx
<Box direction="row" fill>
  {/* Player 1 (left half) */}
  <FocusGroup controller={0} style={{ width: '50%', padding: 16 }}>
    <Text fontSize={18} bold>Player 1</Text>
    <Pressable onPress={() => setP1Ready(true)}>
      <Text fontSize={14}>Ready</Text>
    </Pressable>
    <Pressable onPress={() => setP1Character('mage')}>
      <Text fontSize={14}>Select Mage</Text>
    </Pressable>
  </FocusGroup>

  {/* Player 2 (right half) */}
  <FocusGroup controller={1} style={{ width: '50%', padding: 16 }}>
    <Text fontSize={18} bold>Player 2</Text>
    <Pressable onPress={() => setP2Ready(true)}>
      <Text fontSize={14}>Ready</Text>
    </Pressable>
    <Pressable onPress={() => setP2Character('warrior')}>
      <Text fontSize={14}>Select Warrior</Text>
    </Pressable>
  </FocusGroup>
</Box>
```

examples.title: Custom ring color
examples.code:
```tsx
<FocusGroup ringColor={[1.0, 0.5, 0.0, 1.0]} style={{ padding: 16 }}>
  <Pressable onPress={() => {}}>
    <Text fontSize={14}>Orange-ringed button</Text>
  </Pressable>
</FocusGroup>
```

examples.title: Nested focus groups (isolated navigation regions)
examples.code:
```tsx
<Box fill padding={16} gap={16}>
  {/* Top toolbar (isolated) */}
  <FocusGroup style={{ flexDirection: 'row', gap: 8 }}>
    <Pressable onPress={() => {}}>
      <Text fontSize={14}>File</Text>
    </Pressable>
    <Pressable onPress={() => {}}>
      <Text fontSize={14}>Edit</Text>
    </Pressable>
  </FocusGroup>

  {/* Main content (isolated) */}
  <FocusGroup style={{ flexGrow: 1, padding: 16 }}>
    <Pressable onPress={() => {}}>
      <Text fontSize={14}>Item 1</Text>
    </Pressable>
    <Pressable onPress={() => {}}>
      <Text fontSize={14}>Item 2</Text>
    </Pressable>
  </FocusGroup>
</Box>
```

examples.title: Focus group with TextInput
examples.code:
```tsx
<FocusGroup style={{ padding: 16, gap: 8 }}>
  <TextInput
    placeholder="Username"
    style={{ padding: 8, backgroundColor: '#313244' }}
    textStyle={{ fontSize: 14 }}
  />
  <TextInput
    placeholder="Password"
    secureTextEntry
    style={{ padding: 8, backgroundColor: '#313244' }}
    textStyle={{ fontSize: 14 }}
  />
  <Pressable onPress={handleLogin}>
    <Text fontSize={14}>Login</Text>
  </Pressable>
</FocusGroup>
```

platformNotes.Love2D:
FocusGroup is implemented in lua/focus.lua. It registers as a scoped navigation region on every layout pass. Spatial navigation (D-pad / left stick) uses center-point distance with directional filtering — the closest element in the pressed direction becomes focused. Focus state is preserved when switching between mouse and controller modes. Focused elements automatically scroll into view within ScrollView parents. The focus ring animates with exponential smoothing for visual polish.
Controller binding: joystick IDs are 0-based. Controller 0 is the first connected gamepad, Controller 1 is the second, etc. If a group has controller={0}, only joystick 0 can navigate it. If controller is undefined, any joystick can navigate the group. Unbound groups (no controller prop) default to accepting input from any controller that is not bound to a specific group.
Ring colors: If ringColor is not set and the group is bound to a controller, the framework auto-assigns a player color (P1 blue, P2 red, P3 green, P4 yellow) based on joystick ID modulo 4. If ringColor is set explicitly, that color is used regardless of controller. If ringColor is undefined and controller is undefined, the default blue ring is used.
platformNotes.Web: FocusGroup renders as a <div> with tabIndex management. Tab key navigation is scoped using keyboard event handlers that trap focus within the group. Gamepad navigation is not implemented on web (browser gamepad API support is limited). Ring colors are rendered as CSS outline or box-shadow. Focus state is managed via React state and DOM focus events.
platformNotes.Terminal: Not available on grid targets. Use terminal-specific focus management.

criticalRules: FocusGroup only navigates elements with focusable={true} or interactive handlers (onClick, onPress, onKeyDown, etc.). Non-interactive elements are skipped.
Tab key and D-pad navigation wrap within the group. Pressing right on the last element does NOT jump to the next group — it wraps to the first element.

Controller-bound groups (controller={0}) ignore input from other controllers. If controller 1 presses D-pad and is not bound to any group, it navigates the default group (elements outside all FocusGroups) and any unclaimed FocusGroups.
Nested FocusGroups create independent navigation scopes. A child group's elements are not navigable from the parent group. Each group maintains its own focus state.
If a focused element is removed from the tree (unmounted), focus resets to the first focusable element in the group. If the entire group is unmounted, focus is lost.

Focus restoration: when switching from mouse to controller mode, the framework restores the last focused element per group. If no element was previously focused, it focuses the first focusable element.

Spatial navigation (D-pad) uses center-point distance with directional filtering. The algorithm prefers elements in the pressed direction and penalizes perpendicular distance. This prevents diagonal jumps when pressing left/right in a vertical list.

metadata.title: HorizontalBarChart
metadata.description: Horizontal bar chart component for comparing labeled values side by side

sections.overview: HorizontalBarChart renders a series of labeled bars growing left-to-right, each scaled relative to the maximum value in the dataset. Labels appear on the left, the bar fills proportionally, and an optional value readout follows on the right. When interactive mode is enabled, hovering a bar dims all others and reveals a tooltip showing the label, raw value, and percentage of the maximum.
sections.api:
```tsx
import { HorizontalBarChart } from '@reactjit/core';
```

Data shape:
```tsx
interface HorizontalBarChartBar {
  label: string;   // Row label shown on the left
  value: number;   // Numeric value driving bar width
  color?: Color;   // Per-bar override; falls back to the chart-level `color` prop
}
```

Props:
| Prop         | Type                                                              | Default      | Description                                                         |
|--------------|-------------------------------------------------------------------|--------------|---------------------------------------------------------------------|
| data         | HorizontalBarChartBar[]                                           | required     | Array of bars to render                                             |
| width        | number                                                            | 280          | Total width of the chart in pixels                                  |
| barHeight    | number                                                            | 20           | Height of each bar row in pixels                                    |
| gap          | number                                                            | 6            | Vertical gap between bar rows in pixels                             |
| showLabels   | boolean                                                           | true         | Whether to render the label column on the left                      |
| showValues   | boolean                                                           | false        | Whether to render the numeric value after each bar                  |
| labelWidth   | number                                                            | 60           | Fixed pixel width reserved for the label column                     |
| color        | Color                                                             | '#3b82f6'    | Default bar color used when a bar has no per-item `color`           |
| style        | Style                                                             | undefined    | Additional styles applied to the outer container Box                |
| interactive  | boolean                                                           | false        | Enable hover highlighting and tooltips                              |
| onBarHover   | (index: number \| null, bar: HorizontalBarChartBar \| null) => void | undefined  | Called when the pointer enters or leaves a bar                      |
| onBarPress   | (index: number, bar: HorizontalBarChartBar) => void               | undefined    | Called when a bar is clicked (requires interactive: true)           |

examples.title: Basic labeled chart
Per-bar colors with value readout
Interactive with hover callback

criticalRules: The `width` prop controls the total chart width; bar fill width is computed from the remaining space after subtracting the label column (`labelWidth + 8`) and value column (40px) when those are shown.
When `showLabels` is false, `labelWidth` has no effect but the space is still not reserved — set `showLabels={false}` to reclaim that space.
Per-bar `color` takes priority over the chart-level `color` prop.
`onBarPress` only fires when `interactive` is also `true`; setting `onBarPress` alone without `interactive` has no effect.
Empty `data` arrays render a sized empty Box with no error — safe to pass live data that may start empty.
Do not use Unicode symbols in any `<Text>` within this component or in labels passed as data — use Box-based geometry for visual indicators instead.
seeAlso: BarChart — vertical bars for the same kind of comparison
StackedBarChart — multi-series bars stacked in a single column
Sparkline — compact inline trend line with no axes
ChartTooltip — standalone tooltip primitive used internally by this component

metadata.title: Image
metadata.description: Displays images with sizing and objectFit control

sections.overview: Image displays an image from a file path or URL. It supports sizing via width/height style properties and content fitting via the objectFit style property. Unlike Box, Image has no children. Unlike Text, Image has no intrinsic size derived from content -- you must specify width and height explicitly or let the parent constrain it. In web mode, Image renders as an `` element. In native mode, it outputs an `'Image'` host element that the Lua painter draws using love.graphics.draw() with the image loaded from the src path.
sections.api:
```tsx
import { Image } from '@reactjit/core';
```

```tsx
<Image
  src="assets/logo.png"
  style={{ width: 128, height: 128, objectFit: 'contain' }}
/>
```

Props:
| Prop    | Type                         | Default   | Description                          |
|---------|------------------------------|-----------|--------------------------------------|
| src     | string                       | REQUIRED  | Image file path or URL               |
| style   | Style                        | undefined | Sizing and visual styling            |
| onClick | (event: LoveEvent) => void   | undefined | Click/press handler                  |
| onWheel | (event: LoveEvent) => void   | undefined | Mouse wheel handler                  |

objectFit values (set via style.objectFit):
| Value   | Behavior                                                        |
|---------|-----------------------------------------------------------------|
| fill    | Stretch image to fill the element (may distort aspect ratio)    |
| contain | Scale image to fit entirely within the element (letterboxed)    |
| cover   | Scale image to cover the element entirely (may crop)            |
| none    | Display at original size, no scaling                            |

examples.title: Basic image with fixed size
examples.code:
```tsx
<Image
  src="assets/icon.png"
  style={{ width: 64, height: 64 }}
/>
```

examples.title: Image with objectFit contain
examples.code:
```tsx
<Image
  src="assets/banner.png"
  style={{
    width: '100%',
    height: 200,
    objectFit: 'contain',
    backgroundColor: '#181825',
  }}
/>
```

examples.title: Clickable image
examples.code:
```tsx
<Image
  src="assets/button-sprite.png"
  style={{ width: 48, height: 48, borderRadius: 8 }}
  onClick={() => console.log('image clicked')}
/>
```

examples.title: Cover image filling a card
examples.code:
```tsx
<Box style={{ width: 300, height: 200, borderRadius: 12, overflow: 'hidden' }}>
  <Image
    src="assets/photo.jpg"
    style={{ width: '100%', height: '100%', objectFit: 'cover' }}
  />
</Box>
```

platformNotes.Love2D: Renders as an 'Image' host element. The Lua painter loads the image via love.graphics.newImage() and caches it. The src path is relative to the project root (where main.lua lives). Supported formats: PNG, JPEG, BMP, TGA, and other formats Love2D supports. objectFit is implemented by the painter via quad scaling.
platformNotes.Web: Renders as an element with display: block. objectFit maps directly to the CSS object-fit property. src can be a relative path, absolute URL, or data URI.
platformNotes.Terminal: Image is not supported on grid targets. Terminal, ComputerCraft, Neovim, and other character-grid renderers cannot display raster images. Use colored Box elements to approximate pixel art if needed.

criticalRules: Image has no intrinsic size. You must provide width and height via the style prop, or constrain the image via a parent Box with known dimensions. An Image without sizing will collapse to zero.
The src prop is required. Omitting it will produce an error.
Image paths in Love2D are relative to the project root directory, not the source file. Use paths like "assets/image.png", not "../assets/image.png".
Image accepts no children. It is a leaf element.

metadata.title: Components Overview
metadata.description: Reference index for all ReactJIT primitives and components

sections.overview:
ReactJIT ships a set of universal primitives and higher-level components that work across all render targets. Every visible element on screen is built from these building blocks. Primitives are the lowest-level elements (Box, Text, Image) that map directly to host elements in each target. Components are composed from primitives and provide richer interaction patterns (Pressable, ScrollView, Modal, Slider, and others).

All primitives and components are exported from `@reactjit/core`. Grid targets (terminal, ComputerCraft, Neovim, Hammerspoon, AwesomeWM) use lowercase JSX intrinsics (`<view>`, `<text>`) and typically define local wrapper components; the `@reactjit/core` exports target Love2D and web renderers.

platformNotes.Love2D: All components render as string-typed host elements ('View', 'Text', 'Image') processed by the Lua layout engine and painter. Flexbox is computed by layout.lua. Events go through bridge_quickjs.lua.
platformNotes.Web:
Primitives render as DOM elements (div, span, img) with CSS flexbox. Interaction events use standard DOM event listeners. Styles are converted from the Style type to React.CSSProperties by styleToCSS().
Terminal / Grid targets:
Use lowercase intrinsics (<view>, <text>) with the grid layout engine. The @reactjit/core components are not used directly; grid targets have their own component wrappers built on @reactjit/grid.

criticalRules: Always import from '@reactjit/core', never from internal package paths
Every <Text> must have an explicit fontSize -- the linter will block your build otherwise
Root containers need width: '100%' and height: '100%', not flexGrow: 1
Grid targets use different primitives; do not import @reactjit/core components into grid-target code
Run `reactjit lint` after any component change to catch layout violations early

metadata.title: LineChart
metadata.description: Column-based line chart that plots a series of values as dots with optional area fill

sections.overview: LineChart renders a fixed-size canvas divided into equal-width columns, one per data point. Each column positions a dot at the correct vertical height based on its normalized value, with the minimum value mapped to the bottom and the maximum to the top. An optional area fill extends from each dot to the bottom of the chart. When interactive mode is enabled, hovering a column shows a crosshair line and a tooltip with the optional x-label and raw value, while all other dots fade.
sections.api:
```tsx
import { LineChart } from '@reactjit/core';
```

Data shape:
```tsx
interface LineChartPoint {
  x?:    string;  // Optional x-axis label shown in the hover tooltip
  value: number;  // Y-axis value; min and max across the dataset define the scale
}
```

Props:
| Prop          | Type                                                              | Default    | Description                                                           |
|---------------|-------------------------------------------------------------------|------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------|
| data          | LineChartPoint[]                                                  | required   | Array of data points to plot                                          |
| width         | number                                                            | 280        | Total chart width in pixels                                           |
| height        | number                                                            | 120        | Total chart height in pixels                                          |
| color         | Color                                                             | '#3b82f6'  | Color used for dots, area fill, and the hover crosshair               |
| showDots      | boolean                                                           | true       | Whether to render a dot at each data point                            |
| showArea      | boolean                                                           | false      | Whether to render a filled area from the dot down to the baseline     |
| areaOpacity   | number                                                            | 0.3        | Opacity of the area fill (0–1); has no effect when showArea is false  |
| interactive   | boolean                                                           | false      | Enable hover crosshair and per-point tooltips                         |
| onPointHover  | (index: number \| null, point: LineChartPoint \| null) => void    | undefined  | Called when the pointer enters or leaves a column                     |
| onPointPress  | (index: number, point: LineChartPoint) => void                    | undefined  | Called when a column is clicked (requires interactive: true)          |
| style         | Style                                                             | undefined  | Additional styles applied to the outer container Box                  |

examples.title: Simple line chart with dots
Area fill for cumulative data
Interactive with click handler

criticalRules: The chart uses column-based rendering: each data point occupies a column of width `width / data.length`. Dots are visually positioned within this column but there are no connecting line segments drawn between them. For a filled area presentation, use AreaChart instead.

The vertical scale is relative: the minimum value in the dataset maps to the bottom and the maximum to the top. A flat dataset where all values are equal results in all dots at the bottom of their range.
`onPointPress` only fires when `interactive` is also `true`.
Hovered dots expand by 2px and non-hovered dots fade to 35% opacity. Hover requires pointer events, which are not available on all targets — check platform support before enabling `interactive`.
Empty `data` arrays render a sized empty Box safely.
Do not use Unicode symbols in tooltip labels (`x` field of each point).
seeAlso: AreaChart — convenience wrapper over LineChart with area fill on and dots off by default
Sparkline — minimal no-configuration trend line for embedding in tight layouts
BarChart — vertical bars for the same dataset
ChartTooltip — standalone tooltip primitive used internally by this component

metadata.title: Mesh
metadata.description: A renderable 3D object — built-in geometry or loaded OBJ model — inside a Scene

sections.overview: Mesh is the primary renderable object in a 3D scene. It supports four built-in geometry types (box, cube, sphere, plane) as well as externally loaded OBJ model files. Materials are defined through flat color, image textures, or procedural textures. Mesh supports a UV-based GLSL edge shader for wireframe borders, Phong specular highlights, Fresnel rim glow, and alpha transparency. Position, rotation, and scale are reactive and update each frame without reconciliation overhead.
sections.api:
```tsx
import { Mesh } from '@reactjit/3d';
```

Props:
| Prop | Type | Default | Description |
|------|------|---------|-------------|
| geometry | 'box' \| 'cube' \| 'sphere' \| 'plane' | — | Built-in geometry type to render |
| model | string | — | Path to an OBJ model file |
| color | string | — | Flat color as a hex string — used when no texture is provided |
| texture | string | — | Path to an image file, or a procedural name such as 'planet' |
| seed | number | — | Seed for procedural textures — different seeds produce different terrain |
| position | [number, number, number] | [0, 0, 0] | Position in 3D world space as [x, y, z] |
| rotation | [number, number, number] | [0, 0, 0] | Euler angles in radians as [rx, ry, rz] |
