| scale | number \| [number, number, number] | 1 | Uniform scale as a number, or per-axis scale as [sx, sy, sz] |
| edgeColor | string | — | Hex color for edge/wireframe borders drawn on face edges |
| edgeWidth | number | 0.03 | Edge line width as a fraction of face UV space |
| wireframe | boolean | — | Draw longitude/latitude grid lines — makes spheres visibly 3D |
| gridLines | number | 8 | Number of grid divisions per axis when wireframe is enabled |
| opacity | number | 1 | Opacity from 0 to 1 — values below 1 enable alpha blending |
| specular | number | 32 | Specular shininess power — higher values produce tighter highlights |
| fresnel | number | 0 | Fresnel rim power — 0 disables it, 3–5 produces atmosphere glow |
| unlit | boolean | — | Skip lighting and render with flat texture color only |
| onClick | (event: LoveEvent) => void | — | Click handler (raycasting, Phase 5) |
| onPointerEnter | (event: LoveEvent) => void | — | Pointer enter handler (raycasting, Phase 5) |
| onPointerLeave | (event: LoveEvent) => void | — | Pointer leave handler (raycasting, Phase 5) |

examples.title: Colored box with edge borders
Spinning sphere with procedural planet texture
Multiple meshes at different positions
Loading an OBJ model file
Transparent mesh with Fresnel atmosphere glow

criticalRules: Mesh renders nothing on web targets and returns null — it is Love2D only
Provide either geometry or model, not both; the behavior when both are set is undefined
color is ignored when texture is set — texture takes precedence for the surface material
edgeColor and wireframe are independent features: edgeColor draws borders on cube/box faces via a UV shader; wireframe draws lat/lon grid lines and is most useful on spheres
The fresnel prop controls alpha at glancing angles — it requires opacity to be less than 1 to be visible as transparency
scale as a single number applies uniformly to all axes; scale as [sx, sy, sz] scales each axis independently
rotation values are in radians, not degrees
onClick, onPointerEnter, and onPointerLeave are Phase 5 raycasting features and may not be available in all versions
seeAlso: Scene — the 3D viewport that Mesh must be placed inside
Camera — defines the viewpoint that sees Mesh geometry
AmbientLight — provides base illumination for Mesh shading
DirectionalLight — provides directional shading and specular highlights on Mesh

metadata.title: Modal
metadata.description: Fullscreen overlay with backdrop, animations, and dismiss handling

sections.overview:
Modal displays content in a fullscreen overlay that sits above the rest of the UI. It renders through the Portal system, ensuring correct z-index layering regardless of where it appears in the component tree. Modal supports a customizable backdrop (color, transparency, click-to-dismiss), simple enter/exit animations (none, fade, slide), and Escape key dismissal.

Modal is a controlled component -- you manage visibility via the `visible` prop and handle close events via `onRequestClose`. The backdrop press and Escape key both trigger onRequestClose; the component does not close itself. This gives you full control over dismiss conditions (e.g., confirmation dialogs that ignore backdrop clicks).
sections.api:
```tsx
import { Modal } from '@reactjit/core';
```

```tsx
const [showModal, setShowModal] = useState(false);

<Modal
  visible={showModal}
  onRequestClose={() => setShowModal(false)}
  animationType="fade"
>
  <Box style={{ width: 300, padding: 24, backgroundColor: '#1e1e2e', borderRadius: 12 }}>
    <Text style={{ fontSize: 18, color: '#cdd6f4' }}>Dialog Title</Text>
    <Pressable onPress={() => setShowModal(false)}>
      <Text style={{ fontSize: 14, color: '#89b4fa' }}>Close</Text>
    </Pressable>
  </Box>
</Modal>
```

Props:
| Prop            | Type                            | Default       | Description                                    |
|-----------------|---------------------------------|---------------|------------------------------------------------|
| visible         | boolean                         | REQUIRED      | Show or hide the modal                         |
| onRequestClose  | () => void                      | undefined     | Called on Escape key or backdrop press          |
| onShow          | () => void                      | undefined     | Called when modal finishes appearing            |
| animationType   | 'none' | 'fade' | 'slide'       | 'none'        | Enter/exit animation style                     |
| transparent     | boolean                         | false         | Use transparent backdrop (overrides color)      |
| backdropColor   | Color                           | [0,0,0,0.5]   | Backdrop color (RGBA or CSS string)            |
| backdropDismiss | boolean                         | true          | Whether backdrop press triggers onRequestClose |
| children        | ReactNode                       | REQUIRED      | Modal content                                  |
| style           | Style                           | undefined     | Applied to the content wrapper                 |

Animation types:
| Type  | Enter behavior                   | Exit behavior                    |
|-------|----------------------------------|----------------------------------|
| none  | Instant appear                   | Instant disappear                |
| fade  | Fades in from transparent        | Fades out to transparent         |
| slide | Slides up from below             | Slides down to below             |

Animation duration is 200ms for both enter and exit.

examples.title: Basic confirmation dialog
examples.code:
```tsx
const [show, setShow] = useState(false);

<Pressable onPress={() => setShow(true)}>
  <Text style={{ fontSize: 14, color: '#89b4fa' }}>Delete item</Text>
</Pressable>

<Modal visible={show} onRequestClose={() => setShow(false)} animationType="fade">
  <Box style={{
    width: 320,
    padding: 24,
    backgroundColor: '#1e1e2e',
    borderRadius: 12,
    gap: 16,
  }}>
    <Text style={{ fontSize: 18, fontWeight: 'bold', color: '#cdd6f4' }}>
      Confirm Delete
    </Text>
    <Text style={{ fontSize: 14, color: '#a6adc8' }}>
      Are you sure? This cannot be undone.
    </Text>
    <Box style={{ flexDirection: 'row', justifyContent: 'end', gap: 12, width: '100%' }}>
      <Pressable onPress={() => setShow(false)}>
        <Text style={{ fontSize: 14, color: '#a6adc8' }}>Cancel</Text>
      </Pressable>
      <Pressable onPress={() => { handleDelete(); setShow(false); }}>
        <Text style={{ fontSize: 14, color: '#f38ba8' }}>Delete</Text>
      </Pressable>
    </Box>
  </Box>
</Modal>
```

examples.title: Non-dismissable modal (ignores backdrop click)
examples.code:
```tsx
<Modal
  visible={isProcessing}
  backdropDismiss={false}
  backdropColor={[0, 0, 0, 0.8]}
>
  <Box style={{ padding: 32, backgroundColor: '#1e1e2e', borderRadius: 12, alignItems: 'center' }}>
    <Text style={{ fontSize: 16, color: '#cdd6f4' }}>Processing...</Text>
  </Box>
</Modal>
```

examples.title: Transparent overlay
examples.code:
```tsx
<Modal visible={showTooltip} transparent onRequestClose={() => setShowTooltip(false)}>
  <Box style={{
    position: 'absolute',
    top: 100,
    left: 200,
    padding: 8,
    backgroundColor: '#313244',
    borderRadius: 4,
  }}>
    <Text style={{ fontSize: 12, color: '#cdd6f4' }}>Tooltip content</Text>
  </Box>
</Modal>
```

examples.title: Slide-up modal with onShow callback
examples.code:
```tsx
<Modal
  visible={showPanel}
  onRequestClose={() => setShowPanel(false)}
  onShow={() => console.log('modal fully visible')}
  animationType="slide"
>
  <Box style={{
    width: '100%',
    height: '50%',
    backgroundColor: '#1e1e2e',
    borderRadius: 16,
    padding: 24,
  }}>
    <Text style={{ fontSize: 20, color: '#cdd6f4' }}>Bottom Sheet</Text>
  </Box>
</Modal>
```

platformNotes.Love2D: Modal renders through the Portal system which places content at the top of the instance tree for z-index layering. The backdrop is a full-screen Pressable that intercepts clicks. Escape key handling is wired through the Box onKeyDown handler in native mode. Animations are stepped (4 steps over 200ms) rather than continuous -- adequate for modal transitions but not smooth enough for complex animation needs.
platformNotes.Web: Uses the Portal system (React portal to a PortalHost). The backdrop prevents body scroll by setting document.body.style.overflow = 'hidden'. Escape key handler uses document.addEventListener('keydown'). CSS transitions are used for fade/slide animations in addition to the stepped state machine. The web renderer restores the original body overflow on unmount.
platformNotes.Terminal: Not available on grid targets. For modal-like behavior on character-grid renderers, overlay a view with higher z-index and handle input routing manually.

criticalRules: Modal is controlled: it will not close itself. You must update the `visible` prop to false in your onRequestClose handler.
The `children` prop is required. A Modal with no children renders an empty overlay with just the backdrop.
Do not conditionally render Modal itself based on visibility -- always render it and control with `visible`. The component handles its own mount/unmount animation lifecycle internally.
When backdropDismiss is false, the only way to close the modal is programmatically or via Escape key (if onRequestClose is provided). Design your content with an explicit close control.
onShow fires after the enter animation completes, not when visible becomes true. For 'none' animation type, it fires immediately.
Modal uses Portal internally. You do not need to wrap it in a Portal yourself.

metadata.title: NavPanel
metadata.description: Sidebar navigation panel with grouped sections, active item highlighting, and optional header slot.

sections.overview:
NavPanel renders a vertical sidebar navigation component organized into titled sections. Each section contains a list of selectable items. The currently active item is highlighted with a distinct background and text color, and hovering over items provides visual feedback via Pressable.

An optional header slot renders arbitrary content at the top of the panel, separated from the sections by a divider line. NavPanel uses a fixed width by default but can be customized via the `width` prop or `style` overrides.

examples.title: Basic sidebar navigation
With header and multiple sections
Full-height sidebar in layout

criticalRules: The `sections` prop is required. An empty array renders only the background panel with optional header.
`activeId` is compared against each item's `id` for highlighting. If no item matches, no item is highlighted.
NavPanel has a fixed `width` (default 180). It does not grow to fill its container. Override with `style={{ flexGrow: 1 }}` or set an explicit `width` if needed.
The header slot renders any ReactNode. It is not affected by section styling.

metadata.title: PieChart
metadata.description: Pixel-grid pie and donut chart rendered entirely from Box primitives

sections.overview: PieChart rasterizes a pie chart into a 2x2-pixel cell grid using polar coordinate math, then renders each run of same-colored cells as a single Box. This approach requires no canvas or SVG — it works on any target that supports Box with backgroundColor. Setting innerRadius greater than zero hollows the center to produce a donut chart. When interactive mode is enabled, hovering the chart reveals a legend tooltip listing every segment's label, raw value, and percentage.
sections.api:
```tsx
import { PieChart } from '@reactjit/core';
```

Data shape:
```tsx
interface PieChartSegment {
  label: string;  // Segment name shown in the hover tooltip legend
  value: number;  // Numeric value; proportions are computed from the total
  color: Color;   // Fill color for this segment; required per segment
}
```

Props:
| Prop          | Type                   | Default    | Description                                                                     |
|---------------|------------------------|------------|---------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| data          | PieChartSegment[]      | required   | Array of segments; values are proportional, not percentages                     |
| size          | number                 | 120        | Width and height of the chart square in pixels                                  |
| innerRadius   | number                 | 0          | Hollow center radius in pixels; 0 = solid pie, >0 = donut ring                 |
| interactive   | boolean                | false      | Enable hover to show the full segment legend tooltip                            |
| style         | Style                  | undefined  | Additional styles applied to the outer container Box                            |

examples.title: Basic pie chart
Donut chart with innerRadius
Interactive pie with legend tooltip

criticalRules: The chart is rendered as a pixel grid at 2px cell resolution. `size` must be even for the grid to divide cleanly; odd values are floored internally via `Math.floor(size / cellSize)`.
All segment `color` values are required — there is no fallback color. Omitting `color` from a segment will render that segment transparently.
`innerRadius` is specified in the same pixel units as `size`, not as a fraction. A `size={120}` chart with `innerRadius={60}` produces a ring exactly half the radius.
The tooltip appears to the right of the chart (`left: '100%'`). Ensure the chart container has enough horizontal space for the tooltip or it will be clipped.
Rasterization cost scales with `size`. Very large values (>300) will produce a high Box count; prefer sizes in the 80–200px range.
Segments with a value of 0 are included in the legend but produce no visible arc — this is safe.
Do not use Unicode symbols in segment `label` strings.
seeAlso: RadarChart — another pixel-rasterized chart for multi-axis comparisons
BarChart — vertical bars for the same categorical data
HorizontalBarChart — horizontal bars with proportional fills
ChartTooltip — standalone tooltip primitive; PieChart implements its own inline tooltip

metadata.title: PixelArt
metadata.description: Convert Unicode symbols or string art into Box-based geometry for Love2D compatibility

sections.overview:
PixelArt converts Unicode symbols (▶ ⏸ ● ✓ arrows, etc.) and custom string art into rendered Box grids. Love2D's default font does not include Unicode symbols, so PixelArt renders them as colored Box elements instead. It provides a library of built-in symbols (play, pause, check, heart, etc.) and supports custom bitmap art defined as string arrays.

The component and hook use a "filled pixel" convention: any non-space character in the input string represents a filled pixel, which renders as a Box with the specified color. Spaces render as transparent. Per-row coloring is supported for multi-color designs like gradients or layered effects.
sections.api:
```tsx
import { PixelArt, usePixelArt, getPixelArtSymbols } from '@reactjit/core';
```

```tsx
// Component usage
<PixelArt art="play" size={4} color="#ffffff" />

// Hook usage
const playIcon = usePixelArt('play', { size: 4, color: '#ffffff' });
return <Box>{playIcon}</Box>;

// Get list of available symbols
const symbols = getPixelArtSymbols();
```

PixelArt Component Props:
| Prop   | Type              | Default     | Description                                    |
|--------|-------------------|-------------|------------------------------------------------|
| art    | string \| string[] | (required)  | Built-in symbol name OR array of string art    |
| size   | number            | 4           | Pixel size in logical units                    |
| color  | string            | '#ffffff'   | Single fill color for all pixels               |
| colors | string[]          | undefined   | Per-row fill colors (overrides color)          |
| gap    | number            | 0           | Gap between pixels in logical units            |
| style  | Style             | undefined   | Container style overrides                      |

usePixelArt Hook:
```tsx
function usePixelArt(
  art: string | string[],
  options?: PixelArtOptions
): React.ReactElement
```

Options:
| Key    | Type      | Default    | Description                              |
|--------|-----------|------------|------------------------------------------|
| size   | number    | 4          | Pixel size in logical units              |
| color  | string    | '#ffffff'  | Single fill color for all pixels         |
| colors | string[]  | undefined  | Per-row fill colors (overrides color)    |
| gap    | number    | 0          | Gap between pixels in logical units      |

getPixelArtSymbols():
Returns an array of all built-in symbol names.

Built-in Symbols:
play, pause, stop, skip-next, skip-prev, check, close, arrow-up, arrow-down, arrow-left, arrow-right, circle, circle-outline, dot, plus, minus, heart, star, volume, mute, chevron-right, chevron-left, menu

examples.title: Built-in play icon
Custom string art (single color)
Multi-color gradient heart
examples.code:
```tsx
import { PixelArt } from '@reactjit/core';

function Heart() {
  const heartArt = [
    '  ███   ███  ',
    ' █████ █████ ',
    '█████████████',
    ' ███████████ ',
    '  █████████  ',
    '   ███████   ',
    '    █████    ',
    '     ███     ',
    '      █      ',
  ];

  const heartColors = [
    '#ff6b9d',
    '#ff5277',
    '#e94560',
    '#c9184a',
    '#a4133c',
    '#800f2f',
    '#590d22',
    '#3c0919',
    '#1a040c',
  ];

  return <PixelArt art={heartArt} size={12} colors={heartColors} />;
}
```

examples.title: Using the hook for dynamic icons
Icon grid with all built-in symbols
Animated arrow with pixel gap
Custom navigation chevrons
examples.code:
```tsx
import { PixelArt, Box, Text, Pressable } from '@reactjit/core';

function Pagination() {
  return (
    <Box style={{ width: '100%', flexDirection: 'row', justifyContent: 'space-between', padding: 16 }}>
      <Pressable onPress={() => console.log('prev')}>
        <Box style={{ flexDirection: 'row', gap: 8, alignItems: 'center', padding: 8 }}>
          <PixelArt art="chevron-left" size={3} color="#a6adc8" />
          <Text style={{ fontSize: 14, color: '#cdd6f4' }}>Previous</Text>
        </Box>
      </Pressable>

      <Pressable onPress={() => console.log('next')}>
        <Box style={{ flexDirection: 'row', gap: 8, alignItems: 'center', padding: 8 }}>
          <Text style={{ fontSize: 14, color: '#cdd6f4' }}>Next</Text>
          <PixelArt art="chevron-right" size={3} color="#a6adc8" />
        </Box>
      </Pressable>
    </Box>
  );
}
```

criticalRules: **Never put Unicode symbols in `<Text>`** — Characters like ▶ ⏸ █ ● ✓ arrows, dingbats, and geometric shapes won't render in Love2D's default font. Always use PixelArt or Box-based geometry instead. The linter enforces this via `no-unicode-symbol-in-text`.
**Filled pixel convention** — Any non-space character in the art string = filled pixel. Spaces = transparent. Use any character (█, X, #, etc.) for readability — they all render identically.
**Size units are logical** — The `size` prop is in logical units, not pixels. On a 2x DPI screen, `size={4}` renders as 8 physical pixels.
**Colors array falls back** — If `colors` array is shorter than the number of rows, remaining rows use the `color` prop. If neither is provided, defaults to white.
**Memoization is automatic** — The `usePixelArt` hook memoizes the result. Changing `art`, `size`, `color`, `colors`, or `gap` triggers a re-render, but otherwise the element is cached.
**Gap adds spacing** — The `gap` prop adds space between pixels, useful for retro/chunky aesthetics. Default is 0 (no gap).
**Container style wraps** — When using the PixelArt component with a `style` prop, it wraps the rendered grid in an additional Box. When using the hook, no wrapper is added.
seeAlso: Box — The primitive used to render each pixel
Layout rules (CLAUDE.md) — Critical rule #8: no Unicode symbols in Text
Static linter — Enforces no-unicode-symbol-in-text at build time
Storybook NeofetchDemo — Example of complex pixel art (heart logo)

metadata.title: Portal
metadata.description: Render children at the top level of the tree, outside normal component hierarchy. Used for modals, dropdowns, tooltips, and overlays.

sections.overview: Portal renders children at the top level of the render tree, escaping the normal parent-child hierarchy. This is essential for UI elements that need to appear above all other content, such as modals, tooltips, dropdowns, and context menus. PortalHost is the container that receives portal content in native mode. In web mode, Portal falls back to ReactDOM.createPortal when no PortalHost is present.
sections.api:
```tsx
import { Portal, PortalHost } from '@reactjit/core';
```

**Portal Props**

| Prop | Type | Default | Description |
|------|------|---------|-------------|
| children | ReactNode | required | Content to render at the top level |
| name | string | undefined | Optional identifier for the portal (auto-generated if omitted) |

**PortalHost Props**

| Prop | Type | Default | Description |
|------|------|---------|-------------|
| children | ReactNode | required | The main app content (renders below portals) |

examples.title: Basic Portal Usage
Setting Up PortalHost in Native Mode
Named Portals
Portal with Modal Component

platformNotes.Love2D:
- Portals render with zIndex: 1000 in absolute positioning
- PortalHost must wrap your root component in native-main.tsx
- Multiple portals stack in registration order (later portals appear on top)
- Portal content is positioned absolutely relative to the viewport
platformNotes.Web:
- Falls back to ReactDOM.createPortal when no PortalHost is present
- Creates a fixed-position div appended to document.body
- PortalHost is optional in web mode but recommended for consistency
- Supports CSS transitions and transforms on portal content
Terminal / Grid targets:
- Portals render in absolute coordinates over the character grid
- Use explicit positioning (top, left) to place portal content
- PortalHost required in all grid-based targets
- Z-ordering follows registration order

criticalRules: In native mode (Love2D, terminal, grid targets), you MUST wrap your root component in PortalHost for portals to work
Portal children should use absolute positioning (position: 'absolute') with explicit coordinates
Portal content renders at zIndex: 1000 by default — override via style if needed
Multiple portals stack in registration order (first registered = bottom, last = top)
The name prop is optional but recommended for debugging and stable portal identity
Portal content is NOT clipped by parent containers — it always renders at the top level
In web mode without PortalHost, Portal automatically falls back to ReactDOM.createPortal
Do not nest PortalHost components — only one PortalHost per app root
Portal children receive the same dimensions as the viewport (width: '100%', height: '100%')
Use Portal for overlays, modals, dropdowns, tooltips, context menus, and notifications
Portal does NOT handle backdrop clicks or Escape key dismissal — use Modal component for that
Portals persist until unmounted — conditional rendering ({showPortal && <Portal>...</Portal>}) controls visibility
seeAlso: Modal — Full-screen overlay with backdrop and animations (uses Portal internally)
ContextMenu — Right-click menu system (uses Portal internally)
Pressable — For interactive elements inside portal content
ScrollView — Can be used inside portal content for scrollable overlays
useWindowDimensions — Get viewport size for centering portal content
Box positioning — position: 'absolute', top, left, right, bottom for portal layout

metadata.title: Pressable
metadata.description: Interaction component with press, long press, and hover state tracking

sections.overview:
Pressable is a high-level interaction component that tracks pressed, hovered, and focused states. It wraps Box internally and provides callbacks for press, long press, hover, and focus events. Unlike Box's raw event handlers, Pressable manages interaction lifecycle: it fires onPress only on a clean release (not when a long press fires), supports configurable long press delay, and exposes interaction state to children via a render prop pattern.

Pressable supports both static and function-based style and children props. When passed a function, it receives a PressableState object with { pressed, hovered, focused } booleans, letting you build reactive UI without manual state management. It also supports hitSlop to expand the touch target beyond visible bounds, and integrates keyboard accessibility (Enter/Space triggers press).

examples.title: Simple button
examples.code:
```tsx
<Pressable
  onPress={() => console.log('pressed')}
  style={{ padding: 12, backgroundColor: '#89b4fa', borderRadius: 8 }}
>
  <Text style={{ fontSize: 14, color: '#1e1e2e' }}>Submit</Text>
</Pressable>
```

examples.title: Reactive style and children via render props
Long press with expanded hit area
Disabled state

platformNotes.Love2D: In native mode, Pressable wraps a Box and passes interaction state and hitSlop as props to the host element. The Lua event system handles hit testing with hitSlop expansion. Long press timing runs in JavaScript via setTimeout.
platformNotes.Web: Renders as a <div> with pointer event handlers, tabIndex for keyboard focus, and role attribute for accessibility. Disabled state sets cursor: not-allowed and opacity: 0.5. HitSlop is implemented via negative margin + positive padding trick to expand the clickable area without changing visual bounds. Keyboard accessibility: Enter and Space trigger press.
platformNotes.Terminal: Not directly available on grid targets. Build equivalent interaction with the grid target's own event system.

criticalRules: onPress fires only on release and only if onLongPress did not fire during the same gesture. If you need to know immediately when the finger goes down, use onPressIn.
The children prop is required. Pressable with no children renders an empty, invisible touch target.
When disabled is true, all event handlers are suppressed and state resets to { pressed: false, hovered: false, focused: false }.
If pointer leaves the element while pressed, the press is canceled (pressed becomes false, long press timer is cleared).
hitSlop expands the interactive area, not the visual area. The element looks the same but responds to touches outside its visible bounds.

metadata.title: ProgressBar
metadata.description: Progress indicator bar that fills proportionally from 0 to 1, with optional label, animation, and interactive hover tooltip

sections.overview:
ProgressBar displays a horizontal fill bar representing progress from 0 to 1. The fill width is rendered as a percentage of the track width. An optional label shows the percentage or custom text overlay, and the `animated` prop uses `useSpring` to smoothly interpolate value changes.

The component uses Box and Text primitives, making it compatible with all targets. The track and fill both use pill-shaped border radii derived from the height. When interactive mode is enabled, hovering the bar reveals a ChartTooltip showing the percentage and exact fill fraction.
sections.api:
```tsx
import { ProgressBar } from '@reactjit/core';
```

Props:
| Prop        | Type    | Default    | Description                                                              |
|-------------|---------|------------|--------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| value       | number  | required   | Progress value from 0 to 1; clamped internally to [0, 1]                 |
| color       | Color   | '#3b82f6'  | Fill bar color                                                           |
| trackColor  | Color   | '#1e293b'  | Background track color                                                   |
| height      | number  | 8          | Bar height in pixels                                                     |
| showLabel   | boolean | false      | Show percentage label overlaid on the bar (requires height >= 14)        |
| label       | string  | undefined  | Custom label text; defaults to the computed percentage (e.g. "65%")     |
| animated    | boolean | false      | Animate value changes with a spring (stiffness: 120, damping: 20)       |
| interactive | boolean | false      | Enable hover to show a ChartTooltip with percentage and fill fraction    |
| style       | Style   | undefined  | Additional styles applied to the outer container Box                     |

examples.title: Basic progress bar
Progress bar with visible label
Animated progress with custom label and interactive tooltip

criticalRules: The `value` prop is required and is clamped to [0, 1] internally. Values outside this range are silently clamped — no error is thrown.
ProgressBar needs an explicit `width` either via the `style` prop or from its parent layout. Without width the track collapses to zero.
The `showLabel` prop is silently ignored when `height < 14` — the text physically does not fit within the bar at that height.
When `animated` is true, the fill width is driven by `useSpring` targeting `value * 100`. The hook is called unconditionally, so animated cannot be toggled conditionally at runtime.
Label text is positioned absolutely and centered over the full track width. It is always white (`color: '#ffffff'`) with `fontWeight: 'bold'`.
Label font size is `Math.max(9, height - 4)` — it scales with bar height but never goes below 9px.
When both `label` and `showLabel` are provided, the custom `label` string is shown instead of the computed percentage.
`interactive` adds a ChartTooltip showing the percentage and the raw clamped fraction (e.g. "0.75 / 1.00"). Hover requires pointer support.
seeAlso: BarChart — multi-bar vertical chart for comparative data
ChartTooltip — standalone tooltip primitive used internally by this component

metadata.title: RadarChart
metadata.description: Pixel-grid radar (spider) chart for comparing multi-axis performance profiles

sections.overview: RadarChart rasterizes a spider/radar chart into a 2x2-pixel cell grid. Each axis radiates from the center at equal angular intervals and data values are plotted along each axis, normalized either to a per-axis maximum or to the global maximum across all values. The region formed by connecting those points is filled with the chart color at 60% opacity. Concentric ring grid lines at 25%, 50%, 75%, and 100% are rendered in a separate grid color. When interactive mode is enabled, hovering the chart shows a tooltip listing each axis label, its raw value, and the normalized percentage.
sections.api:
```tsx
import { RadarChart } from '@reactjit/core';
```

Data shape:
```tsx
interface RadarChartAxis {
  label: string;  // Axis name shown in the hover tooltip
  max?:  number;  // Per-axis maximum for normalization; defaults to global max across all data values
}
```

Props:
| Prop        | Type              | Default      | Description                                                                                   |
|-------------|-------------------|--------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| axes        | RadarChartAxis[]  | required     | Axis definitions; length must equal data.length                                               |
| data        | number[]          | required     | One raw value per axis, in the same order as axes                                             |
| size        | number            | 120          | Width and height of the chart square in pixels                                                |
| color       | Color             | '#3b82f6'    | Fill color for the data polygon (rendered at 60% opacity)                                     |
| gridColor   | Color             | '#1e293b'    | Color for concentric ring grid lines and axis spokes                                          |
| interactive | boolean           | false        | Enable hover to show the per-axis value breakdown tooltip                                     |
| style       | Style             | undefined    | Additional styles applied to the outer container Box                                          |

examples.title: Player stats profile
System health with per-axis maximums
Interactive profile comparison widget

criticalRules: `axes` and `data` must have the same length. Extra data values beyond the axis count are ignored; missing data values default to 0 for normalization purposes.

When a per-axis `max` is omitted, that axis is normalized against the global maximum of the entire `data` array. Mix explicit and implicit maxima with care — a single outlier value can compress all other axes.
The rendered polygon fill uses 60% opacity (hardcoded). The `color` prop controls the fill color only; the opacity is not configurable.
Grid ring lines are at 25%, 50%, 75%, and 100% of the radius. They cannot be configured or hidden via props.
The tooltip appears to the right of the chart (`left: '100%'`). Ensure the parent container has horizontal room, or the tooltip will be clipped.
Rasterization cost scales with `size`. Prefer sizes in the 80–200px range.
Axes are arranged clockwise starting from the top (12 o'clock position). The first axis in the `axes` array is always at the top.
Do not use Unicode symbols in axis `label` strings.
seeAlso: PieChart — another pixel-rasterized chart for proportional data
BarChart — simpler vertical bars for comparing the same axes
HorizontalBarChart — horizontal orientation for the same kind of multi-value comparison
ChartTooltip — standalone tooltip primitive; RadarChart implements its own inline tooltip

metadata.title: Radio / RadioGroup
metadata.description: Radio button with RadioGroup for exclusive single-selection within a group.

sections.overview:
Radio and RadioGroup work together for exclusive selection. RadioGroup wraps a set of Radio components and provides shared context so that selecting one Radio automatically deselects the others.

RadioGroup supports both controlled and uncontrolled modes. In controlled mode, pass `value` and respond to `onValueChange`. In uncontrolled mode, optionally set `defaultValue`. Each Radio reads the group state from React context and renders a circular indicator that fills when its value matches the group's selected value.
sections.api:
Import: import { Radio, RadioGroup } from '@reactjit/core';

Usage:
```tsx
<RadioGroup value={selected} onValueChange={setSelected}>
  <Radio value="a" label="Option A" />
  <Radio value="b" label="Option B" />
  <Radio value="c" label="Option C" />
</RadioGroup>
```

RadioGroup Props:

| Prop          | Type                      | Default | Description                                |
|---------------|---------------------------|---------|--------------------------------------------|
| value         | string                    | -       | Controlled selected value                  |
| defaultValue  | string                    | -       | Initial value in uncontrolled mode         |
| onValueChange | (value: string) => void   | -       | Called when selection changes               |
| disabled      | boolean                   | false   | Disables all Radio children                |
| children      | ReactNode                 | -       | Radio components (required)                |
| style         | Style                     | -       | Container style overrides                  |

Radio Props:

| Prop           | Type    | Default    | Description                               |
|----------------|---------|------------|-------------------------------------------|
| value          | string  | (required) | Value this radio represents               |
| label          | string  | -          | Text label rendered beside the circle     |
| disabled       | boolean | false      | Disables this specific radio              |
| size           | number  | 20         | Circle diameter in pixels                 |
| color          | Color   | '#3b82f6'  | Border and inner dot color when selected  |
| uncheckedColor | Color   | '#6b7280'  | Border color when not selected            |
| style          | Style   | -          | Container style overrides                 |

examples.title: Controlled radio group
examples.code:
```tsx
const [plan, setPlan] = useState('free');

<RadioGroup value={plan} onValueChange={setPlan}>
  <Radio value="free" label="Free" />
  <Radio value="pro" label="Pro" />
  <Radio value="enterprise" label="Enterprise" />
</RadioGroup>
```

examples.title: Uncontrolled with custom styling
examples.code:
```tsx
<RadioGroup defaultValue="dark">
  <Radio value="light" label="Light" color="#f59e0b" size={24} />
  <Radio value="dark" label="Dark" color="#f59e0b" size={24} />
</RadioGroup>
```

examples.title: Disabled group
examples.code:
```tsx
<RadioGroup value="locked" disabled>
  <Radio value="locked" label="Locked option" />
  <Radio value="other" label="Other option" />
</RadioGroup>
```

criticalRules: Radio MUST be wrapped in a RadioGroup. A Radio rendered outside a RadioGroup will not respond to clicks.
The `value` prop on Radio is required and must be a string. It identifies which option this Radio represents within the group.
In controlled mode, the RadioGroup does not update its selection unless the parent updates the `value` prop in response to `onValueChange`.

metadata.title: Scene
metadata.description: A 3D viewport component that participates in the 2D layout system

sections.overview: Scene is the root container for all 3D rendering in ReactJIT. It creates an off-screen canvas that the Love2D painter composites into the 2D layout at the node's computed position. Children of Scene must be 3D elements: Camera, Mesh, AmbientLight, and DirectionalLight. On web targets, Scene renders a placeholder div because 3D support is Love2D only.
sections.api:
```tsx
import { Scene } from '@reactjit/3d';
```

Props:
| Prop | Type | Default | Description |
|------|------|---------|-------------|
| style | Style | — | Style applied to the viewport box in the 2D layout (width, height, etc.) |
| backgroundColor | string | '#12121b' | Background color of the 3D viewport as a hex string |
| stars | boolean | — | Render procedural stars in the background |
| orbitControls | boolean | — | Enable Lua-side orbit controls — drag to rotate all meshes with zero latency |
| children | ReactNode | — | 3D child elements: Camera, Mesh, AmbientLight, DirectionalLight |

examples.title: Minimal scene with a box mesh
Scene with starfield background and orbit controls
Scene embedded in a 2D layout

criticalRules: Scene must be given an explicit width and height via the style prop — it does not auto-size from its 3D children
All direct children must be 3D elements (Camera, Mesh, AmbientLight, DirectionalLight); mixing 2D Box/Text children inside Scene is not supported
Scene renders nothing useful on web targets — it shows a placeholder div with text
Only one Scene can own a given region of the layout; nested Scenes are not supported
orbitControls operates on the Lua side with zero JS round-trip latency; rotating via orbitControls does not fire React state updates
seeAlso: Camera — defines the viewpoint inside a Scene
Mesh — renderable 3D geometry or model inside a Scene
AmbientLight — uniform base illumination inside a Scene
DirectionalLight — sun-like directional light inside a Scene

metadata.title: ScrollView
metadata.description: Scrollable container with imperative scrollTo API

sections.overview:
ScrollView is a scrollable container that clips its children to its bounds and allows scrolling to reveal overflow content. It supports both vertical (default) and horizontal scrolling, optional scroll indicators, and scroll lifecycle callbacks. ScrollView also exposes an imperative scrollTo() method via React.forwardRef, letting you programmatically scroll to a specific position with optional animation.

In web mode, ScrollView renders as a `<div>` with overflow: auto and CSS-based scrolling. In native mode, it outputs a `'View'` host element with overflow: 'scroll', and the Lua layout engine handles scissor clipping, scroll translation, scrollbar rendering, and wheel event processing.

examples.title: Basic vertical scroll
examples.code:
```tsx
<ScrollView style={{ width: '100%', height: 200 }}>
  {Array.from({ length: 20 }, (_, i) => (
    <Box key={i} style={{ padding: 12, borderBottomWidth: 1, borderColor: '#313244' }}>
      <Text style={{ fontSize: 14, color: '#cdd6f4' }}>{`Item ${i + 1}`}</Text>
    </Box>
  ))}
</ScrollView>
```

examples.title: Horizontal scroll
examples.code:
```tsx
<ScrollView horizontal style={{ width: '100%', height: 100 }}>
  {Array.from({ length: 10 }, (_, i) => (
    <Box key={i} style={{ width: 120, height: 80, margin: 8, backgroundColor: '#313244', borderRadius: 8 }}>
      <Text style={{ fontSize: 12, color: '#cdd6f4' }}>{`Card ${i + 1}`}</Text>
    </Box>
  ))}
</ScrollView>
```

examples.title: Imperative scrollTo
examples.code:
```tsx
const scrollRef = React.useRef<ScrollViewRef>(null);

<Box style={{ width: '100%', height: '100%' }}>
  <Pressable onPress={() => scrollRef.current?.scrollTo({ y: 0, animated: true })}>
    <Text style={{ fontSize: 14, color: '#89b4fa' }}>Back to top</Text>
  </Pressable>

  <ScrollView ref={scrollRef} style={{ width: '100%', flexGrow: 1 }}>
    {/* long content */}
  </ScrollView>
</Box>
```

examples.title: Hidden scrollbar with scroll tracking

platformNotes.Love2D: The Lua layout engine implements scroll by translating child positions within a scissor-clipped region. Wheel events adjust scroll position. Scroll indicators are drawn as thin overlay rectangles by the painter. The imperative scrollTo() updates style.scrollX/scrollY which the layout engine reads on the next pass. Animated scrolling is handled natively in Lua.
platformNotes.Web: Uses native browser scrolling via overflow: auto. Horizontal mode sets overflowX: auto and overflowY: hidden (and vice versa for vertical). showScrollIndicator: false hides the scrollbar using scrollbarWidth: 'none' (Firefox) and msOverflowStyle: 'none' (legacy Edge). Webkit/Blink scrollbar hiding requires CSS pseudo-element styling not achievable via inline styles alone. The imperative scrollTo() calls element.scrollTo() with behavior: 'smooth' when animated is true. onScrollEnd is detected via 150ms debounce after the last scroll event.
platformNotes.Terminal: Not directly available on grid targets. Grid layouts are fixed-size and do not scroll. For large datasets on grid targets, use pagination or FlatList-style windowing.

criticalRules: ScrollView MUST have a constrained size (explicit width/height or parent constraints). Without a known size, there is nothing to overflow and nothing to scroll.
Do not nest ScrollViews in the same axis. A vertical ScrollView inside another vertical ScrollView creates ambiguous scroll targets. Cross-axis nesting (horizontal inside vertical) is acceptable.
When horizontal is true, the default flexDirection becomes 'row'. Children lay out left to right.
Use FlatList instead of ScrollView for long lists of uniform items. ScrollView renders all children; FlatList virtualizes off-screen items for performance.
The onScrollEnd callback fires after scrolling stops for 150ms. It is debounced, not instantaneous.

metadata.title: Select
metadata.description: Dropdown selection component with web-native select and native accordion-style expand/collapse.

sections.overview:
Select provides a dropdown-style selection from a list of options. Each option has a `label` (displayed) and a `value` (used programmatically).

Select supports both controlled and uncontrolled modes. In web mode, it renders a native HTML `<select>` element with custom styling. In native mode (Love2D), it renders an inline accordion: clicking the header toggles the option list open or closed, and clicking an option selects it and collapses the list.
sections.api:
Import: import { Select } from '@reactjit/core';

SelectOption type:
```ts
interface SelectOption {
  label: string;
  value: string;
}
```

Usage:
```tsx
<Select
  value={selected}
  onValueChange={setSelected}
  options={[
    { label: 'Red', value: 'red' },
    { label: 'Green', value: 'green' },
    { label: 'Blue', value: 'blue' },
  ]}
/>
```

Props:

| Prop          | Type                      | Default      | Description                               |
|---------------|---------------------------|--------------|-------------------------------------------|
| value         | string                    | -            | Controlled selected value                 |
| defaultValue  | string                    | -            | Initial value in uncontrolled mode        |
| onValueChange | (value: string) => void   | -            | Called when selection changes              |
| options       | SelectOption[]            | (required)   | Array of { label, value } options         |
| placeholder   | string                    | 'Select...'  | Text shown when no option is selected     |
| disabled      | boolean                   | false        | Disables interaction and dims opacity     |
| style         | Style                     | -            | Container style overrides                 |
| color         | Color                     | '#3b82f6'    | Accent color for open state border        |

examples.title: Controlled select
Uncontrolled with default
examples.code:
```tsx
<Select
  defaultValue="dark"
  options={[
    { label: 'Light', value: 'light' },
    { label: 'Dark', value: 'dark' },
    { label: 'System', value: 'system' },
  ]}
/>
```

examples.title: Disabled select
examples.code:
```tsx
<Select
  value="locked"
  disabled
  options={[{ label: 'Locked', value: 'locked' }]}
/>
```

criticalRules: The `options` prop is required. Passing an empty array renders a header with the placeholder but no options to select.
In native mode, the open/close state is internal. There is no prop to force the dropdown open.
When `disabled` is true, opacity is set to 0.5 and both the toggle and option clicks are ignored.

metadata.title: SemanticTerminal
metadata.description: Visual terminal with semantic row classification, session recording, and playback support

sections.overview:
SemanticTerminal is a visual capability node that renders a terminal whose output rows are classified into semantic tokens by a Lua-side classifier. Each row is labeled — "command", "error", "assistant_text", "tool" — and rendered in a token-appropriate color. The result is a terminal that communicates meaning through color, not just raw ANSI escape codes.

Unlike the non-visual Terminal component, SemanticTerminal renders and accepts a style prop. Use flexGrow: 1 to fill available space — sizing is required because this is a visual node, not a text container.

Two modes are supported. In live mode (default), SemanticTerminal spawns a PTY and feeds output through the classifier in real time. In playback mode, it replays a recorded .rec.lua session through the same classification pipeline. Switching between modes is a prop change — the rendering pipeline is identical.

The framework ships two classifiers: basic covers any shell session with seven tokens (command, output, error, success, heading, separator, progress). claude_code covers the full Claude Code CLI with 25+ tokens for conversation turns, tool calls, thinking, permissions, diffs, and all other UI states.

For controlled state — tracking classified rows, reading graph state, driving playback — use the useSemanticTerminal hook and spread terminalProps onto this component.
sections.api:
```tsx
import { SemanticTerminal } from '@reactjit/core';
import type { SemanticTerminalProps } from '@reactjit/core';
```

Props:
| Prop            | Type                                                      | Default   | Description                                                       |
|-----------------|-----------------------------------------------------------|-----------|-------------------------------------------------------------------|
| mode            | 'live' \| 'playback'                                      | 'live'    | Live PTY session or recorded session playback                     |
| command         | string                                                    | 'bash'    | Command to run in live mode                                       |
| args            | string                                                    | undefined | Command arguments (space-separated)                               |
| cwd             | string                                                    | undefined | Working directory                                                 |
| rows            | number                                                    | 40        | Terminal rows                                                     |
| cols            | number                                                    | 120       | Terminal columns                                                  |
| classifier      | string                                                    | 'basic'   | Classifier name from lua/classifiers/ (e.g. 'claude_code')       |
| recording       | boolean                                                   | false     | Record PTY output for later playback                              |
| playbackSrc     | string                                                    | undefined | Path to .rec.lua file for playback mode                           |
| playbackSpeed   | number                                                    | 1.0       | Playback speed multiplier (0.25 = slow, 2.0 = fast)              |
| showTokens      | boolean                                                   | false     | Show token type badges on each row                                |
| showGraph       | boolean                                                   | false     | Show semantic graph panel (turn tree)                             |
| showTimeline    | boolean                                                   | false     | Show timeline scrubber (playback mode)                            |
| style           | Style                                                     | undefined | Layout style — use flexGrow: 1 to fill available space            |
| onClassifiedRow | (event: { row: number; token: string; text: string }) => void | undefined | Fires when a row is classified                          |
| onGraphUpdate   | (event: { nodeCount: number; turnCount: number }) => void | undefined | Fires when the semantic graph updates                             |
| onStateChange   | (event: { mode: string; streaming: boolean }) => void     | undefined | Fires when session state changes                                  |
| onRecordingDone | (event: { path: string; frames: number }) => void         | undefined | Fires when a recording is saved                                   |
| onPlaybackEnd   | (event: {}) => void                                       | undefined | Fires when playback reaches the end                               |

examples.title: Live classified terminal (one-liner)
Claude Code with claude_code classifier + token badges
Playback with hook for full transport controls

platformNotes.Love2D: Full support — live PTY, recording, playback, and all classifiers.

criticalRules: Always set style={{ flexGrow: 1 }} or an explicit height. SemanticTerminal is a visual node — it has no implicit height and will collapse to zero if unsized.
Classifiers are files in lua/classifiers/. The classifier prop is the filename without .lua. The framework ships 'basic' and 'claude_code'.
playbackSrc must be a .rec.lua file path (absolute or relative to the app). The recording prop on a live session creates these files.
Recording stores raw PTY bytes. Classification happens at playback time — you can replay a recording with a different classifier without re-recording.
Use the useSemanticTerminal hook to access classifiedRows, graphState, playerState, and transport controls. SemanticTerminal alone only fires events.

metadata.title: Slider
metadata.description: Draggable range input with track and thumb for value selection

sections.overview:
Slider provides a draggable input for selecting a numeric value within a range. It renders a track with an active fill and a draggable thumb. Slider supports both controlled (value prop) and uncontrolled (defaultValue prop) modes, step snapping, vertical orientation, and full visual customization of track and thumb colors and sizes.

In web mode, Slider handles drag via mouse events with global listeners for reliable tracking outside the element bounds. In native mode, it uses the Box drag event system (onDragStart, onDrag, onDragEnd) for pointer tracking. The native renderer uses a three-segment flex layout (active track, thumb, inactive track) rather than absolute positioning for compatibility with the Lua layout engine.
sections.api:
```tsx
import { Slider } from '@reactjit/core';
```

```tsx
const [volume, setVolume] = useState(0.5);

<Slider
  value={volume}
  onValueChange={setVolume}
  minimumValue={0}
  maximumValue={1}
  step={0.1}
  style={{ width: 200 }}
/>
```

Props:
| Prop             | Type                          | Default     | Description                                |
|------------------|-------------------------------|-------------|--------------------------------------------|
| value            | number                        | undefined   | Controlled value (overrides internal state)|
| defaultValue     | number                        | 0           | Initial value for uncontrolled mode        |
| onValueChange    | (value: number) => void       | undefined   | Called on every value change during drag    |
| onSlidingStart   | () => void                    | undefined   | Called when drag begins                    |
| onSlidingEnd     | (value: number) => void       | undefined   | Called when drag ends with final value     |
| minimumValue     | number                        | 0           | Minimum allowed value                      |
| maximumValue     | number                        | 1           | Maximum allowed value                      |
| step             | number                        | undefined   | Snap value to step increments              |
| disabled         | boolean                       | false       | Disable interaction                        |
| style            | Style                         | undefined   | Container style (width sets track length)  |
| trackColor       | Color                         | '#333333'   | Inactive track color                       |
| activeTrackColor | Color                         | '#4A90D9'   | Active (filled) track color                |
| thumbColor       | Color                         | '#ffffff'   | Thumb color                                |
| thumbSize        | number                        | 20          | Thumb diameter in pixels                   |
| trackHeight      | number                        | 4           | Track height in pixels                     |
| vertical         | boolean                       | false       | Vertical orientation                       |

examples.title: Basic horizontal slider
Stepped slider with custom colors
Uncontrolled slider with callbacks
Vertical slider
Disabled slider
examples.code:
```tsx
<Slider
  value={0.3}
  disabled
  style={{ width: 200 }}
/>
```

platformNotes.Love2D: Native mode uses a three-segment flex row layout: active track segment, thumb circle, inactive track segment. This avoids position: absolute which has limitations in the Lua layout engine for this use case. Drag tracking uses the Box drag event system (onDragStart, onDrag, onDragEnd) with totalDeltaX/totalDeltaY from the LoveEvent. Track width defaults to 200px if not specified via style.width. The value-at-drag-start is captured and deltas are applied relative to it for smooth, predictable dragging.
platformNotes.Web: Uses absolute positioning for the active track fill and thumb within the track container. Mouse events use global document listeners (mousemove, mouseup) during drag for reliable tracking even when the cursor leaves the slider bounds. Disabled state shows cursor: not-allowed on the thumb.
platformNotes.Terminal: Not available on grid targets. For value selection on character-grid renderers, build a custom component using keyboard input.

criticalRules: In controlled mode (value prop provided), you MUST update the value in onValueChange. If you do not, the thumb will not move because the component re-renders with the stale controlled value.
The style.width prop (or style.height for vertical) determines the track length. Without it, the slider defaults to 200px. Always set an explicit size for predictable layout.
step snapping rounds to the nearest step increment. A step of 0.1 with range 0-1 produces values: 0, 0.1, 0.2, ..., 1.0. The snapped value is clamped to [minimumValue, maximumValue].

onValueChange fires on every drag movement (potentially many times per second). Do not perform expensive operations in this callback. Use onSlidingEnd for final-value operations like network requests or heavy state updates.
onSlidingEnd fires once when the drag gesture completes. Its argument is the final value after step snapping and clamping.
Disabled sliders render at 50% opacity and ignore all interaction.

metadata.title: Spacer
metadata.description: Flexible spacing component that fills available space or creates fixed gaps

sections.overview: Spacer is a layout utility component that creates space between elements. When used without props, it uses flexGrow to fill all available space in its parent container. When given a size prop, it creates a fixed-height gap. Spacer is the idiomatic way to push elements apart without manual margin calculations.
sections.api:
import { Spacer } from '@reactjit/core';

Props:
| Prop | Type | Default | Description |
|------|------|---------|-------------|
| size | number | undefined | Fixed height in pixels. If omitted, Spacer uses flexGrow: 1 to fill available space |

examples.title: Push footer to bottom
examples.code:
<Box style={{ width: '100%', height: '100%', flexDirection: 'column' }}>
  <Text fontSize={24}>Header</Text>
  <Spacer />
  <Text fontSize={16}>Footer</Text>
</Box>

examples.title: Fixed gap between elements
examples.code:
<Box style={{ flexDirection: 'column' }}>
  <Text fontSize={18}>First Item</Text>
  <Spacer size={20} />
  <Text fontSize={18}>Second Item</Text>
</Box>

examples.title: Multiple spacers distribute space
examples.code:
<Box style={{ width: '100%', height: 200, flexDirection: 'column' }}>
  <Text fontSize={16}>Top</Text>
  <Spacer />
  <Text fontSize={16}>Middle</Text>
  <Spacer />
  <Text fontSize={16}>Bottom</Text>
</Box>

examples.title: Horizontal spacing in row
examples.code:
<Box style={{ width: '100%', flexDirection: 'row', alignItems: 'center' }}>
  <Text fontSize={16}>Left</Text>
  <Spacer />
  <Text fontSize={16}>Right</Text>
</Box>

criticalRules: Spacer with no size prop requires a parent with known dimensions — flexGrow only works when there's space to fill
In row containers, Spacer fills horizontal space (width); in column containers, it fills vertical space (height)
Fixed-size Spacers (with size prop) behave like Box with explicit height — they don't grow
Never use Spacer inside auto-sized containers — flexGrow collapses to zero when there's no available space
Multiple flexGrow Spacers in the same container share available space equally
Prefer Spacer over manual marginTop/marginBottom for cleaner, more maintainable layouts
Spacer is effectively a shorthand for Box with either flexGrow: 1 or a fixed height — use Box directly if you need additional styling
seeAlso: Box — primitive container that Spacer is built on
Divider — visual separator line (vs. Spacer's invisible space)
FlexRow/FlexColumn — helper components that often contain Spacers
Layout Rules — understanding flexGrow behavior and container sizing

metadata.title: Sparkline
metadata.description: Mini inline chart rendered as vertical bars normalized to the min/max of the data, with optional interactive hover tooltip

sections.overview:
Sparkline renders a compact inline chart from an array of numbers. Each data point is represented as a thin vertical bar whose height is proportional to the value's position between the dataset's minimum and maximum. This creates a quick visual representation of trends or distributions within a small footprint.

Unlike BarChart, Sparkline is designed for inline use where labels and axes are unnecessary. It automatically computes bar widths to fill the given width and normalizes heights against the data range. When interactive mode is enabled, hovering a bar dims all others and shows a ChartTooltip with the raw value and index.
sections.api:
```tsx
import { Sparkline } from '@reactjit/core';
```

Props:
| Prop          | Type                                                    | Default    | Description                                                       |
|---------------|---------------------------------------------------------|------------|-------------------------------------------------------------------|
| data          | number[]                                                | required   | Array of numeric data points                                      |
| width         | number                                                  | 80         | Total chart width in pixels                                       |
| height        | number                                                  | 24         | Total chart height in pixels                                      |
| color         | Color                                                   | '#3b82f6'  | Bar color                                                         |
| style         | Style                                                   | undefined  | Additional styles applied to the container Box                    |
| interactive   | boolean                                                 | false      | Enable hover highlighting and per-bar ChartTooltip                |
| onPointHover  | (index: number \| null, value: number \| null) => void  | undefined  | Called when the pointer enters or leaves a bar                    |
| onPointPress  | (index: number, value: number) => void                  | undefined  | Called when a bar is clicked (requires interactive: true)         |

examples.title: Basic sparkline
Custom size and color
Inline with text and interactive hover callback

criticalRules: The `data` prop is required. An empty array renders an empty placeholder Box at the specified width and height.
Sparkline normalizes to the data's own min/max range, not 0-based. If all values are equal, the range defaults to 1, rendering all bars at minimum height (1px).
The component has a fixed size based on `width` and `height`. It does not grow or shrink with its container. Pass `style` to override if needed.
Bar widths are computed as `Math.max(1, (width - (data.length - 1) * barGap) / data.length)` where `barGap` is 1px. Very long data arrays in a narrow width may compress bars to 1px minimum.
For real-time data, pass a new array reference to `data` on each update. Sparkline re-renders whenever the data reference changes.
`onPointPress` only fires when `interactive` is also `true`.
Hover events require pointer support — not available on all targets.
seeAlso: BarChart — full-featured vertical bar chart with labels and axis context
ChartTooltip — standalone tooltip primitive used internally by this component

metadata.title: StackedBarChart
metadata.description: Vertical stacked bar chart for visualizing multi-series data in proportional segments

sections.overview: StackedBarChart renders a column of vertical bars where each bar is subdivided into colored segments representing multiple data series. Series heights are proportional to the maximum column total across all bars, so every bar uses the full chart height at most. Optional column labels appear below the bars. When interactive mode is enabled, hovering a column dims all others and shows a tooltip with the column total and per-series breakdown.
sections.api:
```tsx
import { StackedBarChart } from '@reactjit/core';
```

Data shape:
```tsx
interface StackedBarChartSeries {
  label: string;   // Series name shown in the hover tooltip
  color: Color;    // Color for this series' segment
  data: number[];  // One value per bar column; must be the same length across all series
}
```

Props:
| Prop        | Type                      | Default   | Description                                                                            |
|-------------|---------------------------|-----------|----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| series      | StackedBarChartSeries[]   | required  | Array of data series; each series provides one segment per bar column                 |
| labels      | string[]                  | undefined | Optional column labels rendered below the bars (one per bar column)                   |
| height      | number                    | 120       | Chart height in pixels; the tallest bar fills this exactly                             |
| barWidth    | number                    | undefined | Fixed pixel width per bar; when omitted, bars flex to fill the container              |
| gap         | number                    | 8         | Horizontal gap between bars in pixels                                                  |
| showLabels  | boolean                   | true      | Whether to render the column label row beneath the bars                                |
| style       | Style                     | undefined | Additional styles applied to the outer container Box                                   |
| interactive | boolean                   | false     | Enable hover highlighting and per-column tooltips                                      |

examples.title: Quarterly revenue by product line
Fluid-width bars filling the container
Interactive with breakdown tooltip

criticalRules: All series must have `data` arrays of equal length. The number of bars is taken from `series[0].data.length`; extra values in shorter series are ignored and missing values in shorter series default to 0.
Segments are rendered bottom-to-top by reversing the series array internally. The first series in your array will appear at the top of each bar visually.
When `barWidth` is omitted, bars use `flexGrow: 1` and fill the parent container width. The parent container must have an explicit width for this to produce visible output.
When `barWidth` is set, the chart computes its own total width as `numBars * barWidth + (numBars - 1) * gap`. You do not need to set `width` on the chart itself.
`showLabels` only renders text if `labels` is also provided. Providing `labels` without `showLabels` does render them; set `showLabels={false}` to suppress the label row entirely.
Segments with a computed height of 0 are omitted from the render tree — this is safe and avoids invisible 0-height boxes.
Do not use Unicode symbols in any `<Text>` within this component or in labels passed as data.
seeAlso: HorizontalBarChart — horizontal orientation for the same kind of comparison
ChartTooltip — standalone tooltip primitive used internally by this component
LineChart — trend lines across the same time-series data

metadata.title: Switch
metadata.description: A toggle switch with on/off states, providing a sliding thumb on a colored track.

sections.overview:
Switch is a toggle control that slides a thumb along a track to indicate an on/off state. The track changes color based on the current value, and the thumb moves to the left (off) or right (on) edge.

Switch supports both controlled and uncontrolled modes. In controlled mode, pass `value` and respond to `onValueChange`. In uncontrolled mode, omit `value` and optionally set `defaultValue`. On web, the thumb animates with a CSS transition. In native mode, the thumb position is driven by flexbox `justifyContent`.
sections.api:
Import: import { Switch } from '@reactjit/core';

Usage:
```tsx
<Switch
  value={isEnabled}
  onValueChange={(v) => setIsEnabled(v)}
  trackColor={{ true: '#81b0ff', false: '#767577' }}
/>
```

Props:

| Prop         | Type                                    | Default                                | Description                             |
|--------------|-----------------------------------------|----------------------------------------|-----------------------------------------|
| value        | boolean                                 | -                                      | Controlled value. Omit for uncontrolled |
| defaultValue | boolean                                 | false                                  | Initial value in uncontrolled mode      |
| onValueChange| (value: boolean) => void                | -                                      | Called when the switch is toggled        |
| disabled     | boolean                                 | false                                  | Disables interaction and dims opacity   |
| style        | Style                                   | -                                      | Container style overrides               |
| trackColor   | { true: Color; false: Color }           | { true: '#81b0ff', false: '#767577' }  | Track colors for on/off states          |
| thumbColor   | Color                                   | '#f4f3f4'                              | Thumb circle color                      |
| width        | number                                  | 50                                     | Total switch width in pixels            |
| height       | number                                  | 28                                     | Total switch height in pixels           |

examples.title: Basic controlled switch
examples.code:
```tsx
const [enabled, setEnabled] = useState(false);

<Switch value={enabled} onValueChange={setEnabled} />
```

examples.title: Uncontrolled with custom colors
Disabled switch
examples.code:
```tsx
<Switch value={true} disabled />
```

criticalRules: Switch has a fixed pixel size. It does not stretch to fill its container by default. The `width` and `height` props control the track dimensions directly.
When `disabled` is true, opacity is set to 0.5 and click/toggle events are ignored.
In controlled mode, the component does not update visually unless the parent updates the `value` prop in response to `onValueChange`.

metadata.title: Table
metadata.description: Data table with configurable columns, alignment, striping, and custom cell renderers.

sections.overview:
Table renders a structured data grid with a header row and data rows. Each column is defined by a configuration object that specifies the key to read from each data row, the column title, optional width, alignment, and a custom render function.

Table supports striped row backgrounds, borderless mode, and per-section style overrides for the header, rows, and cells. It uses Box and Text primitives internally, so it works identically on both web and native targets.

Auto-sizing: Table automatically sizes to fit its content when all columns have explicit widths. When at least one column lacks an explicit width, the table uses `width: '100%'` and distributes remaining space via flexGrow. For content-based sizing, specify fixed widths for all columns and omit the outer `style.width` prop.

examples.title: Simple striped table
Custom cell renderer
Borderless table
Auto-sized table (content-based width)
examples.code:
```tsx
<Table
  columns={[
    { key: 'name', title: 'Name', width: 120 },
    { key: 'role', title: 'Role', width: 100 },
    { key: 'status', title: 'Status', width: 80, align: 'center' },
  ]}
  data={[
    { name: 'Alice', role: 'Engineer', status: 'Active' },
    { name: 'Bob', role: 'Designer', status: 'Away' },
  ]}
  striped
/>
```

criticalRules: The `columns` and `data` props are both required. An empty `data` array renders only the header row.
Each column's `key` must match a property name in the data objects. If the key does not exist on a row, the cell displays an empty string.

When using `render`, the custom renderer receives the raw cell value, the full row object, and the row index. It must return a ReactNode. The default renderer wraps the value in a Text element with `numberOfLines: 1`.
Without `rowKey`, rows are keyed by their array index. For data that changes order, provide a stable `rowKey` (either a string property name or a function).
Table does NOT scroll. For scrollable tables, wrap Table in a ScrollView or use FlatList with custom rendering.
For auto-sizing, all columns must have explicit widths and the outer `style` must not include a `width` property. If any column lacks a width, the table defaults to `width: '100%'` behavior.

metadata.title: Tabs
metadata.description: Tab navigation bar with underline and pill variants for switching between views.

sections.overview:
Tabs renders a horizontal tab bar for switching between views. Each tab is defined by an `id` and `label`. The currently active tab is visually distinguished by color, font weight, and variant-specific styling (an underline border or a filled pill background).

Tabs supports two visual variants: `'underline'` (default) draws a colored bottom border on the active tab, while `'pill'` wraps the active tab in a filled rounded rectangle. Both variants use Pressable for hover feedback.

examples.title: Underline tabs (default)
Pill variant
examples.code:
```tsx
<Tabs
  tabs={[
    { id: 'all', label: 'All' },
    { id: 'active', label: 'Active' },
    { id: 'archived', label: 'Archived' },
  ]}
  activeId="all"
  onSelect={handleTabChange}
  variant="pill"
/>
```

examples.title: Tabs controlling content

criticalRules: Both `tabs` and `activeId` are required. The `activeId` must match one of the tab `id` values for the active styling to appear.
Tabs does NOT render tab content. It only renders the tab bar. You are responsible for conditionally rendering the content below the Tabs component based on `activeId`.
The tab bar uses `width: '100%'` by default. Ensure the parent container has a known width.
Tabs is a controlled component. It does not manage its own active state. The parent must update `activeId` in response to `onSelect`.

metadata.title: Terminal
metadata.description: Non-visual PTY capability node for spawning and managing shell processes

sections.overview:
Terminal is a non-visual capability node that owns a PTY (pseudo-terminal) session. It renders nothing — there is no visual output from Terminal itself. Instead, it manages the shell process lifecycle on the Lua side and emits events as the shell produces output.

Use Terminal when you need raw PTY access: capturing command output, running background processes, or building custom terminal UIs with your own rendering. Use SemanticTerminal instead when you want visual rendering with semantic classification.

The recommended pattern is to pair Terminal with the usePTY hook. The hook accumulates output, manages connection state, and provides send helpers. Spread its terminalProps onto Terminal in your JSX tree.

Three session archetypes are available. type="user" (default) inherits the parent process environment and runs a persistent interactive shell. type="root" escalates to a root shell (requires sudo NOPASSWD or will show a password prompt in the PTY). type="template" spawns a fresh bash -c process for each command, providing a clean environment per invocation.

Terminal is Love2D and SDL2 native only — PTY requires a real OS process layer not available in web builds.
sections.api:
```tsx
import { Terminal } from '@reactjit/core';
```

Props:
| Prop        | Type                                                        | Default     | Description                                                   |
|-------------|-------------------------------------------------------------|-------------|---------------------------------------------------------------|
| type        | 'user' \| 'root' \| 'template'                             | 'user'      | Session archetype                                             |
| shell       | string                                                      | 'bash'      | Shell executable (any PATH-resolvable binary)                 |
| cwd         | string                                                      | undefined   | Working directory for the child process                       |
| env         | Record<string, string \| false>                             | undefined   | Environment variable overrides. false = unset the variable.   |
| rows        | number                                                      | 24          | Terminal rows (affects text wrapping in the shell)            |
| cols        | number                                                      | 80          | Terminal columns                                              |
| session     | string                                                      | auto        | Named session ID for RPC targeting                            |
| transport   | 'bridge' \| 'ws' \| 'http' \| 'tor'                        | 'bridge'    | Transport backend for the PTY bridge                          |
| autoConnect | boolean                                                     | true        | Auto-spawn on mount                                           |
| onData      | (event: { data: string }) => void                           | undefined   | Fires with each chunk of PTY output (ANSI-encoded)            |
| onConnect   | (event: { shell: string; ptyType: string; session?: string }) => void | undefined | Fires once when the shell process starts               |
| onExit      | (event: { exitCode: number \| null }) => void               | undefined   | Fires when the shell process exits                            |
| onError     | (event: { error: string }) => void                          | undefined   | Fires on spawn error                                          |

examples.title: Capture command output with usePTY (recommended pattern)
One-shot command runner (template type)

criticalRules: Terminal renders nothing — it is invisible. You must handle onData output yourself.
Always use the usePTY hook for stateful UIs. Calling bridge.rpc('pty:write') manually is fragile.
session IDs must be unique per app. Two Terminal nodes with the same session will conflict.
PTY is native only (Love2D and SDL2). Web builds receive no output and onConnect never fires.
type="template" spawns a fresh shell per runCommand() call. It does not maintain state between invocations.
Raw PTY output includes ANSI escape codes. Render with a component that strips them or use SemanticTerminal for visual rendering.

metadata.title: Text
metadata.description: Renders text content with font styling and optional truncation

sections.overview:
