Selected node persists across DevTools close/reopen — press Escape to clear selection before closing
Tree panel scroll state is preserved when toggling visibility — collapsed nodes stay collapsed
Performance metrics (FPS, layout time, paint time) update every frame in status bar when DevTools is open
Console output is capped at 500 lines — older lines are discarded to prevent memory growth
Watch expressions evaluate every frame — avoid expensive computations in watches
Macro recording captures all commands except :macro start/end/list — recursive macro definitions are not supported
Live style edits via :style bypass React reconciler — changes do NOT trigger re-render, only re-layout if dimensional properties change
DevTools panel height is 40% of screen height (min 200px) — cannot be resized (yet)
Tree panel and detail panel widths are fixed (280px and 300px) — cannot be resized (yet)
seeAlso: Global Text Scale — Ctrl+/- to zoom text globally, works independently of DevTools
Event Handling — DevTools uses same hit-test and event dispatch pipeline as app
Layout Engine — Inspector visualizes layout.lua computed geometry
Error Overlay — Similar rendering approach (raw Love2D calls, zero React tree impact)

metadata.title: Event System
metadata.description: How input events are captured, dispatched, and bubble through the component tree

sections.overview:
ReactJIT's event system handles mouse clicks, keyboard input, touch gestures, gamepad buttons, wheel scrolling, hover tracking, and drag operations. In Love2D native mode, Love2D callbacks forward raw input to the Lua runtime, which performs hit testing against the computed layout tree, constructs bubble paths, and dispatches events to the JavaScript side where React event handlers are invoked.

Events bubble up from the hit target to the root, following the node's ancestor chain. Each event object includes a bubblePath array and a stopPropagation() method.
sections.api:
LoveEvent Interface:
  type: string              - Event type name
  targetId?: number         - ID of the node that was hit
  x?: number                - Pointer X coordinate
  y?: number                - Pointer Y coordinate
  button?: number           - Mouse button (1=left, 2=right, 3=middle)
  key?: string              - Keyboard key name
  scancode?: string         - Physical key scancode
  isRepeat?: boolean        - Whether this is a key repeat event
  text?: string             - Text input content (from love.textinput)
  deltaX?: number           - Wheel horizontal delta
  deltaY?: number           - Wheel vertical delta
  touchId?: number|string   - Touch point identifier
  dx?: number               - Touch delta X
  dy?: number               - Touch delta Y
  pressure?: number         - Touch pressure (0-1)
  gamepadButton?: string    - Gamepad button name
  axis?: string             - Gamepad axis name
  axisValue?: number        - Gamepad axis value (-1 to 1)
  joystickId?: number       - Gamepad/joystick identifier
  startX?: number           - Drag start X
  startY?: number           - Drag start Y
  totalDeltaX?: number      - Total drag delta X from start
  totalDeltaY?: number      - Total drag delta Y from start
  bubblePath?: number[]     - Array of node IDs from target to root
  currentTarget?: number    - Current node in bubble chain
  stopPropagation?: () => void - Stop event from bubbling further

Box Event Handlers:
  onClick          - Mouse press on the node
  onRelease        - Mouse release on the node
  onPointerEnter   - Pointer enters node bounds
  onPointerLeave   - Pointer leaves node bounds
  onKeyDown        - Key pressed (global broadcast)
  onKeyUp          - Key released (global broadcast)
  onTextInput      - Text character entered (global broadcast)
  onWheel          - Scroll wheel moved
  onTouchStart     - Touch point pressed
  onTouchEnd       - Touch point released
  onTouchMove      - Touch point moved
  onGamepadPress   - Gamepad button pressed
  onGamepadRelease - Gamepad button released
  onGamepadAxis    - Gamepad axis moved
  onDragStart      - Drag operation started (after threshold)
  onDrag           - Drag in progress (continuous)
  onDragEnd        - Drag operation ended (release)

examples.title: Event Flow (Native Mode)
examples.code:
When the user clicks at position (150, 200):

  1. Love2D calls love.mousepressed(150, 200, 1)
  2. ReactLove.mousepressed(150, 200, 1) is called
  3. events.hitTest(root, 150, 200) walks the tree:
     - For each node, check if (150, 200) is inside computed rect
     - Respect overflow:hidden clipping
     - Deepest matching node wins (most specific target)
  4. events.buildBubblePath(hit) constructs [hitId, parentId, ..., rootId]
  5. events.createEvent("click", hitId, 150, 200, 1, bubblePath) creates LoveEvent
  6. pushEvent(event) sends the event to JavaScript
  7. In JS, the handlerRegistry looks up which nodes have onClick handlers
  8. For each node in bubblePath, if it has onClick, call it
  9. If stopPropagation() is called, stop traversing

  The entire flow happens synchronously within one frame.

examples.title: Drag Events
examples.code:
Drag operations use a threshold before activating:

  1. mousepressed: events.startDrag(hitId, x, y) records start position
  2. mousemoved: events.updateDrag(x, y) checks if threshold is crossed
     - If distance > threshold: dispatch onDragStart, then onDrag
     - Subsequent moves dispatch onDrag with dx, dy, totalDeltaX, totalDeltaY
  3. mousereleased: events.endDrag(x, y) dispatches onDragEnd

  While dragging, hover tracking is suspended to prevent unintended
  pointer-enter/leave events on nodes the pointer moves over.

examples.title: Interaction Styles (hoverStyle / activeStyle)
examples.code:
Box supports hoverStyle and activeStyle props that apply instantly in Lua
(0-frame latency, no round-trip to JS):

  <Box
    style={{ backgroundColor: '#1e293b' }}
    hoverStyle={{ backgroundColor: '#334155' }}
    activeStyle={{ backgroundColor: '#475569' }}
  >
    <Text style={{ fontSize: 14, color: '#f8fafc' }}>Hover me</Text>
  </Box>

The Lua runtime tracks hover/pressed state and applies style overlays
directly on the node. If the node has a transition property, the
animate module interpolates the change smoothly.

metadata.title: Focus Management
metadata.description: Tab and gamepad D-pad navigation system with spatial scanning, focus groups, and multi-controller routing

sections.overview:
Focus management enables keyboard Tab and gamepad D-pad navigation through interactive UI elements. The focus system lives in `lua/focus.lua` and provides automatic focus detection for nodes with event handlers, spatial navigation for directional input (controller D-pad/left stick), scoped focus regions via FocusGroup for multiplayer, per-controller routing, mode switching between mouse and controller input, scroll-into-view on focus change, and animated focus rings with player-based colors.

The system is input-mode aware: in mouse mode, hover and click work normally with no visible focus ring. When controller input is detected (D-pad, face buttons, or stick movement), it switches to controller mode, restores the last focused element (or picks the first focusable node), and renders an animated focus ring around the active element. Focus state is maintained per group, enabling couch co-op scenarios where Player 1 controls one UI panel via controller 1 and Player 2 controls another via controller 2.
sections.api:
**Auto-Focus Detection**

Any node with an event handler (`onClick`, `onKeyDown`, `onTextInput`, etc.) or explicit `focusable={true}` prop becomes auto-focusable. The focus system scans the instance tree after layout and builds a flat list of focusable nodes, sorted into groups.

**FocusGroup Component**

Scoped focus regions for multiplayer or modal dialogs. Import from `@reactjit/core`:

```tsx
import { FocusGroup } from '@reactjit/core';

<FocusGroup controller={1} ringColor={[0.3, 0.6, 1.0, 0.9]}>
  {/* Only controller 1 can navigate these elements */}
  <Pressable onClick={() => {}}>Button A</Pressable>
  <Pressable onClick={() => {}}>Button B</Pressable>
</FocusGroup>
```

Props (from `FocusGroupProps` in `types.ts`):

| Prop | Type | Default | Description |
|------|------|---------|-------------|
| `controller` | `number` | `undefined` | Joystick ID that owns this group. Omit for any controller. |
| `ringColor` | `[number, number, number, number?]` | auto | Focus ring color as RGBA (0-1). Defaults to player color: P1 blue, P2 red, P3 green, P4 yellow. |
| `style` | `Style` | — | Standard Box layout props. |
| `children` | `React.ReactNode` | — | Focusable elements. |

**Focus Events**

Nodes receive `onFocus` and `onBlur` events when focus changes:

```tsx
<TextInput
  onFocus={() => console.log('focused')}
  onBlur={() => console.log('blurred')}
/>
```

**Input Mode Switching**

The system tracks whether the user is using a mouse or controller:

- **Mouse mode**: Focus ring hidden, mouse hover/click works normally.
- **Controller mode**: Focus ring visible, D-pad/stick navigation active, buttons trigger onClick on focused node.

Mode switches automatically: any mouse movement triggers mouse mode, any D-pad/button/stick input triggers controller mode. On mode switch, the last focused element is restored (or the first focusable node is selected).

**Spatial Navigation Algorithm**

When the user presses a D-pad direction, the system finds the best target using spatial scanning:

1. Start from the currently focused node's center point (cx, cy).
2. Filter candidates in the correct direction (up: dy < 0, down: dy > 0, left: dx < 0, right: dx > 0).
3. Score each candidate: `mainDist + crossDist * 3` (favors aligned nodes over diagonal ones).
4. Pick the candidate with the lowest score.

This produces intuitive navigation for grid layouts, forms, and button panels.

**Multi-Controller Routing**

Each FocusGroup can claim a specific controller via the `controller` prop. When a joystick sends input, the system checks:

1. Are there any groups that explicitly claim this joystick ID? Use only those.
2. Otherwise: route input to the default group (no FocusGroup wrapper) and any unclaimed groups.

This enables split-screen UIs where each player controls their own panel independently.

**Scroll-Into-View**

When focus changes, the system checks if the focused node is inside a ScrollView. If the node is outside the visible viewport, the scroll position is adjusted to bring it into view (minimal scroll, prefers top-left alignment).

**Focus Ring Animation**

Focus rings are rendered by `lua/painter.lua` using exponential smoothing (lerp factor 0.0001 per second). The ring follows the focused node's bounding box with rounded corners and a 4px offset. Ring colors default to player-based colors (P1 blue, P2 red, P3 green, P4 yellow) but can be customized per group via `ringColor` prop.

**Lua API (lua/focus.lua)**

The Lua module exposes:

- `Focus.init(tree, pushEvent)` — Initialize with tree module and event callback.
- `Focus.set(node)` — Set focus to a node (backwards-compatible default group API).
- `Focus.clear()` — Clear focus.
- `Focus.get()` — Get currently focused node (default group).
- `Focus.isFocused(node)` — Check if node has focus in any group.
- `Focus.setControllerMode()` — Switch to controller mode (restores focus).
- `Focus.setMouseMode()` — Switch to mouse mode (remembers focus).
- `Focus.getInputMode()` — Returns `"mouse"` or `"controller"`.
- `Focus.rebuildFocusableList(root)` — Rebuild focusable nodes after layout.
- `Focus.navigate(direction, joystickId)` — Navigate in direction for a controller.
- `Focus.getForController(joystickId)` — Get focused node for a controller.
- `Focus.getRingColorForController(joystickId)` — Get ring color for a controller.
- `Focus.draw()` — Render focus rings (called by painter).
- `Focus.update(dt)` — Animate focus rings (called by main loop).
- `Focus.onGamepadPressed(joystick, button)` — Handle button press (space = click).
- `Focus.onGamepadAxis(joystick, axis, value)` — Handle stick movement (D-pad navigation).
- `Focus.onKeyPressed(key)` — Handle Tab navigation (keyboard).

**Integration Points**

Focus management integrates with:

- **Lua-owned primitives**: TextInput, TextEditor, VideoPlayer, ContextMenu all respond to focus events and participate in focus navigation.
- **Event dispatcher**: `onFocus`/`onBlur` events are pushed to JS via `eventDispatcher.ts`.
- **Painter**: `painter.lua` calls `Focus.draw()` after rendering all nodes.
- **Main loop**: `main.lua` calls `Focus.update(dt)` for ring animation.
- **Layout engine**: `layout.lua` calls `Focus.rebuildFocusableList()` after computing layout.

examples.title: Basic Tab navigation (auto-detected)
Split-screen multiplayer with FocusGroups
examples.code:
```tsx
import { Box, FocusGroup, Pressable, Text } from '@reactjit/core';

function MultiplayerUI() {
  return (
    <Box style={{ flexDirection: 'row', width: '100%', height: '100%' }}>
      {/* Player 1 panel (left half, controller 1) */}
      <FocusGroup controller={1} ringColor={[0.3, 0.6, 1.0, 0.9]}>
        <Box style={{ width: '50%', height: '100%', padding: 20 }}>
          <Text fontSize={18}>Player 1</Text>
          <Pressable onClick={() => console.log('P1 attack')}>
            <Text fontSize={14}>Attack</Text>
          </Pressable>
          <Pressable onClick={() => console.log('P1 defend')}>
            <Text fontSize={14}>Defend</Text>
          </Pressable>
        </Box>
      </FocusGroup>

      {/* Player 2 panel (right half, controller 2) */}
      <FocusGroup controller={2} ringColor={[1.0, 0.3, 0.3, 0.9]}>
        <Box style={{ width: '50%', height: '100%', padding: 20 }}>
          <Text fontSize={18}>Player 2</Text>
          <Pressable onClick={() => console.log('P2 attack')}>
            <Text fontSize={14}>Attack</Text>
          </Pressable>
          <Pressable onClick={() => console.log('P2 defend')}>
            <Text fontSize={14}>Defend</Text>
          </Pressable>
        </Box>
      </FocusGroup>
    </Box>
  );
}
```
Controller 1 can only navigate Player 1's panel. Controller 2 can only navigate Player 2's panel. Each gets their own colored focus ring.

examples.title: Modal dialog with temporary focus scope
Handling focus events
Custom focusable node (no event handlers)

criticalRules: **Auto-focus requires event handlers or explicit `focusable={true}`** — Nodes without handlers are skipped unless you add `focusable={true}` to the props.
**FocusGroup controller prop binds to joystick ID** — The `controller` prop takes a joystick ID (1-based), not a player index. Use `love.joystick.getJoysticks()` to enumerate.
**Focus rings are Lua-rendered** — No React components or CSS involved. The focus ring is drawn by `painter.lua` using `love.graphics.rectangle` with rounded corners.
**Spatial navigation requires computed layout** — The algorithm uses `node.computed.x/y/w/h` to calculate distances. If layout hasn't run, navigation will fail.
**Mode switch is global, focus state is per-group** — Input mode (mouse vs controller) is global, but each FocusGroup maintains its own `focusedNode` and `lastFocused` state.
**Stick deadzone is 0.3** — Analog stick values below 0.3 are ignored to prevent drift. Adjust `STICK_DEADZONE` in `focus.lua` if needed.
**Repeat rate for held directions** — Initial repeat after 0.4s, then 0.15s between repeats. Adjust `REPEAT_INITIAL` and `REPEAT_RATE` in `focus.lua` for faster/slower navigation.
**Tab navigation is keyboard-only** — Gamepad navigation uses D-pad/stick, not face buttons. Pressing A/Space on focused node triggers `onClick`.
**Focus events are boundary events** — `onFocus`/`onBlur` cross the JS bridge, but focus state lives in Lua. Don't try to manage focus state in React — use the Lua API via events.
**Focus group ring colors default to player colors** — P1 blue, P2 red, P3 green, P4 yellow. Override with `ringColor` prop if needed.
**Unclaimed groups accept any controller** — If a FocusGroup has no `controller` prop, any controller can navigate it (unless another group explicitly claims that controller).

**Focus restoration on mode switch** — When switching from mouse to controller mode, the system restores the last focused node (or picks the first focusable node if no history). When switching to mouse mode, focus is remembered for later restoration.
seeAlso: event-handling.txt - Event system, bubbling, dispatch to JS
lua-runtime.txt - All Lua modules including focus.lua
TextInput - Lua-owned primitive that participates in focus navigation
TextEditor - Lua-owned primitive that participates in focus navigation
VideoPlayer - Lua-owned primitive that participates in focus navigation
ContextMenu - Lua-owned primitive that participates in focus navigation

metadata.title: Advanced Topics
metadata.description: Hub page for advanced ReactJIT documentation

sections.overview:
This section covers the internals of ReactJIT for developers who need to understand what happens beneath the component API. Topics include the complete Lua runtime module inventory, focus management and controller navigation, the Lua-owned interaction pattern for zero-bridge-traffic primitives, networking (HTTP, WebSockets, proxies), Tor integration, the event dispatch system, performance optimization strategies, debugging tools, rendering internals, and how to create new render targets.

These topics assume familiarity with the rendering pipeline (see 02-architecture/) and the component API (see 05-components/).

metadata.title: Lua-Owned Interaction
metadata.description: Pattern for complex interactive primitives where all state lives in Lua, eliminating per-interaction bridge traffic

sections.overview:
Lua-owned interaction is an architectural pattern where complex interactive primitives (TextInput, TextEditor, VideoPlayer, ContextMenu, CodeBlock) are fully owned by Lua. ALL state — text content, cursor position, selection range, scroll offsets, playback state, control visibility, hover targets, drag state — lives entirely in Lua and is stored on the node itself (`node.inputState`, `node.editorState`, `node._vp`, etc.). The JS/TypeScript side only hears about boundary events: focus, blur, submit, changeText, timeUpdate, play, pause, ended. There is zero bridge traffic per keystroke, per cursor movement, per frame of video playback, per control interaction.

This pattern contrasts with traditional React controlled components where every keystroke triggers a state update, re-render, and reconciliation pass. In Lua-owned mode, React declares the component once (`<TextInput value="..." />`), Lua renders and manages all interaction, and JS only receives callbacks when the user is done editing (blur/submit) or when critical state changes (play/pause/ended). This eliminates thousands of bridge calls per second for high-interaction widgets.

The pattern is used for: TextInput (single/multiline text editing with cursor, selection, clipboard), TextEditor (multi-line code editor with syntax highlighting, line numbers, scroll), VideoPlayer (video playback with play/pause, seek, volume, fullscreen controls), ContextMenu (right-click menus with nested submenus, keyboard navigation), and CodeBlock (syntax-highlighted read-only code with line numbers, copy button).
sections.api:
**How It Works**

1. **React declares the component**: `<TextInput value="initial" onChangeText={callback} />`
2. **Reconciler creates a host node**: `createElement('TextInput')` in `hostConfig.ts`
3. **Layout engine computes bounds**: `layout.lua` calculates `node.computed.x/y/w/h`
4. **Painter delegates to Lua module**: `painter.lua` calls `TextInput.draw(node)` in `textinput.lua`
5. **Lua module renders everything**: Text content, cursor, selection, placeholder, scrolling viewport, border, all drawn using `love.graphics.*` primitives
6. **Events go directly to Lua**: `events.lua` calls `TextInput.onKeyPressed(node, key)`, `TextInput.onTextInput(node, text)`, `TextInput.onPointerPressed(node, x, y)`, etc.
7. **Boundary events pushed to JS**: When editing is done (blur/submit), Lua calls `pushEvent({ type: 'changeText', targetId: node.id, payload: { text: is.text } })`, which triggers the React `onChangeText` callback.

**State Storage Convention**

Each Lua-owned primitive stores its state on the node:

- **TextInput**: `node.inputState` — `{ text, cursorPos, scrollX, selectStart, selectEnd, blinkTimer, blinkOn, isDragging, lastValue }`
- **TextEditor**: `node.editorState` — `{ lines, cursorLine, cursorCol, scrollY, scrollX, selectStartLine, selectStartCol, selectEndLine, selectEndCol, blinkTimer, blinkOn, isDragging, lastValue }`
- **VideoPlayer**: `node._vp` — `{ showControls, controlsTimer, hoverTarget, draggingSeek, draggingVolume, volume, muted, isFullscreen }`
- **ContextMenu**: `node._cm` — `{ hoverIndex, selectedPath, keyboardIndex }`
- **CodeBlock**: `node._cb` — `{ scrollY, hoverCopy }`

State is initialized on first draw (via `ensureState(node)` or similar) and persists across re-renders. When props change (e.g. `value` prop updated externally), the Lua module detects the change (`props.value ~= is.lastValue`) and syncs state.

**Controlled Value Pattern**

Lua-owned primitives support controlled values:

```tsx
const [text, setText] = useState('');

<TextInput
  value={text}
  onChangeText={setText}
/>
```

The Lua module checks on each draw: if `props.value ~= is.lastValue`, it updates `is.text = props.value` and `is.lastValue = props.value`. This allows external updates (e.g. "reset to empty") while preserving Lua-owned editing state between external changes.

**Event Flow**

Lua-owned primitives subscribe to events in `events.lua`:

```lua
if node.type == "TextInput" then
  TextInput.onKeyPressed(node, key, isRepeat)
  handled = true
elseif node.type == "TextEditor" then
  TextEditor.onKeyPressed(node, key, isRepeat)
  handled = true
-- ...
end
```

Events are handled directly in Lua. Boundary events are pushed to JS:

```lua
pushEvent({
  type = "changeText",
  payload = { type = "changeText", targetId = node.id, text = is.text },
})
```

The event dispatcher in `eventDispatcher.ts` routes these to React callbacks:

```ts
if (event.type === 'changeText') {
  const node = findNodeById(event.payload.targetId);
  if (node?.props?.onChangeText) {
    node.props.onChangeText(event.payload.text);
  }
}
```

**Rendering Integration**

The painter delegates to Lua modules:

```lua
if node.type == "TextInput" then
  TextInput.draw(node)
elseif node.type == "TextEditor" then
  TextEditor.draw(node)
elseif node.type == "VideoPlayer" then
  VideoPlayer.draw(node)
elseif node.type == "ContextMenu" then
  ContextMenu.draw(node)
elseif node.type == "CodeBlock" then
  CodeBlock.draw(node)
-- ...
end
```

Each module's `draw()` function renders the complete widget using `love.graphics.*` primitives: rectangles, text, lines, circles, stencils, scissors, etc.

**Lua Module Structure (Example: textinput.lua)**

```lua
local TextInput = {}

-- Init (inject dependencies: measure.lua for font resolution)
function TextInput.init(config)
  Measure = config.measure
end

-- State management
function TextInput.initState(node)
  node.inputState = { text = "", cursorPos = 0, ... }
end

local function ensureState(node)
  if not node.inputState then TextInput.initState(node) end
  return node.inputState
end

-- Rendering
function TextInput.draw(node)
  local is = ensureState(node)
  -- Sync controlled value
  local props = node.props or {}
  if props.value and props.value ~= is.lastValue then
    is.text = props.value
    is.lastValue = props.value
  end
  -- Render background, border, text, cursor, selection, placeholder...
end

-- Event handlers
function TextInput.onKeyPressed(node, key, isRepeat)
  local is = ensureState(node)
  -- Handle cursor movement, deletion, clipboard, submit...
end

function TextInput.onTextInput(node, text)
  local is = ensureState(node)
  -- Insert text at cursor...
end

function TextInput.onPointerPressed(node, x, y, button)
  local is = ensureState(node)
  -- Position cursor, start selection drag...
end

-- ... more handlers ...

return TextInput
```

**Creating a New Lua-Owned Primitive (Checklist)**

1. **Create Lua module** (`lua/<name>.lua`) with `init()`, `draw()`, event handlers.
2. **Add state field** to node (`node.<name>State`) initialized in `ensureState()` or similar.
3. **Register in painter** (`painter.lua`): add `elseif node.type == "MyWidget" then MyWidget.draw(node)`.
4. **Register in events** (`events.lua`): add event handlers for your widget type.
5. **Create TypeScript component** (`packages/shared/src/MyWidget.tsx`): declares host element, props interface, renders `React.createElement('MyWidget', props)`.
6. **Add host config entry** (`packages/native/src/hostConfig.ts`): add `'MyWidget'` to supported types, implement `createInstance()`, `commitUpdate()`, etc.
7. **Define props interface** (`packages/shared/src/types.ts`): add `MyWidgetProps` with all props and event callbacks.
8. **Export from index** (`packages/shared/src/index.ts`): `export { MyWidget } from './MyWidget';`.
9. **Sync to CLI runtime**: `make cli-setup` to copy Lua files to `cli/runtime/lua/`.
10. **Create storybook story** (`storybook/src/stories/MyWidgetStory.tsx`): demonstrate all features.

examples.title: TextInput - Zero bridge traffic per keystroke
VideoPlayer - Zero bridge traffic per frame
TextEditor - Zero bridge traffic per cursor movement
ContextMenu - Zero bridge traffic per hover/keyboard nav
Building a custom Lua-owned slider

criticalRules: **State lives in Lua, not React** — Never try to manage cursor position, scroll offsets, hover state, or drag state in React. It all belongs in Lua on `node.<name>State`.

**Boundary events only** — Only push events to JS when the user is done (blur, submit, ended) or when critical state changes (play, pause, error). Do NOT push events per keystroke, per cursor move, per frame, per hover.

**Controlled value sync on draw** — Check `props.value ~= state.lastValue` on every draw and sync if changed. This allows external updates (reset button, undo/redo) while preserving Lua-owned editing state between updates.
**Event handlers return handled = true** — Event handlers in `events.lua` must return `true` to prevent event bubbling to parent nodes. Otherwise click-through bugs occur.
**State persists across re-renders** — When React re-renders and calls `commitUpdate()`, Lua keeps the existing `node.<name>State` intact. Do NOT reinitialize state on every render.
**Use ensureState() pattern** — Initialize state lazily on first draw/event, not in `createElement()`. This avoids state allocation for off-screen nodes.
**Font/measure dependency injection** — Lua modules depend on `measure.lua` for font resolution. Inject via `<Module>.init({ measure = Measure })` in `init.lua`.
**Painter delegation order matters** — Render Lua-owned primitives AFTER basic nodes but BEFORE overlays (focus rings, inspector). Check `painter.lua` for correct ordering.
**Bridge event types must match** — Event type string in Lua (`pushEvent({ type: "changeText" })`) must match case in `eventDispatcher.ts` and prop name in `types.ts` (`onChangeText`).
**Clipboard is Lua-owned** — TextInput/TextEditor handle Ctrl+C/X/V using `love.system.getClipboardText()` / `setClipboardText()`. No JS clipboard API involved.
**Cursor blink timer is Lua-owned** — TextInput/TextEditor manage `blinkTimer` and `blinkOn` state in Lua, updated in main loop via `update(dt)` callback. No React state updates for animation.
**ScrollView integration** — Lua-owned primitives can be children of ScrollView. The painter applies scissor/translate for scroll offset automatically.
**Focus integration** — Lua-owned primitives participate in focus navigation. They must handle `onFocus`/`onBlur` events from `focus.lua` and render focus styles accordingly.
**Performance is the goal** — The entire point of Lua-owned interaction is to eliminate bridge traffic. If you're pushing events per keystroke or per frame, you're doing it wrong.
seeAlso: lua-runtime.txt - All Lua modules including textinput.lua, texteditor.lua, videoplayer.lua
event-handling.txt - Event dispatch from Lua to JS, event bubbling
TextInput - Reference implementation of Lua-owned text editing
TextEditor - Reference implementation of Lua-owned code editing
VideoPlayer - Reference implementation of Lua-owned video controls
ContextMenu - Reference implementation of Lua-owned menu navigation
CodeBlock - Reference implementation of Lua-owned syntax highlighting

metadata.title: Lua Runtime Internals
metadata.description: Complete inventory of all Lua modules that power the Love2D target

sections.overview:
The Lua runtime consists of a collection of modules that collectively implement the rendering pipeline for the Love2D target. The entry point is init.lua, which detects the operating mode, loads the appropriate bridge, and initializes all submodules. On every frame, the runtime ticks the JavaScript engine, drains mutation commands, advances animations, runs layout, and paints the tree.

All Lua files live at lua/ in the monorepo root (source of truth) and are copied into projects via `make cli-setup` and `reactjit update`.
sections.api:
Module Inventory:

  init.lua           - Entry point. Detects mode (native/web/canvas), loads all
                       modules, implements love.update/draw/input callbacks.
                       Manages HMR, interaction styles, deferred mount protocol.

  tree.lua           - Retained instance tree. Receives mutation commands from JS
                       (createNode, updateProps, appendChild, removeChild, etc.)
                       and maintains the live node tree. Tracks dirty state.

  layout.lua         - Flexbox layout engine. Computes {x, y, w, h} for every node.
                       Handles flex-grow/shrink, wrap, justify, align, padding,
                       margin, gap, text measurement, min/max clamping.

  painter.lua        - Love2D painter. Walks computed tree and issues love.graphics
                       calls. Handles backgrounds, borders, text, images, shadows,
                       gradients, transforms, opacity, clipping, scrollbars.

  events.lua         - Event handling. Hit testing, hover tracking, drag state,
                       event creation (click, release, keydown, wheel, touch,
                       gamepad), bubble path construction.

  measure.lua        - Text measurement. Font loading/caching by (size, family,
                       weight) tuple. measureText() for layout, getFont() for
                       painter. Letter spacing width calculation.

  bridge_quickjs.lua - QuickJS FFI bridge. Loads libquickjs.so via LuaJIT FFI.
                       eval(), callGlobal(), tick(), drainCommands(), pushEvent().

  bridge_fs.lua      - Module.FS bridge. File-based communication via Emscripten
                       shared filesystem. poll(), flush(), emit().

  animate.lua        - Lua-side transitions and keyframe animations. Processes
                       CSS-style transition declarations and animation keyframes.
                       Interpolates style values per frame.

  images.lua         - Image loading and caching. Manages love.graphics.newImage
                       instances. Handles async loading and placeholder display.

  focus.lua          - Focus manager for Lua-owned inputs (TextEditor). Tracks
                       which node has focus, handles focus/blur transitions.

  texteditor.lua     - Lua-owned multi-line text editor. Handles cursor, selection,
                       line numbers, scrolling, key input, mouse selection.
                       Renders directly via love.graphics calls.

  errors.lua         - Error overlay. Displays runtime errors as a full-screen
                       overlay with stack traces. Uses raw love.graphics calls
                       (independent of the tree/layout system).

  inspector.lua      - Visual inspector (F12). Shows node boundaries, computed
                       dimensions, style properties. Highlights hovered nodes.
                       Displays layout timing and paint timing.

  console.lua        - Interactive eval console (backtick key). Evaluates JS
                       expressions via the bridge and displays results.

  screenshot.lua     - Headless screenshot capture. Waits for the first stable
                       frame, captures the framebuffer, saves as PNG.

  zindex.lua         - Z-index sorting. Returns paint-ordered child lists based
                       on zIndex style property (stable sort, ascending).

  storage.lua        - Persistent storage RPC handlers. Provides get/set/remove
                       methods backed by love.filesystem.

  target_love2d.lua  - Love2D target configuration. Contains target-specific
                       constants and setup.

  http.lua           - Async HTTP client. Supports GET/POST/PUT/DELETE with headers,
                       proxy (HTTP/SOCKS5), and concurrent request pooling via
                       love.thread. Bridges to LuaSocket socket.http library.

  network.lua        - WebSocket network manager. Manages multiple concurrent
                       WebSocket connections with auto-reconnect, backoff,
                       and SOCKS5 proxy support (for Tor tunneling).

  websocket.lua      - Pure Lua WebSocket client (vendored from flaribbit/love2d-
                       lua-websocket). Implements RFC 6455 frame encoding/decoding.
                       Low-level; use network.lua for high-level connection management.

  json.lua           - JSON encoder/decoder. Used internally to serialize/deserialize
                       mutation commands between JS and Lua. Handles objects, arrays,
                       strings, numbers, booleans, null.

  socks5.lua         - SOCKS5 proxy client (RFC 1928 + RFC 1929). Establishes TCP
                       tunnels through SOCKS5 proxies with no-auth and username/
                       password auth. Used by http.lua and network.lua for Tor support.

  tor.lua            - Tor subprocess manager. Auto-starts Tor, creates hidden
                       services, polls for bootstrap completion, graceful shutdown.
                       Opt-in via config.tor in ReactLove.init().

  codeblock.lua      - Syntax-highlighted code block renderer. Direct love.graphics
                       rendering (like texteditor.lua). Highlights keywords, imports,
                       strings, numbers, comments.

  textselection.lua  - Text selection and clipboard copy for Text nodes. Manages
                       global selection state, drag-to-select, multi-line extraction,
                       Ctrl+C / Cmd+C copy support.

  log_colors.lua     - ANSI color tag library for Lua-side logging. Provides color-
                       coded print() wrappers (log.info, log.warn, log.error, etc.)
                       for readable terminal output from Lua modules.

  debug_log.lua      - Structured debug logging with source location. Wraps log_colors
                       and adds file:line prefixes. Used internally by runtime modules;
                       toggle verbosity without editing call sites.

  devtools.lua       - Developer tools aggregator. Coordinates inspector, console,
                       screenshot, and debug_log. Loaded automatically in dev mode;
                       stripped from production builds.

  settings.lua       - Persistent key-value store for framework settings (inspector
                       position, theme selection, window state). Backed by love.filesystem.

  theme_menu.lua     - In-app theme switcher overlay (Ctrl+T). Lists all registered
                       themes, applies them live, and persists the selection via settings.lua.

  system_panel.lua   - System info overlay. Shows frame time, memory usage, node count,
                       layout timing, and paint timing. Toggled via a dev shortcut.

  permit.lua         - Runtime permission guard. Enforces capability-level permissions
                       declared in the app manifest. Blocks RPC calls that exceed
                       declared scope and logs violations via audit.lua.

  audit.lua          - Security audit log. Records all RPC calls, capability invocations,
                       and permission decisions to a local audit trail. Used with permit.lua.

  manifest.lua       - App manifest loader. Reads the app's capability declarations,
                       metadata, and permission scope. Feeds permit.lua and the
                       capability registry.

  cart_reader.lua    - Cartridge reader for self-contained .rjit bundles. Extracts
                       embedded Lua runtime, JS bundle, and assets from a single-file
                       distribution. Enables the portable cart deployment format.

  capabilities.lua   - Capability registry. All native capabilities register here
                       via Capabilities.register(). Provides the capabilities:list
                       RPC used by useCapabilities().

  pty.lua            - PTY process manager. Spawns pseudo-terminal processes, pipes
                       output to the bridge, handles resize (SIGWINCH), and manages
                       session lifecycles for Terminal and SemanticTerminal.

  vterm.lua          - VT100/VT220 terminal emulator (pure Lua). Decodes raw PTY
                       byte streams into a Unicode text buffer. Used by SemanticTerminal
                       to strip ANSI codes before classification.

  semantic_graph.lua - Semantic graph builder. Aggregates classified rows into turns,
                       nodes, and state transitions. Emits onGraphUpdate and onStateChange
                       events to the React layer.

  session_recorder.lua - PTY output recorder. Captures raw PTY bytes with timestamps
                         into a .rec.lua file for later playback. See session-recording docs.

  session_player.lua - Playback engine for .rec.lua recordings. Drives the vterm +
                       classifier pipeline at recorded timing (with speed control and
                       seeking). Used by SemanticTerminal in playback mode.

examples.title: Module Loading Order (Native Mode)
examples.code:
When ReactLove.init() is called in native mode:

  1. resolveBasePath()                  -- Find the lua/ directory
  2. require("lua.bridge_quickjs")      -- Load QuickJS FFI bridge
  3. BridgeQJS.new(libpath)             -- Create bridge with libquickjs.so
  4. require("lua.measure")             -- Text measurement module
  5. require("lua.images")              -- Image cache
  6. require("lua.animate")             -- Animation engine
  7. require("lua.tree")                -- Retained tree
  8. tree.init({ images, animate })     -- Wire dependencies
  9. animate.init({ tree })             -- Wire back-reference
  10. require("lua.layout")             -- Layout engine
  11. layout.init({ measure })          -- Wire text measurement
  12. require("lua.painter")            -- Painter
  13. painter.init({ measure, images }) -- Wire dependencies
  14. require("lua.events")             -- Event system
  15. events.setTreeModule(tree)         -- Wire tree reference
  16. require("lua.texteditor")         -- Text editor
  17. texteditor.init({ measure })      -- Wire measurement
  18. bridge:eval(bundleJS)             -- Load the JS bundle into QuickJS

  Always-loaded (independent, no init):
  - require("lua.errors")              -- Error overlay
  - require("lua.inspector")           -- F12 inspector
  - require("lua.console")             -- Backtick console
  - require("lua.focus")               -- Focus manager
  - require("lua.log_colors")          -- Color logging
  - require("lua.debug_log")           -- Structured debug logging
  - require("lua.devtools")            -- Dev tool aggregator (dev builds only)
  - require("lua.settings")            -- Persistent settings store
  - require("lua.theme_menu")          -- Theme switcher overlay
  - require("lua.system_panel")        -- System info overlay
  - require("lua.permit")              -- Permission guard
  - require("lua.audit")               -- Audit log
  - require("lua.manifest")            -- App manifest loader
  - require("lua.cart_reader")         -- Cartridge format reader

seeAlso: networking.txt - http.lua, network.lua, websocket.lua, json.lua, socks5.lua
tor.txt - tor.lua, Tor hidden service integration
rendering.txt - codeblock.lua and textselection.lua for UI features

metadata.title: Networking (HTTP & WebSockets)
metadata.description: Making HTTP requests and managing WebSocket connections with proxy support

sections.overview:
ReactJIT provides two networking layers: HTTP (via http.lua) for one-off requests, and WebSocket (via network.lua) for persistent connections. Both support proxying through HTTP proxies or SOCKS5 (useful for Tor). HTTP uses love.thread for async non-blocking requests. WebSockets use pure Lua with automatic reconnection.

**For multiplayer games:** Use WebSockets (network.lua) for persistent player connections. WebSockets automatically reconnect on disconnect and maintain state. Perfect for real-time multiplayer where players need low-latency communication.

**Two multiplayer patterns:**

1. **Host-Client Model** — one player hosts (server.listen()), others join (connect()) Use this when one player is "hosting" the game world.

2. **Peer-to-Peer** — players connect directly to each other More complex but lower latency; useful for 2-player games.

**Tor Integration:** WebSockets automatically tunnel through Tor (.onion addresses). See tor.txt for hosting hidden services and anonymous multiplayer.

All networking happens on the Lua side and communicates with JavaScript via the bridge.
sections.api:
---- HTTP Module (http.lua) ----

http.init()
  Initialize the HTTP module. Must be called once at startup. Creates worker
  thread channels and launches initial worker threads.

http.request(id, opts)
  Start an async HTTP request. Returns immediately (non-blocking).

  id:    string or number. You assign this; it's returned in the response.
  opts:  table with keys:
         url      - string, required, full URL
         method   - string, "GET" (default), "POST", "PUT", "DELETE", "PATCH", "HEAD"
         headers  - table, optional { "Content-Type": "application/json", ... }
         body     - string, optional request body
         proxy    - string, optional proxy URL:
                    "http://host:port"
                    "socks5://host:port"
                    "socks5://user:pass@host:port"

  Example:
    http.request("fetch-posts", {
      url = "https://api.example.com/posts",
      method = "GET",
      headers = { Accept = "application/json" }
    })

http.poll()
  Poll for completed responses. Call once per frame (in love.update).

  Returns:  array of response objects (may be empty)
            Each response is:
            { id, status, headers, body, error }
            - status: HTTP status code (0 if error)
            - headers: table { "content-type": "...", ... }
            - body: string response body
            - error: string if request failed (net error, timeout, etc.)

  Example:
    local responses = http.poll()
    for _, resp in ipairs(responses) do
      if resp.error then
        print("Request failed:", resp.error)
      else
        print("Status:", resp.status)
        print("Body:", resp.body)
      end
    end

---- WebSocket Module (network.lua) ----

network.init()
  Initialize the WebSocket network manager. Must be called once at startup.

network.connect(id, url, opts)
  Connect to a WebSocket server.

  id:    number, auto-assigned connection ID. JS and Lua both use this to refer
         to the connection.
  url:   string, ws:// or wss:// URL
  opts:  table, optional:
         proxy - string, socks5 proxy for tunneling (e.g., "127.0.0.1:9050" for Tor)
         reconnect - boolean, auto-reconnect on close (default: true)

  Example:
    network.connect(1, "ws://localhost:8000/chat")
    network.connect(2, "ws://hidden.onion/game", { proxy = "127.0.0.1:9050" })

  Emits: ws:open event when connected, ws:error on failure.

network.send(id, data)
  Send a message on an open connection.

  data:  string, the message to send.

  Example:
    network.send(1, "Hello, server!")

network.close(id, code, reason)
  Close a connection. May be called before it's open. Disables auto-reconnect.

  code:    number, optional WebSocket close code (default: 1000)
  reason:  string, optional close reason

  Emits: ws:close event when fully closed.

network.poll()
  Poll for events. Call once per frame.

  Returns:  array of event objects (may be empty)
            Each event is one of:
            { type = "ws:open", id = N }
            { type = "ws:message", id = N, data = "..." }
            { type = "ws:error", id = N, error = "..." }
            { type = "ws:close", id = N }

  Example:
    local events = network.poll()
    for _, evt in ipairs(events) do
      if evt.type == "ws:message" then
        print("Message from", evt.id, ":", evt.data)
      elseif evt.type == "ws:error" then
        print("Connection error:", evt.error)
      end
    end

network.listen(serverId, port, host)
  Start a WebSocket server. Listens for incoming connections.

  serverId: string, unique identifier for this server instance
  port:     number, port to listen on (e.g., 8000)
  host:     string, bind address (default: "127.0.0.1")

  Example:
    network.listen("game_server", 8000, "127.0.0.1")

  Emits: ws:server:ready event, then ws:peer:connect, ws:peer:message, ws:peer:disconnect
         Each connected client has a clientId (auto-assigned).

network.broadcast(serverId, data)
  Send a message to all connected clients on a server.

  serverId: string, the server ID
  data:     string, the message to broadcast

  Example:
    network.broadcast("game_server", json.encode({ x = 100, y = 200 }))

network.sendToClient(serverId, clientId, data)
  Send a message to a specific client on a server.

  serverId: string, the server ID
  clientId: number, the client ID (from ws:peer:connect event)
  data:     string, the message to send

  Example:
    network.sendToClient("game_server", 2, "You were hit!")

network.stopServer(serverId)
  Stop a server and disconnect all clients.

  Emits: ws:close for each client

network.destroy()
  Shut down all connections and servers. Call on app exit.

---- Server Events ----

When using network.listen(), poll events include:

  { type = "ws:server:ready", serverId = "...", port = 8000 }
    Server started and listening.

  { type = "ws:peer:connect", serverId = "...", clientId = N }
    New client connected (clientId assigned automatically).

  { type = "ws:peer:message", serverId = "...", clientId = N, data = "..." }
    Message from client N.

  { type = "ws:peer:disconnect", serverId = "...", clientId = N, code = 1000, reason = "" }
    Client N disconnected.

---- Proxy & SOCKS5 (socks5.lua) ----

socks5.connect(proxyHost, proxyPort, targetHost, targetPort, user, pass)
  Low-level: establish a TCP socket through a SOCKS5 proxy. Used internally by
  http.lua and network.lua. Supports RFC 1928 (no-auth) and RFC 1929 (user/pass).

  Returns: socket object (LuaSocket), or nil + error string.

  This is for advanced use. HTTP and WebSocket modules handle proxying automatically.

examples.title: Simple HTTP GET
examples.code:
local http = require("lua.http")
http.init()

function love.update(dt)
  http.request("get-json", {
    url = "https://api.github.com/users/octocat",
    method = "GET",
    headers = { Accept = "application/vnd.github.v3+json" }
  })

  local responses = http.poll()
  for _, resp in ipairs(responses) do
    if resp.id == "get-json" and resp.status == 200 then
      local data = require("lua.json").decode(resp.body)
      print("GitHub user:", data.name)
    end
  end
end

examples.title: POST with JSON Body
examples.code:
local http = require("lua.http")
local json = require("lua.json")
http.init()

function love.update(dt)
  http.request("create-post", {
    url = "https://api.example.com/posts",
    method = "POST",
    headers = { ["Content-Type"] = "application/json" },
    body = json.encode({ title = "Hello", body = "World" })
  })

  local responses = http.poll()
  for _, resp in ipairs(responses) do
    if resp.error then
      print("Error:", resp.error)
    elseif resp.status >= 200 and resp.status < 300 then
      print("Post created:", resp.body)
    else
      print("HTTP error:", resp.status)
    end
  end
end

examples.title: WebSocket Real-Time Chat
examples.code:
local network = require("lua.network")
network.init()

local connectionId = 1

function love.update(dt)
  -- Connect on first frame
  if not connected then
    network.connect(connectionId, "ws://localhost:8000/chat")
    connected = true
  end

  -- Poll for events
  local events = network.poll()
  for _, evt in ipairs(events) do
    if evt.id == connectionId then
      if evt.type == "ws:open" then
        print("Connected to chat")
        network.send(connectionId, "Hello chat!")
      elseif evt.type == "ws:message" then
        print("Received:", evt.data)
      elseif evt.type == "ws:error" then
        print("Connection error:", evt.error)
      elseif evt.type == "ws:close" then
        print("Disconnected")
      end
    end
  end
end

function love.mousereleased(x, y, button)
  if button == 1 then
    network.send(connectionId, "User clicked at " .. x .. ", " .. y)
  end
end

examples.title: SOCKS5 Proxy (Tor)
Multiplayer Game Server (Host)
examples.code:
local network = require("lua.network")
local json = require("lua.json")

local gameServer = "game"
local players = {}  -- id -> { x, y, name, score }

function love.load()
  network.init()
  network.listen(gameServer, 8000, "127.0.0.1")
  print("Game server listening on port 8000")
end

function love.update(dt)
  local events = network.poll()
  for _, evt in ipairs(events) do
    if evt.type == "ws:server:ready" then
      print("Server ready on port " .. evt.port)

    elseif evt.type == "ws:peer:connect" then
      print("Client " .. evt.clientId .. " joined")
      players[evt.clientId] = { x = 0, y = 0, name = "Player" .. evt.clientId, score = 0 }

    elseif evt.type == "ws:peer:message" then
      -- Parse player update: { x, y, action }
      local msg = json.decode(evt.data)
      if players[evt.clientId] then
        players[evt.clientId].x = msg.x or players[evt.clientId].x
        players[evt.clientId].y = msg.y or players[evt.clientId].y
        if msg.action == "collect" then
          players[evt.clientId].score = (players[evt.clientId].score or 0) + 10
        end
      end

    elseif evt.type == "ws:peer:disconnect" then
      print("Client " .. evt.clientId .. " left")
      players[evt.clientId] = nil
  end

  -- Broadcast game state every frame
  local state = { players = players, time = love.timer.getTime() }
  network.broadcast(gameServer, json.encode(state))
end

examples.title: Multiplayer Game Client (Join)
examples.code:
local network = require("lua.network")
local json = require("lua.json")

local serverId = 1
local gameState = {}
local player = { x = 100, y = 100, name = "Me", score = 0 }

function love.load()
  network.init()

  -- Connect to game server on localhost:8000
  -- (In real scenario, use server's public IP or .onion address)
  network.connect(serverId, "ws://127.0.0.1:8000/game")
end

function love.update(dt)
  -- Handle movement
  local speed = 200
  if love.keyboard.isDown("up") then player.y = player.y - speed * dt end
  if love.keyboard.isDown("down") then player.y = player.y + speed * dt end
  if love.keyboard.isDown("left") then player.x = player.x - speed * dt end
  if love.keyboard.isDown("right") then player.x = player.x + speed * dt end

  -- Send player state to server
  network.send(serverId, json.encode({ x = player.x, y = player.y }))

  -- Receive game state
  local events = network.poll()
  for _, evt in ipairs(events) do
    if evt.type == "ws:open" then
      print("Connected to game server")

    elseif evt.type == "ws:message" then
      gameState = json.decode(evt.data)

    elseif evt.type == "ws:error" then
      print("Connection error:", evt.error)

    elseif evt.type == "ws:close" then
      print("Disconnected from server")
  end
end

function love.draw()
  -- Draw local player
  love.graphics.setColor(1, 1, 0)  -- Yellow
  love.graphics.circle("fill", player.x, player.y, 10)
  love.graphics.print(player.name, player.x - 30, player.y - 20)

  -- Draw other players
  love.graphics.setColor(1, 0, 1)  -- Magenta
  if gameState.players then
    for id, p in pairs(gameState.players) do
      if id ~= serverId and p.x then
        love.graphics.circle("fill", p.x, p.y, 10)
        love.graphics.print(p.name .. " (" .. (p.score or 0) .. ")", p.x - 30, p.y - 20)
      end
    end
  end
end

sections.commonPatterns:
**HTTP Requests:** The http module manages a pool of worker threads. Each request is fire-and-forget; responses are polled each frame. Useful for API calls, file downloads, webhooks.

**WebSocket Connections (Persistent):** The network module manages multiple open connections by ID. Each connection supports auto-reconnect with exponential backoff. Useful for: - Real-time multiplayer games - Live chat / messaging - Remote shells / terminals - Live data feeds - Persistent bidirectional communication

**WebSocket Server (Host Mode):** One player can host a server that other players join. network.listen() starts a server, network.broadcast() sends to all clients, and events notify of connects/ messages/disconnects.

**Multiplayer Patterns:**

1. **Host-Client** (typical for games): - Host player: network.listen("game", 8000) → broadcasts game state - Client players: network.connect(N, "ws://host:8000/game") → receives state - Simple, one source of truth (the host)

2. **Peer-to-Peer** (advanced): - Each player runs both client and server - Player A connects to B, B connects to A - Share state bidirectionally - More complex but lower latency

3. **Central Relay** (recommended for 4+ players): - Dedicated server (can be Tor hidden service) - Players connect to server - Server broadcasts to all - Single source of truth

4. **Tor-Based** (for p2p without port forwarding): - Enable Tor hidden service (tor.start()) - Host a server on that .onion address - Players connect to .onion address - Tor handles NAT/firewall traversal automatically

**Proxy Support:** Both layers support proxying via http/socks5 URLs. Can be set per-request, via environment variables (HTTP_PROXY, HTTPS_PROXY, ALL_PROXY, NO_PROXY), or for WebSockets connecting through Tor.
criticalRules: *Initialization:**
Always call http.init() and network.init() once at startup (love.load)
Always call network.destroy() on app exit (love.quit)
*Polling:**
Always poll for responses/events each frame (http.poll(), network.poll())
If you don't poll, events queue up and never get processed
Call in love.update() once per frame
*WebSocket (Client):**
Use unique IDs (1, 2, 3...) for concurrent connections
Each connection has its own auto-reconnect behavior
WebSocket auto-reconnect uses exponential backoff (starts at 1s, caps at 60s)
*WebSocket Server (Host):**
One server ID per server (e.g., "game", "chat", "relay")
Each connected client gets a unique clientId (auto-assigned 1, 2, 3...)
server.listen() can be called multiple times for different servers
Call network.stopServer() to stop a server cleanly
*Multiplayer-Specific:**
Host player runs network.listen() (server)
Client players run network.connect() (client)
Send game state regularly (e.g., every 50ms, not every frame if 60 FPS)
Use JSON for serialization (require("lua.json"))
Test with 2+ players before release
*HTTP:**
Use unique IDs for concurrent HTTP requests
HTTP requests timeout after 15 seconds
SOCKS5 connections timeout after 15 seconds
Proxies are optional; omit for direct connections
*Tor Integration:**
For Tor hidden services, call tor.lua functions before network.listen()
Ensure Tor is running on port 9050 (or auto-detected port)
.onion addresses are only available after Tor bootstrap (5-30s)
seeAlso: tor.txt - Tor hidden services for multiplayer, direct .onion connections
lua-runtime.txt - http.lua, network.lua, websocket.lua, socks5.lua modules
06-hooks/useeffect.txt - Using hooks to manage network requests

metadata.title: On-Screen Keyboard (OSK)
metadata.description: Lua-owned virtual keyboard overlay for gamepad and touch-based text entry

sections.overview: The On-Screen Keyboard (lua/osk.lua) is a Lua-owned singleton overlay that enables text input when using a gamepad or controller. When a TextInput gains focus via gamepad navigation, the OSK opens automatically and intercepts all gamepad input. D-pad navigates keys, A button selects, B button closes. Text is injected into the target TextInput using the existing event pipeline (textinput and keydown events). The OSK supports multiple keyboard layouts (QWERTY, AZERTY, QWERTZ, Spanish, numeric, symbols) and is context-aware based on the TextInput's keyboardType prop.
sections.api:
**Lua API (lua/osk.lua)**

The OSK is automatically initialized by the framework. Most apps do not need to interact with it directly.

```lua
local OSK = require("lua.osk")

-- Initialize (called by framework in lua/init.lua)
OSK.init({ measure = measureFunction })

-- Check if OSK is open
local isOpen = OSK.isOpen()

-- Open OSK for a specific TextInput node
OSK.open(node, joystickId, pushEventCallback)
-- node: TextInput instance tree node
-- joystickId: integer ID of the gamepad
-- pushEventCallback: function to inject text events

-- Close OSK
OSK.close()

-- Handle gamepad input (called by framework)
OSK.handleGamepadPressed(button, joystickId)  -- "dpup", "dpdown", "dpleft", "dpright", "a", "b"
OSK.handleGamepadAxis(axis, value, joystickId)  -- leftx/lefty for analog stick navigation

-- Update (called in love.update)
OSK.update(dt)

-- Draw (called in love.draw if isOpen)
OSK.draw()
```

**Keyboard Layouts**

Available layouts (internal, selected based on context):
- QWERTY (default, English)
- AZERTY (French)
- QWERTZ (German)
- SPANISH
- NUMERIC (for keyboardType="numeric")
- SYMBOLS / SYMBOLS2 (accessible via #+= key)

**Key Actions**

Keys can be characters ("a", "1", "!") or actions:
- `shift` — toggle uppercase
- `capslock` — lock uppercase (not implemented in base OSK)
- `backspace` — delete previous character
- `submit` — submit/close (triggers "return" keydown event)
- `space` — insert space character
- `symbols` / `symbols2` — switch to symbol layouts
- `letters` — return to letter layout
- `numeric` — switch to numeric layout

**Context-Aware Behavior**

The OSK checks `node.props.keyboardType` when opening:
- `"numeric"` → opens NUMERIC layout
- `"default"` or undefined → opens QWERTY (or user's locale layout)

**React Integration**

The OSK is fully automatic. React components do not need to invoke it — it opens when a TextInput gains focus via gamepad.

```tsx
import { TextInput } from '@reactjit/core';

// OSK opens automatically when focused with gamepad
<TextInput keyboardType="numeric" />   // opens numeric keypad
<TextInput keyboardType="default" />   // opens QWERTY
```

examples.title: Automatic OSK Behavior
Custom OSK Configuration (Lua)
examples.code:
-- In main.lua or conf.lua, before framework init

-- OSK layout selection is automatic based on Love2D locale
-- or node.props.keyboardType. No manual configuration needed.

-- To customize OSK appearance, edit lua/osk.lua directly:
-- - KEY_WIDTH, KEY_HEIGHT (key dimensions)
-- - KEY_GAP (spacing between keys)
-- - KEY_COLOR, KEY_PRESS_COLOR, KEY_TEXT_COLOR (colors)
-- - NAV_SPEED (analog stick navigation speed)
-- - NAV_REPEAT_DELAY, NAV_REPEAT_RATE (D-pad repeat timing)

examples.title: OSK Event Flow (Internal)
examples.code:
-- When gamepad A is pressed on a focused TextInput:
-- lua/init.lua calls OSK.open(node, joystickId, pushEvent)

-- OSK opens and takes over gamepad input.
-- When user selects a key:

-- For character keys ("a", "1", "!"):
OSK.injectText("a")  -- calls pushEvent("textinput", { text = "a" })

-- For backspace:
pushEvent("keydown", { key = "backspace", node = targetNode })

-- For submit (Enter):
pushEvent("keydown", { key = "return", node = targetNode })
OSK.close()

-- Target TextInput receives these events via normal event dispatch
-- and updates its state accordingly. No special handling needed.

examples.title: Multi-Layout Form
OSK Navigation Patterns
examples.code:
-- Gamepad Controls (automatic, no config needed):

-- D-pad / Left Stick:
--   Up    → move selection up
--   Down  → move selection down
--   Left  → move selection left
--   Right → move selection right

-- A button:
--   Select highlighted key (insert character or perform action)

-- B button:
--   Close OSK without submitting

-- Analog stick navigation:
--   Smooth cursor movement with acceleration
--   NAV_SPEED controls sensitivity

-- D-pad repeat:
--   Hold D-pad direction for continuous movement
--   NAV_REPEAT_DELAY: initial delay before repeat starts (0.4s)
--   NAV_REPEAT_RATE: repeat interval (0.1s)

-- Shift key behavior:
--   Press Shift → next character is uppercase
--   Shift is "sticky" — highlighted after press, deactivated after next char

criticalRules: **Love2D only** — the OSK is Lua-owned and uses Love2D's drawing and gamepad APIs. Not available in terminal, web, or other targets.
**Singleton pattern** — only one OSK can be open at a time. Opening for a new TextInput closes the previous OSK.
**Gamepad-triggered only** — the OSK does NOT open for mouse/keyboard focus. It only opens when a TextInput is focused via gamepad navigation.
**Intercepts all gamepad input** — when OSK is open, gamepad events do NOT propagate to the game/app. Press B to close OSK and resume normal input.
**No touch support yet** — the OSK is designed for gamepad navigation. Touch/mouse support would require additional hit testing and event handling.
**Context-aware layout selection** — `keyboardType` prop determines initial layout. Supported values: `"default"`, `"numeric"`. Other types (email, url, phone) fall back to default.
**Layout switching during session** — users can switch between letters, symbols, and numeric layouts using action keys (ABC, #+=, 123) during a single OSK session.
**Shift is sticky** — pressing Shift highlights the key and uppercases the NEXT character only. Not a toggle. For caps lock behavior, hold Shift and press multiple keys.
**Submit closes OSK** — pressing Enter/submit closes the OSK and triggers a "return" keydown event on the TextInput. Apps can handle this to submit forms.

**Text injection uses existing events** — OSK does NOT directly mutate TextInput state. It pushes "textinput" and "keydown" events, which are handled by the normal event dispatcher. This keeps the OSK decoupled from React.
**Requires measure.lua** — OSK uses the text measurement API to calculate key sizes and layout. `OSK.init({ measure = ... })` is required.
**No custom keyboard layouts from React** — keyboard layouts are Lua-side constants. To add a new layout (e.g., Cyrillic, Arabic), edit `lua/osk.lua` directly.
**Backspace only deletes one character** — no support for word deletion or selection manipulation. Backspace triggers a single "backspace" keydown event.
**No paste support** — OSK is character-by-character input only. For clipboard paste, use keyboard shortcuts (Ctrl+V) when OSK is closed.
**Analog stick navigation is smooth** — left stick provides cursor-style navigation with acceleration. D-pad navigation is discrete (one key per press).
**Visual feedback** — selected key is highlighted. Pressed keys briefly change color. Shift key shows "active" state when pressed.
seeAlso: **TextInput** — primary component that triggers OSK on gamepad focus
**Event Dispatcher** — pushEvent() for injecting text into TextInput
**Accessibility** — OSK enables text entry for users without keyboards (console-style games, Steam Deck, etc.)
**Love2D Input** — love.joystick, love.gamepadpressed for underlying gamepad APIs

metadata.title: Performance Tips
metadata.description: Optimization strategies for ReactJIT applications

sections.overview:
ReactJIT runs a full layout pass on every frame where the tree is dirty. The layout engine, text measurement, and painter all have costs that scale with tree size and complexity. Most applications run comfortably at 60fps, but deeply nested trees, large text blocks, or unvirtualized long lists can cause frame drops.

The key optimization principle is the same as in any flexbox system: keep the tree shallow, give the layout engine as much information as possible upfront, and avoid unnecessary re-computation.

examples.title: Keep Flex Trees Shallow
examples.code:
SLOW: Deep nesting forces the layout engine to resolve more constraints.

  <Box>
    <Box>
      <Box>
        <Box>
          <Text style={{ fontSize: 14 }}>Deeply nested</Text>
        </Box>
      </Box>
    </Box>
  </Box>

FAST: Flat structure with flexDirection row/column does the same layout
in fewer nodes.

  <Box style={{ flexDirection: 'row' }}>
    <Text style={{ fontSize: 14 }}>Flat and fast</Text>
  </Box>

Each additional nesting level adds layout computation. Prefer
flexDirection: 'row' on a single Box over nested column wrappers.

examples.title: Use FlatList for Long Lists
Use AnimatedValue for Smooth Animations
Pre-Compute Grid Dimensions

criticalRules: Keep flex trees shallow. Every nesting level costs layout time.
Use FlatList for any list longer than ~50 items.
Use AnimatedValue for animations. Do not animate via useState.
Pre-compute dimensions when you know them. Do not rely on child
content to infer container size.
Fill the viewport. Love2D is a fixed canvas, not a scrolling page.
Empty space below content is wasted if you do not set explicit sizes.
Avoid letterSpacing on large text blocks. Character-by-character
rendering is expensive.

metadata.title: Advanced Rendering (CodeBlock & Text Selection)
metadata.description: Syntax-highlighted code blocks and interactive text selection

sections.overview:
Two specialized rendering components extend ReactJIT with advanced text features: CodeBlock for syntax-highlighted source code display, and TextSelection for interactive text selection with clipboard support.

Both are Lua-owned modules like TextEditor: they render directly via love.graphics rather than going through the React layout system. This gives precise control over line spacing, highlighting colors, and text metrics.
sections.api:
---- CodeBlock Module (codeblock.lua) ----

CodeBlock.init(config)
  Initialize the CodeBlock module. Call once at startup.

  config: table with keys:
          measure - the measure.lua module (for font metrics)

  Example:
    local codeblock = require("lua.codeblock")
    codeblock.init({ measure = measure })

CodeBlock.render(node, ctx, effectiveOpacity)
  Render a code block node. Called by the painter during tree traversal.
  (Low-level; use React <CodeBlock> component instead.)

  node: the code block instance
  ctx:  painter context
  effectiveOpacity: transparency level (0-1)

CodeBlock.measure(node)
  Measure the dimensions of a code block. Called during layout.
  (Low-level; used internally.)

  Returns: { width, height }

Node Properties:
  style.backgroundColor    - background color (table: {r, g, b, a})
  style.color              - text color (table or table array for syntax coloring)
  children[1].children[1].content  - the code string (Text node)
  children[1].props.language       - optional language hint (js, lua, tsx, py, etc.)

---- Text Selection Module (textselection.lua) ----

TextSelection.init(config)
  Initialize the text selection module. Call once at startup.

  config: table with keys:
          measure - the measure.lua module
          events  - the events.lua module

  Example:
    local textselection = require("lua.textselection")
    textselection.init({ measure = measure, events = events })

TextSelection.isSelectable(node)
  Check if a Text node allows selection.

  Returns: true unless node.style.userSelect == "none"

TextSelection.start(node, line, col)
  Start a text selection on a Text node at a specific line and column.
  Called by event handlers when mouse is pressed on a selectable Text node.

  line: 1-based line number
  col:  0-based character column

TextSelection.update(line, col)
  Update selection endpoint during drag. Called by mousemove handlers.

  line, col: same as start()

TextSelection.finalize()
  Finalize the selection (mouse released). Extracts the selected text and
  stores it. Clears selection if zero-length. Called by mousereleased handlers.

TextSelection.clear()
  Clear the active selection. Called when clicking outside text or
  programmatically.

TextSelection.copyToClipboard()
  Copy selected text to system clipboard. Called when Ctrl+C / Cmd+C is pressed.

TextSelection.render(node, ctx, effectiveOpacity)
  Render the selection highlight and selected text. Called by painter.
  (Low-level; called automatically.)

TextSelection.getSelection()
  Get the current selection state (for debugging).

  Returns: { text, lines, startLine, startCol, endLine, endCol, isDragging }
           or nil if no selection active

Node Properties:
  style.userSelect - "auto" (default, selectable) or "none" (not selectable)

examples.title: Display Syntax-Highlighted Code
Selectable Text Node
Code Block with Custom Styling

platformNotes.Love2D:
Both modules work with love.graphics directly. Performance is GPU-bound for
rendering, CPU-bound for selection hit-testing during mouse moves.

sections.commonPatterns:
CodeBlock: Display source code with syntax highlighting for JavaScript, Lua, TypeScript, JSX, and other languages. Useful for documentation, error messages, tutorials, REPLs.

Text Selection: Allow users to select and copy text from Text nodes. Useful for documentation, code blocks (combined), chat messages, or any read-only text that should be copyable. Supports multi-line selection and Ctrl+C / Cmd+C.
sections.performance: CodeBlock syntax highlighting is done at render time, not cached. For large code blocks (1000+ lines), consider splitting into multiple smaller blocks or optimizing line count. Text selection tracking has minimal overhead (stores selection state, updates on mouse move).
criticalRules: CodeBlock is Lua-owned; dimensions are computed directly, not via React layout
Text nodes with userSelect="auto" are selectable by default
Selection is global (only one selection active at a time)
Ctrl+C / Cmd+C is handled internally; no custom key binding needed
CodeBlock requires a language hint (js, lua, tsx, py, etc.) for best highlighting
TextSelection works only on Text nodes, not TextInput or other components
seeAlso: event-handling.txt - Mouse event handling and hit testing

metadata.title: Semantic Classification
metadata.description: Architecture of the classifier pipeline that converts raw PTY output into typed semantic tokens

sections.overview:
Semantic classification is the process that turns raw PTY bytes into a structured, typed view of terminal output. Instead of displaying ANSI color codes, the pipeline assigns each terminal row a semantic token — 'command', 'error', 'assistant_text', 'tool', etc. — and uses those tokens to drive color rendering, turn detection, graph construction, and session analysis.

The pipeline has three stages: vterm decodes raw PTY bytes into Unicode text rows (stripping ANSI codes); the classifier assigns a token to each row and optionally refines assignments using adjacency context; the semantic graph aggregates rows into turns, nodes, and state transitions. SemanticTerminal runs this pipeline on the Lua side and emits onClassifiedRow and onGraphUpdate events to the React layer.

The framework ships two classifiers: basic covers any interactive shell session, and claude_code covers the full Claude Code CLI. Custom classifiers are Lua files in lua/classifiers/ and are referenced by filename (without the .lua extension).
sections.api:
Classifier module interface (Lua):
```lua
local M = {}

M.name = "my_classifier"   -- identifier
M.version = "1.0"
M.cli = "myapp"            -- what command this classifier is for
M.description = "..."

-- Required: classify a single row of terminal text.
-- Returns: token string, plus optional extra1, extra2 fields.
function M.classifyRow(text, row, totalRows)
  -- text: plain Unicode text of the row (ANSI stripped)
  -- row: 0-based row index in the terminal buffer
  -- totalRows: total rows in the current buffer
  return "output"   -- token
end

-- Optional: refine a token using context from the previous row.
-- Receives the initial token, the previous row's token, the text, and a context table.
-- Return the (possibly revised) token.
function M.refineAdjacency(kind, prevKind, text, context)
  return kind
end

-- Optional: returns true if this row starts a new turn in the semantic graph.
function M.isTurnStart(kind)
  return false
end

-- Optional: group type for this token (e.g. "menu", "picker", "task").
-- Used to group consecutive rows of the same logical unit.
M.groupTypes = {}

-- Optional: tokens where consecutive rows should be merged into a single block.
M.blockTypes = {}

return M
```

Built-in classifiers:
| Classifier  | Tokens | Target           | Description                                      |
|-------------|--------|------------------|--------------------------------------------------|
| basic       | 7      | Any shell        | command, output, error, success, heading, separator, progress |
| claude_code | 25+    | Claude Code CLI  | Full vocabulary covering all UI states (see below) |

claude_code token vocabulary:
| Group        | Tokens                                                       |
|--------------|--------------------------------------------------------------|
| Conversation | user_prompt, user_text, user_input, thinking, thought_complete, assistant_text |
| Tool output  | tool, result, diff, error                                    |
| Chrome       | banner, status_bar, idle_prompt, input_border, input_zone, box_drawing |
| Interactive  | menu_title, menu_option, menu_desc, list_selectable, list_selected, list_info, search_box, selector, confirmation, hint |
| Pickers      | picker_title, picker_item, picker_selected, picker_meta      |
| Permissions  | permission                                                   |
| Plan mode    | plan_border, plan_mode, wizard_step                          |
| Tasks        | task_summary, task_done, task_open, task_active              |
| Misc         | slash_menu, image_attachment                                 |

basic token vocabulary:
| Token     | Description                                      |
|-----------|--------------------------------------------------|
| command   | Shell prompt + command line (isTurnStart = true) |
| output    | Normal command output                            |
| error     | Error messages (stderr-like)                     |
| success   | Success indicators, exit confirmations           |
| heading   | Section headers, bold/underlined separators      |
| separator | Visual rule lines                                |
| progress  | Progress bars, spinners                          |

examples.title: Custom classifier for a REPL
examples.code:
```lua
-- lua/classifiers/python_repl.lua
local M = {}
M.name = "python_repl"
M.version = "1.0"
M.cli = "python3"
M.description = "Classifier for Python interactive sessions"

M.tokens = { "prompt", "output", "error", "traceback", "value" }

function M.classifyRow(text, row, totalRows)
  if text:match("^>>>") or text:match("^%.%.%.") then return "prompt" end
  if text:match("^Traceback") then return "traceback" end
  if text:match("^%s+File .-,") or text:match("^%w+Error:") then return "error" end
  return "output"
end

function M.refineAdjacency(kind, prevKind, text, context)
  -- Lines after traceback frames are part of the traceback
  if kind == "output" and (prevKind == "traceback" or prevKind == "error") then
    return "error"
  end
  return kind
end

function M.isTurnStart(kind)
  return kind == "prompt"
end

M.blockTypes = { output = true, error = true, traceback = true }

return M
```

examples.title: Reading classification events in React

criticalRules: Classifiers are stateless per-row functions. Do not store state between classifyRow calls — the framework may call it in any order during seek or playback.
refineAdjacency receives the output of classifyRow for the current row and the classification of the previous row. It is optional; return kind unchanged if no refinement is needed.
The classifier prop is the Lua filename without .lua. A file at lua/classifiers/my_app.lua is referenced as classifier="my_app".
Classification happens at display time, not at capture time. Recordings store raw PTY bytes; you can replay a session through a different classifier without re-recording.

isTurnStart should return true exactly once per logical conversation turn. For shell sessions this is every command prompt; for Claude Code this is every user_prompt row. Over-triggering creates noise in the semantic graph.

metadata.title: Session Recording
metadata.description: Recording and playback of PTY sessions as .rec.lua files — classifier-independent capture

sections.overview:
Session recording captures raw PTY output bytes with high-resolution timestamps into a .rec.lua file. The recording is classifier-independent: it stores the raw terminal stream, not classified tokens. Classification happens at playback time, so you can replay the same recording through different classifiers and see different semantic interpretations.

The .rec.lua format is plain Lua source that returns a table with a meta section (CLI name, dimensions, timestamp, duration, frame count) and a frames array (each frame: { t = seconds, data = "raw bytes" }). The Lua format needs no JSON parser and loads with a plain loadfile() call.

Recording is controlled via two props on SemanticTerminal: recording={true} enables capture during a live session, and saveRecording(path) on the useSemanticTerminal hook flushes the buffer to disk. Playback is controlled via mode="playback" and playbackSrc pointing to a .rec.lua file.
sections.api:
